Ibiza

by LovettBlack

Summary

Agatha, a young omega, put her dream trip to Ibiza on a twelve-month payment plan. Sun, parties, freedom — everything was perfect… until she came back home with a “souvenir” that would arrive in nine months.

Desperate to get her life together before time runs out, Agatha accepts a job at Vidal International Holdings. The salary is too good to refuse, and she needs it more than ever. What Agatha doesn’t know is that the company’s CEO, the enigmatic and intimidating Rio Vidal, also happened to be in Ibiza that same night.

And more importantly, she’s the other mother of Agatha’s baby.

Now trapped in the same company and tied together by a secret neither of them expected, Agatha and Rio must figure out whether that night was just a summer mistake or the beginning of something impossible to ignore?

Chapter 1: Dreams Come True

 

 

Dreams Come True
┕──━──━─┑◆┍─━──━──┙

Agatha couldn’t believe it when she finally clicked “confirm purchase” on the airline website, sitting between Jen and Wanda, the three of them squeezed onto the small couch in the apartment, sharing a cheap bottle of sparkling wine as if they were already celebrating in Ibiza.

It had been two years saving money, refusing invitations, counting coins and installments. Two years repeating to herself that one day she would step onto that island where summer seemed to never end.

She also didn’t believe it when she boarded the plane crowded with people who definitely looked richer than them. People who probably didn’t have to split their tickets into twelve payments or say “no” to small luxuries for so long. Agatha discreetly observed the designer luggage, the expensive watches, and the striking perfumes. For a moment, she felt small, but the excitement was bigger than any insecurity.

At the airport in Madrid, waiting for the connecting flight to the island, reality still seemed suspended. Even while buying tickets to the most famous parties and raves in the world, even with the confirmation email blinking on her phone screen, it felt like everything was a collective delirium shared by the three of them.

It only truly sank in when the plane landed and they stepped onto the hot runway, feeling the dense, salty air touch their skin. The Mediterranean heat wrapped around Agatha like an embrace. The sky was absurdly blue, and there was an energy vibrating in the air—promises, excess, freedom.

Ibiza.

They managed to get a hotel for a price miraculously below the island’s standard. Still expensive enough to burden their credit cards for the rest of the year. The room was simple but clean, with a small balcony and a partial sea view. For them, it was perfect.

“I can’t believe we actually made it,” Wanda said, throwing herself onto the bed with a dramatic sigh, arms spread as if embracing the ceiling.

“I just want to get a tan and never go back home,” Jen added, dropping her suitcase on the floor and collapsing beside her.

Agatha laughed, leaving her own suitcase on the other bed. She walked to the balcony and opened the glass doors. The distant sound of electronic music seemed constant, as if the whole island breathed to the same rhythm.

“Girls, we can go to the beach now, rest a little…” she turned around, eyes shining. “After all, we have tickets to Ushuaïa. I want to party until the next day.”

“Sounds perfect,” Wanda cheered, already searching for her bikini in her suitcase.

Agatha leaned against the balcony door for a moment, letting the sun warm her face. She was in her twenties, with no immediate responsibilities and an entire week ahead of her.

Dreams come true, she thought.

They didn’t take long to change clothes. The room quickly filled with the sweet smell of sunscreen, laughter, and the sound of zippers opening. Agatha put on a simple black bikini, one that hugged her curves in an almost insolent way. She tied her hair into a casual bun and put on sunglasses as if that instantly transformed her into someone who belonged there.

The walk to the beach was a spectacle on its own. Narrow streets, white façades reflecting the sun, bougainvillea exploding in shades of pink from the balconies. Convertible cars passed slowly, loud music escaping from open windows. There were laughs, different languages, golden confident bodies walking as if the entire world were their personal runway.

When they finally stepped onto the sand, Agatha had to close her eyes for a second.

The sea was an almost unreal shade of blue, crystal clear, reflecting the light in a hypnotic way. Yachts anchored in the distance sparkled like jewels on the water. The sand was warm, fine, almost white.

“This isn’t real,” Jen murmured, already spreading out her beach wrap.

Wanda didn’t answer. She was already removing her cover-up, revealing a vibrant red bikini that immediately attracted attention.

Agatha felt her own heart quicken—there was something in the air. An electric, dense energy. As if every person there lived in a constant state of anticipation.

They settled near the sea. The sound of the waves mixed with the distant bass of a nearby beach club. The wind carried the scent of salt, coconut, and expensive perfume.

Agatha lay on her stomach to sunbathe, feeling the heat slowly sink into her skin. She closed her eyes. For a few minutes, everything was simple. The sound of the water. The laughter of her friends. The occasional touch of wind moving loose strands of hair.

A few minutes later she got up, unable to stay still for long. The island’s energy seemed to run under her skin like a soft electric current.

“I’m going in,” she announced, already walking toward the sea.

“Take my dignity if you find some rich European alpha!” Wanda shouted, making Jen laugh.

The water was warm, more than she expected. When the first wave touched her feet, Agatha let out an involuntary sigh. The contrast between the sun’s heat and the salty freshness made her skin shiver with simple, almost childish pleasure. She walked until the water reached her waist, then dove in all at once.

For a few seconds, everything was silent.

Only the muffled sound of the submerged world, her own heart beating steadily, her hair spreading like a dark cloud around her face. When she emerged, she pushed her hair back and laughed to herself.

Around her, groups toasted with champagne glasses even though it was early afternoon. A couple danced in the water as if the music came from inside them. Farther away, a beach club began raising the volume—the electronic beat blending with the natural sound of the waves.

Agatha returned to the sand with her skin shining under the sun. Jen was almost asleep, while Wanda was animatedly talking to two French tourists who had settled beside them.

“I already love this place,” Wanda declared, gesturing too much. “Here nobody seems worried about anything.”

Agatha sat on the beach wrap, hugging her knees for a moment. She watched the horizon. It was true. There, no one seemed to carry bills, family expectations, or the constant pressure to prove something to the world.

There, they were just three young omegas living their own summer.

“Today we rest,” Agatha said, putting her sunglasses back on. “But tonight…”

“Tonight we become legends,” Jen finished without opening her eyes.

They spent hours there. Alternating between long swims, naps under the sun, and strategic photos to immortalize the moment. Their skin began to gain color, their hair turned salty, and their whole bodies felt looser, more alive.

When the sun slowly began to set, painting the sky in golden and pink tones, Agatha felt a different anticipation growing inside her.

 

◆

The days in Ibiza quickly began to lose their edges.

The first night at Ushuaïa was only the beginning. They spent hours getting ready in the hotel room, trying on clothes as if choosing versions of themselves. Glitter on their shoulders, eyeliner sharper than usual, dresses shorter than anything they would wear in their hometown—but absolutely perfect there.

When they arrived at the club, the impact was physical.

Lights cutting through the sky, jets of smoke rising with the electronic beat, sweaty bodies dancing as if tomorrow didn’t exist. The bass vibrated in their chests. The floor trembled. The air smelled like expensive perfume, alcohol, and freedom.

Agatha danced until her legs hurt. Wanda climbed onto a stranger’s shoulders at some point in the middle of the night. Jen disappeared for twenty minutes and returned laughing, glitter spread across her neck and a new drink in her hand.

They returned to the hotel with burning feet, smudged makeup, and hamburgers bought at four in the morning.

The next day, the hangover was collective.

Curtains closed. Dramatic groans. Wanda swearing she would never drink again—a promise that lasted less than 48 hours. Agatha woke up with a dry mouth and fragmented memories of flashing lights and hands holding her waist while she danced.

The week continued at that almost irresponsible rhythm.

Beach in the morning—even with dark circles hidden behind oversized sunglasses. Naps in the afternoon. And at night, different clubs.

Pacha. Hï. Amnesia.

Each place had its own vibe. At Pacha, the sensuality was slower, more enveloping. At Hï, everything felt modern, almost futuristic. At Amnesia, it seemed like the entire world fit inside a single dance floor.

Agatha began to let go in a way she had never allowed herself before. She danced with strangers without asking names. Accepted drinks that appeared in her hands. Laughed loudly without measuring the volume. She felt desired—and she liked it.

Jen was the first to truly get involved with someone.

Alice appeared on the third night, at Hï. Tall, relaxed posture, confident smile. She was an alpha—you could tell without effort. Not because of aggression, but because of calm confidence. She approached Jen at the bar, leaned close to say something in her ear because of the loud music, and the rest happened naturally.

Jen returned to the hotel at sunrise that day.

“She’s ridiculously beautiful,” she murmured, throwing herself on the bed while Wanda and Agatha stared at her like investigators. “And she smells expensive.”

“Is that important?” Wanda laughed.

“Very.”

Alice began appearing on the following nights. Sometimes she stayed briefly. Sometimes she danced with Jen for hours, hands firm on her waist, guiding her confidently. Nothing explicit happened, but the tension between them was visible, almost tangible.

By the middle of the week, the three of them were sunburned, with small mysterious bruises on their legs from bumping into people on crowded dance floors, and an absurd collection of disconnected stories.

One afternoon, Agatha woke up alone in the room while the others still slept. She sat on the balcony watching the sea. Her skin was more golden. Her body looser. A strange feeling was growing inside her—as if something were approaching, even though she didn’t know what.

On Friday, Jen entered the room holding her phone and wearing a smile that meant trouble.

“Alice got invitations.”

Wanda lifted her head from the pillow.

“Invitations to where?”

Jen bit her lower lip, almost theatrically.

“A private party. Last night of the season. Entry only with a name on the list. Closed villa. International DJs. Businesswomen. Big people.”

Agatha felt her stomach tighten—not from fear. From anticipation.

“And why did she get that?”

“Because she knows the right people,” Jen answered simply. “And because I asked.”

The last night started differently.

It wasn’t just another club. They spent even more time getting ready. Agatha chose a black dress that hugged her body with minimalist elegance. Nothing too flashy—but impossible to ignore. Her hair loose, natural waves falling down her back. The makeup more precise, less exaggerated.

When they arrived at the villa, they understood.

Luxury cars lined up discreetly. Security at the entrance. Music echoing from inside, deep and sophisticated. The pool illuminated in deep blue. People talking in small groups, crystal glasses in their hands.

The entrance to the villa was almost cinematic.

A security guard checked the names on the digital list. Alice appeared behind them with the confident smile of someone who already belonged to that environment. After a discreet greeting, an elegant employee placed thin black fabric wristbands on the three of them—the kind of simple detail that screamed exclusivity.

As soon as they crossed the illuminated garden, the impact came.

The pool reflected golden lights. Bottles Agatha had only seen in display windows were open on marble tables. French champagne, rare vodka labels, aged whisky in wooden boxes displayed like trophies. People dressed in light tailoring, silk dresses, discreet and absurdly expensive jewelry.

“This place pays our rent for a year,” Wanda murmured, picking up a glass that seemed too delicate for her still slightly trembling hands from excitement.

At first, they tried to maintain some composure.

They talked. Walked around. Pretended to be natural.

But the first glass became two. Two became four. Someone brought shots with pink salt. Then a citrus drink served in a crystal glass too heavy to ignore.

Agatha laughed too loudly at something she no longer remembered. Her face felt warm, her body light, the music penetrating her skin like an electric current. The beat there wasn’t as aggressive as in the clubs—it was enveloping, hypnotic, almost sensual.

The world began to blur slightly at the edges.

Wanda danced with a group near the pool. Jen sat on the edge with her feet in the water, talking to Alice who held her glass as if she had been born with it in her hand.

Agatha went to the improvised dance floor near the DJ.

She didn’t know how many drinks she had already had. She only knew her body felt loose, warm, pulsing to the rhythm of the music. The dress clung lightly to her sweaty skin. Her movements became slower, more fluid.

That was when she felt it. A strong smell. Unmistakable, Alpha. Not soft. Not distant. Extremely present.

Agatha swallowed, even with her mind clouded by alcohol. Her body reacted before she could organize any thought.

A presence approached from behind. Warm. Too close.

“What’s your name?” the voice came low, hoarse, equally intoxicated, carrying a slight accent Agatha couldn’t identify.

She partially turned her face, trying to focus.

“Aggie,” she answered, the nickname slipping easily from her lips.

The scent grew stronger as the person stepped even closer. Agatha felt firm—but not invasive—hands rest on her waist.

“May I stay here with this beautiful lady?” the voice said in her ear.

“I wouldn’t want to be anywhere else,” Agatha replied, pushing her hips slightly back against the Alpha’s center, feeling her member harden.

She lifted her gaze. Brown eyes, deep. Too beautiful.

But the face… the face was blurred. She didn’t know if it was the golden light of the party or the alcohol running through her veins, but the features refused to settle. Only the eyes remained clear.

“Do you want to dance, Aggie?” the alpha asked, leaning slightly so her voice could cut through the music.

Agatha should have thought, should have stepped away. But the world was no longer a place for rational decisions.

“I do,” she replied, smiling carelessly.

The hand on her waist tightened slightly. The other slid to her lower back. They began moving together. First to the rhythm of the music. Then to the rhythm of each other.

Their bodies fit naturally, as if that closeness had been rehearsed. The alpha’s breath brushed against Agatha’s neck. Her scent mixed with sweat, alcohol, and expensive perfume.

Agatha placed her hands on the woman’s shoulders to keep her balance—or maybe to shorten the distance even more.

The world around them disappeared, leaving only the bass of the music. The heat. The touch. And those brown eyes that, even blurred, seemed far too fixed on her.

The distance between them slowly ceased to exist.

First it was only the synchronized sway of their bodies. Then the alpha’s hand moved a few centimeters up along the side of Agatha’s dress, firm enough to keep her close. Warm breath touching her skin.

Aggie.

The name sounded different in her mouth. Slower. Heavier.

Agatha felt her heart beat unevenly, amplified by the alcohol. The world spun too slowly and too fast at the same time. Her fingers lightly gripped the fabric of the other woman’s shirt, searching for balance—or courage.

The alpha tilted her head.

“You’re beautiful, Aggie,” she murmured, her lips so close they almost touched.

Agatha should have answered with something witty. She should have teased. But the woman’s scent was too strong. Enveloping. Dominant in a way that wasn’t aggressive, only inevitable.

“You are too,” she managed to say, her voice lower than she intended.

The music changed. The bass deepened, and then it happened. The alpha rested her forehead against hers for a second before sliding a hand to the back of her neck.

The kiss started slowly, a light brush of lips.

Agatha responded immediately.

It tasted like expensive champagne and something warmer. Her hand rose to the alpha’s face, holding her there as if afraid she would disappear if she let go. The kiss deepened naturally, unhurried but with a growing intensity that made Agatha’s entire body tremble.

The world around them dissolved into blurred lights. There was only the warmth, and the perfect fit of their mouths. The sensation of the firm hand on her waist, pulling her closer.

Agatha felt the air leave her lungs when they pulled apart for a second, just to breathe. Her eyes tried to focus again on that face, but the features remained imprecise. Only the brown eyes stayed clear, fixed on her with something that went beyond drunkenness.

“Stay with me tonight,” the alpha whispered, her thumb tracing a slow path along the side of Agatha’s neck.

Agatha was too drunk to analyze consequences. Too drunk to realize that this decision would change everything. She simply smiled, still slightly breathless.

“I will.”

◆

The first thing Agatha felt was silence.

Not the ordinary silence of a hotel room at dawn, but a silence too wide. Too strange.

She moved slowly in the enormous bed, the absurdly soft sheets sliding across her skin. The mattress was firmer than the one in the hotel where she was staying with the girls. The smell on the pillow was different too—more sophisticated, woody, far from the sweet perfume she usually wore.

She slowly opened her eyes.

The room wasn’t hers.

High ceiling. Heavy linen curtains. A wide balcony letting in the golden morning light. Minimalist furniture, far too elegant for the budget she had paid in twelve installments.

The double bed was completely empty.

Agatha stayed still for a few seconds, trying to organize her thoughts. Her body stretched almost instinctively, muscles still relaxed and skin sensitive. As she moved, fragmented memories came like flashes: firm hands on her waist, the heat of another body pressed against hers, a kiss that seemed endless.

But the face… a blur.

She sighed deeply and immediately regretted it. The headache pulsed hard, throbbing behind her eyes. Hangover. She slowly stretched her arm toward the nightstand. Her phone was there. Beside it, a small folded note written in elegant handwriting.

“I loved our night, Aggie.
 With affection, R*

By the way, everything is already paid. Don’t worry.”

She took the pills beside it into her mouth without hesitation, drinking the water right after. She needed that pain to fade before trying to reconstruct any memory.

And she picked up the phone, the screen black.

“Fuck…” she murmured, running a hand over her face.

No battery, of course. She breathed deeply, feeling the slight discomfort in her body that wasn’t pain—it was physical memory. A lingering sensation of having been touched with care and intensity. An involuntary shiver ran down her spine as she remembered lips far too close.

But nothing concrete, only the brown eyes.

Agatha got up carefully. The room spun lightly for a second before stabilizing. She walked to the spacious bathroom, covered in pale marble. The mirror revealed smudged makeup, messy hair, and a different kind of glow in her eyes.

She turned on the shower and stepped under the cold water, letting it run over her shoulders, trying to wash away the excess of the previous night. While the water flowed, she tried to force the memory.

The name, the voice. The face. Nothing. Only warmth. Music. And that unmistakable alpha scent.

When she left the shower, she put on the same dress from the night before. She had no other option. She slipped on her heels, put on sunglasses to hide the dark circles and her undefined emotional state, and took a deep breath before leaving the room.

The hallway was silent and far too luxurious.

In the elevator, she briefly watched herself in the mirror. She looked like someone who had lived something big but couldn’t prove it. When she reached the reception desk, she approached the counter trying to keep her posture steady.

“I was in room 1003,” she said, her voice slightly hoarse.

The receptionist smiled with impeccable professionalism.

“Yes, miss. Everything is fine. Everything has already been paid.”

Agatha’s heart gave a slightly stronger beat.

Paid.

“Thank you,” she replied, trying to sound natural. She left the hotel feeling the sun hit her face strongly. She called a taxi with a hand still slightly trembling.

As the car drove away, she looked one last time at the elegant building disappearing behind her. Something there had been important. Even if her memory insisted on keeping her in the dark.

The taxi stopped in front of their hotel shortly after ten in the morning.

Agatha paid the ride with the last scraps of dignity she still possessed and got out of the car adjusting her sunglasses. Her head still throbbed lightly, but the pills were beginning to work. The dress from the night before revealed exactly the kind of night she had had.

She took a deep breath.

And walked in.

As soon as she crossed the glass door of the simple lobby—much simpler than the hotel of room 1003—the scene made her stop mid-step. Jen was standing there, hair messy, makeup smeared from the night before, gesturing dramatically. Wanda stood beside her, visibly distressed.

And there were two police officers.

“…she just DISAPPEARED!” Jen was saying, her voice far too loud for the hour.
 “We woke up and she wasn’t there! Her phone off! This is Ibiza!”

Wanda held her friend’s arm, trying to maintain some composure.

“We just want to know if something happened…” she explained to the officers.

That was when Jen saw Agatha standing at the entrance. Her eyes widened.

“YOU DISGUSTING LITTLE BITCH!”

The entire lobby went silent.

Agatha raised an eyebrow behind her sunglasses. Wanda immediately turned around, and the relief on her face was so visible it almost hurt.

“Thank God,” she sighed, placing a hand over her chest before turning to the officers.

“Thank you very much, gentlemen. I think… we’ve already found the missing person.”

The officers looked Agatha up and down: party dress, heels in her hand, hair still damp from the shower, an expression of hangover and mystery.

“Miss, are you alright?” one of them asked.

Agatha slowly lifted her sunglasses, revealing eyes still slightly swollen but very much alive.

“I woke up in an expensive hotel,” she said, her voice hoarse and completely serious. “In an expensive bed. With a good morning note. And the room paid for.”

The officers exchanged glances. Jen’s mouth fell open. Wanda blinked twice.

“Excuse me?” Wanda murmured.

Agatha shrugged as if reporting something trivial.

“There were even headache pills next to the bed. Five-star service.”

One of the officers cleared his throat, clearly concluding that this was no longer a matter of public safety but of questionable life choices.

“Alright… well, if everything is fine, ladies, we’ll take our leave.”

“Everything is great,” Agatha assured, putting her sunglasses back on.

As soon as the officers walked out the door, the silence lasted exactly three seconds. Jen moved first.

“YOU DISAPPEAR THE WHOLE NIGHT, AGATHA HARKNESS!”

“I thought you had been kidnapped!” Wanda added, still pale.

“I was kidnapped,” Agatha replied, already starting up the stairs.

“But apparently with French champagne and Egyptian sheets.”

“You’re joking.”

Once in the room, Agatha dropped her heels on the floor and threw herself onto the bed, exhausted.

Jen crossed her arms.

“Who was it?”

Agatha closed her eyes for a moment. Brown eyes. Alpha scent. Firm hands. A kiss that still felt imprinted on her lips. She slowly opened her eyes.

“I… don’t know.”

“HOW DO YOU NOT KNOW?” Jen almost shouted.

“I was very drunk.”

Wanda sat on the edge of the other bed.

“Do you at least remember the name?”

Agatha hesitated.

“Just the initial. R.”

The three of them stayed silent for a moment. Outside, the distant sound of the sea remained the same. As if nothing had changed.

But it had.

The rest of the day passed in a lazy haze. They ordered food to the room, laughed too much remembering disconnected parts of the week, reviewed compromising photos, and promised again that next time they would be more responsible. An empty promise. All of them knew it.

At sunset, they walked along the beach one last time. The sky was painted in shades of orange and violet, and Agatha let the water touch her feet like a silent farewell. Ibiza still pulsed, as if the island did not recognize endings. Only pauses.

She unconsciously brought a hand to her neck, shivering as she remembered a touch she could not fully reconstruct.

R.

The letter felt too small for the size of what she felt.

The next morning, the airport was a collective portrait of what that weekend had been.

Giant sunglasses. People leaning on each other. Smudged makeup. Wrinkled clothes from the night before. Hoarse laughter mixed with expressions of pure physical regret. It was as if the entire island was recovering at the same time.

“Ibiza should issue medical certificates,” Wanda murmured, leaning against her suitcase.

Jen sat on the floor with her head resting on her backpack.

“I’m never drinking again.”

Agatha laughed softly.

“You said that on Tuesday.”

The flight was too quiet. Each of them lost in their own thoughts while the island disappeared behind the airplane window. The blue sea became small, distant, almost unreal. When they landed, the air felt heavier. More ordinary. Routine waited for them. Travel installments. Jobs. Responsibilities. Agatha turned on her phone as soon as the plane landed. Messages began appearing—family, bills, trivial notifications from real life.

Nothing from R.

No unknown number. No clue. She took a deep breath, trying to convince herself it had been just that: a night in Ibiza. An excess. A blurred memory that tasted like champagne.

Dreams come true, she thought again.

 

Chapter 2: The Consequences

Chapter Notes

Helloooo!

Enjoy the chapter! And I hope it brings a few good laughs, because apparently we have Rio Vidal dealing with nausea and having absolutely no idea why. Coincidence? Maybe. Suspicious? Definitely.

But anyway… they’re that kind of couple who shares everything.

Yes. Pathetic.
Yes. I love them for it.

 

 

┕──━──━─┑◆┍─━──━──┙

Six weeks.

Six weeks in which the sound of mornings was no longer the irritating alarm clock or the noise of traffic outside. Now it was always the same sound, dry, repetitive, unmistakable.

Vomiting.

Wanda opened her eyes before fully processing the sound. She stared at the ceiling for a few seconds, breathing deeply as if preparing for a battle whose script she already knew, then pushed the blanket aside and got out of bed. The apartment was still quiet, except for the echo coming from the bathroom.

She walked down the short hallway and found Agatha kneeling in front of the toilet, her body leaning too far forward, shoulders tense. Her face was almost inside the bowl as she vomited again, fingers gripping the white rim as if she needed something solid to keep from collapsing.

Wanda didn’t say anything at first.

She simply stepped closer and held Agatha’s hair back, pulling the dark strands away from her sweaty face. Agatha was breathing with difficulty between spasms.

The acidic smell mixed with the bathroom disinfectant made the air heavy. There were dark circles under her eyes, her skin far too pale for someone who six weeks earlier had been golden under the sun of Ibiza.

When her body finally gave in and the spasms eased, Agatha stayed there, leaning on her own arms, breathing deeply as if she had run miles.

Wanda grabbed a piece of toilet paper and handed it to her in silence.

“Six weeks, Agatha,” she finally said, her voice low but firm. “Six weeks.”

Agatha closed her eyes for a moment, resting her cold forehead against the rim of the toilet.

“It’s just stress,” she murmured hoarsely. “Change of routine. Accumulated hangover. I don’t know.”

Wanda let out a sigh that mixed concern and impatience.

“You haven’t been drinking for six weeks.”

Agatha flushed the toilet and sat down on the cold bathroom floor, leaning her back against the wall. The cool tile seemed to help her keep her head in place.

Wanda crouched in front of her.

“You feel nauseous every morning. You’re sensitive. You’re strange.” She hesitated before finishing: “And I know how to count.”

Agatha’s eyes slowly lifted.

There was something there. Something she had been avoiding naming. The memory of the party. The villa. Room 1003. The note. R.

The strong alpha scent still seemed to exist in the memory of her own body.

“Don’t start,” Agatha whispered.

Wanda held her gaze.

“I’m not starting anything. I’m just saying you need to see a doctor.”

Agatha placed a hand over her own abdomen almost unconsciously. A small gesture. Quick. But enough for Wanda to notice.

The bathroom suddenly felt too small.

“You think…?” Wanda asked, her voice now lower.

Jen had been standing at the bathroom doorway long enough to have heard everything.

She sighed slowly, crossing her arms for a second as if organizing her own courage. Then she turned and walked to the medicine cabinet in the hallway. She opened the small white door with familiarity and, after quickly rummaging among painkillers and bandages, pulled out an unmistakable blue box.

The click of the plastic echoed in the apartment’s silence.

When she returned to the bathroom, Agatha was already sitting on the floor, leaning against the side of the toilet after Wanda had flushed it and handed her a towel to clean her face.

Jen extended the box without ceremony.

“You can’t run anymore.”

Agatha stared at the object as if it might explode. The air grew heavy. She ran her tongue across her dry lips.

“Okay…” she murmured. “Leave. I need privacy.”

Wanda and Jen exchanged a look. Then Jen let out a short laugh.

“As if. I just cleaned the vomit caused by my nephew…” she gestured with her hand. “Hypothetical.”

Wanda laughed with her, too nervous to hold it in. Agatha huffed, rolling her eyes.

“Damn intimacy.”

With slightly trembling hands, she opened the box. The plastic seemed too loud. Every movement seemed too loud. Too real.

She did what she needed to do. Then she placed the test on the sink.

The next minutes were unbearable. Wanda sat on the floor. Jen leaned against the wall. No one spoke now. The humor had evaporated. Agatha was motionless. Her heart was beating so loudly she could hear it in her ears.

Six weeks. Ibiza. Room 1003.

R.

She stood slowly and picked up the test with cold fingers.

She looked and the world shifted on its axis.

Pregnant. 6 weeks.

The air seemed to disappear from the bathroom.

Agatha sat down on the floor again, this time sliding her back down the wall until she was fully supported. The test still in her hand. Jen and Wanda moved closer automatically, one on each side of her.

Silence.

Agatha let out a laugh. A short, incredulous laugh.

“This is… this is ridiculous,” she murmured, eyes fixed on the small display.

The laugh trembled. Broke. And turned into crying.

The tears came too fast, too warm. She brought a hand to her face, trying to contain them, but she couldn’t. It was fear. It was shock. It was the brutal understanding that that night had not stayed in Ibiza.

“I…” her voice failed. “I don’t even know who she is.”

Wanda pulled her first. Jen wrapped her arms around both of them right after. The embrace was tight, firm.

“You’re not alone,” Wanda whispered into her hair.

“Never,” Jen added.

Agatha cried against both their shoulders, the test still trapped between her fingers.

◆

A few minutes later, the crying had turned into a tired silence. The three of them were lying on Agatha’s bed, the faint smell of shampoo and bathroom disinfectant still in the air.

Agatha in the middle. Wanda on one side, Jen on the other.

The white ceiling suddenly seemed far too interesting for someone who had just discovered her life had changed forever.

Agatha kept her hands resting on her own abdomen, still flat.

Six weeks, it was almost nothing, and at the same time, it was everything.

Jen was the one who broke the silence.

“What are you going to do?”

The question carried no judgment. Only reality.

Agatha took a deep breath. Her chest rose slowly. She closed her eyes for a second, as if gathering momentum to jump from somewhere high.

“I’m keeping it,” she finally said.

The words came out firm. Firmer than she thought they could.

Wanda immediately turned her face to look at her.

“Are you sure?”

Agatha nodded slightly.

“I don’t know who she is. I don’t know how this is going to be. But…” she swallowed hard, “I know I can’t pretend this isn’t happening.”

The room fell silent again, but it was a different silence now. Less frightened. More solid.

Wanda draped an arm across her body, pulling her a little closer.

“We will support whatever choice you make. Always.”

Jen nodded in agreement.

“Always.”

Agatha felt a tightness in her chest, this time not from fear, but from gratitude. Then she sighed.

“I’ll have to leave the magazine.”

Both of them turned their heads at the same time.

“What?” Wanda asked.

“I write cultural articles. I get paid per publication. It’s not stable. It doesn’t pay decent health insurance. It doesn’t pay for diapers.”

She let out a dry laugh.

“I need a real job. One that pays more.”

Jen stayed silent for a few seconds, thinking. Then she abruptly stood up, grabbing her phone.

“I talked to Alice yesterday.”

Agatha slowly turned her head.

“The alpha?”

“Yes, the alpha,” Jen rolled her eyes, but she was serious. “She works at a big company. Very big. And she mentioned the boss is looking for a new secretary. The old one retired.”

Agatha’s heart quickened.

“Secretary?”

“Fixed salary. Benefits. Solid company.” Jen was already unlocking her phone. “I can send her your résumé right now and ask about the position.”

Agatha felt something she hadn’t felt since seeing the test result.

Hope.

“That would save my life.”

Jen was already typing.

“Send me your updated résumé.”

Wanda watched the two of them, still lying down.

“You really don’t remember anything?” she asked more gently now.

Agatha stayed silent. She forced the memory. The music. The illuminated pool. The strong scent. The kiss. The voice saying “Aggie” in a way that made her shiver.

“No,” she finally answered. “Just the initial. R.”

Wanda frowned.

“Nothing else?”

Agatha closed her eyes.

“Brown eyes.”

The letter seemed far too small for the size of the impact.

R.

She brought her hand to her abdomen again, almost unconsciously.

◆

One week later, Agatha stood in front of the mirror in her bedroom, trying to convince her stomach to cooperate.

The black pencil skirt hugged her curves with impeccable formality. The white blouse made of light fabric hid well how fragile she still felt inside. The discreet heels completed the image of someone confident.

On the outside.

Inside, she breathed deeply, fighting the light wave of nausea that insisted on appearing at the worst moments.

“Not today,” she murmured to herself, resting her hands on the dresser for a second.

She grabbed her purse, checked the folder with the printed documents, and left the bedroom.

The apartment was empty. Jen and Wanda had already left for work. Their absence made the silence feel larger than usual.

When she stepped outside, the New York morning breeze touched her face. It was different from Ibiza’s. Colder. More concrete. It smelled like coffee, concrete, and hurry.

Agatha breathed deeply.

She thought about walking to the subway. She needed to save money. Every dollar now had weight.

But a few meters ahead she saw the commotion at the corner. Sirens. People gathered. Traffic stopped. She closed her eyes for a second.

“Great.”

She checked the time. Punctuality was everything. She sighed and raised her arm, signaling a yellow taxi coming from the opposite direction. The car braked beside her with a light screech. Agatha got in, adjusting her skirt before closing the door. 

“Vidal International Holdings, please.”

The driver nodded and drove off.

During the ride, she tried to relax. She watched the buildings passing through the window, her own reflection on the glass. She discreetly placed a hand over her abdomen.

There was nothing visible yet.

It was still a secret. Only hers—and the girls’.

“You can do this,” she whispered to herself.

When the taxi turned the last corner, she saw it.

The building was enormous. Actually, two of them. Imposing, facing each other, separated by an internal plaza with minimalist landscaping. Mirrored glass façades reflecting the morning sky. Straight lines, modern architecture, discreet security at the entrance.

That wasn’t just a company. It was power materialized.

The taxi stopped. Agatha paid, stepped out, and stood for a few seconds on the sidewalk looking up. The top seemed to touch the clouds. She swallowed hard. Adjusted her shoulders.

And walked in.

The lobby was far too large to be called simply a reception. Pale marble floors, elegant indirect lighting, contemporary artwork on the walls. People moved at an efficient pace, all well dressed, all with aligned posture.

The air was cold and controlled, too quiet. Agatha walked to the main desk.

The receptionist—impeccable in a tailored gray blazer—looked up.

“Good morning.”

“Good morning. I’m here for an interview. Agatha Harkness.”

The woman typed something into the computer, checked a list, and nodded.

“Yes, Miss Harkness. You’re registered.” She printed a temporary badge and handed it to her.

“Top floor. Mrs. Wu Gulliver is waiting for you.” — Agatha thanked her, fastening the badge to her blouse.

The elevator was too fast. Too silent. The reflection in the metallic walls returned a version of her that looked more confident than she felt.

When the doors opened, the environment was even more impressive. Dark carpet. Large desks. Glass partitions. A panoramic view of the city occupying almost the entire side wall.

“Miss Harkness. Punctual.”

Agatha turned her head. Alice was leaning against one of the desks, dark blazer, relaxed posture, attentive gaze. Agatha’s heart gave a small leap.

“Alice, right? I remember.” — A subtle smile appeared on the alpha’s face.

“It’s good to see you again.” She gestured with her hand. “Come. I’ll conduct the first part of your interview. And you need to fill out some paperwork.”

“Of course,” Agatha replied, smiling back. — As they walked across the floor, Alice spoke naturally.

“Your résumé is excellent, Agatha. Journalism, published articles, university awards…”

Agatha maintained a steady posture.

“Thank you.” — Alice cast a sideways glance.

“Why aren’t you working in the field?”

Agatha took a breath before answering.

“I haven’t secured a solid opportunity yet. Occasional work, freelancing… nothing stable.”
 She kept her tone professional. “Right now, I need to build consistent savings. And I believe this position can offer that.”

Alice nodded slowly, satisfied with the answer.

“I understand.” — They stopped in front of a spacious room with frosted glass doors.

Alice opened it.

The interior was elegant and minimalist. A long dark wooden table. Comfortable chairs. And enormous windows revealing New York in all its grandeur—buildings, bridges, constant movement far below.

Agatha felt the nervousness tighten in her stomach again. But she kept her chin raised. Alice indicated the chair.

“Let’s begin.”

Alice sat on the other side of the table with impeccable posture and opened a slim folder with Agatha’s name printed on the cover.

“Let’s start with a few basic questions.”

The tone was professional, neutral. Nothing like the relaxed atmosphere of the nights in Ibiza. Here, Alice was a different person—precise, strategic.

The questions came in an organized sequence: previous experience, schedule organization, ability to handle pressure, confidentiality.

Agatha answered with surprising calm. Her voice did not falter. She spoke about tight deadlines at the magazine, last-minute interviews, unexpected trips to cover cultural events. She highlighted her adaptability, her objective writing, her discretion.

Alice took quick notes, observing every detail.

“The routine here begins at seven in the morning,” she said, briefly raising her eyes.

“Punctuality is essential. Sometimes earlier than that, depending on the CEO’s schedule. Is that a problem?”

Agatha felt her stomach tighten for a second.

“No,” she replied firmly. “It’s not a problem.”

Alice nodded, apparently satisfied. Then she slid a printed questionnaire across the table.

“I need you to fill this out before the next stage.”

Agatha took the sheet.

Personal data. Emergency contact. Professional history. Availability for travel. Confidentiality commitment. She read carefully, marking the answers with care. Until her eyes stopped.

“Are you currently pregnant or expecting?”

The world seemed to shrink a few inches. Alice noticed the pause.

“The last one left because she couldn’t balance the job with her child,” she explained naturally. 

“The workload here is intense.”  The air felt colder. Agatha felt her heart beating too hard.

Six weeks. She thought about the bathroom. The test. Wanda holding her arm. Jen saying you can’t run anymore.

She needed that job, stability and a fixed salary.

The pen felt heavy between her fingers.

…and… She marked a firm X on: “I am not pregnant and do not intend to be.”

The sound of the paper being turned seemed too loud. Alice took the form back without showing any reaction. Her eyes scanned the answers quickly.

“Alright.” — She closed the folder.

“Wait here for a moment. I’ll check if Mrs. Vidal is available.” — The name hung in the air like something too important to ignore.

Mrs. Vidal.

Alice stood up and left the room with confident steps, leaving Agatha alone in front of the immense view of New York. Agatha let out the breath she hadn’t realized she was holding.

Vidal.

She didn’t know why, but the word sent a slight shiver down the base of her spine. A few minutes later, the door opened again. Alice appeared with the same impeccable posture.

“Mrs. Vidal can see you now.”

Agatha’s heart sped up for no clear reason. She stood up, discreetly adjusted her skirt, and walked across the glass corridor. The door to the main office was slightly ajar.

She entered. And the scent came first. Woody. Elegant. Far too familiar for someone she had supposedly never met.

The office was spacious and quiet, with windows framing the city in the background. Behind the desk, the woman lifted her face from her papers and adjusted her glasses with calculated delicacy.

“Good morning. Welcome.” — The voice was low, controlled. Agatha felt something strange run down her spine.

“Good morning.”

She walked to the desk and sat down. Rio Vidal’s eyes were attentive, almost clinical.

“So, Miss Harkness. Why do you wish to work here? With such a strong résumé, you could have tried for a position at a newspaper.”

Agatha took a breath.

“At the moment, I’m looking for something that offers a bit more stability. I want to organize my financial life… have a more predictable routine.”

Rio tilted her head slightly, observing her as if evaluating something beyond the words.

“Alright. Are you aware of the schedule and the entire process?”

“Yes. Mrs. Wu explained everything.”

“Perfect.”

She flipped through the résumé once more.

“I see you have administrative knowledge and experience organizing schedules. I believe we won’t have any problems, correct, Miss Harkness?”

The voice was cold, but not harsh. It was as if she were distant from everything—detached, almost bored by the world around her.

“No, ma’am. I believe I will do the best job possible.”

An almost-smile curved Rio’s lips.

“Excellent. I like your spirit.”

She picked up the pen and signed the hiring letter with a firm stroke.

“Welcome to Vidal International Holdings.” — The name echoed with weight.

“It’s important that you know that, as my secretary, my schedule will be entirely in your hands. Financial cards, corporate commitments, events. I have many external activities and need someone to accompany me daily.”

Agatha felt her throat go dry.

“R… right.”

“Excellent.” — Rio rested her elbows on the desk, interlacing her fingers.

“Do you have children? I ask because we value total focus on work.”

Arrogant bitch, Agatha thought. The bathroom test flashed in her memory.

“No. As stated in the questionnaire.” Rio observed her for a second longer than necessary.

“Perfect.” — She leaned back in her chair.

“Then follow Alice. She will give you your materials and explain your responsibilities.”

“Perfect.” — Agatha stood, collecting her bag and phone. When she was almost at the door, the voice reached her.

“Miss Harkness.” — She stopped.

“Yes?” — Rio removed her glasses slowly. Her eyes were far too penetrating.

“Have we met somewhere before?” — The world seemed to hesitate for a moment.

Agatha searched her memory. Parties? Interviews? Events? Faces overlapped, but nothing concrete. No. It wasn’t possible.

“I don’t believe so, Mrs. Vidal.”

A brief silence. Rio nodded, putting her glasses back on.

“You may go.” Agatha left with her heart racing without knowing exactly why. And alone in the office, Rio Vidal remained motionless for a few seconds.

◆

A few minutes after Agatha left the room, Vidal International Holdings returned to its usual silence. Rio remained seated, her fingers resting on the desk, her gaze fixed on the closed door. Then she pressed the intercom.

“Alice, come here for a moment.”

The door opened almost immediately. Alice entered with her usual impeccable posture.

“Yes?”

Rio removed her glasses calmly and placed them on the desk.

“Where did you find Miss Harkness?”

Alice raised an eyebrow.

“She was recommended by someone I trust.”

“Name?”

“I’d rather keep that private. But I guarantee it’s someone who wouldn’t risk my reputation.”

Rio held her gaze for a few seconds.

“Okay.”

A brief silence settled.

Alice tilted her head slightly.

“Is everything alright?”

Rio shifted her eyes to the window, the city reflected in the glass.

“I feel like I’ve seen her before.” Alice gave a crooked smile.

“Maybe because she’s exactly your type.” — Rio rolled her eyes with visible impatience.

“She isn’t.”

Alice crossed her arms.

“Sure.” — The sarcasm was almost amusing. Rio rose from her chair with controlled elegance, walking to the sideboard.

“I’m engaged, Alice. I don’t have time to waste on nonsense.”

Alice let out a contained laugh.

“Of course not.”

Her tone suggested otherwise. Rio cast her a cold look but did not respond. Alice simply adjusted the folder she was holding.

“Either way, she starts today.”

“I know.”

“I’ll guide her.”

Rio nodded. Alice walked to the door, still wearing that subtle smile of someone who knows more than she should. Before leaving, she commented:

“I promise I’ll let you know if you ‘remember’ where you’ve seen her before.”

The door closed. And, alone again, Rio remained motionless for a moment.

◆

Saturday dawned far too bright for Agatha’s emotional state. She was sitting in the back seat of the car, arms crossed, an irritated expression on her face.

“She’s super arrogant, you have to see it. ‘Pregnant women can’t work in my company.’” she imitated, sharpening her voice with sarcasm.

Jen, behind the wheel, let out a short laugh.

“Wow. Delicate.”

Wanda turned in the front seat to look at Agatha.

“So what are you going to do?”

Agatha sighed, resting her forehead against the window for a second.

“I’m going to hide it. And save money for as long as I can. I just need time. When I’m stable… I’ll figure out what to do.”

The silence that followed was not judgment. It was concern. Wanda reached her hand back and squeezed Agatha’s knee.

“You’re not alone.”

Agatha nodded, but her fingers were cold.

The car stopped in front of the clinic.

It was a low building, with a façade in light beige tones and smoked-glass details. The discreet metal sign indicated gynecology and obstetrics services. Well-kept plants lined the entrance—rows of lavender and small ornamental palm trees.

Inside, the environment was welcoming without being overly clinical. The light porcelain floor reflected the soft light from recessed fixtures. The smell was a mixture of light disinfectant and something floral, perhaps strategically placed diffusers.

The reception area had a white marble desk with gray veins. Behind it, a wall with light wooden niches displayed framed certificates and small minimalist sculptures of mothers holding babies.

Olive-green upholstered armchairs were arranged around round tables with recent magazines and bottles of water. A discreet children’s corner occupied part of the room: rubber mat, wooden toys, and illustrated books.

The air conditioning kept the place a little too cool. Agatha felt her stomach churn.

“Breathe,” Jen murmured beside her.

“I am breathing.”

“Not like that.”

Wanda took her hand.

“It’s going to be okay. It’s just an appointment. Just an appointment.”

Agatha walked to the desk. The receptionist was a young woman, her hair pulled into a perfect bun, a practiced smile on her face.

“Good morning.”

“Good morning. I have an appointment with Dr. Lorna.”

The receptionist typed quickly on the computer.

“Miss Harkness?”

The name echoed too loudly.

“Yes.”

“You’re confirmed for nine thirty. Just have a seat and we’ll call you shortly.”

Agatha nodded. She returned to the chairs and sat between Wanda and Jen. Her hands were sweating.

“You’re pale,” Jen commented quietly.

“Thank you for the comforting observation.” Wanda rested her head on Agatha’s shoulder.

“What exactly are you afraid of?”—Agatha looked forward, at the wall with the mother-and-baby sculpture.

“Of hearing something that changes everything.”

The silence was heavy. A white door opened in the side hallway. A nurse called:

“Agatha Harkness?” — Her heart raced. Wanda squeezed her hand one last time.

“We’re here.”

Agatha stood up. Her legs felt too light. And she followed the bright corridor, lit by indirect light, with framed ultrasound pictures on the walls. Dr. Lorna’s office was spacious, illuminated by soft light coming from a recessed ceiling panel.

The walls were cream-colored, decorated with delicate watercolor paintings—silhouettes of pregnant women, tiny hands holding adult fingers, phases of the moon beside rounded bellies.

There was an organized desk with digital medical records open on a tablet, a small stainless steel sink on the side, and in the center of the room, the exam table covered with perfectly stretched disposable paper. Next to it, the ultrasound machine rested like a silent device about to reveal secrets.

The three of them entered together. Lorna, a middle-aged Asian woman with black hair tied in a low bun and thin-framed glasses, opened a warm smile when she saw them.

“Oh, we have a full house here.” — Jen replied immediately, amused:

“We’re the aunts.” — Lorna laughed softly.

“In that case, it’s more than allowed.” — She turned her gaze to Agatha, gently.

“You can lie down, dear.”

Agatha nodded. She removed her shoes, climbed onto the exam table, and lay down carefully. The paper beneath her back made a faint sound. Jen and Wanda moved to the other side of the bed, positioning themselves near the monitor.

Lorna approached.

“I’m going to lift your blouse a little, alright?”

“That’s fine,” Agatha replied, helping pull the fabric up below her bust.

The air in the room felt colder against her exposed skin. Lorna picked up the bottle and poured the transparent gel onto her belly. The contact was cold. Agatha tensed slightly.

“I know, I’m sorry,” the doctor said gently.

The transducer touched her skin. The silence that settled was different from any other.

“Alright…”

All three of them stared at the black-and-white screen. Shadows. Lights. Abstract shapes. Then Lorna adjusted the angle.

“Here he is.”

The world seemed to stop. There was something there. Small. Defined. Agatha automatically brought her hand to her mouth, feeling her eyes fill.

“He looks very good. Look how big he is already.”

Wanda let out an emotional sigh. Jen placed a hand on Agatha’s shoulder. Then Lorna pressed a button. A sound filled the room. Fast, strong, and steady.

Thump-thump-thump-thump-thump.

That was when Agatha broke. Tears slid silently down the sides of her face. Jen moved closer, holding her hand. Wanda leaned down and kissed her forehead. The sound continued—steady, alive, undeniable.

“Everything looks good,” Lorna said with a sincere smile. “Congratulations, Agatha.”

“Would you like a print?” — Agatha nodded, trying to wipe her face.

“Yes… please.”

Lorna finished the exam, turned off the machine, and handed her a few paper towels. Agatha sat up slowly, cleaning the gel from her belly. She was breathing differently now—as if something inside her had finally fallen into place.

She climbed down from the exam table and sat in the chair in front of the doctor’s desk. Wanda and Jen remained behind her, like a silent wall.

Lorna typed some information.

“I’m going to request some routine blood tests. I’ll also prescribe prenatal vitamins, especially folic acid and iron. That helps a lot with development.”

She explained about nutrition, about avoiding certain raw foods, about rest, about the coming months.

Agatha held the small ultrasound print with excessive care, as if it were something too fragile for the world. In the grainy image, that small outline seemed impossible.

But it was there, real and alive.

◆

Rio’s apartment occupied nearly half of the top floor of a skyscraper in Manhattan. Floor-to-ceiling windows revealed the city illuminated by the night.

The living room was spacious, minimalist, furnished in neutral tones—gray, black, dark wood. Modern artworks hung on the walls. A climate-controlled wine cellar occupied a discreet corner. 

Everything there breathed luxury. The office was separated by smoked glass doors; Rio was seated in front of the computer, blazer still impeccable, the screen reflecting graphs and financial reports from Vidal International Holdings.

She took a deep breath and rubbed her face with her hands. Fatigue was not something she usually admitted, but that feeling was not just work—it was something else. She closed her eyes for a second, and the music began in her mind.

World (world), hold on (hold on).

Blurred lights, a pool reflecting neon, Ibiza. The smell came before the image—sweet, warm, enveloping. She remembered following that scent through the party as if guided by pure instinct.

Until she stopped. Long brown hair falling down her back.

“What’s your name?” Her own voice sounded rougher in the memory.

“Aggie.”

The memory cut there—an abrupt blank—and then the hotel room, sunlight entering through the curtains. A woman lying beside her. Rio remembered the curve of her shoulder, the hair spread across the pillow. But she hadn’t had the courage to look at her face.

She hadn’t. She felt the weight of guilt crushing her chest before she even got up. She dressed too quickly. Left as if she were fleeing a crime, her phone vibrating nonstop.

Messages from Alice.
Messages from Jessica.

“Are you awake yet?”

 “Rio, we need to talk about dinner with my parents.”

 “baby?”

She remembered stopping in the elevator, staring at her own reflection, feeling like the worst person in the world—a complete idiot—and she was engaged.

Engaged to a woman who loved her, who trusted her. And even so she had slept with another omega. But there was something she couldn’t deny. That night, in the arms of that stranger, she had felt… complete.

Like never before. And that was what frightened her most. Just from the scent. She didn’t even remember the face—only the smell, and the long brown hair sliding beneath her fingers.

Rio opened her eyes abruptly, pulling in air as if she had dived too deep.

“Shit…” she murmured.

“Rio?”

She turned her head. Jessica was standing at the office door, brown hair in a low bun, comfortable end-of-day clothes and a gentle expression.

“Sorry… I drifted off.”

Jessica crossed her arms with a half smile.

“Ri, always.” She stepped inside. “I bought dinner. Shall we eat?”

Rio closed the laptop slowly.

“Of course.” She stood, passing by Jessica and placing a light kiss on the top of her head. The gesture was automatic. Affectionate. Rehearsed by habit.

But as she walked toward the dining room, Rio knew she was lying to herself. Because even there—in her luxurious apartment, beside the woman she was going to marry—another woman continued to haunt her memories. With the scent of the sea and a name whispered in her mind. Aggie.

The apartment’s dining room was integrated with the kitchen in an open-concept space. The white marble table was carefully set: aligned cutlery, wine glasses already poured, linen napkins folded with precision.

The pendant lighting created an intimate atmosphere—golden and soft—contrasting with the cold city lights outside.

Jessica had ordered food from Rio’s favorite Italian restaurant: fresh pasta with seafood, shrimp sautéed in garlic, mussels in white wine, a linguine wrapped in creamy Sicilian lemon sauce. The aroma filled the room intensely.

Normally, that would have awakened Rio’s appetite immediately.

They sat down.

“I thought you deserved something good today,” Jessica said, serving her plate carefully. “You seemed distant when I arrived.”

Rio tried to smile.

“It was a busy day.”

She picked up the fork and twirled the pasta, bringing the first bite to her mouth. The taste was perfect. But the smell… the smell felt too strong, too warm, too marine. She swallowed with effort, trying to ignore the strange sensation forming in her stomach.

Jessica talked about dinner with her parents the following week, about adjustments in the wedding planning, about flowers and vendors. Rio nodded, but her attention was drifting.

The smell of the shrimp seemed to rise straight to her throat. The white wine now had a metallic taste. And then her stomach made a loud noise.

A liquid sound, far too audible in the silence between one sentence and another. They both froze. Rio felt heat climb up her neck.

“Sorry.”

Jessica frowned.

“Are you okay?”

Rio opened her mouth to answer, but a wave of nausea surged with brutal force. She swallowed hard—no, she wasn’t. Unable to form a coherent response, she abruptly pushed back her chair and walked far too quickly toward the hallway bathroom.

She closed the door and barely had time to kneel before her body reacted. The sound of vomiting echoed against the cold tiles. Outside, Jessica stood up immediately.

“Rio?” Her voice was tense. “What’s happening?”

Rio couldn’t answer. Another contraction in her stomach, and again. Her fingers gripped the side of the toilet as she tried to catch her breath. Jessica remained outside, one hand resting on the door.

“Ri, talk to me.”

After a few minutes that felt far too long, the nausea eased. Rio flushed the toilet, resting her forehead against the cold wall for a moment. Her breathing was uneven, ragged.

The door opened slowly.

Jessica stepped in with a damp towel in her hands.

“Here.”

Rio accepted it, wiping her face, feeling her own body strangely sensitive. She sat on the closed lid of the toilet, still breathing deeply. Jessica knelt in front of her, worried.

“What happened? That’s one of your favorite dishes.”Rio swallowed.

“I… don’t know.” She ran a hand through her hair. “The smell. It made me nauseous.”

Jessica frowned slightly.

“But you always order that dish.” — Rio only shook her head.

“I know.”

Silence settled for a few seconds. Jessica stood up.

“I’ll put the food away. Maybe it’s stress. You’ve been working too much.” She tried to sound rational. Reassuring. “Go rest. Tomorrow you’ll feel better.”

Rio nodded, though she wasn’t sure. She stood slowly—her body felt heavier than it should—and left the bathroom while Jessica cleared the plates from the table. And as she walked toward the bedroom, an uncomfortable feeling began to form in her mind.

She wasn’t sick. She knew how to recognize stress. This was different. But she pushed the thought away before it could take shape. She preferred to believe the simplest explanation.

Stress. Just stress.

Even though, deep down, something told her it wasn’t only that.

◆

The apartment was lit only by the warm lights of the living room. The television was on with the volume muted, serving more as visual company than entertainment. The smell of Chinese food filled the space.

Agatha was sitting on the couch with her legs folded beneath her body, holding a bowl with sautéed vegetables and grilled chicken. Since the appointment, she had been trying to follow everything Lorna recommended to the letter. Jen and Wanda shared a large box of Chinese food on the rug, using chopsticks in a completely disorganized way.

“And then I almost fainted,” Agatha continued, gesturing with her fork. “I was walking past a fish market on the way to the subway and just… it was instant. I ran to a trash can like my life depended on it.”

Jen made a face.

“Agatha, your stories are always delightful.”

Wanda laughed, chewing.

 “At least you didn’t throw up in front of your new boss.”

“Small victories,” Agatha replied, shrugging before taking another bite.

Wanda tilted her head.

 “Did Alice recognize you?”

Agatha nodded.

“Yes. She was professional, but she recognized me. She even complimented my résumé.”

Jen raised her eyebrows.

“Of course she did. You’re good.”

Agatha narrowed her eyes at her.

“Well look at that, someone’s very proud. Must be nice dating someone who works in a building that looks like the center of the universe.”

Jen snorted. 

 “I’m not ‘dating’ anyone.”

“Oh, sure. You just casually have dinner with a rich woman who works at a billion-dollar company.” Wanda joined the teasing.

 “A very specific rich woman, by the way.”

Jen rolled her eyes.

 “You two are unbearable.” Agatha gave a crooked smile.

 “Maybe you’ll have a baby with a rich alpha too.”

Jen pointed her chopstick at her. 

“Maybe you’ll have a baby with a rich woman.” The comment lingered in the air for half a second too long. Agatha slowly lost her smile.

“It doesn’t matter. I’m never going to see that person again.” She shrugged, trying to sound indifferent. “I don’t even remember her face.”

Wanda watched her carefully before speaking.

“It’s difficult. But it’s not impossible.” Agatha didn’t answer. She got up from the couch, carrying the bowl to the kitchen.

“Well, I’m going to sleep. I have a real job tomorrow.”

“Look at her,” Jen shouted from the living room. “Corporate.”

“Bitch,” Wanda added, laughing. Agatha turned at the hallway doorway with a crooked smile.

“I’m not a bitch.” She paused dramatically.  “I’m just a mother now.”

Wanda burst into loud laughter.

“My God, you’re ridiculous.”

“And listen carefully—I’m going to be a MILF. I refuse to wear mom clothes,” she added, blowing kisses to the two of them before stepping into the bedroom, still smiling. But when she closed the door, the silence returned.

She rubbed her face with both hands, feeling the exhaustion weighing on her. The day had been far too long for a single week of a new life. She walked to the dresser where she kept her creams. She picked up a neutral moisturizer, fragrance-free—Lorna had told her to avoid strong scents.

She lifted her shirt just below her bust. Her belly was still almost flat, only marked by a small swelling, but visually nothing had really changed. Agatha spread the cream with slow circular movements.

That was when she realized her scent was different. Softer. Sweeter. Mixed with something that wasn’t exactly hers. It had been invaded by the baby’s scent.

Agatha frowned slightly, bringing her wrist closer to her nose.

“You’re really territorial,” she murmured, smiling to the side as she rubbed her belly once more. It was strange, but it wasn’t bad. She took a deep breath, pulled her shirt back down, and walked to the bed. She lay on her side, pulling the blanket up to her shoulders.

The apartment was quiet beyond the door. She closed her eyes, and as sleep began to come, a part of her still wondered about brown eyes she couldn’t remember. But exhaustion won before the memory could insist.

◆

The next morning Agatha woke before the alarm went off. The sky was still a pale bluish tone when she sat up in bed, breathing deeply to contain the first wave of nausea that always came even before she had fully opened her eyes.

She walked slowly to the kitchen, trying not to think about strong smells, and opened the refrigerator already knowing exactly what she needed: frozen fruit—banana, strawberry, and mango.

She placed everything in the blender with plain yogurt and a little ice, the loud whir of the blade echoing through the silent apartment while she rested her hands on the counter waiting for the nausea to pass.

Over the past few days she had realized that cold drinks calmed her stomach in an almost miraculous way.

She poured the smoothie into a thermal cup, took a few long sips, and felt the cold slide down her throat to her stomach like a small antidote to the morning chaos.

Then she got ready with organized movements: a quick shower, a gray business skirt, a cream blouse made of light fabric, her hair tied in an elegant low bun, makeup subtle enough to make her look rested even though she wasn’t.

She grabbed her bag and folder and left before Jen and Wanda were fully awake.

The street still had the slower rhythm of the early hours, and that helped her keep her breathing steady until she reached the mirrored building of Vidal International Holdings, where the security guard already recognized her and allowed her in with a respectful nod.

The lobby was almost empty, and the corporate quiet of the morning gave her a strange sense of advantage.

She went up to the top floor and was one of the first to enter the executive sector. She set her bag on the desk, turned on the computer, and opened Rio’s schedule, organizing appointments, confirming meetings, and replying to pending emails with increasing efficiency. 

She scheduled a conference with investors at nine, an internal meeting at ten thirty, reviewed external commitments, and realized she needed to arrange a visit to a haute couture shop for a dress fitting, since Rio would have a formal dinner with her fiancée’s parents that week.

She picked up the phone ready to call when she heard the discreet sound of the executive elevator opening behind her.

She stood immediately and straightened her posture when she saw Rio step out with slower steps than usual. The impeccable blazer contrasted with her slightly pale face and the faint dark circles that betrayed a bad night.

“Good morning, Mrs. Vidal,” Agatha said with professionalism.

Rio nodded, but took half a second longer than usual to respond.

“Good morning, Miss Harkness.”

Her voice was lower, less steady. Agatha noticed the tension in her jaw and asked before thinking too much about it:

“Is everything alright?”

Rio let out a brief sigh, as if admitting weakness were an uncomfortable concession.

“I’ve had severe nausea since last night. I need you to order some medicine from the building’s infirmary.”

Agatha quickly checked the clock on the monitor and replied carefully:

“I will, but it’s only seven. The infirmary doesn’t open until eight.”

Rio closed her eyes for a moment and sighed with visible irritation.

“Wonderful.”

She brought a hand to her stomach almost imperceptibly.

That was when Agatha glanced at her own thermal cup on the desk and made a simple decision.

She opened the lid, grabbed a disposable cup beside the printer, and poured half of the cold smoothie. She extended it to Rio, keeping her tone neutral.

“Maybe this will help. It’s cold and usually settles the stomach.”

Rio looked at her for a second as if she had been challenged, brown eyes evaluating the offer with suspicion and something undefined behind them.

But the physical discomfort won over her pride and she accepted the cup.

She brought it to her lips and drank slowly. The cold liquid slid down and made her close her eyes involuntarily when it reached her stomach; the tension in her shoulders eased slightly.

“Well… I think that actually helped,” she admitted with controlled reluctance.

Agatha simply nodded, returning to her seat behind the desk.

“I’ve scheduled all the meetings for the day and confirmed your attendance at the conference with the investors. I also booked a fitting at Maison Laurent tomorrow at sixteen hundred because of the dinner with Miss Jessica’s parents.”

Rio seemed to register the information with silent approval.

“We have a meeting in ten minutes in room three. I need you to accompany me and bring the financial reports from the last quarter.”

Agatha picked up the organized folder she had already separated, took a deep breath feeling her own stomach stable thanks to the rest of the smoothie, and answered firmly:

“Of course, Mrs. Vidal.”

And as they walked side by side toward the meeting room, neither of them realized that despite their physical closeness, the scent each unconsciously searched for in the other remained too altered to be recognized.

Chapter 3: Jealousy

Chapter Notes

Guys! Thank you for all the kind comments! You are amazing. This story already has some chapters written a while ago, so don't be surprised by the speed, LOL. But soon they will take a little longer to come out.

Important to know, in case you didn't understand, Rio couldn't recognize Agatha's scent due to her pregnancy, which drastically alters an omega's scent. And Rio reacts so strangely because Agatha is carrying her pup (baby). So she becomes super protective and worried.

YESSS we will have Wandanat! And get ready because it seems that everyone around knows that Agatha is pregnant with Rio's child except Rio! This will be funny and a little tragic to see!

So, happy reading everyone!

 

 

┕──━──━─┑◆┍─━──━──┙

 

Meeting room three occupied a privileged corner of the top floor of Vidal International Holdings. The glass walls offered a wide view of Manhattan still bathed in the soft light of the morning, and the oval dark-wood table subtly reflected the suspended lights from the ceiling.

Screens embedded in the walls displayed financial charts and market projections while several executives were already seated, tablets and folders neatly arranged in front of them.

Agatha entered right behind Rio carrying the folder with the reports and a small tablet where she would take notes. Her role was clear: record everything, keep the meeting flowing, and make sure Rio would not have to interrupt her reasoning to deal with practical details.

Rio took the main seat at the head of the table with the natural ease of someone who dominated that space. Even with the lingering discomfort still present, her posture remained impeccable—spine straight, hands resting on the table while she waited for silence to settle.

“Let’s begin,” she said, her voice firm as it regained its usual authority.

Agatha moved discreetly around the room. First she placed a pitcher of water in the center of the table, then began pouring coffee into the white porcelain cups, also offering water to the executives who thanked her with quick nods.

Her movements were quiet and efficient, almost invisible within the formal dynamics of the meeting.

Rio opened the presentation on the tablet in front of her.

“As you know, the last quarter closed with growth above our initial projections, especially in the international investment sector. Our focus now is to consolidate this expansion before opening new acquisition fronts.”

One of the directors nodded while observing the chart on the screen.

Agatha was already seated beside her, slightly behind Rio’s chair, noting every important point. The tip of her pen moved quickly across the notebook as she recorded decisions, deadlines, and names. At the same time, she kept an attentive eye on the table, ready to intervene if anyone needed anything.

Rio continued.

“We need to strengthen our presence in Europe next semester. Negotiations with the Luxembourg funds are already underway, but I want more detailed reports on currency risk before we move forward.”

As she spoke, she felt that strange distraction again.

Her gaze drifted for a second.

Agatha was leaning over the notebook, brown hair tied back with a few loose strands near her face. She wrote with absolute concentration, unaware she was being observed.

Rio blinked and immediately returned her attention to the table.

“Mr. Kim, could you present the updated data from the Asian market?”

The executive began speaking, projecting new tables onto the screen.

Agatha stood again when she noticed some cups were empty. She moved around the table serving more coffee and refilling water glasses with calm movements. When she stopped beside Rio to place a new cup near her, Rio felt something strange.

It wasn’t exactly the scent. It was… proximity.

Something about Agatha’s presence seemed to pull her attention for no logical reason.

A mild discomfort, but also an involuntary curiosity. Rio picked up the cup and took a small sip while the numbers continued to be discussed.

She tried to focus.

“Right,” she said after a few minutes. “I want a consolidated report on this by Friday.”

Agatha wrote it down immediately.

Deadline: Friday. Consolidated report — Asian market.

The meeting continued flowing, charts being discussed, strategies adjusted, and Rio answered every question with sharp clarity. It was obvious why she stood at the top of that company.

But in small moments between one statement and another, her gaze returned to the same place.

Agatha wrote while organizing papers and serving water, moving through the room with silent efficiency. Rio could not explain why her attention kept drifting toward her, and it irritated her slightly.

She did not like distractions—much less distractions she could not understand.

The meeting progressed, and as the numbers and projections became more complex, the atmosphere in the room changed as well.

Executives discussed margins, risks, and investments with increasing intensity, some leaning over the table to defend their points while the screens displayed new spreadsheets.

Agatha continued recording everything carefully, writing down decisions, deadlines, and important observations while keeping a peripheral eye on the room to ensure nothing disrupted the flow of the meeting.

Then one of the businessmen, an older man seated on the right side of the table, pushed his empty cup a few centimeters forward and spoke without even looking directly at her.

“More coffee.”

The tone was not a request. It was an order.

Agatha lifted her eyes for a moment and started to stand. Before she could take a step, Rio spoke.

“Mr. Davenport.”

Her voice was calm, but firm enough to interrupt the movement in the room. The man looked at her.

“Yes?”

Rio held his gaze.

“Miss Harkness is busy assisting this meeting. If you would like more coffee, you may stand and serve yourself.”

A small silence fell over the table.

Agatha intervened quickly, trying to avoid unnecessary tension.

“It’s alright,” she said with a professional smile as she stood. “I can get it.”

The man muttered under his breath.

“That’s why they put omegas in these positions, isn’t it?”

Some of the people present shifted their eyes to the screens, pretending they hadn’t heard.

Agatha ignored the comment and walked to the side table where the thermos was. She picked up the man’s cup and began pouring the coffee.

But at that moment someone asked Rio a technical question, and the conversation grew louder again. The sudden movement around her distracted Agatha for a second—the hot liquid went past the rim of the cup and spilled over her fingers.

“Ah—”

She pulled her hand back immediately.

The hot coffee had splashed directly onto her skin. Before she could even react properly, Rio was already standing. She hadn’t even noticed the moment she got up—only that she was suddenly beside Agatha.

“Are you alright?” she asked, her voice different now—lower and tense.

Agatha looked at her, surprised, holding her own hand.

“I’m fine, it’s alright,” she said quickly, trying to sound calm. “I just got distracted for a moment.”

She grabbed a napkin and wiped her fingers.

“Sorry.”

The apology came automatically, almost reflexively. That was when Agatha noticed the way Rio was looking at her hand—and at her. Strange.

Too concerned for something so small.

For a second the two of them stood there, too close in the middle of the meeting room. Rio seemed to notice it too. Her expression shifted slightly, as if she had just realized her own reaction.

“Right,” she said, clearing her throat. “Be careful.”

She returned to her chair with the same controlled posture as before, though a hint of embarrassment had crossed her face for a moment.

Agatha finished placing the cup in front of the businessman and went back to her place beside the table. But for a few minutes afterward, both of them took slightly longer than usual to focus completely on the meeting again.

 

◆

 

The apartment was filled with the comforting smell of food being prepared when Agatha arrived home from work.

The small kitchen was lit by the yellow ceiling light, and the open windows let in the distant noise of the street. Jen was leaning against the counter cutting vegetables while Wanda stirred a pot on the stove.

Agatha dropped her bag onto a chair and rolled up the sleeves of her blouse.

“You won’t believe what happened today.” Wanda looked over her shoulder, curious.

“That usually means something very good or very bad.”

“Strange,” Agatha replied, opening the fridge to grab a few more ingredients.

She started assembling a simple salad while she spoke.

“A guy at the meeting was a complete asshole. Asked for coffee like I was some fucking waitress.”

Jen let out an irritated sigh.

“Businessmen. What a surprise.”

Agatha continued, cutting already-cooked chicken into smaller pieces.

“Then Rio just said I was busy assisting the meeting and that he could get up and serve himself.”

Wanda turned fully around now.

“She said that?”

“She did.”

Jen raised an eyebrow.

“Okay… that’s kind of amazing.”

Agatha shrugged.

“That wasn’t the end of it.”
