She put the chicken pieces into a bowl and continued telling the story while mixing everything together.

“I went to serve the coffee anyway because I didn’t want to turn the meeting into a corporate battlefield… and I ended up spilling hot coffee on my hand.”

Wanda made a face.

“Ouch.”

“It was just a splash,” Agatha continued, “but Rio got up from her chair like I had lost a finger.”

Jen laughed.

“Dramatic.”

Agatha shook her head while mixing the salad.

“No, seriously. She came straight to me, asked if I was okay, kept staring at my hand like it was something serious.”

Wanda grabbed three plates from the cabinet and started placing them on the table.

“Maybe she got angry about the asshole businessman’s comment.”

Jen nodded.

“Yeah. Sounds more like that.”

Agatha shrugged.

“Maybe.”

She picked up a large bowl and carried it to the table.

“But it was extremely weird.”

Jen placed the utensils beside the plates.

“Why?”

Agatha sat down and rested her elbows on the table.

“Because she’s an alpha.”

Wanda raised her eyes.

“So?”

“So… I don’t know.” Agatha ran a hand through her hair. “It wasn’t a professional reaction. It was almost… instinctive.”

Jen pulled out a chair and sat down.

“You just discovered she’s a decent person.”

Wanda pointed at Agatha with the chopsticks she had picked up.

“Besides, she’s known for being pretty progressive.”

Agatha sighed as she finished arranging the food on the table.

“I know.”

She stayed silent for a second before repeating, more to herself than to them:

“It was still really damn weird.”

Jen and Wanda finished setting the glasses and sat down.

“Well,” Jen said, picking up her fork, “if your billionaire boss wants to protect you from idiot businessmen, I honestly don’t see the problem.”

Wanda laughed.

“Neither do I.”

Agatha laughed too, although a small knot of curiosity still lingered in her mind that she couldn’t quite explain.

They began eating while the conversation continued lightly, dishes passing from one to another across the small kitchen table. The clinking of utensils and the steam rising from the food created a comforting sense of domestic routine after a long day.

Agatha was finishing mixing the salad when she commented, almost casually:

“Oh, and this weekend I’ll have to accompany Rio to a lunch.”

Wanda immediately looked up, a teasing smile appearing on her face.

“Accompany.”

The way she said the word was clearly a provocation.

Agatha narrowed her eyes at her.

“Don’t start.”

Jen let out a quiet laugh while cutting the chicken on her plate.

“Let me guess… business lunch?”

“Not exactly,” Agatha replied, picking up the glass of water. “It’s a lunch with the fiancée.”

Wanda made a dramatic expression.

“Oh, of course.”

Agatha rolled her eyes.

“And with the fiancée’s family.”

Jen whistled.

“That already sounds like a social nightmare.”

“It’s at a horse club,” Agatha added.

Jen stopped chewing and started laughing.

“No.”

“Yes.”

“One of those absurdly rich clubs?”

“A jockey club,” Agatha confirmed. “Something like that.”

Jen rested her elbow on the table.

“Alice would hate that.”

Agatha frowned.

“Why?”

Jen pointed her fork at her.

“Because her mother is a doctor and owns two horses.”

Wanda widened her eyes.

“Two?”

“Two,” Jen confirmed. “And Alice thinks it’s a complete drag.”

Agatha laughed.

“I can imagine.”

Jen continued, amused.

“She says the whole horse culture is full of rich people pretending they understand nature.”

Wanda gave a small laugh.

“Alice sounds like the partying type.”

Jen agreed immediately.

“Totally.”

Agatha rested her chin in her hand for a moment while eating a little more.

“Well… anyway, I’ll basically be there working. She always brings a secretary, even when it’s something personal. She says it’s to keep track of the schedule, take work notes, handle calls.”

Jen raised an eyebrow.

“So your billionaire boss can’t have lunch without bringing an assistant.”

Agatha shrugged.

“Yeah.”

Jen shook her head.

“She must be boring.”

Agatha thought for a second, absentmindedly moving her food with the fork.

“…Yeah.”

But the way she said that sounded more thoughtful than convinced. Wanda noticed immediately.

“You don’t sound so sure about that.”Agatha looked up at her and made a small grimace.

“I’ve been working with her for three weeks.” Jen smiled to the side.

“Long enough to figure out if someone’s boring.”

Agatha let out a small sigh, leaning back in the chair.

“Honestly?”

The two of them looked at her.

“She’s… strange.”

Jen laughed.

“That description just got even more interesting.”

Agatha thought for a moment before answering.

“Not bad strange.”

She shrugged.

“Just… hard to read.”

Wanda smiled slightly.

“Some people are like that.”

Agatha went back to eating, but the thought still lingered there, floating in the back of her mind. Hard to read. That was exactly it.

 

◆

 

The morning was clear when Agatha finished getting ready.

The blue dress she chose was simple, but elegant enough for a formal lunch; the fabric fell well over her body, still discreet, although now it was beginning to reveal changes she could no longer ignore.

Her hair was tied into a high, neatly aligned ponytail, and over the dress she wore a light coat to protect herself from the cold morning wind.

She walked to the pair of heels resting near the door and let out a small sigh before putting them on. As soon as she stood up, she immediately noticed the discomfort in her feet.

They were more sensitive, a little swollen, and she knew exactly why. The pregnancy was already beginning to demand small, silent prices throughout the day.

Then, as she passed the living room mirror, Agatha stopped.

For a moment she watched her own reflection.

The belly that had once been completely flat now showed the beginning of a subtle curve beneath the dress. Nothing obvious to someone looking quickly, but for her it was impossible not to notice.

Her fingers rested there for a moment over the blue fabric, feeling the small volume that was beginning to exist.

She let out a slow breath, soon she would have to invest in looser clothes.

The idea still felt too distant to be completely real.

Agatha grabbed the bag from the table and left the apartment, locking the door behind her. The street was busy with the start of the day, cars passing and a few people walking quickly along the sidewalks.

The ômega took out her phone already prepared to call an Uber when a black car stopped smoothly in front of her.

The vehicle was large and elegant, with dark windows, and the rear window slowly rolled down. Rio Vidal appeared behind dark sunglasses.

“Good morning, Miss Harkness.”

Agatha blinked in surprise, still holding the phone in her hand.

“How—”

Rio rested her arm casually on the car door.

“I know where all my employees live.”

She paused briefly before adding, with absolute calm:

“Security reasons.”

Agatha remained silent for a second before replying.

“Of course…”

At that moment the driver had already stepped out of the car and opened the rear door for her.

Agatha got in, placing her bag beside her while the quiet and comfortable interior of the vehicle surrounded her.

The door closed softly and a few seconds later the driver returned to the wheel.

The car started moving.

Rio removed her sunglasses and placed them on her lap as the vehicle pulled away from the building.

“Usually this kind of lunch is calm,” she began in a professional tone. “But I still need you to keep an eye on urgent calls and emails.”

Agatha opened the tablet she carried in her bag.

“Of course.”

Rio continued, briefly glancing at her before turning her attention forward again.

“Truly urgent ones.”

A small, almost imperceptible smile appeared at the corner of her mouth.

“In general you can stay at your table, order whatever you want in the restaurant, and you don’t need to worry about the bill.”

Agatha raised her eyebrows.

“Alright.”

Rio crossed her legs elegantly as she continued.

“It will be a lunch with my fiancée’s parents, Jessica. They will be at the main table with me.”

Her tone remained neutral, almost mechanical.

“You will stay at a nearby table. Just keep an eye on your inbox and notify me if anything relevant appears.”

Agatha nodded.

“Alright.”

She hesitated for a moment before adding, with sincere politeness:

“And… congratulations on the wedding.”

Rio gave a small shrug, as if that were simply another appointment on her schedule.

“Thank you.”

The way she said it didn’t convey much enthusiasm. It sounded more like a formality being acknowledged.

Agatha noticed that, but didn’t comment.

Her eyes eventually turned toward the car window as the city passed outside, buildings giving way to more open areas as they headed toward the club.

Inside the vehicle, silence settled for a few minutes. Rio remained apparently relaxed in the seat, but at some point her gaze drifted discreetly toward Agatha beside her.

For some reason she still couldn’t explain, her attention had been returning to her frequently over the last few days.

After some time on the road, the car slowed as it entered the long tree-lined avenue leading to the club.

The place was exactly the kind of environment Agatha imagined when she thought of a jockey club: perfectly trimmed lawns, impeccable white fences, horses being led in the distance by uniformed handlers, and an elegant main building that blended classical architecture with glass and dark wood.

The car stopped smoothly at the main entrance.

The driver got out first, opening the rear door. Rio stepped out first, adjusting her blazer with a natural gesture and putting her sunglasses back on before lifting her gaze toward the club building.

Agatha stepped out right after, holding the tablet against her body and adjusting the bag on her shoulder.

The air there had a different smell.

Grass, leather, and a faint scent of damp earth.

Before they had taken more than two steps, a man in a light suit approached with a perfectly trained smile.

“Mrs. Vidal, it’s a pleasure to welcome you again.”

Rio nodded elegantly.

“Thank you.”

The manager made a polite gesture with his arm, indicating the way.

“The table is already prepared.”

He guided them through the interior of the club, which was even more luxurious than Agatha had expected.

Tall chandeliers illuminated the main hall, large windows revealed the racetrack outside, and the distant sound of sophisticated conversations created a carefully polished social atmosphere.

That was when Agatha saw the transformation.

As soon as they approached the table where the others were already seated, Rio seemed to put on a social mask Agatha had not witnessed before.

Her smile appeared with perfect naturalness, warm and almost charming.

She first greeted a middle-aged couple who stood up to receive her, shaking their hands elegantly and exchanging a few formal words.

Then she leaned slightly to quickly kiss the woman sitting beside them.

The woman was tall, beautiful in a classic way, with neatly styled dark hair and an expensive dress that screamed old money.

The fiancée.

Agatha recognized the face immediately. In the last few days, during a few moments of quiet curiosity, she had stalked Rio’s Instagram and discovered who Jessica was.

Jessica Jones.

Heiress to a massive real-estate conglomerate.

While the four of them began talking, Agatha remained a few steps behind, observing with professional discretion. After a few moments, Rio looked in her direction again and discreetly pointed to a table slightly farther away.

“Miss Harkness will stay there.”

The table was solitary, positioned near the large windows.

Agatha walked there and immediately realized that, despite being separated, the position was perfect. The view of the racetrack was absolutely spectacular, with the horses being prepared while the morning sun illuminated the green grass.

Rio approached slightly before returning to her own table.

“If you have any problem, send a message.”

Agatha nodded.

“Of course.”

Rio gave one last brief nod before returning to the table with Jessica and her parents.

Agatha sat down, organizing her small workspace efficiently. She placed the tablet on the stand she carried in her bag, connected the small portable keyboard, and positioned the mouse beside it, creating a mini workstation on the table.

She had just begun opening her inbox when a waiter approached with an elegant leather-covered menu.

“May I bring something for you, miss?”

Agatha took the menu and, for the first time since sitting down, opened a genuine smile. Lately she felt hungry all the time. It was almost absurd. She quickly scanned the options and felt her mouth literally begin to water.

“I’ll have a burrata appetizer with tomato and rustic bread,” she said first.

The waiter wrote it down.

“And for the main course… the tagliatelle with meat ragù.”

He kept writing as she continued:

“And a glass of fresh juice. Whatever you have in season.”

“Of course.”

The waiter collected the menu and stepped away.

Agatha turned her eyes back to the tablet, but for a moment she still watched the racetrack outside. The hunger, the sunlight coming through the windows, and the distant smell of good food made that lunch feel almost… divine. Even though technically she was still working.

Lunch at Rio’s table began with the predictable social choreography of people accustomed to that kind of meeting.

Glasses clinked softly, dishes were served with impeccable precision by the waiters, and conversations followed a polite rhythm of restrained laughter and carefully chosen comments.

Jessica seemed comfortable in that environment; she spoke naturally with her parents while including Rio in conversations about investments, properties, and the club’s social events.

Rio participated with the same controlled elegance as always.

The smile she wore there was polished, almost choreographed, and Agatha realized that this version of her was different from the one she saw in the office.

More sociable, more diplomatic, as if she had learned to perform that role from a very young age.

Meanwhile, at the table near the window, Agatha worked.

The tablet was open on its stand, the small keyboard positioned in front of her while her attention alternated between the inbox and Rio’s corporate calendar.

Some emails were simple confirmations of meetings for the following week; others required more elaborate replies.

She drafted a formal response to a European investor who requested a change of time for a videoconference, forwarded documents to the legal department, and scheduled two new commitments for the following week in Rio’s agenda.

From time to time she lifted her eyes from the screen.

The racetrack was beginning to grow more active.

Men in uniform were leading the horses to the preparation areas while the club’s loudspeakers announced the first race of the afternoon.

The murmur in the hall increased slightly, and several people approached the windows to watch more closely.

Agatha continued working while the appetizer she had ordered arrived at the table.

The fresh burrata with tomatoes and rustic bread was absurdly good, and she took advantage of a few minutes between emails to eat, feeling the constant hunger of pregnancy manifest with almost comical intensity.

When the race began, the distant sound of hooves echoed across the field. The audience in the hall reacted with small excited comments, some leaning forward to see better as the horses shot down the track.

Hours passed like that.

Agatha finished the main course, answered the last urgent messages, and finally allowed the tablet to remain simply open beside her while she enjoyed the dessert she had ordered—a small tiramisù served in a glass cup.

She was watching the second race begin when a shadow fell over the table.

“I’ve never seen you here before.”

Agatha lifted her eyes.

The woman standing before her was absolutely beautiful. Tall, blonde, with an easy smile and clear blue eyes that seemed used to drawing attention anywhere. The elegant dress and relaxed posture revealed someone who frequented that environment naturally.

Agatha replied politely.

“It’s my first time here.”

The woman tilted her head slightly, curious.

“Really?”

“I’m just accompanying my boss,” Agatha explained, discreetly pointing toward the table where Rio was.

The blonde followed the gesture with her eyes and then looked back at her.

“I see.”

She pulled out the chair in front without waiting for an invitation and sat down.

“Carol Danvers.”

Agatha blinked once, surprised by the naturalness of the approach.

“Nice to meet you.” She extended her hand.

“Agatha Harkness.”

Carol shook her hand with a smile that seemed to contain a slight flirtation.

“The pleasure is mine.”

She rested her elbow on the table, observing Agatha with open interest.

“I have to admit, it’s the first time I’ve seen someone this beautiful around here.”

Agatha let out a small embarrassed laugh, looking away for a second.

“I think you’re exaggerating.”

“Not at all,” Carol replied casually.

Despite the light tone, Agatha maintained her professional posture. The tablet remained open beside her and her eyes occasionally returned to the screen to check notifications.

At the main table, Rio was still talking with Jessica and her parents, but something began to bother her.

At first it was only a strange sensation in her chest, a restlessness that was difficult to identify. She tried to ignore it, continuing to listen to a story Jessica’s father was telling about real estate investments.

Jessica laughed at something he said.

But Rio’s attention had already drifted. Her gaze eventually moved toward the table near the window.

Agatha was sitting there.

And there was a woman in front of her. A very beautiful woman. Talking and laughing.

Something tightened in Rio’s chest in an abrupt way. It was uncomfortable and almost possessive, and she had absolutely no idea why.

“Excuse me,” she said suddenly.

Jessica looked up.

“Everything alright?”

“I’ll be right back.”

Rio stood up from the table before anyone could ask anything else. She walked toward Agatha’s table with firm steps.

Agatha was saying something when she noticed the sudden presence beside her and looked up with a slight start.

Rio was standing there. Her expression was closed.

“What is going on here?”

Carol immediately stood up with a calm smile.

“Nothing much,” she replied casually. “We were just talking.”

She looked at Agatha with sympathy.

“Your employee is an excellent professional. I was just complimenting the young lady.”

Rio did not respond. Her expression remained rigid.

Carol immediately noticed the strange tension in the air and raised her hands lightly.

“Well… it was a pleasure, Agatha.”

She winked at her.

“Maybe we’ll see each other around.”

Then she walked away.

As soon as Carol left, Agatha spoke quickly.

“I’m still working.”

Rio turned her gaze to her.

“Miss Harkness is here to work,” she said sharply. “Not to socialize with strangers.”

Agatha frowned, surprised by the tone.

“I am working. I haven’t failed to complete any tasks.”

Rio crossed her arms.

“It didn’t look that way.”

The coldness in her voice was evident now.

“Perhaps it would be better to remember exactly what your role here is.”

The sentence fell heavily between them.

Agatha remained silent for a second, surprised by the tone. Rio seemed about to say something more, but stopped, as if realizing her own excess in the middle of the sentence. For some reason, she herself seemed confronted by her own reaction.

Without adding anything else, she turned and walked back to the main table with firm steps.

Agatha remained seated for a few seconds after she left, feeling her chest tighten in an irritatingly emotional way.

A tear threatened to slide from the corner of her eye, and she blinked quickly, taking a deep breath to contain the feeling.

Damn hormones.

Agatha then carefully closed the tablet, grabbed the bag beside the chair and discreetly stood up.

Agatha entered the bathroom trying to maintain her professional composure until the last second, walking to the sinks as if she were just touching something up before returning to work. The room was silent, elegant like the rest of the club, with large mirrors and soft lighting reflecting the pale marble of the counters. She took a deep breath once, then another, trying to control the strange pressure in her chest.

But it didn’t work.

Before anyone could enter, Agatha quickly crossed the small corridor and entered one of the stalls. She closed the door behind her and turned the lock with a sharp click.

As soon as the lock slid into place, the control she had been holding since the table finally gave way.

She sat on the closed toilet lid, bending slightly as she brought one hand to her face.

The tears began to fall before she even realized it.

Agatha pressed her lips together, trying to keep the crying silent. It was ridiculous. She knew it was ridiculous. It was just a reprimand at work, something small, something she would normally ignore easily.

But nothing seemed small lately.

The hormones made everything feel bigger, more intense, harder to hold in. She ran a hand over her eyes with irritation, trying to wipe away the tears.

“Great…” she murmured to herself, her voice low and shaky. “Very professional, Agatha.”

She took another deep breath, resting her elbows on her knees while lowering her head forward. She needed to calm down. She needed to return to the table as if nothing had happened. Even though, at that moment, her chest was still tight and her mind too full to appear completely steady.

After a few minutes, the crying began to fade until it turned into uneven breaths. Agatha remained seated for a while longer inside the stall, her hands resting on her knees as she breathed deeply several times, waiting for her body to finally slow down. Little by little, the pressure in her chest eased and her thoughts began to organize again.

When she realized she could compose herself, she carefully wiped her face and stood up.

Agatha unlocked the stall door and stepped out to the sink area.

The reflection in the mirror was not exactly encouraging.

Her eyes were swollen, her nose slightly red, and there was an obvious exhaustion in her expression. She let out a small frustrated sigh and opened her bag on the marble counter.

First she took out a pair of sunglasses and put them on, observing the effect in the mirror. That helped a lot to hide her still sensitive eyes. Then she opened the small makeup case she carried in her bag and began fixing what she could: a bit of concealer under her eyes, a light touch-up of foundation, and a quick powder to neutralize the redness around her nose.

She worked with careful, silent movements, focused on the mirror.

A few minutes later, she put everything away again.

It wasn’t perfect, but it was presentable.

Agatha took one last deep breath, picked up her bag and left the bathroom.

The club hall was still lively with the sequence of races, voices mixing with the distant sound of loudspeakers announcing the horses for the next event. She walked back to the table near the window without drawing attention, keeping the sunglasses on her face.

She sat down silently.

The tablet was still where she had left it, but at that moment she didn’t open the screen. She simply rested her hands on the table and turned her eyes to the racetrack outside.

The horses were lining up again at the starting gate. The sun illuminated the grass and the distant sound of the crowd created an almost hypnotic atmosphere.

Agatha simply watched.

The sunglasses hid her eyes, but even so something in her posture seemed different. Quieter. More restrained. And, for the first time since she had stood up to confront her, a small hint of guilt began to settle in Rio’s chest.

Lunch stretched on much longer than Agatha had imagined. By the time the last glasses were cleared from the main table and the movement in the dining hall began to thin, the sun had already lowered in the sky, casting a golden light over the racetrack. Some of the tables around them were already being vacated, and the club’s staff moved about collecting plates and adjusting the atmosphere for the late afternoon.

Rio stood from the main table with the same controlled elegance as always, saying goodbye to Jessica’s father with a firm handshake. Her mother received a cordial smile and a few kind words about the lunch. Jessica stood beside them, chatting animatedly with her mother about wedding details.

Agatha remained a few steps behind, silent, holding the tablet against her body and her bag over her shoulder. Her posture was professional, but her feet throbbed inside her heels. The swelling from the pregnancy was already beginning to take its toll after so many hours.

She simply waited.

Jessica then turned to Rio with a smile.

“I’m going with my mother to check a few wedding details now.”

Her mother nodded immediately.

“The florist wants to confirm a few things.”

Jessica turned back to Rio and leaned in to kiss her quickly. It was a short, social gesture.

“We’ll talk later.”

“Of course,” Rio replied calmly.

The two of them walked away toward the club’s side exit, talking about flowers and invitations. When they disappeared down the corridor, the brief silence between Rio and Agatha became more noticeable.

Rio then turned to her.

“I’ll take you home.” Agatha answered almost immediately.

“That won’t be necessary.”

Rio frowned slightly.

“Don’t be silly.”

The tone wasn’t aggressive, but it carried the same firm authority as always. Agatha took a breath before answering.

“It really isn’t necessary.” She adjusted the bag on her shoulder. “I’m meeting my girlfriend.”

Rio stopped.

It wasn’t a big movement.

But something inside her tightened again, that uncomfortable and inexplicable feeling that had been surfacing since earlier. A small pulse of something that felt dangerously close to jealousy, even though it made absolutely no sense.

It took her a second longer than usual to reply.

“Right.”

The word came out neutral, almost cold.

Agatha didn’t fully notice the shift. She simply raised her hand to signal a taxi passing by the club entrance. The car stopped, and she opened the door without looking at Rio again.

“See you Monday, Mrs. Vidal.”

“See you Monday,” Rio replied automatically.

Agatha got into the taxi and closed the door. The car pulled away seconds later, leaving along the same avenue they had arrived from hours earlier.

Rio remained standing at the club entrance.

Watching the car disappear.

The silence around her felt strange now.

And that sensation in her chest was still there, pulsing in an uncomfortable, insistent way… completely inexplicable.

The ride back was silent.

Rio remained in the back seat of the car, her gaze turned toward the window while the scenery of the club slowly faded behind them. The driver drove with his usual discretion, and inside the car there was only the soft hum of the engine and the distant movement of the city at the end of the afternoon.

But Rio’s mind was far from there.

The scene in the restaurant kept replaying in her head.

Agatha sitting with that blonde woman.

The light smile.

The seemingly innocent conversation.

Her own reaction — disproportionate, sharp, almost territorial — still echoed in her conscience.

She clenched her jaw slightly.

It didn’t make sense.

When they finally arrived at the building where she lived, the sky was already beginning to darken. The driver parked at the private entrance and stepped out to open the door.

Rio entered the lobby and went straight up to the apartment. The space was large, silent, and luxurious, with huge windows revealing the illuminated city. As soon as the door closed behind her, the silence of the place fell like a comfortable weight.

She walked slowly into the living room.

First she removed her shoes, leaving them on the wooden floor.

Then she slipped the blazer from her shoulders and tossed it over the back of a nearby armchair.

Finally, she dropped onto the sofa.

Her body relaxed against the cushions as she tilted her head back for a moment, but her mind was still restless.

Rio pulled her phone from her pocket and unlocked the screen almost without thinking.

A few seconds later, she opened Instagram.

Her thumb hovered briefly over the search bar.

Then she typed: Agatha Harkness.

The profile appeared almost immediately.

She opened it.

The first photos were recent. Simple portraits, a few moments with friends, some casual images.

Rio slowly scrolled down the page, enlarging a few images without realizing she was doing it.

Zooming in on the face.

The eyes.

The curve of the smile.

She kept scrolling.

More photos. More weeks.

Then she reached posts from about six weeks earlier.

Ibiza.

The simple caption accompanied several images from a recent trip.

Bright sun. Deep blue sea. Parties. Smiling people. Light dresses and wind in their hair.

Rio felt her body stiffen slightly.

Her finger stopped on the screen.

She opened one of the photos.

And froze.

Agatha was seen from behind in the image, walking down a street illuminated by the golden light of sunset. She wore a thin white dress, the kind typical of Ibiza, the light fabric outlining her silhouette while the wind moved her long, full hair.

The same hair.

The same hair she had seen spread across the pillow that confusing morning.

For a second, the memory returned with brutal clarity.

The room still smelling of alcohol and expensive perfume.

The sun coming through the window.

The warm body beside her.

That soft, husky voice whispering when she asked the name.

“My name is Aggie.”

Rio gripped the phone tighter.

Aggie.

Fuck.

A nickname for Agatha.

Her heart beat a little faster as the memory slowly began to fit into place.

The hair, The voice. The body.

But then something else invaded her thoughts.

The scent. She closed the image for a moment and took a deep breath. Agatha’s scent in the office was completely different.

Completely.

Rio slowly ran a hand over her face, trying to organize the confusion forming in her mind. If it was her… why wasn’t the scent the same?

Rio continued staring at the phone screen for a few seconds, the photo still open while her thoughts spun too fast to follow. The image of Agatha in Ibiza seemed to burn on the screen.

The white dress.

The long hair.

The posture.

That insistent memory of the morning she had woken beside a stranger whose voice had whispered “Aggie” with a lazy smile.

She locked the phone.

Something inside her was far too restless to simply ignore. Without thinking much further, Rio opened her contacts list and pressed the name she was looking for. Alice.

The phone rang only twice before it was answered.

“Rio?” Alice’s voice sounded on the other side, immediately alert. “What happened?”

Rio ran a hand through her hair, pacing slowly across the living room.

“I need you to come here.”

There was a brief silence.

“Now?”

“It’s urgent.”

On the other end of the line, Rio heard a muffled sound, as if Alice had turned her head toward someone nearby. Another female voice appeared faintly in the background.

“Is everything okay?”

Alice returned to the phone.

“I’m on a date.” Rio closed her eyes for a second, trying to control the anxiety tightening in her chest.

“But I need you to come. It’s really urgent.” A few more seconds of silence.

Alice sighed.

“I’ll come.” Rio stopped pacing.

“Thank you.”

“But I’m bringing the person I’m with,” Alice continued. “I’m not just disappearing in the middle of a date.”

Rio pressed the bridge of her nose, breathing deeply.

“That’s fine.”

She paused briefly before adding:

“As long as we can talk alone.”

“We’ll figure something out,” Alice replied.

Another brief silence.

“I’m on my way.”

The call ended. Rio stood in the middle of the living room for a few seconds, still holding the phone. The restlessness inside her had not faded. Some time later, the apartment doorbell rang.

Rio was already waiting near the living room when she opened the door. Alice entered first, with her usual relaxed posture, her coat slung over her shoulder and a curious expression on her face.

Beside her was a dark-skinned woman, elegant, with a polite smile and attentive eyes. Alice made a casual gesture with her hand.

“Rio, this is Jen.”

Rio nodded immediately and extended her hand.

“Nice to meet you, Jen.”

“Nice to meet you,” she replied, shaking her hand politely.

Rio let out a small sigh and ran a hand through her hair before speaking.

“I’m sorry for interrupting your date. I really wouldn’t have done this if it wasn’t something serious.”

Jen studied her face for a moment, noticing the obvious nervousness.

“It’s okay,” she replied calmly.

Rio gestured toward the kitchen and the apartment’s bar.

“Please, make yourself comfortable. Help yourself to anything you want to eat or drink.”

Jen nodded again.

“Thank you.” Without wasting time, Rio lightly touched Alice’s arm.

“I need to talk to you.”

She pulled her toward the apartment office and closed the door behind them. As soon as they were alone, Alice crossed her arms immediately.

“Okay.” She tilted her head.

“Why the hell am I here?” — Her gaze scanned Rio’s face carefully.

“And what’s so urgent?”

The panic on Rio’s face was impossible to ignore. Rio began pacing around the office, running a hand over her face before speaking.

“Do you remember when I disappeared in Ibiza?”

Alice let out a short laugh.

“I remember. Your drunk ass ended up sleeping on your own yacht.”

Rio stopped.

“I wasn’t on a yacht.”

Alice blinked.

“What?”

“I wasn’t on a yacht,” Rio repeated, sighing.

Alice slowly uncrossed her arms.

“So… where were you?”

Rio ran a hand through her hair again.

“In a room.”

She paused briefly before adding:

“With a girl.” Alice’s eyes widened.

“Rio… holy shit.” Rio raised her hands in surrender.

“I know, I know. You can save the judgment for later.”

She took a deep breath.

“Her scent drew me in. I… can’t really explain it.”

Alice frowned.

“Her scent?”

Rio nodded.

“Yes.” Alice thought for a moment before replying.

“Nowadays it’s pretty rare for an alpha to be attracted to an omega just by scent. That was something from our grandparents’ time.”

Rio stopped.

Before Alice could finish the sentence, a noise came from the living room — the clear sound of glass shattering. Both of them immediately looked at the door.

Outside, Jen was standing near the bar, completely frozen, a fallen glass on the floor beside her while the liquid slowly spread across the surface. The expression on her face was pure disbelief.

For a full second, nobody said anything. Then Jen slowly turned and walked toward the exit door. Rio let out a heavy sigh, closing her eyes for a moment, while Alice ran a hand over her face before saying, already going after her:

“I’ll be right back.”

Alice left the office quickly and crossed the living room, passing the bar where the broken glass still lay on the floor. The liquid slowly spread over the wooden floor while she grabbed her coat without even really looking back.

Then she opened the apartment door and stepped into the building hallway.

The elevator hadn’t arrived yet.

Jen was standing there with her arms crossed, staring at the illuminated floor panel.

Her posture was rigid.

“Jen,” Alice called, approaching carefully. “Calm down.”

Jen slowly turned her face to look at her.

“What is it?”

Her tone was cold.

“Calm down,” Alice repeated, raising her hands slightly.

“Agatha is pregnant.”

Jen let out a small humorless laugh.

Alice froze completely.

Jen continued, her voice steady.

“And I’m going to tell my friend.”

“No,” Alice replied immediately. “No, no!”

She stepped forward quickly and grabbed Jen’s arm before she could press the elevator button again.

“Calm down. Let’s talk.”

Jen looked at the hand holding her arm for a second before answering.

“…Okay.”

The elevator arrived with a soft sound, they stepped inside, the doors closed. Jen turned toward Alice as soon as the elevator began descending.

“What the hell is going on?” Alice took a deep breath before answering.

“Does Agatha intend to keep the baby?” Jen didn’t hesitate.

“Yes.”

Alice closed her eyes for a second, processing that. Then she spoke carefully.

“Rio can’t know.”

Jen turned fully to face her.

“You want to spare your friend?” Alice shook her head.

“No.”

She sighed before continuing.

“Rio was raised in an extremely strict way by her grandmother. It was… heavy. Very heavy.”

The elevator descended slowly as she spoke.

“She grew up hearing that children and family were weakness, distraction, a mistake. That a woman in power couldn’t allow herself that kind of thing.”

Jen kept looking at her in silence.

“She made it very clear her entire life that she doesn’t want anything related to children,” Alice continued. “Nothing.”

Jen frowned.

“That’s ridiculous.”

“It’s not that simple,” Alice replied tiredly. “And Agatha doesn’t deserve to hear that.”

She leaned her head briefly against the metal wall of the elevator.

“She doesn’t deserve to be rejected like that.”

Jen stayed quiet for a few seconds. Then Alice added something else.

“And there’s another thing.”

Jen raised an eyebrow.

“What?”

Alice sighed.

“Agatha could be sued by the company.” — Jen froze.

“…What?”

“In the hiring questionnaire,” Alice explained, “there’s a specific clause about pregnancy. Lying there can be considered contractual fraud.”

The elevator doors opened in the lobby, the two of them stepped out and walked slowly toward the hall. Jen seemed to be processing several things at once now. Alice looked at her.

“What was Agatha’s plan?” Jen let out a heavy sigh.

“She wants to save money. Stay until the fifth month and then leave.” Alice nodded slowly.

“Right.” She thought for a moment before saying:

“I’ll try to hide it.” Jen looked at her in surprise.

“Why would you do that?” Alice stepped a little closer, lowering her voice slightly.

“Because any friend of yours is going to be my friend too.”

Jen exhaled slowly, still clearly conflicted.

“I’ll think about it.”

Alice nodded.

“Just… be careful.”

Her tone was serious now.

“This could get very complicated very fast.”

◆

 

When Jen finally arrived home, the apartment was lit only by the warm light of the living room. The television was off, but the glow of a phone illuminated the couch where Wanda and Agatha were sitting, wrapped in a large comforter that looked like it had been pulled straight from the bedroom.

As soon as the door opened, Wanda lifted her head.

“Jennn!”

She practically jumped off the couch and ran to the entrance, hugging Jen tightly.

“Hey!” Jen laughed, a little surprised by the reception. “What’s all this excitement about?”

Wanda let go of her, still grinning from ear to ear, and pulled her by the hand back to the living room.

Agatha was sitting on the couch, her legs folded beneath the comforter, the phone resting in her hands. Her face still looked a little tired, but when she saw Jen she gave a calm smile.

“Hey… how’s it going?”

Jen tried to smile back, hiding the exhaustion weighing on her shoulders after everything that had happened that night.

“What’s going on here?” she asked.

Wanda grabbed Agatha’s phone excitedly.

“I found an app!” She turned the screen toward Jen with childish enthusiasm.

“An app that tracks the baby’s size week by week.”

Agatha chuckled softly while Wanda kept explaining.

“And I shared it with the three of us! Now we can all follow along.”

She slid the screen to show the illustration.

“Look! Our nephew is the size of a piece of gum now!”

Jen looked at the screen. A small drawing compared the embryo to a pink piece of gum. She smiled, but there was something tired in that smile, and Agatha noticed immediately.

“Jen?” Jen looked up.

“Hm?”

“Are you okay?” Agatha asked. “You’re kind of… quiet.”

For a second Jen just looked at her. Then she walked to the couch and leaned down, placing a light kiss on Agatha’s cheek.

“It’s fine,” she said softly. “I’m just tired.”

She ran a hand through her hair.

“It was a long day.”

Agatha nodded.

“Then go lie down.”

“I will,” Jen replied.

She started walking down the hallway. Behind her, Agatha looked at her own belly under the comforter and spoke in a dramatic tone:

“See this? Your aunt is very strange.”

Jen stopped halfway down the hall and looked over her shoulder.

“That’s because he still doesn’t know his own mother.”

Wanda burst out laughing immediately. Agatha grabbed a pillow and threw it in Jen’s direction.

“Go to sleep!” Jen laughed as she walked down the hallway, but when she entered her bedroom and closed the door behind her, the smile slowly faded. The conversation with Alice was still echoing in her mind.

Chapter 4: The Secret

 

 

 

┕──━──━─┑◆┍─━──━──┙

Alice arrived at the company earlier than usual that morning. The building was still quiet, with the soft echo of footsteps in the marble lobby as she crossed the reception area holding her folders against her chest. The conversation from the previous night spun in her head like a scratched record, every sentence returning with more weight now that the reality was there, concrete.

She pressed the elevator button and waited in silence.

The doors opened with a soft sound and Alice stepped in, watching the numbers slowly climb on the illuminated panel. Her reflection in the metallic walls looked more tired than she cared to admit. She hadn’t slept well. When the elevator reached the company floor, the doors opened to an office that was still almost empty. The lights were on, but the typical movement of the morning hadn’t started yet.

Alice walked down the corridor toward the desk area. That was when she saw Agatha’s things. The bag was on the chair, half open, and the computer was already on the desk, as if she had arrived only a short time ago.

Alice hesitated for a second.

Then she stepped closer. On the computer screen there was a page open. A paused video. Something about a maternity class.

She stared for a few seconds, reading the title, feeling the weight of the confirmation slowly tighten in her chest. Carefully, almost not wanting to be too invasive, Alice glanced lightly inside the open bag on the chair.

Inside were a few bottles.

Vitamins. Prenatal vitamins. Alice took a deep breath, letting the air out slowly as she closed her eyes for a moment. When she opened them again, she looked around the office.

"Agatha?" she called softly.

No answer. The floor was still quiet. That was when she heard a faint sound, a heavy breath. Alice turned her head toward the corridor that led to the bathrooms and walked slowly. The closer she got, the clearer the sound became — a gag, a strained effort.

She entered the women’s bathroom carefully. The white lights reflected on the spotless tiles, all the stalls closed except the last one at the back, whose door was slightly ajar. Alice approached slowly. The sound was coming from there.

She pushed the door gently.

Agatha was kneeling in front of the toilet, her body bent forward as she vomited. Her hair fell over her face and her shoulders were tense with the effort. For a second Alice just watched. Then she knelt beside her and, with an automatic and gentle gesture, held Agatha’s hair back.

Agatha immediately lifted her face. Her eyes widened. Startled.

She had been caught.

A few minutes later, Agatha was sitting in Alice’s office, holding a glass of water with both hands while trying to catch her breath. The soft air conditioning in the room helped reduce the heat that still rose to her face after the nausea.

Alice was sitting on the other side of the desk, watching her carefully.

"Are you okay?" she asked calmly.

Agatha nodded slowly, taking another sip of water.

"I am… this just… happens sometimes."

Alice nodded slightly. There was a small silence before she asked, carefully:

"How many weeks?"

Agatha took a deep breath before answering.

"I’ll be entering my seventh week soon."

Alice gave a small confirming nod.

"Alright." She rested her elbows on the desk, joining her hands.

"You know the seriousness of the situation you’re in, don’t you?"

Agatha nodded immediately.

"I do."

Alice sighed, choosing her words carefully.

"Lying on the hiring questionnaire can be considered contractual fraud. The clause provides for a fifty-thousand-dollar fine for damages to the company."

Agatha turned completely pale.

"What?" The glass almost slipped from her hands. "I don’t have that kind of money."

Panic appeared in her voice instantly. Alice raised a hand calmly.

"Hey. Calm down."

Agatha was breathing fast now.

"I don’t have fifty thousand dollars, Alice. I—"

"Agatha," Alice interrupted with gentle firmness. "Breathe."

Agatha closed her eyes for a second and tried to compose herself. Alice waited until she calmed down a little before continuing.

"What’s your plan?" Alice asked.

Agatha remained silent for a moment before answering.

"Save money." She looked at the glass of water. "Stay until the fifth month… and then leave."

Alice nodded slowly.

"Alright." She leaned back in the chair.

"You can break the contract in the fifth month without generating that fine."

Agatha immediately lifted her gaze.

"I can?"

"You can." Alice paused before adding, "But until then you’ll need to hide this pregnancy."

Agatha remained silent for a few seconds.

"Why… are you helping me?"

Alice sighed lightly.

"Because I like Jen very much." She gave a small sideways smile.

"And I know the two of you are practically sisters."

Alice crossed her arms.

"So I’m helping for that reason."

But in the back of her mind another sentence echoed with unsettling clarity. I know who the other mother of that baby is.

Agatha took a deep breath, clearly relieved.

"Thank you."

She said it sincerely.

"I promise I’ll keep doing my job normally."

Alice nodded.

"I know you will." She discreetly pointed toward the door.

"You can go back to your desk."

Agatha stood up slowly, still holding the empty glass.

"Thank you, Alice."

Alice simply nodded. When Agatha left the room, Alice stared at the closed door for a few seconds, completely silent.

◆

Rio was running on the treadmill when the morning sun was already streaming through the enormous apartment windows. The floor-to-ceiling glass allowed a cold winter light to enter, illuminating the minimalist living room, the perfectly lined furniture, and the impeccable silence of the place. The treadmill’s rhythm was fast, constant, her steps firm as the impact of her body struck the platform while she tried to turn the restlessness into physical effort.

Normally, at that time of the morning, Rio would already be at the office. Punctual. Impeccable. In control.

But that morning she was still there.

And deep down, she knew exactly why.

She was avoiding Agatha.

The thought was irritating enough to make her increase the treadmill’s speed for a few seconds, her heart accelerating as sweat began to run down the back of her neck.

Then the phone rang.

The screen lit up on the side panel of the treadmill.

Rio looked at it and sighed.

"Abuela."

She reduced the speed until the treadmill stopped completely, taking a deep breath before grabbing the phone. Her body was still warm from the exercise, her chest rising and falling slowly as she passed a towel over her face. For a second she considered not answering.

But she did.

"Yes, Abuela."

The voice on the other end came firm, elegant, and authoritarian as always.

"Rio."

There was no warm greeting. There never had been.

"Will you attend the family gathering this weekend in Long Island?"

Rio sat on the edge of the treadmill, resting her elbows on her knees while she caught her breath.

"Yes. I will."

There was a small evaluative silence on the other end of the line.

"Bring Jessica."

Rio closed her eyes for a second before answering.

"I’ll bring her."

Her grandmother made a faint sound of approval.

"Good."

The next pause carried that familiar sensation of silent judgment that had followed Rio since childhood.

"It seems you’ve finally found a suitable bride."

Rio ran a hand over her face, still feeling the heat from the workout.

"Don’t waste that."

The words came sharp, spoken like an order disguised as advice.

Throughout Rio’s childhood, that woman had been the dominant presence in the house. The matriarch of an old fortune, a widow, relentless, accustomed to commanding everything around her as if the entire world were merely another extension of the family empire.

When Rio’s mother returned to that house years earlier — pregnant, widowed, and broken after her husband, a poor alpha, died in an accident on an oil platform — the decision had already been made before they even crossed the gates of the mansion.

The daughter had failed.

And now it was up to the granddaughter to redeem the family name.

Rio was the only alpha among her sisters, and that had defined her fate from the very first day.

Her grandmother shaped every detail of her upbringing with discipline. Business, strategy, emotional control, social dominance. Love was described as distraction, affection as weakness.

And children…

Children were tools.

"An alpha who allows herself to be distracted by motherhood too early destroys her own future," her grandmother often said.

"Children should only exist when there is something worthy for them to inherit. Legacy and continuity."

Never love. Never choice. Never weakness.

On the other side of the line, her grandmother’s voice returned, breaking the silence.

"Jessica seems suitable. A good family."

Rio looked out the window, observing the city far below.

"She is."

"Excellent. Don’t ruin it."

Rio took a deep breath.

"I won’t."

On the other end, the call ended without goodbyes.

Rio stared at the phone for a few seconds before letting it fall beside the treadmill. Silence filled the apartment again. She ran a hand through her hair, still damp with sweat, and rested her elbows on her knees, looking at the floor while her thoughts slowly returned to the place she had been trying to avoid since the night before.

Agatha.

The face. The photo in Ibiza. The nickname.

"Aggie."

And the scent. Rio closed her eyes for a moment, because no matter how much she tried to organize it logically, one question kept hammering in the back of her mind.

If it was her…

why wasn’t the scent the same?

A few minutes later, Rio finally got up from the treadmill. Sitting there thinking wasn’t helping at all. She had already spent too much time trying to escape her own mind that morning.

It was ridiculous.

Rio Vidal didn’t run from anything.

She walked to the apartment bathroom, turned on the shower, and let the hot water fall over her shoulders while she tried to reorganize her thoughts. Steam slowly filled the light marble space as she ran her hands over her face, breathing deeply. She needed to regain control. She needed to return to routine.

When she stepped out of the shower, she dressed in one of her favorite suits: a deep navy blue wool set, tailored to perfection and structured impeccably on her body. The white shirt underneath was simple, perfectly pressed. On her feet were polished black leather loafers. The jewelry was discreet — a thin gold ring, small minimalist earrings, and an absurdly expensive Swiss watch on her wrist, the kind that didn’t need to draw attention to make its value clear.

Silent elegance.

Absolute control.

Exactly as she had been taught.

Rio grabbed the car keys from the entry table before leaving the apartment and went down the private elevator to the building’s garage.

The elevator opened directly into a wide, silent space, illuminated by recessed lights in the concrete ceiling. It was a nearly empty garage, reserved for only a few residents of the building.

Rio’s car was parked in the center of the space.

A black Bentley Continental GT, immaculate, with classic and elegant lines. Rio had always liked driving.

Especially when she was anxious.

She got into the car, closed the door with the muted sound typical of extremely expensive vehicles, and started the engine. The deep, smooth rumble filled the silence of the garage as she slowly pulled out of the parking spot.

Minutes later, she was already on the city streets.

Driving helped her mind slow down. The focus on traffic, on the lights, on the turns. For a few moments she could push the thought of the weekend in Long Island to the back of her mind.

Her grandmother’s house.

That house always brought a strange weight to her chest even now.

Even after so many years.

Rio turned the corner that passed in front of the company building, her distracted gaze observing the reflective glass façade as she slowed down because of traffic.

That was when she saw Agatha.

She was standing near a food cart on the sidewalk.

And she was absolutely focused on devouring a hot dog.

It wasn’t an elegant dinner. It wasn’t a discreet meal.

It was a full attack.

Agatha held the bun with both hands, taking a generous bite while trying to keep the sauce from dripping. Even so, a little mustard had ended up on the tip of her nose.

Rio blinked.

And then… she laughed.

A short laugh, almost involuntary.

The scene was absurdly funny, and strangely… cute.

Agatha seemed completely oblivious to the world around her, chewing with that childlike concentration of someone who was simply very hungry.

Rio watched for another second, and then she frowned slightly.

Cute?

She immediately looked back at the street, correcting her own thought with irritation.

"Ridiculous."

Without slowing much, she turned the steering wheel and entered the ramp that led to the company building’s garage. The car descended smoothly as the automatic gate opened.

Rio parked the car and turned off the engine, remaining silent inside the vehicle for a few seconds before stepping out. The cool air of the garage filled the space when she opened the door. A valet was already approaching, attentive.

The alpha stepped out of the car and handed over the keys with a brief gesture. The man nodded respectfully before taking the vehicle.

She crossed the garage toward the building entrance, the loafers echoing softly on the polished floor. When she entered the lobby and walked toward the main elevators, she spotted a familiar figure standing in front of the closed doors.

Agatha.

She was waiting for the elevator, holding her bag and tablet, still discreetly finishing wiping a small trace of sauce from the napkin she held.

Rio stopped beside her.

"The hot dog looked good."

Agatha nearly jumped.

She turned quickly.

"Mrs. Vidal… good morning."

Rio tilted her head slightly.

"Good morning."

Agatha adjusted the bag on her shoulder, clearly surprised to have been caught in that less-than-elegant moment.

"And… yes," she said, trying to sound casual. "The hot dog was great. You should try one."

Rio raised an eyebrow.

"It isn’t healthy."

Agatha gave a small crooked smile.

"Sorry, health generation."

Rio cast a sideways glance at her, her expression slightly disapproving.

"Agatha."

The tone came firm, almost automatic.

"You should eat properly."

The sentence came out before she even realized how… personal it sounded.

Rio frowned slightly at herself. She didn’t know exactly why the image of her eating street food had triggered that sudden concern.

Agatha blinked, a little confused.

"Right…"

The elevator still hadn’t arrived. Rio let out a small sigh before speaking again.

"I will be traveling this weekend."

Agatha looked at her.

"Family trip."

Rio kept her gaze fixed on the elevator doors as she continued.

"I would like you to accompany me. To handle work matters."

Agatha thought for a moment.

"I… have a commitment on Saturday morning."

"That’s not a problem. The trip will be at night. The less time I spend there, the better."

Agatha relaxed a little, although a certain curiosity was beginning to form in her mind.

"In that case… I can go."

Rio turned her face slightly toward her.

"Excellent."

There was a small silence before she added:

"There will be an additional payment of three thousand dollars per day."

Agatha blinked.

Her eyes practically lit up.

"Three thousand… per day?"

Rio nodded naturally.

"It’s the weekend. Your time is more expensive and I pay bonuses."

Agatha broke into an immediate smile, clearly trying to contain her excitement.

"Alright." She adjusted the tablet against her chest.

"It will be great."

The elevator doors finally opened in front of them.

◆

When night arrived, Agatha was sitting on the bedroom floor, cross-legged in front of the bed while the open suitcase occupied almost the entire mattress. Clothes were scattered around like small islands of fabric: shirts half folded, a dress still hanging on the chair, a pair of shoes she still hadn’t decided whether it was worth bringing.

The room was illuminated by the warm light of the late afternoon coming through the window, and the apartment carried that comfortable noise of a lived-in home — footsteps in the hallway, the distant sound of the refrigerator opening, the television on somewhere in the living room.

Wanda was sitting cross-legged on the bed, completely relaxed, scrolling through her phone while occasionally observing the mess with interest. Jen, on the other hand, was leaning against the dresser with her arms crossed, watching everything with the amused smile of someone who had already accepted that this suitcase was going to take longer than it should.

"Seriously," Agatha said, folding a shirt with more force than necessary before throwing it into the suitcase and standing up to organize the pile of clothes, "it was weird as hell."

Jen slowly raised an eyebrow.

"What?"

Agatha lifted her gaze to the two of them.

"Her telling me to eat properly."

Wanda burst into immediate laughter, one of those laughs that came quickly and without any attempt to hide how funny she found the situation.

"She’s right."

"That doesn’t make it any less weird as hell. Like, who does she think she is?"

Wanda laughed, tossed her phone aside, and crawled across the bed until she reached the edge, leaning her body forward dramatically. Without asking for any permission, she pressed her ear against Agatha’s still almost-flat belly as if she were trying to hear something important.

“My nephew deserves good things.”

Jen laughed, shaking her head.

“You two are weird.”

Agatha let out a small huff, but the corner of her mouth betrayed a smile as she picked up another piece of clothing to fold.

Jen uncrossed her arms and shifted slightly.

“So… three thousand dollars?”

Agatha lifted both hands into the air as if she still couldn’t believe her own luck.

“Can you believe that shit?”

She grabbed a pair of pants from the chair beside her and began folding them carefully before placing them in the suitcase.

“I’m going to be able to save a lot to buy the baby’s things. I saw a crib the other day and almost fell over when I saw the price.”

Wanda dramatically lifted her head from Agatha’s belly.

“Don’t even tell me.”

Agatha kept organizing the clothes while she spoke.

“There’s a ton of stuff I still need to buy.”

She paused, frowning slightly as she remembered something she had researched the night before. Then she looked up at the two of them with a slightly incredulous expression.

“I’m going to have to buy pads for my boobs. Seriously?”

Wanda and Jen started laughing at the same time. Jen pointed at her while laughing.

“You can’t be a milf without the milk part.”

Agatha immediately made a face.

“I need to have milk. I’m not giving formula to my baby.”

Wanda lifted her head from Agatha’s belly again with an exaggeratedly serious expression, as if she were about to deliver a very important medical diagnosis.

“Relax.”

She gave Agatha’s belly two light taps.

“You’re usually a mean cow. You won’t have any problems with that.”

Agatha stared at her in complete silence for two seconds before rolling her eyes.

“Why did I let you become my baby’s aunt?”

Wanda flashed a victorious smile, clearly proud of her place in the life of a child who hadn’t even been born yet.

“Because you don’t have a choice.”

Agatha let out a resigned sigh while folding another T-shirt and tossing it into the suitcase.

“We’ll see.”

◆

The next morning Agatha was sitting in the hospital waiting room, alone for the first time since everything had begun.

The environment carried that characteristic smell of a medical place — a light mix of disinfectant, distant coffee coming from a machine somewhere in the hallway, and the soft scent of decorative plants scattered around the space.

Agatha held a magazine she had picked up from the small central table, one of those home décor magazines that probably nobody actually read. The glossy pages showed minimalist living rooms, perfect kitchens, and absurdly expensive beach houses.

But Agatha wasn’t reading.

Her eyes moved across the pages without really registering anything.

Every now and then she lifted her gaze and looked around. Most of the people there were in pairs.

Couples.

Some were talking quietly, others holding hands, and others looking together at phone screens as if that were some kind of silent waiting ritual. A pregnant woman rested her head on her partner’s shoulder while a man absentmindedly stroked the belly of the wife sitting beside him.

Agatha let out a small sigh.

Wanda had really tried to come.

But in the middle of the night her parents had called about some problem and she had to leave in a hurry. Jen, on the other hand, had worked late and barely managed to sleep a few hours.

Agatha understood. Of course she did.

Even so, the feeling of being there alone was… strange.

A little heavy.

She turned another page of the magazine without paying attention.

That was when a soft voice spoke beside her.

“Are you alone?”

Agatha lifted her eyes.

A woman had approached and was standing beside the empty chair. She had short blonde hair, a gentle smile, and a relaxed posture that seemed naturally comfortable in any environment.

“Yes,” Agatha replied.

“May I?” The woman pointed to the chair beside her.

“Of course.” Agatha nodded.

The woman sat down and then made a small gesture toward the magazine.

“Sorry for interrupting your reading.”

Agatha looked at the magazine in her hands and gave a small smile.

“It’s okay. I wasn’t really reading.”

The woman extended her hand.

“Natasha.”

Agatha shook it.

“Agatha.”

Natasha nodded, settling into the chair.

“I’m accompanying my sister and my sister-in-law.”

Agatha tilted her head, curious.

“They’re in the appointment?”

“Yes. My sister, Yelena, broke her arm and can’t drive. And my sister-in-law, Kate…” she let out a small laugh, “…is way too pregnant to drive.”

Agatha smiled.

“So you became the driver.”

Natasha shrugged.

“Basically.”

“That’s very kind of you,” Agatha said.

Natasha tilted her head in thanks.

“Thank you.”

She crossed her legs before continuing.

“Actually, I usually give some preparation classes for pregnant women. I’m a personal trainer.”

Agatha raised her eyebrows.

“Really?”

“Yes,” Natasha replied. “But I started investing more in that after watching my sister-in-law’s pregnancy and my sister freaking out about absolutely everything.”

Agatha laughed.

“That sounds fair.”

Natasha smiled.

“So… you’re welcome to come to one of the classes. You can come with your partner.”

She handed Agatha a card, which she took.

Agatha hesitated a little before answering.

“I’ll probably go alone.”

She gave a small polite smile.

“But I appreciate the invitation.”

Natasha raised her hands in surrender.

“Sorry if I was intrusive.”

Agatha shook her head.

“I forgive you.”

Natasha laughed softly.

At that moment, the door of one of the offices opened. Natasha’s eyes immediately turned toward it.

A couple came out of the room.

A short woman with dark hair and a very advanced belly walked slowly while holding her lower back.

“My spine is going to fall onto the floor,” she complained dramatically.

Beside her came another blonde woman, very similar to Natasha, but with her arm in a cast held against her body with a sling.

“I told you to stop complaining in front of the doctor,” the blonde replied.

“I’m not complaining,” the pregnant woman shot back.

Natasha stood up immediately.

“There they are.”

She looked back at Agatha.

“It was a pleasure meeting you.”

Agatha smiled.

“Likewise.”

Natasha began walking toward the two women but turned her head over her shoulder before leaving completely.

“I want to see you in class.”

Agatha lifted the magazine in a small farewell gesture.

“I’ll be there.”

Agatha’s eyes followed the trio as they walked away down the hospital corridor. Natasha moved between her sister and her sister-in-law with the ease of someone already used to taking care of both of them at the same time.

The pregnant woman kept complaining about her back while the blonde with the cast tried to balance a huge bag on her shoulder, and for a moment the scene felt so domestic, so full of small intimacies, that Agatha felt a strange pang in her chest.

The corridor door closed behind them and the silence of the waiting room returned. Agatha was still holding the magazine in her hands when she heard her name being called.

“Agatha Harkness?”

She immediately looked up.

A nurse was standing near the consultation room door with a tablet in her hands.

Agatha stood up, adjusting her bag on her shoulder before walking toward her. She followed the white corridor illuminated by cold lights into a familiar room, where the doctor was already organizing some papers on the desk.

“Good morning, Agatha,” the doctor said with a gentle smile.

“Good morning, doctor,” Agatha replied, sitting in the chair in front of the desk.

Lorna picked up the tablet and quickly reviewed the notes.

“How are you feeling?”

Agatha let out a tired sigh before answering.

“The nausea is… strong.” She rested her elbows on her legs. “Very strong.”

“It’s worse in the morning,” she continued. “Sometimes it feels like any smell makes me sick. And I’m tired all the time.”

Lorna nodded calmly.

“That’s quite common in early pregnancy.”

She placed the tablet on the desk.

“Especially around the seventh week.”

Agatha ran a hand over her face.

“Wonderful.”

Lorna smiled slightly.

“In some cases, the presence of the alpha can also help reduce certain hormonal discomforts.”

Agatha stayed silent for a second. Then she let out a small sigh.

“That would be helpful… if I knew who it was.”

Lorna looked up.

Agatha rested her hands in her lap, clearly a little embarrassed.

“I… had a fling in Ibiza a few weeks ago.” She looked down at the desk. “It was irresponsible, and I really don’t know who got me pregnant.”

The doctor remained silent for a few seconds, absorbing the information with professional calm. Then she tilted her head thoughtfully.

“Curious.”

Agatha looked up.

“Curious?”

Lorna picked up the phone that was on the desk and slid her finger across the screen.

“My daughter was in Ibiza some time ago too.”

Agatha blinked.

“Really?”

“Yes.”

Lorna turned the phone screen toward her and showed a photo.

Agatha looked.

And froze.

“I… know her.”

Lorna raised her eyebrows.

“You do?”

Agatha pointed to the screen.

“Alice.”

She let out a small nervous laugh.

“She’s one of my bosses.”

Lorna’s face lit up immediately.

“What a wonderful coincidence.”

Agatha still looked slightly surprised while staring at the photo. Lorna placed the phone back on the table.

“Wouldn’t you like to try to find out who the other mother is? Maybe Alice knows the person,” Lorna continued.

Agatha hesitated and then sighed.

“Alice… knows about my situation.”

The doctor tilted her head curiously.

“She does?”

“Yes.” Agatha intertwined her fingers.

“She’s helping me hide the pregnancy until the fifth month.”

Lorna remained silent for a moment.

Agatha let out a small sigh.

“And the truth is… I don’t even really remember what the person I spent that night with looked like.”

The office remained quiet for a few seconds. Lorna observed Agatha carefully. Then she spoke gently.

“Either way… you are very strong.”

Agatha looked up.

“Going through this alone is not easy.”

The doctor folded her hands on the desk.

“Even when we have friends, pregnancy can still be a very lonely experience.”

She smiled kindly.

“So you are being very brave.”

Agatha let out her breath slowly, as if those words had lifted a small weight from her shoulders.

“Thank you.”

Lorna stood up from her chair.

“Let’s see how this baby is doing.”

She gestured toward the next room.

Agatha stood up as well and followed the doctor to the other side of the room.

◆

A few hours later, Alice entered the small Chinese restaurant where she used to meet her mother when they needed to talk privately.

The place was discreet, elegant in its traditional way: red lanterns hung from the dark wooden ceiling, and the air carried the warm smell of ginger, sesame oil, and broth simmering in some pot in the open kitchen at the back.

It was an old restaurant, frequented almost exclusively by regular customers, and Alice had been coming there since her teenage years.

She still found the message she had received strange.

Her mother’s call had been too short.

Too direct.

Lorna never scheduled last-minute meetings. Never. She was methodical, organized, disciplined — exactly the type of person who planned important conversations days in advance.

When something was scheduled on the same day…

It was because there was a problem.

And a big one.

Alice crossed the dining room quickly toward the maître d’, slightly nodding in greeting before speaking in low, quick Mandarin. The man immediately recognized the family name and responded in the same language, pointing toward the back of the restaurant.

“Your mother has already arrived.”

He led her to a reserved table near the window.

Lorna was already seated.

Impeccable as always. Straight posture, hands resting on the table, a steaming cup of tea in front of her.

Alice immediately pulled out the chair.

“Mā (mom).”

The word came out quickly.

“What happened? Why did you call me like that out of nowhere?”

Lorna didn’t answer immediately. She simply lifted her eyes to her daughter.

“Sit.”

Alice was already sitting.

“I am sitting,” she replied impatiently.

“What happened?”

Lorna picked up the teacup and took a small sip before speaking. The sentence came in Mandarin, calm and precise.

And completely devastating.

“Please don’t tell me that Rio Vidal is the one who got that poor girl who works as her secretary pregnant.”

Alice opened her mouth, but no words came out. Her eyes blinked slowly while her brain tried to process the fact that her mother had reached that conclusion entirely on her own.

“Y-you’re talking about Miss Harkness?”

Lorna immediately narrowed her eyes.

“Don’t play dumb.”

The reprimand came quickly in Mandarin.

“It’s absurd that you’re covering for Rio.”

Alice let out a heavy sigh, running a hand over her face.

“Mom…”

“She’s your friend,” Lorna continued, still in Mandarin. “It’s not your responsibility.”

Alice lowered her voice.

“The situation is more complicated than that.”

Lorna placed the teacup on the table firmly.

“A young pregnant woman, alone, working for a rich alpha who clearly has something to do with this,” she said slowly.

“And you think it’s acceptable to hide this?”

Alice pinched the bridge of her nose between her fingers.

“I’m trying to protect Agatha.”

Lorna raised an eyebrow.

“Protect her?”

“Yes.”

Alice leaned forward, also switching fully to Mandarin.

“If Rio finds out now, it will be a disaster.”

“Why?”

Alice stayed silent for a second. Then she spoke more quietly.

“Because Rio hates the idea of having children.”

Lorna observed her daughter for a few seconds.

“That doesn’t change the fact that she’s responsible.”

Alice sighed tiredly.

“I know.”

Lorna folded her hands on the table.

“Then you should tell her.”

Alice immediately shook her head.

“No.”

“Alice.”

“No.”

The silence between them lasted for a few seconds.

Finally Alice spoke again.

“Agatha wants to wait until the fifth month. And I can’t tell Rio. If I tell her, Rio will reject Agatha, and I don’t want her to feel rejected. Agatha is a close friend of someone I’m seeing, so I care about her.”

“Did you make her friend lie to her?”

Alice froze.

“Not lie… omit, Mā. If Rio finds out about this, her grandmother will find out too. And when that woman finds out something, she doesn’t simply accept it. She interferes.”

Alice spoke softly in Mandarin, leaning over the table, her fingers intertwined while she tried to organize her thoughts.

“And you know exactly what she will want.”

Lorna remained silent.

“She will demand that Agatha get rid of the child.”

The sentence fell heavily between them.

The restaurant continued alive around them — the clinking of porcelain, low voices in parallel conversations, the smell of hot broth being served at nearby tables — but at the small table it felt as if the world had stopped for a moment.

Alice took a deep breath.

“And you know that woman is cruel.”

Lorna didn’t answer immediately. She simply watched her daughter, her dark eyes attentive.

Alice ran a hand through her hair before continuing.

“You remember when Rio had to leave home.”

Lorna lowered her gaze for a second.

She remembered.

The Vidal house had always been a rigid environment, almost military, governed by the grandmother with an iron hand. Rules, expectations, discipline — everything shaped to turn Rio into a perfect successor to the family empire.

But there were things that not even money or tradition could hide. Alice continued, her voice slightly lower.

“That woman doesn’t forgive weakness.”

She lifted her eyes again to her mother.

It was a long, black sedan — classic and silent — its headlights illuminating the small stone road that led deeper into the property. A uniformed driver opened the rear door with a respectful gesture.

Rio entered first. Jessica followed right after, settling on the other side, and Agatha entered last, pulling her suitcase inside before the driver closed the door.

The car began to climb the road.

Outside, the landscape was almost entirely dark, except for the discreet lights positioned along the path. The road wound between tall, ancient trees, their branches forming dense shadows that moved across the hood of the car as they advanced.

A few minutes later, the mansion began to appear between the trees.

It was large.

Classic.

An old construction of pale stone with tall columns supporting the front veranda. The windows were high, framed in dark wood, and the golden light coming from inside made the house look almost like a silent palace in the middle of the estate. The garden was vast, perfectly maintained, with white gravel paths, discreet sculptures, and trees trimmed with precision.

The car stopped in front of the main entrance.

Immediately two employees descended the veranda steps and opened the vehicle doors.

Rio stepped out first.

Jessica came right after, adjusting her clothes while looking around with curiosity.

Agatha stepped out last, pulling her suitcase from the seat and holding the handle while quickly observing the facade of the house. The main door was already open and the warm light from inside illuminated the large entrance hall.

They walked inside.

The interior was as imposing as the exterior. The marble floor reflected the light from a large crystal chandelier hanging in the center of the high ceiling. The walls were decorated with dark wooden panels and old paintings in golden frames.

As soon as they entered, a woman appeared in the side corridor.

She walked toward Rio with quick steps.

She was elegant, but there was something restrained in the way she moved — as if she were always trying to occupy less space than she truly had the right to.

"Rio."

The woman opened her arms and hugged her. The smile on her face was small.

Contained. Almost as if it did not have permission to be bigger.

"Mamá," Rio replied, returning the hug briefly.

Before they could continue the conversation, another presence appeared in the corridor.

An older woman.

Her hair was completely white, tied impeccably at the nape of her neck. Her face was marked by deep lines, but her eyes were still extremely sharp and attentive. Her posture was rigid, elegant, and her serious expression seemed permanently molded there.

Rio immediately turned her head.

"Abuela."

The woman observed her granddaughter for a few seconds before replying.

"Rio. It's good to see you."

Then her eyes slowly moved to the side.

They stopped on Jessica.

Rio made a small gesture.

"This is Jessica."

The older woman observed her from head to toe with almost clinical attention. Then she nodded.

"She is beautiful."

A short pause.

"Finally you got something right."

Jessica let out a small awkward laugh.

"Thank you..."

Rio ignored the comment.

"Where are my sisters?"

Rio’s mother answered, her voice low.

"In the living room."

The grandmother nodded.

"Very well. Prepare yourselves for dinner."

It was then that her eyes finally turned to Agatha.

She analyzed her for an entire moment.

"And who is this?"

"My secretary. She will handle work matters while I am here," Rio replied calmly.

The older woman nodded once.

"Good."

Then she added, in the same cold tone:

"Dinner will be served soon. Subordinates eat in the kitchen."

Agatha remained silent for a moment. Her eyes moved discreetly toward Rio.

Rio met her gaze and simply nodded slightly — the decision had already been made.

Rio’s grandmother spoke again while walking away down the corridor.

"The family dinner will be served shortly."

And she walked away without looking back.

Jessica let out a quiet sigh.

Agatha remained still for a second.

And, honestly, she almost felt grateful she would not have to spend dinner looking at that woman’s face.

Agatha ended up having dinner in the kitchen with the house staff.

The mansion's kitchen was enormous, larger than many apartments she had seen. Tall cabinets of light wood covered an entire wall, while a large marble island occupied the center of the room. Copper pots hung neatly above the industrial stove, and the air was filled with the comforting smell of hot food — garlic, herbs, broth, and bread fresh from the oven.

The staff were kind.

One of the cooks insisted that she sit, placing a generous plate of food in front of her. Another employee brought wine, but Agatha declined and asked only for water. They talked a little while she ate — simple questions, light comments about the weather, about the movement in the house when the family gathered.

Nothing hostile.

In fact… it was almost welcoming.

Much more comfortable than the family’s large dining hall probably would have been.

After she finished, Agatha thanked them several times, carried her plate to the sink despite the cooks' protests, and finally headed upstairs guided by one of the maids.

The room they had prepared for her was simple.

But still… luxurious.

The bed was large, a double, with perfectly stretched white sheets and a heavy comforter that looked absurdly soft. There was a small stone fireplace on one wall, two armchairs near the window, and a thick rug covering much of the dark wooden floor.
