The decoration was minimal, but elegant — an old painting above the bed, a golden lamp on the bedside table, and heavy curtains framing the window that overlooked the dark garden of the property.

Agatha dropped her suitcase in the corner and simply threw herself onto the bed, the mattress sinking beneath her weight.

She let out a long sigh.

Every muscle in her body seemed sore — the exhaustion of the trip, the stress of the entire day, the constant nervousness since they had arrived at that house.

After a few seconds staring at the ceiling, she grabbed her phone.

She called Wanda.

The call was answered almost immediately.

"Finally!" Wanda's voice came from the other side.

Jen was there too, visible on the screen, leaning over her shoulder.

Agatha didn’t even try to be diplomatic.

"What a weird place."

"What happened?" Wanda raised her eyebrows.

Agatha told them everything.

The boat trip, the isolated mansion, Rio’s mother who seemed to smile as if she were afraid to do so.

And especially the grandmother.

"She literally said that subordinates eat in the kitchen," Agatha finished, still a little incredulous.

Jen let out a short laugh.

"Holy shit."

Wanda shook her head.

"Sounds like one of those dysfunctional TV families."

Agatha made a face.

"It feels like more than that."

She rubbed her face.

"It’s strange. Like… really strange."

Wanda crossed her arms.

"Well, you survived the first day."

Jen added:

"That’s already a victory."

Agatha laughed softly.

After a few more minutes of conversation — comments about the place, some jokes about Rio’s terrifying grandmother and quick updates about what was happening at home — Agatha sighed.

"I'm going to take a shower."

Wanda nodded.

"Go get some rest."

"Good night, grumpy pregnant woman," Jen said.

Agatha showed her middle finger to the camera before hanging up.

She got up from the bed and walked to the bathroom.

The bathroom was almost as big as the bedroom.

Light marble covered the floor and walls, a deep bathtub sat near the window, and a huge glass shower occupied the corner.

Agatha began removing her blouse as she walked.

Within seconds she was wearing only her bra.

That was when someone knocked on the door.

"Who is it?"

The voice on the other side answered immediately.

"It's me, Rio."

Agatha frowned for a second before walking to the door. She opened it only slightly, hiding her body behind the wood and sticking only her face out.

Rio was standing outside.

And she looked… slightly embarrassed, like a child caught stealing candy.

"Is everything alright?" she asked.

Agatha nodded.

"Yes… I'm fine." Rio hesitated for a moment before continuing.

"I wanted to apologize for my grandmother’s behavior."

Agatha shrugged.

"It’s not a problem."

She tilted her head slightly.

"The staff were very polite to me."

Rio let out a small sigh.

"Tomorrow there will be a small gathering here. You may attend."

Agatha nodded.

Then she tilted her head.

"Are you okay?"

Rio froze for a second at the question.

"Why?"

Agatha answered naturally.

"Because your grandmother was rude to you in a completely unnecessary way."

Rio looked away for a moment before replying.

"Don't worry about that. It’s just her way."

Rude way, Agatha thought. But she didn’t say it out loud.

Rio seemed strangely unsettled — almost as if she didn’t quite know why she had gone there.

She simply nodded slightly.

"Good night, Miss Harkness."

Then she turned and walked down the corridor.

Agatha closed the door slowly.

She stood there for a second.

That had been… bizarre.

Agatha shook her head, pushing the thought away, and returned to the bathroom.

It was better to simply take a shower and try to sleep.

◆

The next morning, Rio woke early.

The room was still immersed in the soft dimness of dawn, the pale sunlight beginning to pass through the heavy curtains of the mansion and drawing subtle golden lines across the dark carpet. The silence of the house was almost absolute at that hour, broken only by the faint sound of wind passing through the trees in the garden and the distant creaking of the old wooden structure.

Rio remained lying on her back in bed.

She wore a closed dark silk pajama set, elegant even in something so simple, the smooth fabric fitted to her body with the same precision as all the clothes she wore. Beside her, Jessica slept deeply, turned to the side, her hair spread across the pillow and her breathing slow and calm.

Rio turned her head slightly to observe her.

She stayed like that for a few seconds.

In silence. But her mind was already somewhere else.

Ibiza.

The memory appeared with almost uncomfortable clarity: the warm hotel room, the windows open letting in the smell of the sea and the night, the golden late-morning light passing through the pale curtains.

And Agatha there, beside her in the sheets, dark hair spread across the pillow, her skin still warm.

Rio should have done something in that moment.

She should have said something, made a decision, prevented that morning from becoming only an awkward silence between two people who had clearly shared something more than a simple night.

But she didn’t.

The only thing that came to her mind at that moment was fear.

A strange, cold fear that crossed her chest like a warning. The sensation that any step forward could break something she did not yet fully understand.

She had been a coward.

Rio took a deep breath and slowly got up.

She picked up a dark robe folded over an armchair and put it on before leaving the room, closing the door carefully so as not to make noise.

The mansion corridor was silent and cool, illuminated only by the soft light coming through the tall windows along the wall.

As she walked slowly, another memory crossed her mind with the same uncomfortable clarity.

The lunch at the equestrian club.

Her saying she had to meet her girlfriend.

She has a girlfriend.

Rio tightened her jaw slightly as she continued walking down the corridor.

The simple idea of Agatha with someone else — laughing, talking, or receiving some kind of flirtation — caused a strange discomfort in her chest.

It was almost physical.

Like a silent pressure settling somewhere between her stomach and her ribs.

And she didn’t know how to explain why.

And when she realized where she was, Rio had already stopped.

She was standing in front of a door.

Agatha’s door.

She remained still for a moment, looking at the dark wood as if only then realizing how she had gotten there.

Pathetic. It was the only word that came to mind.

Even so, almost without noticing, Rio placed her hand against the surface of the door.

There was something deeply absurd about that gesture, something her own reason immediately recognized as inappropriate. And yet she stayed there for a second.

A strange and silent part of her almost wanted to smell Agatha again up close — that soft perfume mixed with the natural warmth of her skin that still seemed etched into her memory since Ibiza.

That was when she heard something.

A sound coming from inside the room.

Quick footsteps.

Then the bathroom door of the suite opening abruptly.

And closing again.

Rio’s heart beat faster. She frowned slightly, her hand still resting on the door while her mind tried to organize that small fragment of information.

Something was wrong. For a full second she considered the possibility of entering and asking if everything was alright.

But immediately the reason returned with force. Rio removed her hand from the door, almost irritated with herself, and took a few steps back before forcing herself to continue walking down the corridor.

It was absurd. She had absolutely no reason to be standing in front of her secretary’s bedroom door at six in the morning.




Chapter 5: Blood Issus

Chapter Summary

Hi everyone, I'm reposting this chapter because the previous one somehow got mixed up with my Portuguese texts.

For those who don't know, I'm Brazilian and I also publish in Portuguese on Wattpad, and I ended up uploading it here in Portuguese. I apologize and wish you a good read.

 

 

┕──━──━─┑◆┍─━──━──┙

Agatha woke up with her stomach churning. The room was still quiet, illuminated only by the soft morning light filtering through the pale curtains and spreading across the carpet. For a few seconds, she remained lying down, trying to ignore the familiar sensation that slowly rose from her stomach to her throat.
It didn’t work.

She sat up quickly in bed, bringing a hand to her abdomen and taking a deep breath as she hurried to the bathroom. The door was opened in a rush and closed right after.

A few minutes later, the worst of the wave of nausea finally passed.

Agatha leaned against the sink for a moment, her hair partially falling over her face as she breathed deeply to steady herself. The cold water she splashed on her face helped clear her mind, and little by little the unpleasant feeling began to fade.

"Great start to the day…" she muttered to herself.

She turned on the shower right after.

Hot water began to fall, filling the bathroom with steam as Agatha stepped under the stream, letting the temperature slowly warm her skin and ease the tension that still lingered in her muscles. She stayed there for a few minutes in silence, letting the water run over her shoulders and down her back, as if it could also wash away the accumulated exhaustion from the trip, the previous night, and the constant strangeness of that house.

When she finally finished, she turned off the shower and stepped out, quickly wrapping herself in a towel.

Back in the bedroom, she began getting dressed for the day.

She chose one of the outfits she had brought in her suitcase—something discreet, professional, appropriate for the role of secretary she needed to maintain there. As she pulled the piece over her body, however, she frowned slightly.

It was tighter.

Not absurdly so, but enough for her to notice. Agatha let out a small sigh as she adjusted the fabric.

"Okay… I definitely need to buy new clothes."

That was when a noise from outside caught her attention. She turned her head toward the window.

Curiosity got the better of her. Agatha walked over and slightly pulled the curtain aside.

Outside, the mansion’s enormous garden was in motion. Staff members were setting up tables, tent structures, and elegant floral arrangements. Waiters crossed the lawn carrying trays, boxes, and perfectly folded white tablecloths.

A party, ou something very close to it. Agatha’s eyes then found Rio.

She was a few meters ahead, at the center of the movement, dressed in light-colored clothes and talking to two women who looked very much like her.

The same elegant bearing, the same face shape.

Certainly her sisters.

Rio spoke to them with a naturalness Agatha had never seen before. There was no perfectly calculated social smile like the one she wore in formal settings, nor the controlled posture she maintained at the company or at public events.

Here she seemed… relaxed.

At one point, one of the women said something that made Rio genuinely laugh—a brief laugh, but completely spontaneous.

Agatha watched the scene for a few seconds. And realized she was smiling too.

There was something genuinely comforting about seeing Rio like that. She let the curtain fall back into place and returned to the mirror.

She finished adjusting her outfit, applied light makeup—just enough to look put together—and carefully tied her hair. The result was simple but elegant, exactly what she needed for that kind of environment.

When she was done, she picked up her phone, quickly checked the time, and walked to the door.

She opened it. And stepped into the hallway. She had barely taken two steps when a firm voice interrupted her.

"Hey, young lady."

Agatha stopped immediately. She turned around, and in the middle of the hallway stood Rita Vidal.

Rio’s grandmother.

The older woman walked slowly toward her, her completely white hair perfectly arranged, her posture rigid, and her sharp gaze seeming to examine everything around her as if she were constantly evaluating people and situations.

Agatha straightened her posture automatically.

"Mrs. Vidal. Good morning."

Rita stopped in front of her, analyzing her for a full moment before speaking.

"So you’re the one working as my granddaughter’s secretary."

Agatha nodded.

"Exactly."

Rita tilted her head slightly, as if confirming something in her own mind.

"Right. Then make sure absolutely nothing interrupts the event she will be attending today."

Her voice was calm. But there was something hard in it.

"Do your job properly."

Agatha kept her tone neutral.

"Yes, ma’am." Rita took another step forward, her eyes fixed on her.

"Because if you fail… I can pull some strings to have you fired."

Agatha kept her face impassive.

"Understood."

The woman seemed satisfied with the answer.

"This is an important event. Wealthy families, relevant contacts. Rio needs to establish connections." Rita slightly adjusted one of the gloves she was wearing. "Your duty is to ensure she doesn’t get tripped up by small things."

Agatha nodded again.

"Of course."

Rita watched her for another second. Then she simply turned her back and began walking down the hallway, disappearing toward the main staircase.

Agatha remained still. She waited a few seconds.

Long enough to be sure the old woman was no longer around. Only then did she raise her hand and show her middle finger to the empty hallway.

"Old bitch…" she muttered quietly.

The party began to fully take shape throughout the morning.

When Agatha finally went down to the garden, everything was practically ready. The wide lawn in front of the mansion had been transformed into an impeccably organized setting, the kind that seemed to exist more to be admired than actually used. Round tables covered with white linen tablecloths were spread across the space, each decorated with small arrangements of pale flowers in crystal vases. Fine glasses were already aligned beside delicate plates, reflecting the soft sunlight rising slowly in the sky.

A large light-fabric tent had been set up further back, supported by discreet light wooden structures. The white fabric moved gently with the breeze coming from the sea, creating soft shadows over the temporary wooden flooring installed there. Waiters moved between the tables with silver trays, adjusting glasses, cutlery, and small platters of appetizers prepared in the house kitchen.

It was the kind of event that screamed old money.

Nothing too flashy.
Nothing excessive.

But absolutely everything—every detail, every object, every movement—seemed chosen with almost obsessive precision.

The guests began arriving shortly after.

Elegant cars stopped at the entrance of the property while men in light suits and women in discreet dresses walked through the garden greeting each other with polite smiles and brief embraces. There was an air of familiarity among many of them, as if the gathering was less a social event and more a ritual repeated for decades among the same families.

Agatha remained most of the time near the area where the staff circulated.

It wasn’t exactly forbidden for her to be in the middle of the party, but there was also no reason for her to mingle too much. So she moved between the two spaces—sometimes helping organize something small, sometimes simply observing the flow of people discreetly while checking the tablet she carried with her.

From time to time, she walked through the garden.

She passed among the guests naturally enough not to draw attention, but always maintaining the professional posture of someone who was there working. And whenever her eyes met Rio’s, there was a brief silent understanding.

A quick glance.
A small, almost imperceptible nod. Everything is under control. Rio responded in the same way.

A minimal gesture, a look that said she understood. And then she immediately returned to the role she was playing. Because that was exactly what it seemed.

A role.

Rio spoke with the guests with almost perfect naturalness. The smile appeared at the right moment, the tilt of her head came at the appropriate time, and every response seemed carefully measured to please without revealing too much.

She seemed to know exactly when to laugh.
When to compliment.
When to ask a question that would keep the conversation flowing.

Agatha watched that from afar a few times. And noticed something that made her frown slightly. Rio wasn’t just participating in the party.

She was performing.

It was like watching someone who had rehearsed that role her entire life—every refined gesture, every carefully chosen word, every perfectly controlled expression.

A socially impeccable version of herself.

But also… completely constructed.

For a moment, as she watched Rio talking to an older couple near the tables, Agatha felt something unexpected.

Almost pity.

Because for the first time it became very clear to her that the woman everyone there admired seemed to exist inside an invisible script.

And that perhaps Rio had spent her entire life learning exactly how to perform it.

The party continued through the afternoon with the same controlled elegance that seemed to sustain the entire property. Discreet laughter rose among small groups of guests scattered across the garden, glasses clinked softly against each other, and the sea breeze moved the linen tablecloths and the tent fabrics.

Agatha kept moving between the staff and the guest area, always holding the tablet, checking schedules, confirming details with the house employees when necessary. From time to time, her eyes inevitably returned to Rio.

And Rio remained impeccable.

Conversing naturally, Laughing at the right moments, greeting people as if she had known each of them for years.

But as time passed, a small point of tension began to form in the farthest corner of the garden.

One of the guests—a middle-aged man with a face already reddened by alcohol—was speaking louder and louder. At first, it was just exaggerated laughter and dragged-out comments, but soon the tone began to bother those around him. Some women exchanged discreet glances. A couple tried to politely step away from the conversation.

The man didn’t seem to notice.

Or simply didn’t care.

He gestured too much, waving his wine glass while speaking loudly about some topic no one else seemed interested in hearing. With each passing minute his voice rose a little more, his balance seemed to worsen, and the discomfort around him became almost palpable.

Agatha noticed when Rio’s grandmother’s eyes turned in that direction.

Rita Vidal stood near one of the central tables, watching the scene with her usual cold expression. Her lips pressed into a thin line before she slowly turned her head toward Rio, who at that moment was speaking with two older women.

"Rio."

The voice wasn’t loud, but it cut through the space with enough authority for Rio to turn immediately.

"Yes, abuela."

Rita made a brief gesture with her head toward the drunk man.

"Handle it. He’s already embarrassing us enough."

The tone was dry.

More an order than a request.

Agatha, who stood a few meters behind, felt a slight tightening in her stomach as she heard that. There was something in the way the woman spoke to Rio—that almost humiliating coldness—that deeply bothered her.

But she remained quiet. It wasn’t her place to say anything. Rio, on the other hand, simply nodded.

"Of course."

She calmly walked toward the group where the man was still speaking loudly. Agatha watched from afar as Rio approached him with that same social smile that never seemed to fully leave her face.

"Mr. Davenport," she said calmly. "Perhaps we could talk for a moment."

The man turned to her, clearly already quite intoxicated.

"Ah! The Vidal heiress."

He laughed too loudly.

"I was wondering when you’d show up."

Rio maintained her calm posture.

"Perhaps we could go to the veranda. Fresh air usually helps."

The man tilted his head, looking at her with an expression that was beginning to shift between amusement and irritation.

"You think I’m drunk?"

Some nearby people began to move away discreetly.

Rio took a breath.

"I think it might be better—"

"Don’t tell me what’s best for me."

The sentence came out louder.

More aggressive. Agatha noticed the exact moment the conversation stopped being a polite attempt at containment.

Rio still tried to keep her tone calm.

"Mr. Davenport—"

But before she could finish, the man made a sudden gesture with the hand holding the glass. And the red wine flew directly onto Rio’s white blouse. The dark liquid spread across the fabric almost instantly. For a full second, the garden fell silent. A heavy silence.

Embarrassed. Rio looked down at her blouse. Then back at the man. The social smile had completely disappeared.

"Security."

The word came out firm.

Two of the security guards who had been discreetly positioned near the garden approached immediately. Without making a scene, they grabbed the man by the arms while he still tried to protest, his voice slurred and irritated.

"This is ridiculous! I—"

But they were already escorting him out of the garden. The conversation around resumed slowly, though still with that uncomfortable atmosphere that always lingers after a public scene.

Rio remained standing for a moment.

The wine slowly dripping down the front of her blouse. Then she turned and walked toward the house. Agatha followed her with her eyes. But before she could move, Rita’s cold voice appeared behind her.

"Miss Harkness." Agatha turned immediately.

"Yes, ma’am."

Rita looked her up and down with that impassive expression.

"Do your job." She discreetly pointed toward the house.

"Go get a new blouse for Rio."

Agatha pressed her lips slightly.

"Of course." She gave a small nod and began walking toward the mansion. As she crossed the garden, she let out a quiet sigh.

Agatha quickly crossed the garden toward the mansion, still hearing in the distance the murmur of the party trying to reorganize itself after the incident. As soon as she entered the house, the silence inside enveloped her immediately, muffling the soft music and conversations that continued outside.

She climbed the stairs quickly.

The upstairs hallway was almost empty, illuminated by natural light coming through the tall windows facing the sea. The thick carpet completely muffled the sound of her footsteps as she walked to Rio’s bedroom door.

Agatha knocked once.

No response.

She frowned slightly and opened the door just enough to peek inside.

The room was empty.

The bed impeccably made, the curtains partially open letting in a strip of soft light, and a few pieces of clothing folded over an armchair. Everything seemed perfectly organized—almost cold.

Agatha walked to the closet without wasting time.

She opened the doors and quickly scanned the neatly arranged clothes inside until she grabbed a new blouse—also white, though made of slightly heavier fabric. She closed the closet and was already leaving when she heard footsteps in the hallway.

One of the house staff was passing by with a tray.

"Excuse me," Agatha said quickly. "Have you seen Mrs. Vidal?"

The woman nodded.

"She went to the guest bathroom, miss."

"Thank you."

Agatha went down the stairs almost at the same hurried pace she had gone up. The guest bathroom was located in a side hallway on the ground floor, near the smaller living room that opened to the garden.

When she reached the door, she raised her hand to knock.

But stopped.

The door was ajar. Agatha hesitated for a second, then gently pushed it open with her fingers.

"Mrs. Vid—" The sentence died halfway through.

Rio was standing with her back to the door.

Without a blouse. For a second, Agatha simply froze. Rio’s back was covered in scars. Not small marks, nor discreet ones.

They were deep, old scars, spread across her entire back. Some long and irregular, others crossing the skin in thick lines distorted by time. The skin looked as if it had been torn and rebuilt many times—wide, pale marks, stretched by the years, some crooked as if they had healed under tension.

They looked like blows. Many. Repeated blows over the years. Agatha felt the air leave her chest before she even realized it.

A small sound of shock escaped her throat.

Rio turned immediately, dark eyes meeting hers. For a second, both of them stood still. Agatha blinked, completely stunned.

"I… I’m so sorry."

She looked away almost instantly, her face burning with embarrassment. She took two steps inside, just enough to place the folded blouse on the counter near the sink.

"I just… broug… the blouse."

Her voice came out low, completely disorganized. She was already stepping back.

"Sorry."

Agatha turned before Rio could say anything and quickly left the bathroom, pulling the door closed behind her while her heart still pounded in her chest.

For a few seconds, she just kept walking down the hallway. The image of the scars still etched in her mind.

Agatha returned to the garden, trying to compose her expression before even stepping again through the glass door that led to the party. The sea air seemed stronger now, carrying the scent of salt and garden flowers, while the event continued as if nothing had really happened.

The soft music still played under the tent.

The guests continued talking in small groups, waiters circulated with trays, and the sound of glasses occasionally meeting punctuated the atmosphere with a delicate clink.

Agatha resumed her position almost automatically. Tablet in hand, eyes attentive.

But her mind was elsewhere. The image of Rio’s back wouldn’t leave her thoughts, those scars.

Deep. Old. Violent.

They weren’t the marks of an accident. Nor something isolated. It looked… repeated. Systematic. As if someone had hurt that woman many times over a long period.

Agatha swallowed hard while pretending to check a schedule on the tablet. When Rio returned to the garden some time later, wearing a clean blouse, Agatha noticed immediately.

And did exactly what her instinct told her to.

She avoided her.

Whenever Rio approached a group near where she was, Agatha found some reason to move to another part of the party. Check something with the staff. Adjust a list. Walk to the kitchen.

Anything. Not because she was afraid of Rio. But because she didn’t know what to say, or how to act after seeing something so intimate… so brutal.

And so the afternoon passed.

Until the sun began to set on the horizon and the guests started saying their goodbyes one by one. Cars began arriving again at the entrance of the property, and the staff discreetly started collecting tables, glasses, and arrangements while the last groups dispersed through the garden.

Agatha helped check a few final details with the staff before finally deciding to go upstairs to her room.

The exhaustion weighed on every muscle in her body.

She was walking down the upstairs hallway when she heard footsteps behind her.

Before she could fully turn, a hand grabbed her wrist.

Agatha turned immediately.

Rio.

Agatha’s heart gave a small leap.

"I—"

The words came out too quickly.

"I’m so sorry."

Rio blinked once, but Agatha was already continuing.

"I shouldn’t have gone in without knocking. The door was open and I thought you— I swear I didn’t see anything, I just—"

The sentence stumbled over itself.

Rio gently tightened her grip on her wrist, not forcefully, but enough to interrupt that nervous stream of words.

"It’s alright."

Rio’s voice came out calm.

"Agatha, breathe."

Agatha stopped for a second, pulling in air.

Rio continued:

"You didn’t do anything wrong."

She let out a small sigh before adding:

"I just need you not to say anything." Agatha nodded immediately.

"I swear."

The answer came quickly. Sincerely.

"I won’t tell anyone."

She hesitated for a second, looking at Rio with something softer in her expression.

"I’m sorry."

This time it wasn’t about the door. Rio understood. Her eyes shifted away for a moment, as if that part of the conversation was harder to hold.

"It was a long time ago," Rio said, with a small, almost automatic shrug. "It’s fine." Agatha slowly shook her head.

"Pain like that doesn’t go away easily."

For a moment, the hallway fell silent.

It was only then that Rio seemed to realize something—that in their closeness, they were standing very near.

Close enough to feel each other’s warmth. Agatha’s wrist still held between Rio’s fingers.

Rio slowly let go of her hand.

"Sorry." Agatha immediately shook her head.

"It’s okay."

Rio seemed tired now.

Much more than she had appeared during the entire party. Agatha took a small step back, creating space between them.

"Good night, Mrs. Vidal." Rio simply nodded.

"Good night, Agatha." Agatha turned before the silence between them could grow again. She walked the few remaining steps to her room, opened the door, and quickly went inside.

As soon as the bedroom door closed behind her, Agatha leaned her back against the wood for a second.

The silence of the room felt heavier after an entire day surrounded by people, polite conversations, and hidden tensions. She let out a long sigh, bringing a hand to her face and rubbing her eyes with her fingers.

Her whole body felt tired.

Not just physically.

Mentally too.

The image of Rio’s scars still flashed in her mind without warning, like something she couldn’t avoid.

Agatha took a deep breath.

"Forget it…" she murmured to herself, almost in a whisper.

She walked to the bed while undoing the zipper of the dress she had worn to the party. The fabric slid slowly down her body as she pulled it off her shoulders, falling into a small heap on the floor beside the bed.

Her skin still felt warm from the long day.

She let her hair down, running her fingers through the strands to undo the hairstyle she had done that morning. Some pins fell onto the nightstand as she removed them one by one.

For a moment, Agatha stood in the middle of the room in just her underwear, breathing slowly.

Then she grabbed a towel and walked to the bathroom.

The bright bathroom light illuminated the space of pale marble and polished metal. Agatha turned the shower knob and waited a few seconds for the water to heat up, resting her hands on the edge of the sink as she looked at her reflection in the mirror.

She looked tired.

Light dark circles.
Her hair already a bit messy.

But what really weighed on her was her expression. Agatha pushed the thought away again.

When she stepped into the shower, the hot water fell over her shoulders like immediate relief. She closed her eyes for a moment, letting the warmth run down her neck and back, taking some of the tension of the day with it.

Steam slowly began to fill the bathroom.

Agatha washed her face, running her hands over her skin as if trying to erase the entire day. Water ran down her body as she took a few deep breaths, trying to reorganize her thoughts.

The scars. She squeezed her eyes shut again.

"It’s none of your business," she murmured quietly to herself.

After a few minutes, she turned off the shower and stepped out, wrapping the towel around her body while steam still lingered in the air.

Agatha quickly dried her hair with another towel and put on her sleepwear—a loose soft cotton shirt and a comfortable pair of shorts. The fabric felt light against her clean, warmed skin.

She brushed her teeth slowly, almost on autopilot, before turning off the bathroom light and returning to the bedroom.

The room was now bathed in the soft light of the bedside lamp.

Agatha walked over and finally let herself fall onto the soft mattress. Her body sank slightly into the surface as she pulled the light blanket over herself.

Every muscle seemed to finally relax.

She let out a long sigh, sinking deeper into the bed. The room was quiet. Outside, only the distant sound of the sea and the wind passing through the trees of the property. And finally, she closed her eyes, trying to let exhaustion overcome the thoughts that still insisted on returning.

◆

The balcony light felt golden and warm, like the end of an afternoon that refused to end. The breeze gently moved the pale curtains tied to the wooden pillars, and the sound of the sea could be heard just below, crashing against the rocks in a steady, distant rhythm. Agatha was sitting at the table.

Across from her, Rio.

Sitting in the opposite chair, wearing light clothes, her dark hair loose over her shoulders. The soft afternoon light traced the outline of her face in a way that felt almost too intimate, as if that scene had been created just for the two of them.

Between them was a small white iron table. On it, two bowls of ice cream slowly melting under the afternoon heat.

Rio held a spoon.

Agatha wasn’t paying attention to anything else.

Her eyes were fixed on Rio’s lips, following every movement as she brought the spoon to her mouth. Her tongue touched the metal edge before slowly pulling it in, savoring the ice cream with a calmness that felt too deliberate.

Agatha swallowed hard. Rio seemed to notice.

Her dark eyes lifted to meet hers, and a small smile appeared at the corner of her lips, slow and almost amused. Without saying a word, she dipped the spoon into the bowl again, scooping more ice cream before leaning slightly forward.

The spoon was brought to Agatha’s mouth. Agatha parted her lips almost without realizing.

The cold, sweet taste touched her tongue as she accepted the ice cream, and for a moment Rio remained there, too close, watching. The silence between them felt dense, charged with something neither of them named.

Then Rio’s thumb appeared. Her finger gently touched the corner of Agatha’s mouth, wiping away a small trace of ice cream left there. The gesture was slow, her thumb sliding over her lower lip with almost studied calm.

Agatha let out a soft breath before she even realized it.

And then she kissed her.

The kiss came deep and intense, as if something kept for too long had finally decided to exist without asking permission. Rio’s hand slid along Agatha’s waist as she pulled her closer, easily guiding her onto her lap.

Agatha felt the warmth of her body, the firmness of her hands holding her waist, their lips still pressed together as the kiss deepened.

And then—

Agatha woke up suddenly.

Her eyes opened in the dark room, her heart pounding in her chest. Her breathing came fast, uneven, as if she had run a long distance. For a full second she just lay there, staring at the ceiling as air rushed in and out of her lungs in short bursts. Slowly, the room began to make sense again around her: the bed, the silence of the early morning, the distant sound of wind moving through the trees of the property. The dream.

Agatha ran a hand over her face in exasperation.

"Get that shit out of your head…" she muttered to herself, still breathless.

Agatha sat on the bed for a few seconds after waking, trying to steady her breathing while her heart still beat too fast. The room remained submerged in the dimness of early morning, and the silence of the house felt even heavier after the intensity of the dream. She ran her hands over her face, irritated with herself, and finally pushed the blanket aside before standing up. The wooden floor was cold under her feet as she walked to the bathroom, determined to do the only thing that seemed capable of erasing that feeling from her skin: a shower.

She turned on the shower and waited for the water to heat up, resting her hands on the sink while looking at her own reflection in the mirror. Her face still carried traces of sleep and frustration, her hair messy over her shoulders. When steam began to rise, Agatha stepped into the shower and let the hot water fall over her head and slowly run down her body. The warmth helped relax her tense muscles and dissolve the lingering restlessness from the dream. She closed her eyes for a few moments, running soap over her arms, shoulders, and back, taking deep breaths as the steam filled the space around her.

She stayed there a bit longer than usual, as if wanting to ensure that any trace of that dream disappeared along with the water draining away. When she finally turned off the shower, she wrapped herself in a soft towel and returned to the bedroom. She sat in front of the vanity mirror to apply moisturizer to her still-warm skin, spreading the cream slowly over her arms, neck, and legs. At one point, her eyes lingered on the reflection of her own body.

Agatha turned slightly to the side.

Her hand slid to her abdomen.

Her belly was just beginning to change, but the difference was already noticeable to her. It wasn’t something anyone else would easily notice, but Agatha knew her own body too well to ignore it. She let out a tired sigh and kept her hand there for a moment.

"Just until the fifth month… I promise," she murmured softly to herself.

Then she finished getting ready. She put on comfortable clothes for the day, tied her hair simply, and left the room, intending to go downstairs to the kitchen to get something to eat before the house filled with movement again.

But when she reached the main hall, she stopped.

At the mansion entrance, Rio was saying goodbye to Jessica. The other woman was ready to leave, an elegant bag on her arm while the driver waited beside the car parked in front of the house. The two spoke in low voices for a moment before Jessica got into the vehicle. The driver closed the door, and the car began to pull away down the long driveway.

Rio turned to go back inside the house.

That was when her eyes met Agatha’s.

"Is everything okay?" Agatha asked automatically.

Rio nodded, though she seemed a bit tired.

"Yes. We had some issues with wedding suppliers. Jessica went to take care of that."

Agatha let out a small sigh and nodded in understanding.

"I see."

Rio rested one hand on the back of a nearby chair and continued:

"We’re leaving this afternoon."

"Alright," Agatha replied.

Rio then added, in a slightly softer tone:

"Sorry for dragging you into all this." Agatha shrugged.

"It’s fine. It’s part of the job."

Rio nodded slowly.

"Right… work."

For a moment, the two stood in silence, but something still seemed to bother Agatha. She hesitated before speaking again.

"I apologize if I’m being intrusive…"

Rio raised her eyebrows slightly.

"But why does your grandmother treat you so rudely?"

Rio shrugged, as if the question were trivial.

"She’s always been like that." Agatha kept looking at her.

"Honestly, I don’t care," Rio added.

The image of the scars immediately returned to Agatha’s mind.

But she didn’t mention it, instead, she simply sighed.

"Even so… I’m sorry. No one should go through that." Rio seemed to consider the statement for a moment before replying:

"Thank you."

Then she added, with almost casual calm:

"But in time, I’ll get my payback."

Agatha frowned.

"What do you mean?"

Rio glanced around the hall for a moment, checking if anyone was nearby. Then she stepped a little closer to Agatha, closing the distance between them.

"I’m going to end the lineage she admires so much."

Agatha blinked, still trying to understand.

"How?"

"I’m not going to have children," Rio replied simply.

She shrugged, as if it were obvious.

"No children, her lineage dies." Then she lifted her chin slightly.

"That’s my answer."

Agatha stayed silent for a moment. Cruelly brilliant, she thought.

"Well… I hope you succeed." Rio gave a small smile.

"I will. Don’t worry."

She then made a light gesture with her head.

"Excuse me."

Agatha nodded. Rio passed by her and walked down the hallway, disappearing toward the office. Agatha remained still for a moment. Then she turned to head toward the kitchen. As she walked, her hand instinctively moved to her own abdomen.

◆

The weekend passed faster than Rio expected.

On Sunday afternoon, she dropped Agatha off at her home in New York. The drive back was quiet for most of the time, the kind of silence that forms when two people are too tired to talk, but not enough for the silence to become uncomfortable.

Traffic began to thicken as they approached Manhattan, and the familiar skyline of buildings appeared on the horizon as an inevitable reminder that routine always finds a way back.

When the car stopped in front of Agatha’s building, Rio kept her hands on the wheel as she watched her pick up her bag and the small suitcase she had taken for the trip.

"Thanks for the ride," Agatha said.

Rio simply nodded.

"Get some rest today. Tomorrow we have a lot of work."

Agatha gave a small smile, that short smile that always seemed to carry a hint of irony.

"As always."

She got out of the car and walked along the sidewalk, pulling the suitcase behind her. Before entering the building, she turned just enough to give a quick wave. Rio followed the movement in silence, watching as the glass door closed and Agatha disappeared inside of the building. For a few seconds, Rio remained there, her hands still on the steering wheel, the engine still running. There was something strange about that feeling. Because Agatha seemed… completely normal.

No hesitation. No tension. No different look.

As if absolutely nothing had happened in Ibiza. No mention, no embarrassment.

No memory crossing her eyes.

It was as if that night simply didn’t exist. Rio didn’t know exactly what to feel about it. Part of her felt a certain relief. Another part… didn’t.

In the following days, the routine resumed its usual rhythm at the hospital. Meetings, surgeries, reports, social commitments. The kind of schedule that kept anyone busy enough not to think about anything beyond the next appointment.

Agatha continued playing the role of secretary with the same impeccable efficiency as always, organizing Rio’s schedule with precision, anticipating problems before they even arose. And she continued acting exactly the same way.

Polite, professional, and slightly sarcastic at times.

But absolutely normal. No sign of memory. Rio began to notice something unsettling inside herself. She caught herself watching Agatha more than before.

Small things that would have gone unnoticed before began to draw her attention: the way Agatha tied her hair when she was focused on a document, the way she slightly frowned when reading something complicated, or the fast rhythm with which she typed on her phone while walking down the hallway.

Details.

Small.

Almost insignificant.

But that now seemed to occupy too much space in her mind. That same night, late in her apartment, Rio opened her phone almost without thinking.

And ended up on Agatha’s Instagram. The photos were there, arranged in a casual sequence of moments from her life. Agatha laughing at a bar with friends, on some old trip, her hair messy from the wind.

Agatha sitting in a café, holding a cup while smiling at the camera. Rio slowly slid her finger across the screen, the images like brief flashes of memory.

The smell of the sea in Ibiza, the warmth of Agatha’s skin under her fingers.

Rio stopped her finger on the screen. One of the photos showed Agatha smiling directly at the camera. Her blue eyes carried that restless energy that seemed to exist inside her all the time, something too alive to fit into a simple photograph.

Rio closed her eyes for a second.

When she opened them again, Agatha’s photo was still on the phone screen. The carefree smile, the coffee cup in her hands, her dark eyes looking directly at the camera as if nothing in the world could disturb her.

Rio took a deep breath.

Then she left the social media app and opened the messaging app. Her fingers hovered over the screen for a moment before typing just one short sentence.

"I need you this week."

She sent it.

And added right below:

"I want you to go hard on me."

◆

"I need you to go hard on me? What kind of message is that, Rio?"

Natasha walked into the apartment without ceremony, wearing black leggings, a sports top, and a light jacket thrown over her shoulders. Her blonde hair was tied in a high ponytail, and she already carried that annoyingly energetic vibe of someone who lives in gyms.

Rio was in the kitchen, finishing a coffee.

"I want you to double my workout," she replied calmly. "I need to get my head straight."

Natasha crossed her arms and tilted her head, analyzing her friend with a crooked smile.

"You know doing countless strength workouts won’t solve all your problems, right? Ever thought about a psychiatrist?"

Rio took a sip of coffee.

"I have. But I don’t want to deal with that right now."

Natasha let out a small mocking laugh.

"Rio, I’ve known you since college. And even back then I already knew you needed therapy."

Rio raised an eyebrow.

"I pay you to be my personal trainer."

"Not that much," Natasha replied immediately.

She was already walking toward the private gym area of Rio’s apartment, a spacious room with huge windows, modern equipment, and dark rubber flooring.

"So," Natasha said, dropping her bag on the floor, "what’s going on?"

Rio rested her hands on the weight bench.

"I need to get a woman out of my head." Natasha raised her eyebrows dramatically.

"For your sake, I hope it’s your fiancée."

Rio shot her a dry look.

"Since when are you a comedian?"

Natasha shrugged.

"Sometimes."

Rio grabbed a towel.

"Anyway. Let’s go."

Natasha was already adjusting weights on the bar.

"Today you’re going to hate me."

Rio simply replied:

"Great."

The workout started heavy.

Natasha really doubled the intensity of the sets. Deadlifts, squats, bench press, intervals too short to fully catch her breath. The kind of workout that made the body burn and the mind too busy to think about anything else.

Rio lifted the weights with controlled movements, her breathing growing heavier as the sets progressed.

Between exercises, Natasha still found room to talk.

"So," she said while adjusting the timer, "how are those yoga classes for pregnant women going?"

Natasha smiled proudly.

"Since you don’t pay me well, I’m making a lot of money from that."

Rio let out a small scoff while adding more weight to the bar.

"Impressive."

Natasha watched her as she started another set.

"And you?" she asked. "Have you given up that stupid idea of dragging your lineage through the mud?"

Rio finished the movement before answering.

"No." She sat on the bench and grabbed the towel.

"It’s the right thing to do."

Natasha watched her for a few seconds.

"You’re going to regret that."

Rio shook her head.

"I can’t go back."

Natasha sighed. Then decided to change the subject.

"I met an interesting girl the other day."

Rio raised an eyebrow.

"That’s rare?"

"Very funny." Natasha leaned her hip against one of the machines.

"She’s twenty-five. Omega. Pregnant."

Rio grabbed the water bottle and took a sip.

"Where did you meet her?"

"I went to a check-up with my sister and sister-in-law. The girl was there."

Natasha shrugged.

"Funny, actually. Very quick-witted."

Rio rested her elbows on her knees, still breathing heavily from the exercise.

"They don’t make alphas like they used to."

Natasha let out a short laugh.

"That was oddly specific." Rio just grabbed the bar again.

Rio rested her hands on her knees as she caught her breath after the last set. Her chest rose and fell heavily, sweat already running down her temple and along her neck. Natasha was adjusting weights again when she noticed Rio still seemed stuck on the previous conversation.

"So she was alone?" Rio asked, looking up.

Natasha shrugged.

"She was." Rio immediately frowned.

"Alone?"

"Yes."

Rio grabbed the towel and wiped her face, clearly bothered.

"That’s absurd."

Natasha raised an eyebrow.

"What?"

Rio stood up and started walking slowly around the gym space, still trying to control her breathing after the intense workout.

"An omega… alone at a medical appointment."

She shook her head in irritation.

"That’s exactly why I don’t want children."

Natasha crossed her arms, watching her.

"Because someone was irresponsible?"

Rio stopped.

"Because this kind of thing happens all the time."

Her voice was harder now.

"People get abandoned. Relationships end. Promises disappear."

She made a vague gesture with her hand, as if it were too obvious to need explanation.

"And who is left carrying everything?"

Rio rested her hand on the back of a machine.

"The pregnant one." Her gaze hardened slightly.

"No one deserves to go through that alone." For a moment, Natasha stayed silent. Then she uncrossed her arms and let out a small sigh.

"She didn’t exactly look fragile."

Rio looked at her.

"What do you mean?"

Natasha shrugged.

"She looked… strong."

A small smile appeared at the corner of Natasha’s mouth as she remembered the scene.

"Sarcastic too. The kind who answers fast."

Rio picked up the water bottle again, taking a sip before asking:

"And did you talk to her?"

Natasha nodded.

"A little."

She leaned her hip against the weight rack.

"She was waiting for her appointment too. We ended up talking."

Rio tilted her head, curious despite herself.

"And?" Natasha smiled slightly.

"She seemed to handle herself just fine."

She paused briefly before adding:

"The kind who doesn’t take kindly to people pitying her." Rio stayed silent for a moment. Then grabbed the bar again.

"Next set."

◆

The weekend arrived bringing a rare moment of rest to Agatha’s routine. That morning, she and Wanda walked through the wide corridor of a shopping mall in Manhattan, both wearing gym clothes.

Agatha wore a simple black set—fitted leggings and a sports top covered by a light open jacket. Her hair was tied in a high ponytail, and she looked slightly suspicious of the whole situation. Wanda, on the other hand, walked with energy, wearing wine-colored leggings and a cropped hoodie, clearly excited about the plan.

Natasha’s studio was located inside the mall itself, on a floor dedicated to gyms and sports activities. As they walked toward it, Agatha let out a light sigh.

"I’m telling you right now," she said, adjusting the strap of her sports bag on her shoulder. "I’m only taking this trial class and never setting foot here again."

Wanda shot her an amused look.

"Why?"

Agatha subtly pointed at the luxury storefronts surrounding the corridor.

"Because this place costs a fortune, Wanda."

Wanda shrugged, completely unconcerned.

"After class we can walk around and look at some things for the baby."

Agatha immediately turned her head toward her.

"Okay…" she replied, raising a finger in warning. "But no crazy spending."

Wanda just smiled like someone who clearly wasn’t promising anything.

A few minutes later, they arrived at the studio. The space had large glass windows facing the inside of the mall, letting in plenty of light.

The environment was elegant, with light wooden floors, large plants in the corners, and a minimalist reception area right at the entrance.

Agatha approached the counter while Wanda looked around curiously.

"Hi," Agatha said to the receptionist. "I’m here for the trial class."

The woman looked up from the computer.

"Name?"

"Agatha Harkness."

She gestured slightly toward Wanda.

"And this is my guest, Wanda Maximoff."

The receptionist typed quickly on the computer.

Before she could respond, a familiar voice appeared behind them.

"Agatha."

Agatha turned immediately.

Natasha was walking toward them. She wore workout clothes—a dark sports top and black leggings—her red hair tied in a high bun. She carried that natural posture of someone who commanded the space effortlessly.

A small smile appeared as she approached.

"Good to see you here."

Agatha returned the smile politely.

"Good to see you too."

Natasha rested her hand on the reception counter.

"You can go in. I’ll finish organizing a few things here and join you in the room."

Agatha nodded.

"Thank you."

She and Wanda walked down the corridor leading to the main studio. As soon as they were a few steps away from the reception, Wanda slipped her arm around Agatha’s shoulders conspiratorially.

"I just thought of something," she murmured.

Agatha glanced sideways.

"That’s never a good sign."

Wanda smiled.

"I’m going to pay for your classes."

Agatha frowned.

"Why?"

Wanda tilted her head slightly back toward the reception.

"Because the view in class is very good."

Agatha took half a second to understand.

Then she glanced back over her shoulder toward Natasha. When she looked at Wanda again, she started laughing.

"You’re shameless."

Wanda shrugged with a completely guilty smile.

"Not denying it."

Agatha and Wanda walked through the studio’s inner corridor until they reached the main class room.

The space was large, quiet, and lit by an entire wall of windows that let the late morning light in softly.

The light wooden floor was covered with several yoga mats already arranged in rows, each with a small kit beside it: support blocks, an elastic band, and a firm cushion for breathing and posture exercises.

The room had a light lavender scent, probably coming from some diffuser hidden somewhere in the space.

A few women were already there, speaking softly or stretching their legs while waiting for the class to begin.

Some had small bellies, others more advanced. There were also two or three companions trying to look comfortable in clearly awkward positions.

Agatha paused for a second at the entrance and looked around.

"My God…" she murmured quietly to Wanda. "I feel like I’m walking into some kind of cult."

Wanda laughed softly.

"Relax."

She pulled Agatha by the hand to two empty mats near the back of the room.

They placed their water bottles on the floor and settled in. Agatha sat cross-legged on the mat, eyeing the accessory kit with suspicion.

"What is all this for?" she asked, picking up one of the blocks.

Wanda grabbed the elastic band and stretched it between her hands.

"I think this is like… preparation for childbirth."

Agatha froze.

"I am not psychologically prepared to hear the word childbirth out loud."

Wanda burst out laughing.

A few minutes later, Natasha entered the room.

The chatter immediately quieted down. She walked to the front of the room with that natural posture of someone who commanded the space effortlessly.

"Good morning, everyone."

The students responded in unison.

"Good morning."

Natasha placed her hands on her hips and looked over the group.

"For those who are here for the first time, this class is a mix of breathing, pelvic mobility, strengthening, and positions that help during labor."

Agatha leaned toward Wanda.

"Pelvic mobility sounds threatening."

Wanda was already trying not to laugh.

Natasha continued explaining while demonstrating some basic deep breathing movements. Everyone sat on the cushions, inhaling slowly through their noses and exhaling through their mouths.

Agatha tried to follow.

Inhaled.

Exhaled.

Inhaled again.

Then whispered:

"Am I breathing wrong?"

Wanda opened one eye.

"How does someone breathe wrong?"

"I don’t know, but it feels like everyone here is doing it better than me."

The first sequence of positions began with something relatively simple: tilting the pelvis forward and backward while sitting on the cushion.

"Smooth movement," Natasha explained. "Release your lower back."

Agatha tried to follow. The movement seemed simple. But after a few seconds she murmured:

"This feels like I’m dancing to electronic music very slowly."

Wanda started laughing.

"Stop."

"I’m trying."

The next position was on all fours on the mat, with hands and knees on the floor. Natasha demonstrated:
"Arch your back… now release… breathe."

Agatha followed the movement. Then she turned her head to Wanda.
"This is literally the cat position."

Wanda, also on all fours, replied:

"Meow."

The two of them started laughing again. Some students in the front were also laughing quietly.

Then came the positions with the pilates ball. Some women sat on the balls and began making small circular movements with their hips. Agatha climbed onto the ball very carefully.
"If I fall from here I’m never coming back."

Wanda was already swaying from side to side.

"Relax, it’s easy." Agatha tried to make the circle. The ball rolled a little.

She froze.

"Wanda."

"Yeah."

"I’m about to die in an extremely humiliating way."Wanda almost fell off her own ball from laughing so hard.

Natasha, who was walking between the mats correcting postures, passed by them at that moment. She quickly observed Agatha balancing on the ball as if she were on a wild animal

"Relax your hips," Natasha said casually.

Agatha looked at her.

"I’m relaxing everything I have."

Natasha just gave a small smile before continuing to walk around the room. After a few more positions — including a strange stretch where they had to lean forward hugging a block between their legs — Agatha finally dropped down onto the mat, completely defeated. She ran a hand over her face.

"Okay."

She took a deep breath.

"I admit it." Wanda turned her head toward her.

"What?" Agatha sighed.

"This is kind of fun."

Agatha remained seated on the mat for a few seconds after the last exercise, still trying to catch her breath. Her legs were stretched out in front, her arms braced behind her supporting her weight as she breathed deeply. Some students were still chatting quietly around them, others remained lying on their mats enjoying the final moment of relaxation Natasha had suggested.

The silence in the room was softer now, punctuated only by deep breaths and the distant sound of movement from the mall on the other side of the windows.

Agatha let out a long sigh. She turned her head toward Wanda, who was still sitting beside her, fixing her messy hair after so many strange exercises.

For a moment, Agatha simply watched her friend. There was something about that moment — that calm environment, the fact that Wanda was there with her — that made everything feel a little less frightening.

She swallowed before speaking.

"Thank you."

Wanda turned her face to her immediately.

"For what?"

Agatha looked up at the ceiling for a second, as if trying to steady her voice.

"For being here."

The sentence came out simple, but it carried more emotion than Agatha had probably intended to show. She tried to mask it with a small tired smile.

"I probably wouldn’t have come if I were alone."

Wanda watched her in silence for a moment, understanding more than what had been said out loud. Then she smiled softly.

Without saying anything, she simply lay down on her side on her mat, resting her head on her bent arm while looking at Agatha.

"I know."

Agatha let out a small, slightly awkward laugh through her nose.

After a few seconds, she also lay down, staring at the ceiling as her body finally relaxed against the mat. The comfortable silence settled between them again.

Wanda slowly stretched out her arm and gave Agatha a small shoulder nudge.

"Besides," she murmured with a lazy smile, "someone had to make sure you didn’t fall off the pilates ball and turn into a viral video." Agatha turned her head immediately.

"That was sabotage." Wanda laughed.

"Sure." Agatha let out another sigh, this time lighter.

After some time lying down and recovering, Agatha and Wanda left the classroom still laughing. Their bodies were slightly tired, but the feeling was strangely good after so many stretches and absurd positions neither of them had imagined they’d be doing that morning.

"I still can’t believe that pilates ball almost killed me," Agatha grumbled as they walked down the studio hallway.

Wanda ran a hand through her hair, still smiling.

"You looked like a cat trying to walk on ice."

"I was in a high-risk situation."

"You were being dramatic." Agatha opened her mouth to reply, but when she lifted her gaze toward the reception, the words simply died in her throat.

She stopped.

Literally froze mid-step. Leaning casually against the reception desk was Rio. Arms crossed, relaxed posture, talking to the receptionist as if she were just waiting for someone. The mall light streamed in through the glass walls behind her, partially illuminating her face.

Then Rio’s eyes lifted, and met Agatha’s.

The whole world seemed to freeze for a second.

Agatha felt her stomach drop.

“Fuck.”

Panic settled instantly in her mind.

“Fuck, fuck, fuck. She’s seeing me in a prenatal yoga class.”

The movement was automatic. Agatha quickly pulled the zipper of her workout jacket all the way up to her neck, as if that could hide something. She took a deep breath and kept walking, trying to look casual.

Wanda was still talking about something related to stretching when she noticed Agatha had completely changed posture.

"What is it—"

Then she saw Rio too, Wanda immediately put on a perfect social smile and kept walking beside Agatha as if nothing was happening.

Agatha approached the desk trying to look calm.

Rio slowly uncrossed her arms. Her gaze briefly moved over Agatha’s face, her zipped-up jacket, and then returned to her eyes.

"Miss Harkness," Rio said, in that same calm tone as always. "What are you doing here?"

Agatha opened her mouth.

But before any words came out, Natasha appeared behind the desk, looking between them curiously.

"Wait… you know each other?" Rio slightly turned her head.

"Yes." The tone was simple.

"Agatha is my secretary."

Agatha’s brain officially crashed.

But Wanda was faster. She literally took a small step forward, positioning herself between them with impressive naturalness.

"Agatha came to keep me company," she said with a calm smile. "I’m pregnant."

She made a small gesture toward herself.

"And Agatha is a good friend. She came with me."

Agatha’s eyes immediately flew to Natasha. For a full second, there was silence.

Then Natasha understood. Instantly.

"Oh!" she said with perfect naturalness, resting her arms on the desk. "And did you like the class?"

Wanda answered without hesitation.

"I liked it a lot."

"Is it your first time?"

"Yes."

"Then you did really well."

The two of them continued the conversation with almost professional ease, as if it were just a normal studio interaction.

Agatha remained silent beside them, internally praying that her face wasn’t giving absolutely anything away.

Rio watched everything. In silence. After a few seconds, she looked back at Agatha.
"Good to see you," she said calmly.

Agatha nodded quickly.

"Yeah… good to see you too."

Wanda, realizing the situation needed to end immediately, took a small step back.

"Well, we need to go."

She grabbed Agatha’s arm.

"It was great meeting the studio."

Natasha smiled.

 "Come back anytime."

"We will."

Wanda waved quickly.

"See you." The two of them turned and started walking out of the studio. As soon as they passed through the glass door, Agatha let out the breath she felt like she had been holding for five full minutes.

Behind them, Rio was still leaning against the desk.

She slowly turned her head toward Natasha.

Natasha crossed her arms.

"Wanda is a good student," she said simply.

Rio remained leaning against the desk for a few seconds after Agatha and Wanda disappeared down the mall corridor. Her gaze was still fixed on the empty space where they had been just moments ago. Natasha watched her expression with silent curiosity while organizing some papers at the reception.

Then Rio spoke, still looking ahead.

"Wanda is really the one who’s pregnant?"

Natasha raised an eyebrow.

"Of course."

She leaned her hip against the desk.

"As far as I know, you don’t exactly work with pregnant women." Rio blinked once, as if returning to reality.

 "Yeah…"

The answer came out more vague than she intended. But the truth was, the question hadn’t even been about the class.

It was about something else. Because, for a second — a stupid and completely irrational second — the idea of Agatha possibly being pregnant by someone else had crossed Rio’s mind like a blade.

And it had been disturbing in a way she couldn’t explain.

Why?

Rio didn’t know. It didn’t make sense. Agatha was her secretary, an adult woman, with her own life.

It was perfectly natural that she had relationships, that she went out with people, that she… had children, if she wanted to. Even so, the mere idea had left a bitter taste at the back of Rio’s throat.

She didn’t notice when she said goodbye to Natasha. Didn’t notice when she left the studio. Not even when she walked through the busy corridors of the mall.

When she realized it, she was already sitting in a bar a few floors above, with a glass in front of her. Alice was sitting across the table.

The bar had dim lighting, with amber lights reflecting off the bottles behind the counter. The music was soft, something instrumental blending with the constant murmur of conversations around.

Rio was staring fixedly at the liquid in her glass. Alice tilted her head.

"Rio."

No response. Alice snapped her fingers once in front of her.

"Earth to you." Rio blinked.

"Hm?"

Alice frowned.

"Rio, what the fuck?" Rio rested her elbow on the table and brought her hand to her face, rubbing her forehead quickly.

"Nothing." She picked up the glass and took a sip.

 "I’m just distracted."

Alice watched her for a few seconds, clearly unconvinced.

 "You look like someone who just realized they accidentally killed someone."

Rio let out a small breath through her nose.

 "Dramatic."

"I know you," Alice replied, crossing her arms. "You only get like this when something is bothering you."

Rio didn’t answer immediately.

She just rotated the glass between her fingers, watching the reflection of the light in the liquid. Then she said, almost casually:

"Do you think it’s strange for someone… to feel bothered by something that shouldn’t bother them?"

Alice let out a short laugh.

 "That describes half of the human experience."Rio finally lifted her eyes. Alice leaned forward slightly.

 "What happened?"

Rio opened her mouth to answer. But at the exact moment she thought about explaining, Agatha’s image surfaced again in her mind. The jacket zipping up quickly. The nervous look. The way she had avoided speaking much.

And before that…The absurd idea that had crossed her mind. Agatha pregnant, by someone else. Rio tightened her fingers around the glass. Alice noticed immediately

"Okay," she said. "Now I’m sure." Rio let out a tired sigh.

"I need a drink." Alice gave a crooked smile.

"Finally something that makes sense today."

Chapter 6: Ghosts of the Past

Chapter Notes

Enjoy your reading! Things will be revealed!

Sorry for the delay, I was having my Lollapalooza Chapelle Roan moment, forgive my absence!

 

 

 

┕──━──━─┑◆┍─━──━──┙

It hit Agatha when she finally realized she needed to buy new clothes. She had officially entered her tenth week of pregnancy, and her belly was more noticeable than she had imagined. It wasn’t anything huge yet, but it was no longer possible to pretend nothing was changing.

The pants that used to fit perfectly now tightened when she sat down, and some blouses were beginning to stretch subtly, revealing a volume that hadn’t existed a few weeks ago.

Agatha decided to attribute it to the gas that now seemed to follow her like some kind of permanent biological punishment. It was the explanation she repeated to herself whenever the mirror seemed to suggest something else.

It was Saturday morning, after a chaotic workweek. Endless meetings, piled-up reports, and worst of all, Rio’s strange looks over the past few days.

Looks that lingered too long, too silent, as if she were trying to figure out something Agatha preferred to keep buried.

Finally, she decided to take some time for herself.

She entered the mall adjusting her sunglasses on her face, walking through the automatic doors as the cold air conditioning wrapped around her body immediately. The contrast with the heat outside made her skin prickle slightly. The morning movement was still calm: a few families walking with shopping bags, couples standing in front of display windows, ambient music echoing through the wide, polished corridors.

Agatha walked for a few minutes until she reached the hallway where the clothing stores were. She stopped in front of a specific display window and slowly lifted her gaze.

The store’s name was written in elegant letters across the glass.

A boutique specialized in maternity clothing.

Agatha let out a small sigh and bit the inside of her cheek.

She remembered perfectly the moment, weeks ago, when she had told Wanda she wouldn’t need those things. That she could adapt her normal clothes for quite some time still.

Clearly, she was wrong.

Even so, she went in.

The interior of the store was very different from what Agatha had imagined. No overly childish colors or pieces that were too obvious. The space was elegant, with soft lighting and racks organized by style and occasion: work, casual, exercise, social events.

Soft fabrics, smart cuts, neutral tones. It was the kind of place that tried to convince pregnant women they could still look sophisticated.

Agatha walked between the racks with some caution, observing the pieces. Some pants had high elastic waistbands that covered the belly. Others looked identical to normal dress pants, with discreet adjustments hidden in the structure of the fabric.

She stopped in front of a rack with work pants. She picked one up, then another, and finally wrinkled her nose.

"My God…" she murmured under her breath.

A young sales assistant approached with a professional smile. She couldn’t have been more than twenty, hair tied back in a ponytail and a discreet name tag pinned to her blouse.

"Good morning, can I help?"

Agatha looked up.

"Good morning. Yes."

She held two pairs of pants in her hands as she spoke.

"I’d like to find some work pants that make the belly less noticeable… and that don’t scream maternity clothes."

The assistant nodded immediately, as if that were an extremely common request.

"Of course." She gestured for Agatha to follow her to another rack.

"Many clients ask for exactly that at the beginning of the pregnancy."

Agatha froze for half a second.

"Beginning of the pregnancy." The words echoed in her mind with a strange weight. The assistant pulled a pair of dark fabric pants from the rack.

"These usually work really well for work."

She showed the waistband.

"The adjustment is here on the side, so the front keeps a normal appearance. And the fabric has enough stretch to accommodate the growth of the belly without marking too much."

Agatha ran her fingers over the fabric.

It was soft and elegant.

"It looks… normal," she commented.

"That’s the idea," the assistant replied with a smile.

Agatha chose a few pieces to try on. Dark pants, a business skirt, and two blouses with looser cuts around the waist. She also grabbed, almost without thinking, a simple dress made of light fabric. The assistant pointed to the fitting rooms.

"You can try them on there. If you need another size, just call me."

Agatha nodded. She stepped into the fitting room and closed the curtain behind her.

The space was small, with a large mirror covering almost the entire wall. She placed the clothes on the small bench and took a deep breath before starting to remove her own pants. When she was left only in her underwear and bra, her gaze inevitably went to the mirror.

The belly was still subtle. But it was there. A small curve that hadn’t existed before. Agatha exhaled slowly and ran her hand over her abdomen.

"This is still just gas," she murmured to herself.

She picked up the first pair of pants and put them on.

The fabric slid easily over her legs. When she pulled the waistband up, she immediately noticed the difference. The pressure that used to be there disappeared. It was comfortable. Very comfortable.

Agatha looked at the mirror. From the front, the belly practically disappeared under the cut of the pants. She tilted her body from side to side, analyzing the fit.

"Okay…" she murmured. "But then," when she turned her body slightly in profile, something in the reflection made her stomach tighten.

Agatha stayed in the fitting room for several more minutes, trying piece after piece. Some pants fit perfectly, others were too loose in the legs, some marked the belly in a way she immediately discarded.

Each time she changed clothes, she looked at herself in the mirror with almost clinical attention. Facing forward, sideways, testing sitting on the small bench to see if the fabric tightened. It was strange to think about clothes now in terms of hiding something.

Even so, some pieces worked surprisingly well.

A structured black pair of dress pants, a graphite gray one with nearly invisible side adjustment. 

A straight skirt that fell just below the knee and looked absolutely normal from the front.

Agatha also chose two lightweight blouses that fell loosely over her abdomen without looking too baggy. She also picked a simple dark blue dress that, interestingly, made her look elegant in the mirror without marking almost anything.

After a few minutes analyzing everything, she set aside a small pile of approved clothes and got dressed again in her own pieces.

When she stepped out of the fitting room, she was carrying several folded clothes in her arms.

The assistant saw her immediately and smiled.

"So? Did it work?"

Agatha placed the items on the counter.

"Better than I expected."

The young woman began folding everything carefully while scanning the codes.

"These pants are great for work," she commented. "Many clients come back later to buy other colors."

Agatha rested her hands on the counter as she thought.

"I’ll take two."

She pointed to the black and the graphite ones.

"And this skirt too."

The assistant nodded and continued separating the items.

Agatha walked around the store a little more while the order was being organized. She ended up stopping in another section, where there were more comfortable sets to wear at home.

She picked up a soft sweat set.

Then another, and almost without noticing, she stopped in front of a rack of pajamas. She ran her fingers over an extremely soft cotton fabric.

"Okay… this is dangerous enough to trap me in bed wearing it," she murmured to herself.

She chose two pajamas. One light gray, the other blue, and when she returned to the counter with a few more items, the assistant smiled again.

"Good choice."

Agatha rested her arms on the counter while the total was being processed. The soft beeping of the scanner and the movement of folding fabrics filled the comfortable silence of the store. That’s when she heard a voice.

"Agatha." The sound was enough to make her entire body stop.

Agatha turned slowly.

Her heart beating faster, and then she saw.

Evanora. The woman stood a few steps behind her, holding a small stack of religious pamphlets. Her face looked older than Agatha remembered. Deep lines marked the corners of her mouth and eyes, and her hair was grayer.

Even so, the look was the same. Hard, critical, just as Agatha had seen her entire life.

Agatha blinked a few times.

"Mom…" she finally said, her voice lower than she intended. "What are you doing in New York?"

Evanora lifted her chin with that characteristic air of rigid pride.

"I’m with the church. Missionary work." She lifted the pamphlets as proof.

"I was walking around the mall handing out material and I almost didn’t believe it when I saw you here."

Agatha picked up the shopping bags the assistant had already begun organizing.

"Oh… right." She tried to keep her tone neutral. "Well, I have to go."

But before she could take a step, Evanora spoke again.

"I can’t believe you’re pregnant."

The sentence fell into the air like a stone. Agatha stopped and turned slowly.

"That’s none of your business." Evanora slowly shook her head, her gaze heavy with judgment.

"I always knew." Agatha felt her stomach tighten.

"I always knew you’d end up like this." Evanora’s voice wasn’t loud, but it was sharp.

"Turning into a slut."

Some people nearby began to look.

"And now clearly pregnant… and alone."

The silence around them seemed to grow heavier. Agatha felt tears rise to her eyes before she even realized it, her hands tightening around the bag handles. She opened her mouth to respond, but another voice appeared.

"Is everything alright, dear?" Agatha turned her face.

Alice was standing beside her. Agatha hadn’t even seen where she came from. Alice had simply appeared, as if she had always been part of the scene. With a natural gesture, she placed her hand on Agatha’s shoulder.

"Yes…" Agatha said, trying to recover her voice. "I am."

Alice lifted her gaze to Evanora, her look cold. Agatha took a deep breath and looked back at her mother.

"What were you saying, Mom?"

Evanora opened her mouth to continue.

But Alice interrupted her immediately.

"I think the conversation is over."

The tone was firm, not aggressive, but absolutely final. Alice pulled Agatha slightly closer to her.

"And don’t address my partner like that again."

The word partner fell into the air with perfect naturalness. Evanora fell silent for a second, her gaze moving from one to the other.

But Alice was already guiding Agatha out of the store. The two of them walked down the mall corridor for a few meters until they were far enough away.

Agatha stopped and took a deep breath.

"Thank you." Alice observed her face carefully.

"Breathe," she said calmly. "Are you okay?"

Agatha ran a hand over her face quickly.

"No." She gave a small humorless laugh. "But I will be."

Alice nodded.

"Do you want to tell me where that woman came from?" Agatha looked ahead as they walked slowly down the corridor.

"My mother." Alice made a small grimace.

"I figured." Agatha sighed.

"Evanora is… complicated."

She adjusted the bags in her hands.

"I left home early."

She paused before continuing.

"She was involved in some pretty extreme religious cults. Thought I should marry some awful alpha from the neighborhood and become an obedient housewife."

Alice shook her head.

"I’m sorry." Agatha gave a small crooked smile.

"I deal with it in therapy."

Alice let out a short laugh. Then she looked at the display windows around them.

"Since we’re here… do you want to help me with something?" Agatha raised an eyebrow.

"What?"

"I was going to buy a birthday gift for Jen."

Alice made a slight face.

"Honestly, I’m completely lost." Agatha laughed for the first time since the encounter with her mother.

"That’s easy." She pointed to a jewelry store a few shops ahead. "My girl Jen loves jewelry."

"My luck finding you," Alice said with a light smile, extending her hand to open the jewelry store door for Agatha. The small bell above the door chimed softly as they entered, and the air inside felt even cooler and more perfumed than the mall corridor.

The store was elegant in an almost quiet way. The light marble floor reflected the soft glow of the chandeliers hanging from the ceiling, and the glass displays were arranged like small islands throughout the space, each illuminated by delicate spotlights that made the metals and gemstones shine subtly. Necklaces, rings, and bracelets rested on velvet stands in shades of cream and pale pink. It was the kind of place where each piece looked more like a work of art than an accessory.

Agatha walked slowly beside Alice between the displays, observing the jewelry with almost automatic professional interest. Alice seemed a bit more lost, looking from one piece to another as if they were all too beautiful to choose just one.

A friendly saleswoman, dressed in an impeccable beige suit, approached with a practiced smile.

"Good afternoon. Can I help you?"

Agatha nodded slightly.
