"Yes. We’re looking for a piece of jewelry that matches a specific person."

The saleswoman tilted her head curiously.

"May I see?"

Agatha took out her phone and opened a photo of Jen. The image showed Jen smiling at some event, impeccable makeup, perfectly styled hair, the confident expression of someone completely comfortable in any environment.

The saleswoman observed for a few seconds.

Then she smiled.

"I think I have exactly what you need."

She made an elegant gesture for them to follow her to another display further in the store. Carefully, she opened the glass and took out a small pink velvet box.

"We just received this collection."

She opened the box.

Inside was a delicate choker, formed by a row of small pale pink diamonds that captured the light in an almost magical way. The stones were tiny, but perfectly cut, forming a soft curve that seemed made to rest on the collarbone of someone used to being the center of attention.

It was absurdly beautiful.

Something almost overly elegant.

Very princess.

Very preppy.

Agatha lightly tapped Alice’s shoulder.

"This one."

Alice leaned in to look more closely, her eyes shining slightly.

"Wow…"

The saleswoman smiled, satisfied.

"I can prepare it as a gift."

"Please," Alice replied.

While the saleswoman stepped away to organize everything at the counter, Alice turned to Agatha.

"Do you think she’ll like it?"

Agatha crossed her arms with a confident smile.

"She’s going to love it."

Alice let out a relieved sigh.

"Thank you."

The two of them walked to a small velvet couch near the wall and sat down while they waited. The store was quiet, with only soft instrumental music playing in the background. Alice observed Agatha for a few seconds before speaking.

"Are you still upset?" Agatha shrugged.

"A little." She ran her hand over the strap of the shopping bag.
"But it’ll pass."

Alice nodded slowly. She stayed silent for a moment before asking, carefully:

"Can I ask you something?"

Agatha turned her face toward her.

"Of course." Alice clasped her hands over her lap.

"Aren’t you interested in knowing who the other parent of the baby is?"

Agatha stayed silent for a few seconds. Then she gave a small shrug.

"No."

Alice waited for her to continue. Agatha sighed.

"I… don’t want to deal with expectations."

She looked down at the floor for a moment.

"I don’t know." Alice nodded slowly.

"I understand." But she added gently:

"I just think the other person should take responsibility too."

Agatha looked at her and gave a small tired smile.

"I appreciate the concern." She took a deep breath.

"But I prefer it this way."

Alice didn’t insist. She just nodded.

"Alright." She paused briefly before adding:

"But if you ever change your mind… I can help."

Agatha frowned slightly.

"What do you mean?"

Alice smiled to the side.

"The party was mine."

Agatha blinked.

"I… didn’t know that." Alice shrugged.

"It was." She tilted her head slightly.

"So, if you want, I can check who was on the guest list."

Agatha thought for a moment.

"I… I’ll think about it."

Alice nodded.

"No pressure." At that moment, the saleswoman returned, carrying an elegant white bag with the jewelry store’s logo in gold.

"Here you go."

She handed the box to Alice.

Alice took the card from her wallet without even looking at the price and made the payment quickly. The black, heavy card slid through the reader as if it were something absolutely routine. A few seconds later, the transaction was complete.

The two of them thanked her and left the store, returning to the mall corridor.

Agatha adjusted the shopping bags on her arm.

"Jen is going to love having you at the party."

Alice raised an eyebrow.

"Do I really have to wear a blazer?" Agatha turned her head to her immediately.

"Yes."

Alice sighed dramatically.

"I hate blazers." Agatha smiled.

"The theme is Sex and the City." She pointed at Alice.

"You need to stay on theme."

"I fought wars to avoid wearing that kind of clothing."

Agatha laughed.

"You bought a pink diamond choker."

Alice pointed a finger at her immediately.

"That was for love."

"Then you can wear a blazer for love too." Alice let out a dramatic sigh, looking up at the mall ceiling as if searching for divine strength.

"Jen is going to pay me back for this."

Agatha laughed softly.

"No, but she’ll hug you really tight and tell you you look beautiful." Alice thought for a second.

"…okay, that’s fair." The two of them kept walking down the corridor, now much lighter than when they had entered the store.

 

◆

 

The late afternoon slowly settled over Manhattan, and the golden light streamed through the large windows of Rio’s office, reflecting across the impeccably organized desk. Neat stacks of documents were aligned with almost obsessive precision, the open laptop displaying financial graphs while Rio typed a few notes into a quarterly report.

Or at least, she tried to. Because every few minutes, her phone vibrated beside the computer.

Jessica. Message after message. Photos of flowers. Supplier links.

Questions about color palettes. Details about invitations. Rio picked up the phone, read the latest notification for a second… and turned it face down on the desk, as if that could also silence the pressure that came with it.

She let out a small breath through her nose, ran a hand over the bridge of her nose, and looked back at the computer.

After a few seconds of productive silence, she pressed the intercom button.

"Miss Harkness."

The response came almost immediately from the other side.

"Yes, Mrs. Vidal?"

"Can you bring the quarterly spreadsheets from the research department?"

"Of course."

Rio hung up the intercom and returned to the report.

A few minutes later, the office door opened with a light knock.

"Excuse me."

Rio looked up.

And immediately wished she hadn’t.

Agatha stood in the doorway, holding a few folders and a tablet. Her hair was down today, falling in soft waves over her shoulders. The light coming through the windows seemed to highlight every detail of her in an almost irritatingly perfect way.

And there was something different. Maybe it was the outfit, maybe it was the new cut of the pieces she was wearing. But Agatha’s curves seemed… more pronounced.

Softer.

More present. Rio realized her gaze had dropped, and immediately tried to bring it back up. Especially because the neckline of Agatha’s blouse was, to say the least… generous. Very generous.

Rio looked back at the desk with almost disciplinary speed.

"Here you go," Agatha said, approaching and placing the spreadsheets on the desk.

Rio cleared her throat lightly.

"Thank you."

Agatha arranged the papers in front of her while Rio pulled them closer.

"You can sit, if you’d like," Rio said. "We need to review a few points."

Agatha pulled one of the chairs in front of the desk and sat down.

"Of course." Before they began, however, she added casually:

"Miss Jessica left a few calls for you today."

Rio flipped through the first page of the spreadsheet.

"I can imagine." Agatha continued, professional.

"She asked that you return them as soon as possible."

Rio made a small neutral sound, something between a "hm" and a restrained sigh. She didn’t seem exactly interested. Agatha noticed, but didn’t ask anything. Instead, she leaned slightly forward in the chair, pointing to one of the columns in the spreadsheet.

"Here are the numbers from the last quarter."

Rio followed the gesture with her eyes.

The problem was that Agatha’s position… didn’t help. Because the movement made the neckline of her blouse open a little more. Rio fixed her gaze on the paper firmly.

"Right," she said, trying to keep her tone perfectly professional. "Let’s go over it."

Agatha began explaining the data with her usual efficiency, pointing out graphs, percentages, and small budget variations.

Rio tried to pay attention.

She really tried, but every few seconds, her awareness drifted back to two very specific things:

Agatha’s voice, and the way the fabric of her blouse seemed to defy gravity every time she moved. Rio took a deep breath and made a note on the report.

"Are these numbers correct?"

Agatha leaned a little closer to look.

"Yes. I checked twice."

"I see." Agatha gathered the tablet and stood up.

"If you need any more spreadsheets, just let me know."

"Of course."

Agatha walked toward the door.

Before leaving, she turned slightly.

"Oh… and it might be a good idea to return Jessica’s call."

Rio looked up. Agatha gave a small, almost innocent smile.

"She called four times."

Rio was still looking at the spreadsheets when Agatha gathered a few sheets, as if she were about to leave.

"Miss Harkness," Rio said calmly.

Agatha looked up.

"Yes?"

Rio pulled another page from the report.

"We still need to review a few points."

Agatha nodded and sat back down.

"Of course."

She leaned over the desk again, bringing the spreadsheets closer so they could both see better.

The silence that followed was the kind of professional silence that usually filled that room — papers being moved, pens writing numbers, small, precise observations.

But today there was something… different.

Rio noticed it first.

Her gaze briefly drifted away from the report when something blue caught her attention.

On Agatha’s wrist there was a delicate bracelet, made of small light-blue stones that reflected the window light.

Rio frowned slightly. She knew that piece.

"That bracelet," Rio said, pointing lightly with the pen. "Is it from Ibiza?"

Agatha looked at her own wrist.

"Oh… it is."

She turned her arm slightly, observing the small stones.

"I bought it there."

Rio leaned back slightly in her chair.

"So you’ve been to Ibiza."

Agatha nodded.

"I have." A small smile appeared on her face.

"It was a trip with some friends. It’s been a while… but it was great."

Rio tilted her head.

"I imagine."

She briefly rested her chin on her hand.

"I’ve been to Ibiza too."

Agatha raised her eyebrows, surprised.

"Really?" Rio nodded.

"But we were certainly in very different places."

Agatha let out a small laugh.

"Probably."

She looked back at the spreadsheets.

"I think I’d remember you."

Rio watched her profile for a second. Then she said, calmly:

"And I would certainly remember you."

Agatha looked up. For a second, just one second, their eyes met.

Agatha gave a small smile — quick, almost shy — before turning her attention back to the papers.

"Right…" she said, flipping through a few pages. "Here are the numbers I mentioned earlier."

She pulled a sheet clipped with a metal fastener, trying to loosen it.

The paper slipped slightly. The clip scraped her finger.

"Ah—" A small cut opened on the tip of her index finger. Nothing serious, but enough for a few drops of blood to appear immediately.

One of them fell onto the paper, and before Agatha could even react properly, Rio was already moving.

She took Agatha’s hand carefully, pulling it slightly closer. The gesture was quick.

Agatha blinked, surprised.

"It’s fine," she said. "It’s just a small cut—"

But Rio was already pressing her finger firmly, trying to stop the bleeding. Her touch was warm. Firm. Almost… too careful.

Agatha felt the air change.

It was subtle.

But unmistakable.

Rio’s pheromones, that soft, protective, almost enveloping sensation that came from her without her seeming to notice. Agatha swallowed hard. A slight dizziness began forming at the back of her head.

"Mrs. Vidal…" Agatha murmured. "It’s fine, really."

Rio was still holding her hand.

Her eyes were fixed on the small cut.

As if it were something far more serious than it actually was.

"What are you doing…?" Agatha asked, her voice a little lower now.

Rio blinked.

As if she were waking up from something. She looked at her own hand holding Agatha’s.

"I…" Rio frowned slightly.

"I don’t know." The answer came out almost too honest. She took a deep breath.

"For some reason… I feel… protective around you."

The silence between them grew dense. Rio let go of her hand for a second… only to grab a tissue from the desk. But before she could think too much, she held the injured finger again, and then, almost without realizing the gesture…

She brought Agatha’s finger to her own mouth, gently pressing the wound. Sucking the blood to clean the cut.

Agatha froze. A sudden heat rose through her body. Under the table, her legs pressed together involuntarily.

Rio’s scent was stronger now, protective and enveloping. Rio took only a few seconds, then slowly pulled her finger away.

Their eyes met again. Much closer now. And the silence that fell in the room was anything… but professional.

Agatha exhaled and stood up, trying to escape the trance. Rio blinked and stood as well.

"I’m sorry, I… I’m sorry, Agatha, I didn’t mean to scare you," Rio murmured, the two of them stepping closer without noticing.

All the memory returned to Rio’s mind, everything about Ibiza, but in Agatha, the darkness remained in her gaze.

"You don’t remember me?" Rio whispered, stepping closer, gently touching Agatha’s chin. Agatha blinked, trying to pull something from deep within her mind.

"No… I don’t think so."

Agatha’s eyes met Rio’s, Rio’s eyes perfectly almond-shaped, a beautiful honey-brown.

"I have to go." Agatha stepped back, grabbing her bag. Rio lowered her hands, almost as if reprimanding herself. She watched Agatha leave in silence.

And disappear down the hallway. Rio then, in a surge of fury and guilt, swept the papers off her desk.

"Why? Why was she doing this? Why was she doing this? Why couldn’t she follow what had been set for her?"

 

◆

 

Saturday arrived dragging itself in, heavy, as if the very air inside the room had gained density overnight. Agatha was still wrapped in the comforter, her body sunk into the mattress, her muscles loose with exhaustion and her mind far too restless to truly rest. The morning light filtered through the curtain in soft bands, drawing pale lines across the floor and slowly climbing onto the bed, but even that didn’t seem enough to make her move.

Outside, life was already happening.

She could hear muffled voices, footsteps, the sound of something being dragged across the floor, the faint snap of plastic — balloons, probably. Jen and Wanda were already up, organizing everything for the birthday. The contrast was almost cruel.

Agatha took a deep breath.

She forced herself to sit up in bed, running a hand over her face, trying to shake off the residue that still clung to her skin — not sleep, but the memory.

Agatha closed her eyes for a second and shook her head lightly, as if she could push it away, but it didn’t work.

With a tired sigh, she slid her legs out of bed and slipped on her slippers, feeling the cold floor beneath her feet before the soft fabric softened the contact. She stood up slowly, as if every movement had to be negotiated with her own body.

And then she left the bedroom.

The living room was filled with shades of pink and silver. Balloons scattered around, some already inflated, others deflated waiting their turn. Ribbons coiled across the table, an open box on the floor with more decorations, and Jen and Wanda in the middle of it all, kneeling, laughing softly as they tried to tie a particularly stubborn balloon.

The scene was light. Agatha paused for a second at the entrance, watching.

"Happy birthday," she said, her voice still hoarse, leaning down to place a light kiss on the top of Jen’s head.

Jen smiled automatically.

"Thank you, sister."

Agatha let herself drop onto the couch like someone giving up fighting gravity, letting her body sink into the cushions and breathing deeply, her eyes closing for a moment longer than they should have. Wanda and Jen exchanged a quick glance, and Wanda was the first to speak.

"Why do you look like a bus ran over you?"

Agatha let out a weak laugh.

"Because that’s exactly how I feel."

She ran her hand lightly over her belly, almost unconsciously, and took another deep breath.

"What happened, Agatha?" Wanda asked, her voice softer now.

And then it came. The flashes, quick and disordered. Rio’s hand holding hers, the warmth, the thick scent in the air. Dark eyes, too close.

The question that hadn’t been spoken out loud, but had filled all the space between them. Agatha slowly opened her eyes.

"Rio Vidal almost kissed me yesterday."

The silence that followed was immediate.

"What?!" Jen turned completely, eyes wide, irritation surfacing before anything else. "Did she harass you?"

Agatha let out a short, humorless laugh.

"No." She ran a hand over her face again.

"It was… mutual. And I really wanted it."

Wanda closed her eyes for a second, taking a deep breath, as if organizing a thousand thoughts at once.

"Agatha… fuck."

Agatha laughed again, softer this time.

"I ruin everything." She shook her head, her eyes starting to burn.

"I can’t keep a job without almost getting involved in an affair."

The laugh that came next died halfway through and turned into crying. The tears came fast, heavy, silent at first, until her face completely fell apart.

Wanda and Jen didn’t hesitate. They stood almost at the same time and went to her, wrapping Agatha in a hug from both sides, pulling her close, holding the weight she clearly couldn’t carry alone anymore.

Agatha clung to them.

Her fingers gripping the fabric of Wanda’s clothes.

Her face buried against Jen’s shoulder.

"My mother is right…" her voice came out muffled, breaking. "I’m a slut."

"Hey. Hey!" Jen pulled back just enough to look at her, holding Agatha’s face in her hands. "Don’t say that."

Wanda nodded immediately.

"That woman doesn’t know anything about you."

Agatha swallowed hard, trying to pull air back into her lungs.

"She knows I’m pregnant…"

Her voice dropped.

"She saw me at the mall."

Their eyes narrowed, attentive.

"Luckily… Alice showed up." Agatha wiped her face with the back of her hand.

"She… scared my mother off."

Jen let out a heavy sigh, clearly irritated. Wanda squeezed Agatha’s shoulder firmly.

"Agatha, fuck whatever that woman says." Her tone was calm, but there was steel in it.

"It’s not true." Jen nodded, more direct, warmer.

"You’re a woman in control of your life. You don’t owe that old woman anything."

The silence that followed was softer. Agatha breathed slowly, still trembling a little, but grounded again in their presence, between them, safe — at least for now.

Agatha took another deep breath, trying to organize her thoughts, wiping her face with still slightly shaky hands. The crying gradually faded, replaced by that emotional exhaustion that lingers after the storm.

She sniffled softly and let out a long sigh.

"I don’t want to be sad today…"

Her voice came out lower, but steady enough.

She lifted her eyes between Jen and Wanda, still wrapped in their arms.

"It’s your birthday, Jen."

Jen’s expression softened immediately, her thumb still wiping the damp corner of Agatha’s eye.

"Hey…" she said with a half-smile. "Today is exactly the day you can feel however you want."

Wanda nodded, resting her chin lightly on Agatha’s head.

"Including dramatic, emotional, and needy. It’s part of the package."

Agatha let out a small breath of laughter, small but real this time.

Jen then pulled back just enough to grab a glass from the coffee table.

"Also," she said, raising the glass with a certain pride, "I made strawberry juice for you."

Agatha blinked.

"Juice?"

"Juice," Jen confirmed. "Since you won’t be able to drink at the party."

Agatha immediately made an exaggerated pout, almost childish.

"Not even a little?"

Wanda crossed her arms, already anticipating it.

"No."

Jen followed, shaking her head.

"Not a drop."

Agatha narrowed her eyes, looking from one to the other, clearly evaluating her chances.

"You two are very strict."

"We’re responsible," Wanda corrected.

"Boring," Agatha muttered.

"Alive."

Agatha huffed, but the corner of her mouth lifted anyway. She threw herself back into them, burying her face between Wanda’s shoulder and Jen’s neck, hugging them tighter.

"I hate you both."

"We know," Wanda said, running a hand through her hair.

"Lies," Jen added, kissing the top of Agatha’s head. "You love us."

Agatha tightened the hug, not answering.

She stayed curled between them for a few more seconds, enjoying the warmth of the embrace, the familiar scent, the safety that only existed there. Then, still with her face half-hidden, her voice came out muffled, but laced with tired humor:

"Well… I’m going to need you when my kid becomes a teenager and decides to say “i hate you” —

"Oh no," Jen said immediately, pulling back just enough to look at her with mock seriousness. "That’s the day we disown them."

Wanda added, in the same tone:

"Completely." Jen nodded.

"We disown them as a nephew."

Agatha lifted her head, outraged, her eyes still slightly swollen.

"What?!" Wanda shrugged, very calm.

"We didn’t raise them for that."

Jen crossed her arms.

"Ingratitude will not be tolerated in this family."

Agatha stared at the two of them, eyes wide.

"You’re horrible!"

"We’re consistent," Wanda corrected.

"Exactly," Jen said, serious for half a second… before breaking into a smile.

And then the three of them laughed, a light, easy laugh that filled the room and dissolved the weight that still lingered in the air. Agatha ended up laughing too, shaking her head, letting her body fall back against the couch, pulling them with her.

"Fine…" she murmured. "Then I’ll teach my child to love you first."

"Smart," Wanda said with a sideways smile. Agatha closed her eyes for a moment, still smiling, while the two of them remained there, pressed close to her. And for a moment, everything felt… in place.

◆

Night fell, bringing with it a completely different energy from that morning. The apartment, which had once been filled with silence and heavy breathing, now vibrated. Indirect lighting in warm pink tones mixed with small points of light scattered through the decorations.

Pink and silver balloons floated across the ceiling, some tied with long ribbons that fell to shoulder height, swaying as people passed by. On the main table, lined-up glasses, open bottles, trays of carefully arranged snacks — everything planned with almost theatrical care.

The theme was unmistakable.

Sex and the City was in every detail.

An improvised poster of the four characters on the wall, drinks labeled with little signs — "Carrie’s Cosmo," "Charlotte’s Classic" — and even a small improvised rack with accessories: heels, bags, and scarves.

And them, Jen was radiant.

Dressed as Carrie, in a striking, light, and bold dress, Jen wore a blonde lace wig with carefully messy curls. She moved through the apartment as if the space were an extension of her, greeting each person, laughing loudly, receiving compliments.

Wanda, on the other hand, was pure elegance dressed as Charlotte in essence. She moved among the guests with a glass in hand, discreetly organizing small details, making sure everything flowed smoothly.

And Agatha…

Agatha was Miranda. The structured blazer, the polished hair, the firm posture — but with that hint of irony in her gaze that gave everything away. The neckline, still generous even within the more “serious” concept, drew enough attention to earn a few discreet glances throughout the night.

The apartment was full.

Neighbors from the building, coworkers, acquaintances. Laughter mixed with background music, overlapping conversations, and the clinking of glasses. In one corner, Kingo chatted animatedly with his boyfriend, gesturing as if he were telling the best story in the world. Closer to the window, Loki — an old college friend — leaned casually, observing everything with a slightly cynical smile, clearly enjoying the organized chaos.

The doorbell rang, and Jen practically jumped to the door. When she opened it, her smile widened immediately.

Dressed as Big, of course Agatha had leaked what Jen’s costume would be so she could take inspiration. Alice wore an impeccable suit. A delicious contrast to the theme and yet, perfect.

"Happy birthday," Alice said, smiling slightly as she held out the elegant bag.

Jen didn’t hesitate. She leaned in and kissed her first, quick and spontaneous, drawing playful "ooohs" from those closest to the door.

"Get in here already," Jen said, pulling her by the hand.

Alice stepped in, her eyes quickly scanning the room, clearly approving everything.

"Wow… you really committed to the theme."

"Always," Jen replied, already pulling at the gift.

She didn’t wait. She opened it right there.

"AH!"

The squeal was automatic.

Loud and genuine. She brought her hand to her mouth, eyes shining when she saw the pink diamond choker reflecting the room’s light.

"Alice…" her voice came out almost breathless. "This is… perfect."

Agatha, from across the room, met Alice’s gaze for a second.

And winked, a small, knowing gesture. Alice responded with a slight nod, almost imperceptible. Jen didn’t resist, throwing herself into Alice’s arms, wrapping her arms tightly around her neck.

"I love it," she murmured against her.

Alice laughed softly, holding her by the waist.

"I figured."

The party grew as the night went on, gaining body, sound, and warmth. The music gradually got louder, filling the apartment with soft beats that blended with the lively, overlapping voices of the guests.

In the kitchen, someone improvised drinks, mixing vodka with cranberry juice and handing out cosmopolitans in glasses that quickly passed from hand to hand. The sweet scent of the drinks mixed with food — mini sandwiches, delicate canapés, and something lightly toasted that Wanda had insisted on preparing, making sure no one went hungry amid the improvised glamour.

Kingo had already taken over the center of the room as if it were a stage, telling an absurd story with wide gestures, drawing loud laughter while his boyfriend tried, unsuccessfully, to make him lower his volume. Near the balcony, Loki leaned casually with a glass in hand, debating whatever topic came up with his ironic tone and sharp gaze, completely at ease in that elegant chaos.

In one corner, a game started forming almost by accident. Cards were spread across the coffee table, rules invented on the spot, and soon a small group leaned in, laughed loudly, and accused each other of cheating without any commitment to the truth. Jen, with the pink diamond choker shining around her neck, took part in everything, laughing easily, touching Alice whenever she could, as if that constant contact were as necessary as the air she breathed.

Wanda moved through the space with ease, balancing elegance and attentiveness, laughing here, commenting there, adjusting small details without anyone noticing, maintaining the harmony of the party as if it were second nature to her.

Agatha, on the other hand, remained quieter, almost out of place within her own space. Sitting at one end of the couch, she held a glass of strawberry juice that slowly spun between her fingers, the liquid swirling without ever reaching her lips. Her smile appeared occasionally, soft and automatic when someone spoke directly to her, but most of the time her eyes were distant, wandering without a fixed direction.

Or maybe with too much direction.

Rio.

The memory came like an involuntary reflex, vivid and persistent. The touch still felt too recent, the scent still too present, the way her fingers had held her hand… the way her mouth had come close… Agatha closed her eyes for a moment, pressing her lips together lightly as if she could contain it, push the memory somewhere it couldn’t reach her. But it didn’t work. The image returned, insistent, cutting through any attempt at distraction.

She exhaled slowly and leaned her head back against the couch, trying to anchor herself in the present, in the laughter around her, the music, the warmth of the crowded space. That was when she felt the first twinge, low and almost shy, just enough to make her frown slightly as her hand instinctively went to her abdomen. It was nothing, probably. Gas, like it had been over the past few days. She shifted position, ignoring it, trying to stay there, inside the party.

But the second came stronger.

A sharper tightening that stole her breath for a full second. Agatha went still, waiting for it to pass, holding her body as if any movement might make it worse. It didn’t pass. The pain grew slowly, like a wave that starts far away and inevitably approaches, gaining shape and weight inside her.

Carefully, she placed the glass on the table, controlling every movement so as not to draw attention. She straightened her posture, forcing a neutral expression while inside the discomfort increased. Another contraction came, more intense, and this time she pressed her lips together to hold back any visible reaction.

Her legs felt heavier than they should, each step calculated as she crossed the hallway, one hand discreetly resting over her body. The sound of the party became muffled as she moved away, as if it were being left behind in layers.

When she entered the bedroom, she closed the door with a click that sounded too loud in the silence that followed. The contrast was immediate. The absence of voices, of music, of movement.

Agatha turned on the light, blinking a few times as she adjusted to the brighter clarity, and walked to the bed with controlled steps. She sat down carefully and let out a breath all at once, as if she had been holding it for too long.

Her shoes were the first to go; she removed them slowly, one at a time, pushing them aside without patience, relieved to feel her feet free.

She leaned slightly back, resting her hands on the bed as she tried to regulate her breathing. Another wave came, stronger, more insistent. Agatha closed her eyes, bringing her hand to her belly and pressing lightly, as if she could soothe the pain through touch.

"Shit…" she whispered, her voice barely there.

On the other side of the door, life went on. The music still echoed, muffled, mixed with laughter and conversations that continued unaware of what was happening inside. But in that room, the world had shrunk. There was only her body, her breathing trying to steady, and that growing pain that slowly began to demand all of her attention.

Wanda was already slightly drunk when she decided she needed to find Agatha. The idea came in the middle of a loud laugh, remembering some absurd story Loki had told, something that would clearly be much funnier if retold with exaggeration. With her glass still in hand, she started crossing the party, dodging people with steps a little less coordinated than usual, smiling at acquaintances as she moved down the hallway, convinced Agatha had just gone to the room to grab something.

She pushed the door open without knocking, still laughing.

"Agatha, you need to hear—" The laugh died before it could finish.

The sound simply disappeared from her throat.

Agatha was curled up on the floor.

Her body bent over itself, one hand pressing her abdomen while the other searched for support on the floor. Her face was pale, strained, and for a second Wanda couldn’t understand what she was seeing — until she saw it.

The blood.

Running down her pants.

The world seemed to tilt.

"AGATHA!" the scream tore out, filled with immediate terror that cut through any trace of alcohol in her system.

Wanda dropped to her knees beside her, her hands trembling, not knowing exactly where to touch, what to do, how to help.

"Agatha, look at me, look at me—"

"It hurts…" Agatha’s voice came out weak, broken, her eyes squeezed shut as her body reacted in waves of pain. "Wanda… it hurts so much…"

Panic rose like fire.

Wanda swallowed hard, trying to keep her head working, trying not to fall into complete panic.

"I’ll be right back, I’ll be right back— stay here, stay with me—"

She stood up too fast, almost slipping, and ran out of the room.

"JEN!" Wanda shouted, her voice loud enough to cut through the noise around.

Jen turned immediately, her smile disappearing when she saw the look on Wanda’s face.

"Agatha— something’s wrong— she’s bleeding—"

"Fuck." Jen didn’t hesitate.

She was already moving before she even finished processing the sentence. Alice, who was nearby, caught the tone and followed without needing further explanation.

The three of them rushed back down the hallway, and when they entered the room, the scene seemed even worse. Agatha was still on the floor, her breathing irregular.

Her body tense. Jen dropped beside her immediately.

"Agatha— hey— hey, look at me—"

Alice stayed a step back for half a second, then understood far too quickly what was happening.

"She’s miscarrying." The sentence fell into the room like something solid, heavy. Jen looked up immediately, panic cutting across her face.

"What?!" her voice came out loud, shaking. "Alice, do you have a car?!"

"No—" Alice was already pulling her phone from her pocket, her brain working at a different speed now. Her fingers moved across the screen almost automatically until they found a specific name.

Rio.

Her thumb hesitated for less than a second before pressing it. She turned her back to the room as the call connected, her heart beating too fast.

Now you need to take responsibility.

The line was answered on the second ring.

"What is it?" Rio’s voice came firm, controlled, but in the background it was possible to hear the ambient sound of a bar, voices, glass, movement.

Alice took a deep breath.

"I need urgent help," she said directly. "I’m at Jen’s party. Agatha’s not well, she’s bleeding, we need to take her to the hospital now."

On the other end of the line, the silence lasted less than a second, but it was enough. A shiver ran through Rio’s body like a shock.

"I’m coming," she replied, without hesitation.

And the call ended.

Rio didn’t think twice. The glass was left behind, abandoned on the counter still half full, while a fifty-dollar bill was carelessly dropped beside it. She was already moving before anyone could try to call her, crossing the bar with quick steps, pushing the door open and being swallowed by the cold night air. Her heels echoed sharply on the asphalt as she hurried to her car, her heart beating in a strange rhythm, too fast for something she didn’t yet understand.

She got in and pressed the ignition button firmly, as if that could make it respond faster. The engine roared, and within seconds she was already driving, cutting through the streets with precision and urgency, her eyes fixed, her jaw tight, every thought colliding inside her head.

She arrived fast.

Too fast.

When the car stopped in front of the building, the door was already opening before the engine had fully shut off. Alice appeared first, carrying Agatha in her arms carefully but without time for delicacy, while Jen and Wanda came right behind, panic written all over their faces.

Rio got out of the car immediately and opened the back door.

"Here—"

Alice didn’t hesitate, placing Agatha in the seat as carefully as possible. Her body looked smaller, too fragile, and the blood… the blood left no room for doubt about how serious it was.

Wanda got in right after, pulling Agatha close, holding her as if she could prevent something worse just by the force of her own body. Jen got in from the other side, her breathing uneven, her eyes fixed on her.

Alice went around and sat in the front seat.

"Go."

Rio didn’t answer.

She was already accelerating.

The car surged forward down the street, cutting through traffic with almost aggressive precision. The silence inside the vehicle was heavy, broken only by Agatha’s breathing and the low sounds of Wanda trying to keep her conscious, talking to her nonstop, as if silence itself were too dangerous.

That was when Rio saw Alice’s blazer stained with blood.

Rio’s fingers tightened on the steering wheel, her knuckles turning white, her jaw locking harder as something inside her began to unravel. The need to understand grew along with the discomfort, with the tension building in her chest without a name.

The hospital appeared ahead, the car barely coming to a complete stop before the doors were opening.

Alice got out first, already moving, crossing the entrance toward reception. Jen and Wanda helped Agatha out of the seat, but before they could do more, Rio was already beside them. Without asking permission, without thinking, she simply picked her up.

Agatha’s body against hers.

Warm and fragile. Their eyes met for a second, and something tore through Rio. A raw, instinctive panic that made no sense, that didn’t fit anything she knew about herself.

She didn’t question it, didn’t search for logic or explanation, she just moved, guided by a direct and urgent impulse that made her cross the space quickly. The automatic doors opened in front of her, and when they reached reception, a stretcher was already waiting, ready, as if anticipating the gravity of the situation. Rio placed Agatha there with precise care, without excessive gentleness, aware that every second carried real weight, and soon the professionals took over, guiding her down the hallway efficiently, disappearing from sight almost as quickly as they had appeared.

Rio stayed where she was.

Still.

Suddenly, the space around her felt too large, too empty, as if everything had expanded without warning. Jen collapsed into one of the nearby chairs, her body giving in to the impact of the moment, her eyes fixed on the hallway where Agatha had been taken, while Wanda remained at reception, her voice trembling as she answered questions, her hands still betraying the shock she was trying to contain.

Rio looked around, pulled in a deeper breath than necessary, and tried to reorganize herself internally, but something was wrong, displaced, an uncomfortable sensation that didn’t find a place within her. Her eyes found Alice and, without saying anything, she started walking toward her, as if that were the only possible line to follow at that moment.

Alice was already outside the hospital when Rio pushed through the doors, the cold air hitting her face like a light shock that wasn’t enough to clear what churned inside her.

"Alice, what the fuck was that?" her voice came out sharper than usual, loaded with a tension she didn’t try to hide. "I thought she had just gotten drunk, had too much to drink."

Alice didn’t answer immediately; she simply held her gaze, firm, direct, leaving no room to soften what came next.

"Agatha is pregnant."

The world didn’t stop completely, but it slowed enough to distort time around her. Rio remained still, her body reacting before her mind could catch up, while a cold sensation spread inside her, as if that information had been thrown directly into her system without any preparation.

Before she could form any response, Alice stepped forward and pressed Agatha’s bag against her chest, forcing the contact, forcing reality.

"And it’s yours," she stated firmly. "The child is yours."

This time, the silence didn’t settle in the environment — it settled inside her.

Absolute.

Chapter 7: Taking Responsibility

Chapter Notes

Enjoy your reading!

 

 

Agatha woke up slowly, as if she were emerging from somewhere too deep to be reached all at once. The first thing she felt was the weight in her own body, a dense, spreading exhaustion that seemed to have settled into every muscle.

The air entered her lungs with difficulty when she took a deep breath, her eyes still adjusting to the white, static light of the room until, little by little, reality began to reorganize itself around her.

Hospital.

The recognition came silently, accompanied by a fragmented memory of the previous night that she still couldn’t fully grasp.

A nurse approached carefully, her voice low and professional.

"How are you feeling?"

Agatha took a second to respond, her throat dry, her body still slow.

"Tired…" she murmured, her voice hoarse, distant.

The nurse nodded with understanding.

"I’ll call Dr. Wu, alright?"

Agatha simply agreed with a slight movement of her head, without energy for anything more, as the woman left the room, allowing the silence to settle back into the space. She took another deep breath and tried to move a little in the bed, adjusting her body carefully, as if she still expected some sharper pain at any moment.

The door opened again.

"Hello, dear… how are you feeling?"

Lorna entered with a soft but attentive smile, the kind that doesn’t fully hide the concern behind it.

Agatha didn’t return the smile.

Her eyes went straight to the point.

"Did I lose it?" she asked, anxiety cutting through her voice immediately.

Lorna stepped a little closer to the bed before answering, keeping her tone calm and steady.

"No, you didn’t lose it."

The answer came firm enough to hold Agatha for a full second of relief, even if brief.

"But you had significant bleeding, so I need you to stay under observation for a few days, just to make sure everything is alright."

Agatha nodded slowly, absorbing every word, still trying to keep up with her own body, her own time. Lorna continued, now with a slight shift in tone, more analytical.

"Your bleeding wasn’t very characteristic of a miscarriage. At first, I really thought it was, but…" she paused briefly, "I’d rather be sure."

Agatha watched her in silence, her heart starting to race again.

"I want to do an ultrasound with you awake, okay?"

There was a small pause. A second of hesitation.

"Yes…" Agatha replied, softer this time, but firm enough. And, without realizing it, her fingers curled slightly into the sheet.

The nurses returned shortly after, organized and efficient, exchanging a quick glance before carefully moving the gurney.

The faint creak of the wheels accompanied the movement through the corridor, while the ceiling passed slowly above Agatha, light after light, in a hypnotic sequence.

Lorna walked beside her, keeping a steady pace, her presence firm enough to anchor her in that moment.

"Everything is under control, dear," she said calmly, giving her a brief look. "I know it’s scary, but you arrived in time, and we’re going to monitor everything closely."

Agatha nodded slightly, still silent, absorbing more the tone than the words, letting that borrowed sense of security keep her steady.

When they reached the ultrasound room, the environment was darker, more contained, lit mainly by the monitor.

The nurses positioned the gurney and, gently, helped adjust the hospital gown.

Agatha also pulled the fabric up, exposing her abdomen, while Lorna prepared the equipment with precise movements.

The gel came next.

Cold.

Agatha let out a small involuntary sigh at the contact, her body reacting to the temperature contrast, while Lorna positioned the transducer carefully and turned her eyes to the screen.

There was a brief silence.

Focused.

Then Lorna spoke:

"Look."

Agatha turned her face toward the monitor, her eyes still filled with expectation and fear, and it took her a second to understand what she was seeing. The grayscale image seemed abstract at first, but then…

There, a small shape.

"Here is the baby," Lorna said softly.

The air left Agatha’s lungs in a trembling breath, immediate relief washing through her body, her shoulders relaxing for the first time since she had woken up.

But Lorna didn’t stop. She moved the device slightly, adjusting the angle.

"And here…" she continued, with a careful tone, "there is another gestational sac."

Agatha blinked, not understanding.

"…the other baby."

Time seemed to falter for a moment.

Agatha went still, her eyes fixed on the screen as the information took time to find a place inside her, as if there wasn’t enough space to accommodate it immediately.

"What…?"

Lorna kept her tone calm, explanatory, and didactic, yet still gentle.

"In twin pregnancies, especially early on, it’s relatively common for some bleeding to occur."

Lorna continued.

"The uterus is adapting more quickly, there’s a greater increase in vascularization, and sometimes small areas of detachment can cause this kind of situation." she sighed. "It’s frightening, but it doesn’t always mean loss."

Agatha kept staring at the screen.

At the two shapes.

Two.

"In your case, everything indicates that’s what it was, but we need to evaluate a possible iron deficiency," Lorna added, then allowing a slight smile to appear. "And, from what everything shows…"

She looked back at Agatha.

"You are having twins."

The silence that followed was different from all the others. Denser. Deeper. Agatha finally looked away from the screen, as if she needed to confirm it in the real world.

"Twins…?" she repeated, her voice low, breathless.

Lorna nodded calmly.

"Twins."

Lorna kept the device steady, moving it carefully while her eyes remained attentive to the monitor, analyzing every detail with the precision of someone who had seen this many times—and still respected the unique impact each discovery carried.

"Easy…" she said softly, noticing Agatha’s rigid silence. "They’re both okay."

The statement came with certainty, without hesitation, like an anchor thrown into the middle of the storm that was clearly beginning to form inside her.

"The heartbeats are present, the structures are consistent with the gestational age… there are no signs of fetal distress at the moment."

Agatha still didn’t respond.

Her eyes remained fixed on the screen, but it was no longer about understanding the image—it was about trying to process what it meant. Her breathing was shallower, her body still tense, as if any movement could make it more real than it already was.

Lorna noticed.

And softened her approach even more.

"This happens more often than you think," she continued calmly.

"In some cases, one of the gestational sacs is positioned in a way that, in previous exams or even initial evaluations, it doesn’t appear clearly."

She moved the transducer slightly again, pointing gently at the monitor.

"The second sac was partially ‘hidden,’ probably due to the position of the uterus or overlapping structures. With the bleeding, we investigated more carefully… and that’s when we were able to visualize it better."

Agatha blinked slowly.

Still in shock.

"You didn’t do anything wrong," Lorna added, with gentle firmness.

"And what you felt… this bleeding… can happen precisely because your body is adapting to a twin pregnancy. It’s more demand, more vascularization, more change happening at the same time."

Silence returned, but now filled by Lorna’s voice, steady and present.

"I know it’s a lot of information all at once," she said, finally looking directly at Agatha.

"But right now, the most important thing is: they’re okay… and so are you."

Agatha swallowed hard. Her fingers still gripping the sheet.

"Two…" she murmured to herself, as if still testing the word.

This time, there was no immediate response, only reality settling, slowly and inevitably, inside her.


◆

 

The doorbell rang once, twice, three times in a row—insistent, aggressive, echoing through the silent apartment like a warning impossible to ignore.

Alice didn’t wait. Right after, her knuckles struck the door hard, the dry sound filling the hallway as her breathing came out heavy, loaded with contained irritation.

"You can’t hide!" she shouted, her voice cutting through the wood without any attempt to soften her tone.

She ran a hand over her face, pulling in a deep breath, trying to maintain some control, but the ignored calls—all going straight to voicemail—still reverberated like enough fuel to keep her there, firm. Another knock.

This time, the door opened.

Rio appeared on the other side with a closed-off expression, hard, the kind of look that dismissed any formality.

"Why didn’t you answer?" Alice fired immediately, leaving no room for pauses.

Rio took a step forward, irritation evident in her posture.

"How about the fact that you hid this from me?" she shot back, her voice low but loaded with tension. "Hm? And you hired her outside of all company rules."

Alice raised her hands for a moment but didn’t back down.

"Hey, hey… I didn’t know," she replied, firm. "And another thing—you were a coward. You slept with her, got involved, and—"

"She doesn’t even remember me!" Rio cut in, her voice rising for the first time, frustration slipping out unfiltered.

Alice didn’t hesitate.

"And you didn’t even try!" she snapped back in the same tone, stepping closer.

"Sorry for thinking you’d react badly, but you’ve spent your whole life condemning this idea. And Agatha… Agatha is a good person. She’s my girlfriend’s best friend, and I wanted to protect her."

Rio let out a dry laugh, completely humorless.

"You decided that on your own." Alice scoffed, crossing her arms for a second before replying.

"Because you’re usually pretty selfish," she said, bluntly. "I understand that you’ve suffered, I understand where it comes from… but you’re not the only person that exists on this planet, Rio."

Silence fell for a moment, heavy.

Alice didn’t stop.

"That woman is carrying your child," she continued, her voice firm, without softening. "A child that only exists because of your recklessness."

Rio didn’t answer this time. She stayed silent. And the worst part was that she knew—she knew Alice was right.

Alice exhaled sharply through her nose, still tense.

"But anyway… even in that, you’re lucky," she added, with a half-smile lacking humor.

"She doesn’t want to look for the other parent of the baby. Again, you can just get away with it… just because you don’t want to face that old hag you call a grandmother."

"You wouldn’t understand."

Alice tilted her head slightly, tired.

"I do understand. I understood," she replied, quieter now, but no less firm. "And I’m telling you: if you fire that girl, I walk out of the company… and I take half of it with me."

Rio crossed her arms, assessing, defensive.

"And why do you care so much about Agatha?" Alice closed her eyes for a brief second, bringing a hand to the bridge of her nose, pressing it as if trying to contain her own exhaustion.

"Because, despite everything…" she began, slower, "she’s carrying my best friend’s child."

She opened her eyes again, looking at Rio intensely.

"Your child."The silence between them stretched.

"And you don’t even consider having that… because of some stupid revenge."

Rio took a deep breath but said nothing. The silence that remained between them wasn’t empty—it was heavy, dense, full of things left unsaid. Alice crossed her arms, watching her for a second before continuing, more practical, more direct.

"Her plan is to leave the company in the fifth month of pregnancy."

The information landed like another weight.

"So you have time to decide what you’re going to do," she finished, firm. "But I really hope you don’t hurt her by making some stupid decision."

Without waiting for an answer, Alice turned and walked toward the elevator, pressing the button with a sharp movement. The metallic sound of the mechanism preparing to rise echoed through the hallway.

Rio remained standing at the door, looking at the floor, as if still trying to organize something inside herself.

And then, without realizing it, she spoke:

"But… I’m engaged."

Alice turned her head over her shoulder, giving her a tired look, the kind that no longer had patience to pretend softness.

"And you don’t even believe that."

The elevator doors opened, and she stepped inside, disappearing.

Rio closed the apartment door slowly, the click sounding too loud in the empty space. For a moment, she just stood there, feeling her own heart still racing from the argument, as if her body hadn’t received the message that it was already over.

She ran a hand over her face and took a deep breath, walking into the living room.

The phone was on the table. When she picked it up, the screen lit up, revealing several missed calls from Jessica, the name flashing there, insistent.

Rio stared at it for a few seconds, her thumb motionless over the screen as thoughts began to pile up without a clear order.

The plan… had always made sense. It was clean, logical, elegant in the way it solved everything. Ending her grandmother’s bloodline. Not continuing something she had always hated.

Jessica was part of that. She always had been.

She knew Jessica couldn’t have children, not after the emergency surgery she had undergone in her adolescence. It was safe. A silent agreement that never needed to be spoken out loud.

But now… now it didn’t seem so simple. Because, for the first time, Rio realized that maybe she wasn’t the one controlling when to let go of the rope. And that destabilized her more than anything else.

A thought crossed her mind, quick, unsettling: what if I repeat the patterns?

Another came right after: what if I’m just like her?

The image came without permission into Rio’s mind—the scars, the pain, the inheritance. And then, deeper, the doubt: what if I hurt my own child?

"What if I fail?"

Rio closed her eyes for a second, as if she could block it out, but it didn’t work. Nothing seemed to work. She pulled in a sharper breath, trying to regain some control, and then, almost automatically, pressed the button to return the call.

Jessica answered on the first ring.

"Rio, my God, you disappeared!" The voice on the other side came fast, worried. Rio exhaled slowly.

"Sorry… a lot of work."

"We need to sort out the wedding things," Jessica continued, already moving into the subject. "Are you okay?"

Rio rubbed her face with her free hand, feeling the exhaustion finally start to weigh on her for real.

"Jessica…" she began, hesitating for a brief second. "We need to talk."

There was a small silence on the other side.

"Okay…" Jessica replied, more cautious now. "Are you home?"

"I am."

"Then I’ll come over."

The call ended right after, and Rio lowered the phone slowly, her gaze lost somewhere in the room.

 

◆

Agatha was lying on her side in the hospital bed, her body still heavy, as if every muscle carried the accumulated exhaustion of the past few hours.

The room was quiet, illuminated by a soft light coming through the window, and for a moment she just stayed there, staring at nothing, trying to organize thoughts that kept colliding with each other.

A soft knock came at the door, she turned her head, and before she could even respond, the handle turned and the door opened.

Wanda and Jen walked in, both wearing small smiles—the careful kind, like people who don’t want to scare you, but also don’t want to show too much concern.

"Hey…" Wanda said first, her voice soft as she approached the bed. "How are you?"

Agatha let out a low sigh.

"Okay…" she replied, even though her voice gave away the exhaustion. "Just… tired."

Jen approached from the other side, slightly lifting a bag.

"We brought you some clothes… in case you need to stay a few more days."

Agatha looked at her and nodded, a small smile appearing.

"Thank you…"

Wanda tilted her head, observing her face more closely.

"And the baby?"

Agatha hesitated.

"It’s…" she started, but the sentence trailed off for a second. She took a deep breath, placing her hands on the bed to sit up a little better, the movement slow, as if even that required more energy than usual.

"It’s fine."

Jen frowned slightly.

"Then why do you look like that?"

Silence fell for a moment. Agatha looked at the two of them, and something inside her… gave in. She let out a slow breath, her eyes beginning to fill with tears.

"Because…" her voice faltered, and she had to try again. "Because things were going to change…"

A tear slipped down. "…but now they’re really going to change."

Jen and Wanda exchanged a quick look.

"I don’t know how I’m going to do this," Agatha continued, her voice trembling, lower now.

"I don’t know."

Wanda took a step closer.

"Agatha… what happened?"

Agatha didn’t answer with words. She simply reached out to the bedside table, grabbed the paper, and handed it to them.

The ultrasound. Jen took it first, her eyes scanning the image before stopping, and Wanda leaned in to look as well, going still.

"…twins?" Jen murmured, not quite believing it.

Agatha nodded, wiping a tear with the back of her hand.

"I can’t force you to deal with this…" she said, her voice heavy with guilt. "I’m going to have to go back to Westview."

"No," Jen replied immediately, firm, without hesitation.

Agatha blinked.

"No," Jen repeated, stronger. "You’re not going back to live with your mother."

Wanda shook her head, agreeing right away.

"Not even an option, Agatha."

Agatha took a deep breath, her eyes still wet.

"I can’t put this on you… it’s two babies, Wanda… Jen… this changes everything."

"So what?" Jen shot back, crossing her arms for a second before relaxing. "You’re our friend."

"Exactly," Wanda added, stepping closer to the bed. "We’re not going anywhere."

Agatha lowered her gaze, still struggling with it.

"You don’t understand…"

"We understand enough," Wanda said, softer now. "And you’re not alone in this."

She sat on the edge of the bed.

"And why don’t you try to find the other person responsible?" she added, looking directly at Agatha. "You didn’t make these babies on your own."

Agatha let out a weak, humorless laugh, wiping her tears.

"I don’t even know where to start…" Silence returned, but this time different.

Wanda wrapped an arm around her, gently pulling her into a side hug. Jen did the same on the other side, surrounding Agatha without asking permission, just like she always did.

And then Agatha cried, this time without trying to hold it back.

After a few minutes, Agatha’s crying slowly faded, until it became just deeper, spaced-out breaths. Her body was still heavy, but no longer trembling. She ran a hand over her face, wiping the last traces of tears while remaining between Wanda and Jen, still leaning on them.

"Lorna said that…" she began, her voice calmer now, though still heavy, "that it was a type of bleeding that can happen."

Both of them paid attention immediately.

"In twin pregnancies," Agatha continued, looking at her hands for a second before raising her eyes again. "That the body is adapting… a lot happening at the same time."

Wanda nodded slowly.

"And…" Agatha took a deep breath, "she also mentioned iron deficiency. That I’ll have to take care of that."

Jen made a slight face.

"Okay, that explains why you’ve been looking like a ghost these past few days."

Agatha let out a weak laugh.

"Thanks for the kindness."

The mood softened for a moment, but Jen soon grew more serious.

"Maybe you’ll have to leave your job for a while," she said carefully. "For your own good."

Agatha shook her head immediately.

"I can’t. Not now."

"Agatha—"

"I need the money, Jen," she cut in, but without aggression, just firm. "I need to save as much as possible before all of this… gets even more expensive."

Wanda sighed, lightly resting her chin on Agatha’s shoulder.

"Then you need to be careful," she said, more practical. "Very careful. So no one finds out too soon."

Agatha nodded.

"I bought new clothes…" she said, absentmindedly running a hand over her hospital outfit, as if she were already thinking about it. "Looser ones, that hide it better."

"Good," Wanda replied, approving with a small nod.

Silence lingered for a few seconds, until Agatha spoke again, this time a little more hesitant.

"I… had a conversation with Alice."

Jen lifted her gaze.

"Hm?"

"She said that the party in Ibiza…" Agatha paused, organizing her thoughts, "was hers."

Wanda frowned slightly.

"Really?"

Agatha nodded.

"And that… she can help me find out who the other mother is."

The air in the room seemed to shift slightly. Wanda didn’t hesitate for even a second.

"What are you waiting for, Agatha?" the question came directly.

Agatha looked away, her fingers intertwining over the sheet.

"I…" she started, but her voice faltered for a moment. "I’m scared."

Jen tilted her head.

"Scared of what?"

Agatha took a deep breath.

"Of expectations…" she admitted, more quietly. "Of creating something in my head and… being rejected afterward."

Jen was the first to react.

"Hey," she said firmly, getting her attention. "If that person is stupid enough to reject these children…"

She gave a light touch to Agatha’s arm.

"They’re still going to be very loved."

Wanda nodded immediately.

"With us."

Jen finished, without hesitation:

"And that person will still have to pay what’s due."

Agatha took a deep breath, absorbing that, and slowly, her hand slid instinctively to her belly, resting there carefully, as if the gesture itself were already a promise.


◆

 

Later, the hospital room was already immersed in that typical end-of-night quiet, when the corridors grow more silent and even sounds seem muffled.

Agatha remained lying in bed, slightly sunk into the pillows, the remote control loose in her hand as she flipped through channels without really paying attention to any of them.

The television light flickered across her face, changing color every second, but nothing held her attention for more than two minutes.

Lorna had been clear: one more night under observation. Iron replacement, monitoring, rest. Agatha let out a small sigh, bored, switching channels once again.

That was when she heard the knock on the door. She frowned slightly, surprised by the hour, adjusting herself a bit in bed before answering:

"Come in."

The door opened, and Agatha froze for a second as Rio walked in.

Her presence seemed to shift the air in the room instantly. Agatha discreetly pulled the hospital gown a little higher, covering herself better, like an automatic reflex.

"Hi… Ms. Vidal," she said, still a little surprised.

Rio closed the door behind her calmly, maintaining her usual controlled posture.

"Miss Harkness. Good evening."

Agatha blinked a few times, trying to understand.

"What are you doing here?"

Rio took a few steps into the room, approaching the bed.

"I came to visit you," she replied naturally. "After all, my personal secretary decided to get completely drunk."

Agatha let out a short, slightly embarrassed laugh, glancing away for a moment.

"Yeah… maybe I overdid it a little."

Rio extended her hand, offering a small bouquet of flowers. The gesture was simple, but unexpected enough to bring a genuine smile to Agatha’s face.

"Thank you…" she said, holding the bouquet carefully.

Rio watched her for a moment before asking:

"How are you feeling?"

"Better," Agatha replied. "I think it really… was just that."

Rio nodded slightly.

"Even so, you need to be careful," she said, her tone a bit firmer. "With your diet as well."

Agatha opened her mouth to respond, but stopped midway when something occurred to her. She looked to the side, as if mentally reorganizing the days.

"Today is Tuesday…" she murmured, then looked back at Rio.

"Shouldn’t you be at your wedding rehearsal dinner? I remember putting it on your schedule."

Rio let out a small sigh.

"Things were postponed," she replied simply. "Some issues came up. It will be later."

"Oh…" Agatha nodded slowly. "Okay."

A small silence settled between them, comfortable enough, and then Rio continued:

"You can take the week off to recover." Agatha reacted immediately.

"No, I’m already better," she said, adjusting herself slightly in bed. "I’m not going to let something like this affect work."

Rio watched her for a second longer than usual, as if evaluating something beyond her words.

"Either way, we’ll be having meetings at home this week," she said then. "The executive office will be under renovation."

Agatha let out a small sound of understanding.

"Finally," she commented automatically. "That ceiling is all uneven…" She stopped mid-sentence, blinking as she realized what she had said.

"Sorry, I talked too much."Rio tilted her head slightly.

"It’s fine," she replied, her tone subtly lighter. "The ceiling is uneven."

Agatha let out a small side smile.

Rio then stepped away from the bed, adjusting her posture.

"I’ll let you rest."Agatha nodded.

"Thank you for coming… and for the flowers."

Rio held her gaze for a brief second, a faint smile appearing before disappearing again into her usual composure.

"Get some rest, Miss Harkness." And then she left, the door closing softly behind her. The room returned to silence.

Agatha exhaled slowly, looking at the bouquet in her hands for a few seconds, her fingers absently touching the petals.

And, without realizing it, a thought crossed her mind with uncomfortable clarity:

That… didn’t feel like just work. And little by little, Agatha was stepping into unstable ground—one with no way back.

 

◆

The morning was still fresh when Rio walked along the sidewalk, her steps firm, her gaze distant as if she were more occupied with her own thoughts than with the path ahead. The movement of the city was already beginning to grow—people going in and out of cafés, cars passing by, the constant sound of New York waking up once again.

She pushed the café door open.

The aroma of freshly brewed coffee and warm bread filled the space, but Rio barely noticed. Her eyes found Alice immediately. She was already seated at a table farther in the back, posture straight, expression serious, like someone who hadn’t come there for a light conversation.

Rio walked over to her and sat down.

"Why are we here so early?" Alice asked, direct, without preamble.

Rio rested her arms on the table for a moment, letting out a small sigh before answering:

"Thank you for coming." Alice raised an eyebrow slightly.

"That doesn’t answer my question." Rio ignored her for a second, organizing her thoughts.

"I’ve already decided what I’m going to do."

"Wow… what a surprise." Alice let out a small humorless laugh.

Rio remained calm.

"I’m going to keep acting as if I don’t know anything." The silence fell for a second, Alice blinked, incredulous.

"I don’t understand." Rio tilted her head slightly.

"I don’t want to push Agatha away right now," she explained. "You said it yourself—she’s unsure about looking for the other mother of the baby… so I’m not going to force anything."

Alice crossed her arms, leaning back in her chair.

"Right…"

Rio continued, now more firm:

"I’m going to keep her at the company, as usual. For as long as she wants."

Alice stared at her.

"So you’re not going to be involved in the pregnancy at all?"

Rio took a deep breath.

"I need to get closer to her little by little," she said. "Make her trust me. Make her feel comfortable… so she can talk about it with me."

Alice let out a short laugh, shaking her head.

"Okay. That’s stupid." Rio didn’t react.

"You two need to be honest," Alice continued. "This is just… delaying the inevitable."

"She doesn’t remember," Rio shot back, quieter but firm. "And I’m not going to stress her out right now. I want her to trust me. Completely."

Alice watched her for a few seconds, assessing.

"Okay…" she finally said, still unconvinced. "Fine."

She then leaned slightly forward.

"And Jessica?" Rio blinked, as if for a second, she truly hadn’t thought about her.

"I asked for some time." Alice let out a small “hm,” crossing her arms again.

"Oh… okay."

Silence lingered for a moment, less tense now, but still loaded with implications. Then Rio shifted her posture slightly, as if wanting to end that subject.

"I’m hungry," she said simply. "Order something." Alice let out a mocking laugh, finally breaking the rigidity of her expression.

"You’re being weird." Rio lifted her gaze to her, impassive.

"I’m normal."

Alice tilted her head, analyzing her with a faint smile.

"Sure you are.”

After the café, Rio didn’t prolong the goodbye. She simply stood up, left some cash on the table, and walked out, her mind already distant again. The air outside felt colder than before—or maybe it was just the way everything inside her still felt… misaligned.

She got into the car.

The engine started with a low hum, familiar, comforting, and during the drive, her phone vibrated more than once on the console beside her.

Jessica. The name lighting up the screen. Rio didn’t answer. Her fingers remained firm on the steering wheel as the memory of the night before came back, far too clear.

“We need to talk.” —Jessica’s silence on the other end.

And then— “I need some time.” She remembered the expression.

The way Jessica went rigid, surprised, then irritated.

“Time for what, Rio?”

And Rio… didn’t really answer. She just repeated that things were moving too fast, that she needed to think. Jessica hadn’t taken it well.

She left furious, and even so…Rio didn’t go after her.

Now, driving, the thought slipped through inevitably. Agatha. The name came before she even realized it. Rio tightened her grip slightly on the wheel, shifting her attention back to the road ahead.

She parked at the mall shortly after, in the VIP customer area.

The environment was quiet, distant from the chaos of the rest of the parking lot. She stepped out of the car with the same impeccable posture as always, closing the door with a precise motion before heading inside.

The mall was already busy, but still organized—lit storefronts, people walking at different paces, low conversations blending with the ambient sound.

Rio walked without hurry until she stopped in front of a bookstore and went in.

The smell of paper and ink greeted her immediately. It was a calmer environment inside, isolated from the rest of the mall. Tall, organized shelves stretched through well-lit aisles.

Rio walked between them, passing fiction, biographies, business…

Until she found a more discreet section about family and parenthood.

Her eyes scanned the titles, analyzing, choosing with the same precision she used for anything else important. One book caught her attention first:

“Alpha Instinct: Protection, Bonding, and Leadership in Early Childhood”

Rio picked it up and flipped through it quickly, then another beside it:

“Between Two Hearts: How to Support an Omega During Pregnancy”

She ran her finger over the cover before taking it as well.

One more:

“Practical Guide for Alphas: From Discovery to the Baby’s First Year”

And then a fourth, a bit more technical:

“Primary Bonds: Emotional Development in High-Risk Pregnancies” Rio gathered the four books in her arms. For a moment, she stood there, looking at the covers as if they said more about her than any other recent choice.

Then she turned and went to the register. The clerk smiled politely, scanning the items without asking questions. Rio paid without hesitation, taking the bag right after.

After choosing the books, Rio stepped up to the counter with all four copies resting in her arms, her expression still controlled, but with something different underneath—a lighter tension.

The clerk smiled as soon as she approached.

“Is that all?”

Rio nodded.

“Yes.” There was a brief pause as the woman scanned the codes. Then she looked up again, studying Rio with a discreet, knowing smile.

“First time?”

Rio raised an eyebrow slightly, a minimal smile appearing, involuntary.

“Do I give it away that much?”

The clerk let out a soft laugh.

“Well… four books all at once,” she said, amused. “You seem pretty eager to learn. Not all alphas are this engaged in their partner’s pregnancy.”

She tilted her head slightly, still smiling.

“She’s lucky.” For a moment, Rio didn’t respond. Her gaze dropped briefly to the books, and something softer passed through her expression.

“Thank you…” she said, quieter, almost shy. The clerk finished ringing up the purchase.

“That’ll be this much.”

Rio didn’t hesitate. She simply took her card and tapped the machine, completing the payment with her usual precision. As she arranged the books in the bag, the woman spoke again, now with a calmer tone.

“As an omega who’s had three children…” she began, resting her hands lightly on the counter, “take it slow. Everything is a process.”

Rio looked up.

“In the end, everything will be alright… if you love each other above all else.” There was a small silence. Rio nodded slowly.

“Thank you for the advice.”She took the bag and left.

The movement of the mall surrounded her again, but this time she didn’t stop. She walked for a few more minutes, without a clear destination, until her steps slowed in front of a different storefront.

A baby store. Rio stood there for a second before going in.

The environment was softer inside—light colors, delicate lighting, the kind of place that seemed to slow time. Small clothes carefully arranged, tiny shoes, perfectly folded blankets.

“Welcome,” said a sales associate, approaching with a gentle smile. “Can I help you?”

Rio shook her head slightly.

“No… I’m just looking. I don’t know exactly what I want yet.”

“Feel free,” the woman replied, stepping away.

Rio began to walk between racks and shelves, running her fingers over fabrics that were too small, too light. Tiny dresses. Onesies. Socks so small they barely seemed real.

And then it came—that feeling. That bad thing. A strange, tight emotion, mixed with something heavier.

Guilt.

She wasn’t there. Not when she should have been. Rio had missed the first ultrasounds, the nausea, the hard days—and everything she could have shared with Agatha… everything she could have helped with.

Rio closed her eyes briefly for a second. She wasn’t going to miss the rest. Not anymore.

When she opened them again, her attention was drawn to a small detail on one of the lower shelves.

Two pairs of tiny shoes sat side by side, too small to even seem real, delicately finished, with little embroidered bunnies on the front—one in a soft blue shade, the other in pale yellow.

Rio stopped in front of them for a few seconds, watching in silence. Her gaze shifted from one to the other as if, somehow, she needed to choose. But there wasn’t enough logic there to sustain a decision.

In the end, she simply reached out.

And took both. Without thinking much further, she walked to the register with the tiny shoes in her hands, the silent decision saying more than any words could in that moment.



◆

 

One week later, Agatha returned to the company. The movement on the floor seemed the same as always — people walking back and forth, quiet conversations, the constant sound of keyboards and phones — and yet there was something different in the way she moved through that space. 

More aware. More attentive. Even so, she kept her pace, greeting a few employees along the way with small nods and restrained smiles.

That was when she saw her. Rio was already there.

Standing near the meeting area, talking to the head of marketing — Daniel Moretti — with that same impeccable posture as always, serious expression, low and controlled voice. The moment her eyes met Agatha’s, the conversation was interrupted.

“Miss Harkness, good morning.”

Agatha approached.

“Good morning.” Rio gave a slight nod, dismissing Daniel naturally before taking a few steps toward Agatha.

“How are you feeling?”

There was a small pause between the question and the answer.

“Good,” Agatha said, firmly. “I’m ready to get back to work.”

Rio nodded.

“Good. I need you to come to my office.” Agatha agreed with a nod, and the two walked down the hallway together. As soon as they entered, Rio got straight to the point, moving around the desk as she spoke.

“The company is starting a partnership with a preservative-free food brand. It’s an important campaign for the next quarter.”

Agatha remained standing for a moment before sitting down, listening attentively, already in work mode.

“I see.” Rio continued, picking up some documents.

“Because of this, new guidelines have been implemented. Starting today, the consumption of ultra-processed foods is prohibited within the building.”

Agatha blinked, surprised.

“What do you mean… prohibited?”

“The company will provide proper meals in the internal restaurant,” Rio added naturally, as if stating something completely logical.

Agatha looked at the papers, still processing, but signed them anyway.

“All this… because of a campaign?” Rio raised her gaze, calm.

“Time and money are also investments, Miss Harkness.”

Agatha held back a small smile, shaking her head lightly. “What a tyrant… controlling even food.”

“Alright,” she said finally. “I’ll check this week’s schedule.”

“Do that,” Rio replied. “And get ready. We’ll be traveling on Friday.” Agatha stopped.

“We will?”

“A business conference in Chicago,” Rio explained. “I need you to accompany me.”

Agatha nodded.

“Alright.”

“It’s nothing major,” Rio continued, resting a hand on the desk. “Just… an important event. Full of annoying people.”

That drew a small smile from Agatha.

“Sounds promising.”

“You may be more relaxed during the event, if you want,” Rio added, her tone slightly softer.

Agatha nodded again.

“I’ll organize everything then… including clothes. I don’t think I have anything appropriate.”

Rio didn’t respond immediately. She simply opened her wallet calmly and took out a card, extending it toward her.

“Take care of that.”

Agatha looked at the card, surprised.

“I can’t accept this.”

“It’s part of the job,” Rio said simply. “You can’t show up wearing just anything.”

Agatha let out a small laugh.

“Okay… you win.”

She took the card, still finding it slightly absurd, and stood up.

“I’ll check the schedule.”Rio nodded.

“Do that.”

Agatha left the room, and the door closed behind her. For a moment, silence took over the space.

And then, alone, Rio turned slightly, letting a small, contained smile slip — quick, almost imperceptible — before returning to her usual neutral expression.

At the end of the workday, Agatha decided not to go straight home.

She took the subway and headed to SoHo, where the storefronts looked more like works of art than actual shops. The neighborhood was illuminated, elegant, full of well-dressed people moving naturally through an environment that… definitely wasn’t hers.

Earlier during her break, she had looked up the event’s website. And that had made one thing very clear — she had absolutely nothing appropriate.

Now, walking among those stores, that only became more obvious. Everything there was… too luxurious. Too polished. Too expensive. And still, she took a deep breath and kept going.

Rio’s card was in her bag.

And whether she liked it or not… she had a job to do. Agatha stopped in front of a specific display window, and the dress caught her attention immediately.

Red.

Long.

Impeccable fit.

Shoulders exposed, elegant in a dangerous way, as if it had been made to draw attention effortlessly. She bit her lip lightly.

“Okay…” she murmured to herself. “Maybe this will work.”

Without overthinking, she walked into the store.

The environment was quiet, refined, with soft lighting and that subtle scent of expensive perfume in the air. A saleswoman approached immediately, with a polite… but slightly too rehearsed smile.

“Good evening, may I help you?”

“Good evening,” Agatha replied with a small smile. “I’d like to see the dress in the window. The red one.”

The saleswoman glanced briefly at the mannequin, then back at Agatha. The smile didn’t disappear, but something in her expression shifted slightly.

“Of course…” she said, slowly. “But due to store policy, I’ll need you to present a card first. It’s a piece from an exclusive designer.”

Agatha didn’t find it strange and simply nodded.

“No problem.”

She opened her bag, took out her wallet, and retrieved Rio’s card, handing it over naturally. The saleswoman took it. Looked at it… and then looked again, for a few seconds longer than necessary.

“I’ll be right back,” she said, returning a smile that now seemed even more rigid.

“Thank you.”
