Agatha stepped aside a bit, starting to look at other items while she waited. She passed a shelf of bags, then shoes — high heels, elegant, completely outside her usual routine.

Minutes passed. Too many. And then, two men appeared — tall, with closed-off expressions — the saleswoman right behind them.

And she pointed directly at Agatha.

“There’s the thief.” The world seemed to freeze for a second.

“What?” Agatha turned quickly, confused. “This must be a mistake—”

The world seemed to freeze for a second. She didn’t finish.

The security guards were already on her.

They grabbed her arms firmly, leaving no room for reaction.

“Hey—wait!” Agatha tried to pull free. “You’re making a mistake!”

“Ma’am, cooperate,” one of them said coldly.

“I’m pregnant, you can’t—!” It didn’t matter.

They forcefully escorted her to a back room, completely ignoring any attempt at explanation. The door was opened roughly, and she was pushed inside.

Before she could react, one of the men pulled out a pair of handcuffs.

“This is ridiculous, you can’t do this!” her voice was already edged with panic.

The cold metal snapped shut around her wrist, secured to a pipe with no way out, the other guard crossed his arms, staring at her with suspicion.

“Did you really think you could go shopping with a stolen card in a place like this?”

Agatha was breathing fast now, her heart racing.

“It’s not stolen!” she shot back immediately. “Rio Vidal is my boss!”

The two exchanged a look  and laughed.

Without humor. Without believing a single word. One of them pulled the card from his pocket and lifted it slightly.

“Do you have any idea how many people in this country have one of these?”

Agatha fell silent for a second.

“Six,” he continued. “And Mrs. Rio Vidal is one of them.” The other one was already pulling out his phone.

“We’re calling the police.”

He paused, looking at her with faint disdain.

“And Mrs. Vidal herself.” Agatha felt her stomach drop.

“Just to make sure,” he added, “that you’re punished the way you should be.”

The silence that followed felt crushing.

A few minutes later, the sound of the main door opening harder than usual broke the store’s false calm.

Firm footsteps echoed across the polished floor before anyone even announced her presence, and when Rio Vidal entered accompanied by two police officers, the atmosphere shifted instantly.

She didn’t look angry. She looked… dangerous.

Her gaze swept the room with precision, tense, far too alert, like someone already prepared for the worst possible scenario.

Concern came before anger — the possibility that Agatha had been robbed, that something had happened to her, still echoed too strongly in Rio’s chest for any other reaction to come first.

The store manager approached quickly, smiling with presumptuous confidence.

“Mrs. Vidal, I’m so glad you came in person. We managed to act in time,” he said, with a certain pride. “We caught a thief using a card—”

Rio didn’t hear the rest.

Because in the back, one of the guards was opening the handcuff, and Agatha was there. Restrained, being treated like a criminal.

Rio’s world flipped in a second.

“What are you doing?!” her voice cut through the room, firm, loud, carrying an authority that left no room for questioning.

Everyone froze.

“She works for me,” she continued, stepping forward. “I authorized the use of that card!”

The silence that followed was absolute. The manager lost all color, the saleswoman froze, the guards exchanged looks, now visibly unsettled.

“Let her go. Now.”

The guard hurried, his hands less steady this time as he removed the cuff from Agatha’s wrist.

“Ma’am, we… we’re very sorry, there was a misunderstanding—”

“Unacceptable,” Rio cut in, cold. “It is absolutely unacceptable to treat a client this way.”

The saleswoman tried to speak, stammering.

“I… I thought—” Rio didn’t even look at her, her focus had already shifted.

“Agatha.” Her voice was lower now, but still firm.

“What do you want to do?”

Agatha took a deep breath, still feeling her wrist throb faintly where it had been restrained. Her gaze moved quickly over everyone around — the embarrassment, the tension, the curious looks — before returning to Rio.

She exhaled slowly.

“I… just want to try on the red dress.” For a second, there was silence.

Then Rio nodded, without hesitation.

“Bring the red dress. Now.”

She turned and walked to one of the store’s armchairs, sitting down elegantly, crossing her legs as if the place belonged to her — and, in that moment, in a way, it did.

“Agatha,” she added, looking directly at her. “Make yourself comfortable. Take whatever you want.”

A brief pause.

“I’m not leaving.” Agatha let out an almost imperceptible breath, still processing everything, but nodded slightly

The saleswoman returned in a hurry, holding the dress with reverent care this time.

“The fitting room is this way…” Agatha took the piece.

“Thank you.” And walked toward the fitting rooms, still feeling her heart racing, but now for completely different reasons.

Meanwhile, Rio already had her phone in hand, as if nothing had happened, making calls and handling business, but her gaze… her gaze remained attentive, subtly fixed in the direction Agatha had gone.

A few minutes later, the soft sound of the fitting room curtain opening drew attention in an environment that still felt far too tense for a store of that level. Agatha appeared slowly, adjusting the fabric at her side by reflex, as if still getting used to the way it fell on her body.

The red dress looked like it had been made for her.

The fabric flowed precisely along her body, tracing her curves elegantly, without excess, enhancing every line naturally.

Her bare shoulders gave her a delicate, yet confident air, while the long cut elongated her silhouette with a quiet authority. The red wasn’t loud — it was deep, sophisticated, dangerous.

For a full second, the room fell silent.

And Rio… just looked.

Her gaze traveled over Agatha in a restrained way, but impossible to fully hide. There was something there — it wasn’t just aesthetic approval, it wasn’t just professional evaluation. It was deeper. More… personal. Something she quickly tried to reorganize within herself.

Even so, she stood.

“It’s perfect.”

The statement came firm, leaving no room for doubt. Agatha let out a small, shy smile.

“I like it too.”

Rio stepped a little closer, analyzing the fit with technical attention — or at least trying to appear technical.

“Have you looked at shoes and jewelry?”

Agatha shook her head lightly.

“Not yet.”

Rio immediately turned her head toward the rest of the store, and the shift in posture was theatrical.

“Impressive,” she began, crossing her arms. “Completely disorganized service, flawed, embarrassing… and yet no one had the initiative to offer a complete set to a client.”

The manager swallowed hard, the saleswoman nearly collapsed.

Agatha bit the corner of her lip, clearly holding back a laugh.

“Mrs. Vidal, we can arrange that immediately—”

“I would hope so,” Rio interrupted, sitting down again with exaggerated elegance, as if evaluating a mediocre performance. “Because so far, the experience has been… disappointing.”

Agatha looked away, laughing softly, and then as if by magic, boxes started appearing.

Shoes being brought, jewelry arranged on trays, options presented with a speed that bordered on desperation.

The contrast with the previous treatment was almost comical. Agatha stepped closer, still wearing the dress, observing the options.

She chose a pair of heels — delicate, elegant, complementing the dress without competing with it. Then a few more subtle pieces of jewelry, still sophisticated enough for the event.

Rio watched everything, attentive and silent this time.

“And don’t limit yourself to that,” she said after a moment, naturally.

“You also need clothes for the day-to-day during the trip.”

Agatha looked at her, raising an eyebrow slightly.

“Are you sure?”

“Absolutely.”

There was a short pause, and then Agatha gave in.

She began selecting other pieces — lighter dresses, but still elegant, well-tailored sets, fluid fabrics that enhanced her body without looking too formal. Nothing excessive, but everything… far too beautiful for what she usually wore.

As the choices piled up, the atmosphere in the store shifted completely.

Now there was no more suspicion, only efficiency, and an exaggerated care not to make another mistake.

Agatha, between one choice and another, ended up laughing more than once at the absurdity of the complete turnaround, at Rio’s dramatic manner, and at the number of boxes that kept appearing nonstop.

And Rio watched calmly now… but still unable to fully look away from her.

When the shopping was finally done, the entire store seemed to revolve around Rio as if her every step were part of a silent protocol. The manager personally reappeared to finalize the payment, visibly more careful, organizing everything with impeccable speed.

Rio didn’t hesitate.

She paid directly, without even looking at the amount for more than a second, as if it were irrelevant. As soon as the process was finished, she took out another card — black, discreet, even more intimidating — and handed it to the manager.

“My lawyer.” The man took it, confused.

“I’ve already contacted him,” Rio continued, her voice low, controlled. “You are being sued. And so is the store.”

The silence that followed was uncomfortable, the manager opened his mouth, but no words came out.

Rio didn’t wait for a response.

She turned.

Agatha was standing a few steps behind, holding several bags, still a bit dazed from everything that had happened. Before she could react, Rio approached and, with a natural gesture, took most of them from her hands.

“Let’s go.”

Agatha simply nodded, walking out right behind her. Outside, the air felt lighter, but the tension still lingered faintly between them as they walked toward the car.

“Are you really going to sue them?” Agatha asked, glancing sideways.

Rio gave her a brief look.

“Of course. They were prejudiced and aggressive toward you.”

A short pause.

“And I sue a lot of people.”

Agatha let out a laugh, shaking her head.

“Of course you do.”

When they reached the car, Rio stopped and made a small gesture with her chin.

“Can you grab the key from my pocket?”

Agatha hesitated for a second.

“Excuse me…” She stepped closer, her hand sliding carefully into the pocket of Rio’s coat. The touch was brief, but enough to make Rio hold her breath for an imperceptible moment, a faint shiver running up her spine, involuntary, unexpected — and she hated how much it affected her.

But she said nothing.

Agatha found the key, stepping back right after and pressing the button. The trunk unlocked with a soft click, opening automatically.

Rio placed the bags inside calmly and, at the end, pressed the control again, closing the compartment with precision.

Agatha smiled, slightly impressed.

“Do you want a ride?” Rio asked, already walking around the car.

Agatha crossed her arms lightly, with an amused smile.

“Do I have a choice? You kidnapped my bags.”

Rio raised an eyebrow, playing along.

“Well… would you look at that, Miss Harkness. Always getting me into trouble.”

Agatha let out a quiet laugh.

“You handled it pretty easily in the end.”

Rio sighed, resting her hand on the door before getting in.

“Yeah…” she said distractedly. “If every time you use one of my cards they decide to arrest you, I think I might have to marry you, Miss.”

The silence came quickly. Agatha blushed immediately, looking down at the ground, completely caught off guard.

And Rio… noticed right away.

“I’m sorry,” she said, faster than she intended. “That was… inappropriate.”

Agatha shook her head, still avoiding looking directly at her.

“No… it’s fine.” A pause.

“I’m just tired.”

Rio watched her for a second longer than she should have, as if trying to read something beyond those words, then nodded.

“I’ll take you home.”

And this time, there was no room for jokes.

 

Chapter 8: Suspicions

Chapter Notes

GUYS I literally can’t thank you enough for all the love in the comments, it makes me so happy that you’re enjoying my story 😭💖

also sorry for any grammar mistakes, english isn’t my first language 🫶

LADIES, I present to you: THE adultery. GOOD READING

 

 

┕──━──━─┑◆┍─━──━──┙

The porcelain clinked softly as the cup was placed onto the saucer, the aroma of tea rising in delicate spirals through the heavy air of the room. The woman seated on the sofa kept her hands firmly wrapped around the cup for a few seconds, as if searching for some kind of stability there before speaking.

"I don’t know what happened…" Jessica’s voice came out lower than she intended; there was frustration there, and something close to insecurity.

She sighed, shifting her gaze to the pale liquid inside the cup.

"I don’t know what happened…" she continued, now more firmly. "Rio was so focused on the wedding. Everything was… on track."

Across the room, Rita settled into the armchair with her usual rigid elegance, movements controlled, calculated. Her eyes, far too attentive, analyzed every nuance of Jessica’s expression as if it were just another game to be understood.

"Something changed," she said simply.

Her voice was calm, but it carried a weight that allowed no argument.

Jessica lifted her gaze.

Rita tilted her head slightly.

"And you can’t give up because of that."

A brief pause followed.

"Your family owes a great deal to the Vidals."

The sentence fell into the room like a reminder that needed no explanation. Jessica nodded, tightening her fingers slightly around the cup.

"I know." She took a deep breath, organizing her thoughts.

"And I won’t give up. I’ll win back Rio’s attention."

Rita watched her for a few more seconds, evaluating.

"And what is your theory?" she asked then, narrowing her eyes slightly. "For this… distance."

Jessica hesitated; for a moment, she seemed to consider the possibility.

"I don’t know," she admitted at last. "But… it doesn’t seem like her."

Rita let out a low, humorless laugh.

"Clearly, omegas like you don’t understand the mind of an alpha." The comment came like a cut. Jessica stiffened slightly but did not respond. Rita continued, resting her hands calmly on her lap.

"You’re being naive." A brief silence settled. "If you want to keep Rio by your side, you need to use… strategies."

The word was chosen carefully, but the meaning was clear. Jessica lowered her gaze for a second, absorbing it.

"I’ll fix this. I will marry her." Rita held her gaze for another moment, as if weighing the promise. Then she leaned back into the chair, satisfied—just enough.

"I hope so."

◆

The black car came to a smooth stop in the airport drop-off area, far from the usual passenger flow, in a reserved lane where everything felt quieter, more organized, isolated from the rest of the world. Before Agatha could even touch the handle, the driver had already circled the vehicle and opened the door for her.

"Miss Harkness."

Agatha nodded with a small polite smile and stepped out, adjusting the black dress she wore—it was classic, elegant, with a clean cut that echoed iconic haute couture pieces. Simple at first glance, but expensive enough to be recognized by those who knew.

The morning air was fresh; the omega took a deep breath, trying to ground herself.

The driver was already removing her luggage from the trunk, placing everything carefully onto an impeccably clean metal cart.

"Thank you, I can—" Agatha began, extending a hand to help.

"That won’t be necessary," he interrupted with professional politeness. "Just follow me."

Agatha raised her eyebrows slightly, but nodded.

They walked together, and within minutes, along a path Agatha didn’t recognize, the common airport was left behind—replaced by quieter corridors, indirect lighting, fewer people—and the few present clearly belonged to the same world as Rio.

They continued to a more discreet, hidden entrance, where two well-dressed security guards stood by a dark glass door.

The driver presented a card, and the guards exchanged a quick glance before opening the door immediately.

Agatha stepped inside—and paused for a second. The space was on another level. Nothing resembled an airport. It felt closer to a five-star hotel lounge—no, better. Wide light leather sofas, marble tables, warm low lighting, discreet artworks on the walls, a comfortable silence broken only by quiet conversations and the occasional clink of fine china.

It was exclusive. Private.

And then she saw Rio.

Seated elegantly on one of the sofas, legs crossed, a magazine open in her hands. Her posture, as always, impeccable; her hair perfectly arranged, as if this were just another ordinary morning.

Rio lifted her eyes and smiled faintly.

"Miss Harkness." Agatha approached.

"Mrs. Vidal."

"Did you sleep well?" Rio asked, closing the magazine calmly. "I know you had to wake up early."

Agatha nodded.

"I managed to rest a bit."

Rio tilted her head slightly.

"You can rest better on the jet."

"We’re taking a jet?" Agatha blinked.

Rio looked genuinely confused by the surprise.

"Yes. I don’t take commercial flights." As if it were the most obvious thing in the world.

Agatha let out a small "right," trying not to show how much it still surprised her. Rio then made a light gesture with her hand.

"Make yourself comfortable. You can have something to eat before we leave."

Agatha didn’t need more encouragement.

The buffet was set up at the back, and it was… absurd.

Perfectly cut fruits arranged like artwork. Artisan breads, flaky croissants, imported cheeses, smoked salmon, small portions of carefully plated hot dishes. Fresh juices, specialty coffees, teas in elegant containers. Everything impeccable.

And everything… expensive. Agatha bit her lip for a second.

Then she went to the buffet, with a hunger she was no longer pretending didn’t exist, and began assembling a plate—a bit of everything, without much guilt. Fruits, bread, something more substantial, something sweet. Then she went back for more.

And more.

And when she finally sat at the table, she let out a satisfied sigh, taking the first bite with a relieved expression.

A few minutes later, Rio approached carrying a plate—visibly more modest. Some neatly cut fruits, a small piece of bread, and a cup of coffee. Nothing compared to the small "mountain" Agatha had built for herself.

Agatha noticed and blushed slightly. Rio noticed immediately—and found it… adorable.

"Are you enjoying the food?" she asked, sitting calmly across from her.

Agatha nodded, still chewing.

"Very much, I… was hungry."

Rio tilted her head slightly, watching her for a second longer than necessary.

"Then enjoy it."

Agatha resumed eating more comfortably, though every now and then she cast a quick glance at Rio’s more discreet plate, as if she were being mildly indecent. Rio, on the other hand, sipped her coffee calmly, eating small portions of fruit—but behind her controlled posture, there was something different.

She watched silently.

Every bite, every small satisfied sigh—and inevitably, the thought came: the baby. She had read in one of the books she bought that it was a normal reaction. Rio had been feeling all the responses of a typical alpha who coexists with the omega carrying her pup, and she felt more at ease when she realized she wasn’t losing control for no reason.

And seeing Agatha eat well brought a strange kind of relief.

Minutes later, a staff member approached discreetly, informing them that the aircraft was ready for boarding. The two stood and walked together. Outside, the black Vidal company jet awaited, imposing on the private runway. Its flawless finish reflected the morning light, and even the boarding stairs looked more elegant than they should.

Agatha climbed first, and upon entering, paused for a moment—the interior was surreal.

Wide cream leather seats arranged comfortably, like a moving living room. Polished dark wood details, soft embedded lighting, perfectly integrated retractable tables. A discreet bar at the back, compartments sealed with precision—everything minimalistic and absurdly luxurious.

Agatha walked to a window seat and sat down, immediately leaning slightly to look outside. The runway, the other planes—everything looked different from that angle.

Rio sat across from her.

Watching.

Agatha pulled the seatbelt and fastened it carefully, subtly adjusting it so it wouldn’t press against her stomach. The gesture was small—but enough to make Rio shiver, her alpha instincts fighting the urge to kneel and listen to her pup resting inside Agatha’s womb.

Rio saw all the care Agatha had for that pup, and fighting her instincts, she pretended she hadn’t noticed anything.

A well-dressed man—clearly the head of the crew—approached with a professional posture.

"Mrs. Vidal, we will depart in a few minutes. Estimated flight time to Chicago is approximately one hour. Weather conditions are stable."

"Thank you." Rio nodded, and the man stepped away.

◆

Their arrival at The Peninsula Chicago carried the same silent precision that seemed to follow everything in Rio’s life. The car glided to the main entrance without a sound, and before the engine was even fully turned off, staff were already in position—aligned, attentive—as if anticipating every movement before it existed.

The door was opened carefully.

Agatha stepped out first, her heel touching the smooth stone floor, the black dress fitted to her body catching the soft light at the entrance. Chicago’s cold air wrapped around her for a brief, sharp moment… and then it was gone.

As soon as they crossed the revolving doors, the world changed.

The interior absorbed everything. The lobby was vast, imposing without excess. The high ceiling made the space feel even larger, while sophisticated chandeliers cast a soft, intimate golden light over polished marble that reflected each step with silent perfection. Giant floral arrangements occupied strategic points.

There was a scent in the air—subtle and refined—white flowers, light wood, something almost imperceptible… yet impossible to completely ignore.

Agatha walked beside Rio, trying to maintain a neutral, professional posture—but her eyes betrayed her, scanning the space, failing to hide the impact.

Her luggage was no longer a concern. At some invisible point in Rio’s flawless logistics, everything had already been handled. No tags, no effort, no delay. As if practical problems simply didn’t exist in that world.

Rio walked straight to the reception, unhurried.

"Rio Vidal."

That was all she said.

The receptionist immediately straightened.

"Mrs. Vidal, your suites are ready."

The reply came quickly, without questions—as it should.

Rio nodded slightly.

"Thank you."

As she turned, the gesture came before the thought. Her hand lightly touched Agatha’s hip, guiding her toward the elevators with automatic ease. The contact was minimal—but enough. Agatha felt it, and so did Rio, who in the next second withdrew her hand, as if she had crossed a boundary she herself had not fully defined.

They walked in silence, the sound of their steps disappearing into the thick carpet of the corridor leading to the elevators. The doors closed behind them.

Agatha let out a soft sigh, shifting her weight slightly—and Rio noticed immediately.

"Are you alright?"

"Just… a bit of back pain." Agatha nodded, discreetly placing a hand on her back.

Rio frowned briefly—a quick reflex.

"Rest when we get there. Take a bath… the tub should help."

Agatha nodded again.

"I will."

The doors opened. The hallway on their floor was silent in an absolute way. The carpet absorbed any sound, embedded lights created a smooth, continuous path, and even the air felt more controlled there.

There were two doors, side by side.

"The suites are connected by a living room," Rio explained, gesturing lightly. "But you have complete privacy."

Agatha nodded, bringing the card close to the door.

"See you later."

"See you."

The door opened with a soft click, and Agatha stepped inside. Rio remained still for a second longer—just enough to feel the silence fill the space where she stood. Then she took a deep breath.

And entered her own room.

The environment revealed itself slowly as she activated the controls. The curtains opened in a smooth, silent motion, allowing natural light to flood the space and reveal the city outside—Chicago stretching in lines of glass and steel, distant and imposing.

The room was spacious, sophisticated, impeccable. Neutral tones, soft textures, a large perfectly made bed, discreet designer furniture—everything designed for absolute comfort without losing elegance.

But Rio didn’t stop to take it in. She went straight to the closet, removing her travel clothes and replacing them with impeccably cut sweatpants and a comfortable sweater—soft, still too elegant to be truly casual.

It was a less armored version of her, but still controlled.

She returned to the living area, sitting in the armchair with her MacBook on her lap, and opened it.

Emails organized themselves on the screen with habitual precision, forming a logical sequence Rio knew far too well to need to think about. Administrative reports, surgical updates, internal requests, schedule confirmations—everything followed an expected flow, comforting in its predictability.

She moved through each message, replying when necessary, archiving the irrelevant. Still, among those ordinary messages, there was a small deviation.

Subtle—but impossible to ignore.

A set of attached files that did not belong to that routine. They were not institutional reports or official communications. They were detailed medical records, with restricted access levels, protected by protocols that under normal circumstances would never allow them to reach her.

But Rio Vidal did not live under normal circumstances.

The influence she carried — it went beyond formal positions — was enough to open silent pathways. No one had said “no.” No one ever really did. And even if there had been resistance, she would have known how to work around it. It wasn’t right, she knew that with clinical clarity. It was a direct violation of Agatha’s privacy.

And yet, she opened it.

Without truly hesitating.

The cursor hovered for less than a second before the click.

The screen changed, and the cold light of the monitor illuminated her face more intensely, highlighting the slight tightening of her jaw, the absolute focus that emerged whenever something demanded her full attention. At first, her gaze moved through the information with the same technical detachment she applied to any medical record: clinical data, medical notes, follow-up observations.

Until the image appeared.

The ultrasound occupied the center of the screen, grainy, difficult to read for untrained eyes, but immediately understandable to someone with Rio’s background. She leaned slightly forward, her eyes adjusting focus, analyzing the contours, the still-developing shapes, the visual rhythm that revealed life even before any conscious interpretation.

Until Rio read the report, and then the technical reasoning failed — the information was there, far too clear for any ambiguity: twin pregnancy.

The effect was immediate, even if silent. Rio’s body remained still, but something inside her disorganized abruptly, as if an internal structure — rigid, built over years — had been struck at an exact breaking point.

Her breathing, previously controlled, caught for a second too long, enough for the impact to settle before any defense mechanism could act.

It wasn’t just the idea of one child — it was the multiplication of that, the complete absence of planning, the fact that it existed independently of her will.

Her mind tried to rationalize, to fit it into numbers, probabilities, manageable consequences. But there was no distance for that. No coldness sufficient in that moment. What was before her was not a concept — it was a fact already in motion, growing, evolving, existing without her having authorized, foreseen, or controlled it.

Her hand slowly closed around the notebook, fingers pressing against the edge with more force than necessary, as if physical contact could stabilize something that was clearly no longer stable. Her gaze remained fixed on the screen, revisiting the image, the report, the details that confirmed it again and again, as if at some point she might find a mistake.

She didn’t, because regardless of what she did from that point on, it was already in motion — and this time, it wasn’t something she could simply end, ignore, or rearrange in her own way.

◆

Night arrived slowly, taking over the suite with dimmer, more intimate light, while the city outside began to glow in golden and cold tones. Inside the room, Agatha stood before the mirror, adjusting the final details while the phone propped in front of her kept the video call open.

Wanda and Jen filled the screen, attentive and eager.

"Come on, Agatha, turn around," Wanda insisted, resting her chin on her hand, impatient.

Agatha rolled her eyes, but obeyed. She stepped back, allowing her whole body into frame, then turned slowly, the red fabric following the movement with fluid elegance.

Deep red, fitted perfectly to her body, enhancing every curve with precision while still hiding her belly. Her bare shoulders exposed her skin in an elegant way, and the long fall created a silhouette that seemed designed to be watched.

Her hair was pulled up high, a clean, sophisticated style that elongated her posture even more.

"My God…" Wanda covered her mouth. Jen widened her eyes.

"Agatha, you look ridiculously gorgeous."

Agatha let out a soft, slightly embarrassed laugh, glancing away for a second.

"Okay, you two are dramatic."

"No, we’re honest," Wanda shot back immediately. "If no one falls in love with you tonight, that person is dead inside."

Agatha was about to respond, but a soft knock on the door interrupted the moment. She turned toward the sound, her heart giving a small, inexplicable jump.

"I have to go."

"Go shine," Jen smiled.

"And send pictures later," Wanda added.

"I will," Agatha said, ending the call. The room immediately felt quieter.

She picked up her small purse, checked her reflection one last time, and took a deep breath, as if preparing for something bigger than just an event.

Then she stepped out.

The hallway seemed quieter at that hour, soft lighting following her steps until she reached the connecting living room.

Rio was there, standing, on the phone. Her posture impeccable, her voice low, firm, speaking about numbers, deals, something clearly important. The suit she wore was perfectly tailored, dark, elegant, with a subtle slit that broke the formality almost imperceptibly.

Her tied hair completed the sophisticated and dangerous image.

Agatha paused for a second, watching — then Rio’s eyes lifted and met hers. The sentence she had been saying died midway.

A brief silence formed on the other end of the line before Rio, still looking at Agatha, said calmly:

"I’ll call back later."

She hung up without looking away.

The silence between them shifted.

Agatha stepped forward slightly, adjusting her purse in her hands.

"I’m ready."

Rio still watched her, as if processing the image before her more carefully than usual. Her gaze moved — inevitably — over the dress, the hair, the whole composition…

"You look… beautiful."

Her voice came out lower than usual.

"Thank you." Agatha smiled lightly, shy. "You too."

Rio blinked, as if the response had come too quickly, and looked away just slightly, recomposing herself.

"The driver is waiting."

Agatha nodded.

"Right."

Side by side, they walked toward the exit, their footsteps accompanying the heavy silence growing between them.

The car slowed as it approached the event venue, and even before it came to a complete stop, it was already possible to grasp the scale of it. Lights, movement, overlapping voices, flashes cutting through the night at constant intervals — the main entrance was crowded with photographers, journalists, and guests being announced as if each arrival were a small spectacle.

Security organized the flow, creating a corridor between the public and the guests, while important names were called out loud, accompanied by cameras that missed nothing.

Agatha watched through the window, feeling her heart beat a little faster. It wasn’t the kind of environment she was used to.

Beside her, Rio seemed… indifferent, as if it were just another formality.

"Go through the side," Rio told the driver, her voice calm.

The car veered off.

The main entrance was left behind.

And with it, the noise faded as well.

Agatha frowned slightly, her gaze still lingering for a second on the receding lights. A thought came too quickly, before she could filter it.

She doesn’t want to be seen with me.

The idea came with a small tightening in her chest, imperceptible on the outside, but enough to make her shrink slightly into her seat.

The car stopped at a much more discreet entrance, protected, with less movement, but still highly monitored. Security approached immediately, recognizing Rio without the need for introduction.

The doors were opened, and the two stepped out. The atmosphere there was more contained, but no less sophisticated. A reserved access point, where only selected guests passed, far from the spectacle of the main entrance.

At the reception, elegant attendants approached with identification bracelets, placing them on both of their wrists quickly and carefully.

"Good evening, Mrs. Vidal."

Rio nodded slightly. Agatha simply followed.

They walked through an elegant corridor, lit by indirect lighting, until they reached an area where a short stretch of carpet still allowed for more controlled photos — less chaotic, more selective. Rio turned slightly toward Agatha.

"Do you want to take a picture?"

Agatha blinked, surprised by the question, but the answer came along with the doubt still echoing in the back of her mind.

"Why didn’t we come in through the main entrance?"

Her tone came out neutral, but not entirely. Rio didn’t hesitate.

"It’s too crowded."

No further explanation.

Agatha nodded slowly, but inside, the feeling didn’t fully fade. There was something about the hormones, the recent instability, that made everything feel more intense, more personal than it might actually be.

She doesn’t want to be seen with me.

The thought returned, more insistent. But again, she said nothing. The omega simply adjusted her posture, took a deep breath, and walked beside Rio, crossing through the VIP entrance and into the event, trying to convince herself it meant nothing beyond practicality.

The event progressed as the hours passed, and the atmosphere gradually shifted into something more relaxed — or at least, a rehearsed version of it.

The music grew slightly louder, conversations broke into smaller groups, and glasses began circulating more frequently among hands no longer as concerned with formality.

Rio maintained her impeccable posture for most of the time, moving between businessmen, investors, and influential figures with her usual confidence. At a quieter moment, she leaned slightly toward Agatha.

"You can enjoy the night more freely today," she said softly. "No pressure."

Agatha nodded, surprised by the concession.

The omega initially followed Rio through a few conversations, staying by her side as important names were introduced, hands were shaken, and calculated smiles exchanged.

Most of them, alpha men, barely directed their gaze at Agatha beyond what was necessary — as if she were just a functional extension of Rio’s presence.

Invisible.

Or ignored by choice. And after a while, it became exhausting.

Agatha slipped away discreetly, walking through the hall with lighter steps, allowing herself to move without a specific destination. She stopped at a table of appetizers, sampling small portions without really paying attention to the taste, her eyes wandering across the room.

Everything felt staged — laughter too loud, interest calculated, a well-executed social choreography.

"I think it’s boring too."

The voice beside her came naturally.

Agatha turned her head.

The woman was elegant, with a strong presence, striking blue eyes contrasting with bold eyebrows that gave even more expression to her gaze. There was something about her — confidence, perhaps — that didn’t seem forced.

"Melina Vostokoff," she said, extending her hand slightly.

Agatha accepted.

"Agatha Harkness."

Melina smiled, studying her with genuine interest.

"I’ve never seen you around here."

"I came with Mrs. Vidal."

The reaction was immediate. A soft laugh escaped Melina.

"Oh… so you’re one of Vidal’s girls?"

Agatha kept her face neutral, but inside something tightened. “Vidal’s girls?” The term lingered, echoing uncomfortably.

"Just her secretary," she replied firmly. "And she’s engaged."

Melina tilted her head slightly, as if amused.

"That’s what they say… but they also say she postponed the wedding."

Agatha fell silent for a second. She hadn’t known that. The information hung in the air, uncomfortable.

"Well… I’m just here for work."

Melina smiled to the side.

"And tonight… are you free?"

Agatha hesitated just enough to notice it.

"In a way, yes."

"Then would you like to dance?"

The answer came before she could think too much.

"I would."

The main hall had a reserved space for dancing, where some couples were already moving to the sound of soft music. Melina made an exaggerated, theatrical bow, drawing a spontaneous laugh from Agatha before she accepted the offered hand.

The dance began lightly.

Melina led with ease, and the conversation flowed along with the movement.

"My family owns a real estate company," she commented casually. "Some good parts of the city… and others too."

"Sounds… boring," Agatha replied honestly.

Melina laughed.

"It is. But it pays well enough for us to pretend it isn’t."

Agatha smiled slightly.

"Fair."

The closeness, the rhythm of the music, the ease of the conversation… for a few minutes, everything felt simple.

Until it wasn’t.

"Three minutes," a firm voice cut through the space between them, "and you’ve already got your hands on my employee, Vostokoff."

Agatha recognized it before even looking. Melina smiled, not stepping away immediately.

"Your employee is adorable."

And then, with provocative ease, she leaned in and brought Agatha’s hand to her lips, placing a light kiss on her skin.

When Rio approached, the shift in energy was almost physical. She stopped close enough for Agatha to feel her presence before even processing the tension that came with it.

"Let’s go, Miss Harkness," she said, her tone too calm to be neutral. "The winds of Scotland brought trouble."

The reference made Melina laugh.

"Still dramatic, Vidal?"

The two exchanged a brief greeting, something clearly old, layered with history — a subtle mention of the boarding school in Scotland they had both attended.

But there was no affection there, only recognition. And something sharper.

Melina gave Agatha one last smile.

"It was a pleasure."

And then she walked away. Agatha remained there for a second, her body still adjusting after the dance, but her mind stuck on something else.

Vidal’s girls. The phrase came back, uncomfortable like a punch to the stomach. Beside her, Rio didn’t seem comfortable either.

Her expression was controlled, as always, but there was something different — more rigid, more restrained, as if that interaction had left a weight she didn’t intend to show.

The tension between them didn’t dissipate even when they left the brightly lit hall behind. It simply changed shape, becoming denser, quieter, harder to ignore.

They walked side by side to the exit without saying much, as if any poorly chosen word could ignite something bigger than they were ready to handle. The night air was cold when the car arrived, but neither of them seemed to notice.

Inside the car, the silence grew heavy. Agatha kept her gaze fixed on the window the entire way, watching the city lights blur as her reflection mixed with the glass.

Rio, on the other hand, stared straight ahead, her jaw slightly tight, her hands too firm on her own legs, as if she were holding something inside by force.

When the car finally stopped in front of the hotel, the driver quickly stepped out to open the door.

Agatha was the first to leave, not waiting, walking straight into the lobby as if she needed movement to avoid thinking. Rio followed right behind, maintaining the same controlled distance, but her gaze fixed on her.

The walk to the elevator was silent, but charged.

As soon as they stepped inside, the doors closed with a soft click, isolating them in a space too small for everything happening between them.

That was when Rio moved. Her hand wrapped around Agatha’s arm firmly enough to make her stop.

"Are you okay?"

Her voice came out lower than usual, but there was something there — real concern, undisguised.

Agatha nodded, without looking directly at her.

"I’m… just tired."

Rio watched her for a moment, as if not fully convinced.

"You’ve changed."

Agatha let out a small, humorless laugh as she stepped further into the elevator.

"So have you."

Rio frowned, bothered.

"I’m normal."

Agatha turned her head, finally looking at her, and there was something harder there now.

"No, you’re not."

The silence that followed was heavier than any argument. Rio took a breath before trying again.

"I just… don’t like Melina. It’s a long story."

Agatha’s laugh came low, edged with disdain.

"Funny. Because she also had an interesting story to tell… about ‘Vidal’s girls.’"

The elevator doors opened at that exact moment, but neither of them moved immediately.

Agatha continued:

"And apparently, I’m one of them."

Rio ran a hand through her hair, irritated.

"That’s ridiculous. That’s exactly why I don’t like her."

Agatha stepped out of the elevator, walking into the corridor, but stopped a few steps ahead and turned back.

"So it’s not true?"

Rio hesitated for a fraction of a second — and that was enough of an answer.

"I… dated a few secretaries. But that was a long time ago."

Agatha tilted her head slightly, analyzing.

"So it’s a habit."

"Not with you," Rio replied too quickly. "You’re not like them."

Agatha stepped closer, her eyes fixed on hers.

"I’m not?"

And then, lower, firmer:

"And still, Mrs. Vidal… you don’t have the right to be jealous of something that doesn’t belong to you."

Rio blinked, as if the words had struck directly.

"I’m not jealous."

Agatha arched an eyebrow slightly.

"Oh, really? Because it really looked like you wanted to rip that woman’s face off."

The distance between them closed without either of them fully realizing when it happened.

Rio woke to the movement of the blanket being pulled off her. Her eyes opened fully, finding Agatha standing there, wrapped in the sheet, her face slightly flushed, her breathing uneven — she was crying.

“Agatha? What is it?” she asked, sitting up, still confused.

“This is wrong, it can’t happen again, please.” Rio moved closer, wrapping her arms around her waist.

“Please, don’t think about that,” she asked, caressing her face. “You didn’t do anything wrong, you—”

“No, Rio! I know my fault in this, I need to go.” Rio’s hands held Agatha more firmly, and then Rio kissed her lips. Agatha’s tears still fell in cascades, guilt spilling over her skin.

“I don’t want to be the other woman anymore.” Agatha sniffled between the kiss. Rio felt a stab of guilt pierce through her, and she pulled Agatha into her arms, moving backward until she sat on the bed. Her face rested against the omega’s stomach, Agatha stood still, watching. “I don’t want to be the other woman, I don’t want to cry myself to sleep.”

Rio’s kisses along Agatha’s body intensified. Rio pulled at the blanket Agatha was wrapped in, seeking the warmth of the brunette’s body, the scent. The omega gasped at the contact, a sensation she had never felt before. Rio’s kisses trailed downward, reaching her pubis, the alpha’s nose brushing against the thick layer of dark hair covering Agatha’s mound.

“You’re not leaving,” the alpha said before burying her mouth in Agatha’s cunt. The brunette moaned, gripping Rio’s shoulders tightly, Rio’s tongue sliding between the omega’s folds, drawing a louder moan as tears that had been guilt turned into tears mixed with pleasure.

Rio gripped Agatha’s ass firmly, watching her stomach tighten as the peak approached. Discreetly, Rio kissed Agatha’s belly, and then finished working her core with intensity as Agatha’s orgasm hit violently against her mouth.

The omega pulled away, breathless, grabbing the sheet and turning to leave, entering her own room and closing the door. Rio sighed, collapsing back onto the bed. In her mind, there was no longer space for guilt, only for Agatha.

Hours passed without Agatha noticing. She stayed beneath the covers of her room, processing everything that had happened the night before.

When the clock struck noon, she finally found the courage to leave the bed and face the living area that connected the two rooms. There were traces of the previous night scattered around the space.

Agatha tightened the tie of her robe, deciding that now was not the time to deal with the mess. She walked to Rio’s room and pushed the door open slowly.

The bed was empty, as was the comfortable armchair. The omega stepped inside and walked slowly toward the bathroom door, which was slightly ajar. Rio was in the bathtub, one of her legs resting along the opposite edge. Her gaze slid toward Agatha.

“Hello, beautiful,” she said, now looking at the omega, who opened the door a bit more and stepped inside, closing it behind her.

“Can I come in?” she asked, gesturing toward the bathtub.

“You don’t need to ask,” Rio replied. Agatha nodded and asked her to move forward a bit. Rio did, and Agatha removed her robe and sat behind her, sliding her legs over Rio’s body. The alpha relaxed, leaning her back against Agatha’s chest.

“Why didn’t you stay in front of me?” she asked, touching Agatha’s hand.

“I’m embarrassed.” The omega replied, picking up a sponge and running it over Rio’s chest, spreading some foam. The alpha sighed, melting under Agatha’s gentle touch as she washed her.

“You don’t have to be embarrassed with me, you know you can tell me everything, don’t you?” Rio laid the card on the table. A dangerous thought crossed Agatha’s mind, but she dismissed it immediately.

What alpha would lie with a pregnant omega without feeling repulsed? So Agatha simply answered without going into it.

“I know that.”

Rio lightly took Agatha’s hand and placed it on the edge of the tub, then turned around, making some water spill over, now completely facing Agatha. The omega’s face was flushed from the heat of the water, the steam bringing a rosy tint even to the tip of her nose.

Rio caressed Agatha’s face, sliding her thumb along it. The omega sighed, feeling the tension gather in her core. Rio’s thumb brushed harder against Agatha’s cheek, then parted her lips.

Agatha looked at Rio, her blue-green eyes shining like jewels in a Parisian display window. Then she ran her tongue over the tip of Rio Vidal’s thumb.

“Agatha,” she whispered softly. “I can’t think about anything other than staying in this room and having sex with you.”

The omega let out a low moan at how filthy that sounded, but answered silently, wrapping her legs around Rio’s waist, feeling the alpha’s hard, rigid member press against her belly.

Agatha sucked Rio’s thumb almost at the same time the alpha slid her cock into the omega’s cunt. Agatha moaned, and Rio kissed her parted lips, making sure to bite her lower lip.

Rio gripped the edge of the bathtub with her hands while moving her hips back and forth. Agatha moaned louder, grabbing Rio’s back completely, squeezing hard and tracing her scars with her fingers. The kiss deepened intensely, and Rio only broke it to suck around Agatha’s hardened nipples.

Rio’s tongue circled one of the omega’s pink nipples and sucked it firmly. The brunette arched slightly as another orgasm hit her hard. Rio came, breathless, their lips meeting again in a softer, more restrained kiss. Rio held Agatha in her lap as if holding onto a mast.

Agatha rested her face against Rio’s shoulder, panting, remaining silent, with only the sound of their breathing mixing with the heat of the room, which seemed to burn more and more.

The silence that settled after that morning was not comfortable, nor light — it was dense, heavy with everything that had been said, done… and especially what still had no name.

Agatha remained in the bathroom for a few moments, the heat of the water still wrapping around her body, but it was no longer enough to calm the storm inside her. Her fingers rested lightly on the edge of the bathtub, while her breathing tried to find a steady rhythm, something that didn’t depend on Rio’s presence so close, so inevitable.

Rio, on the other hand, did not move away completely.

Even after the intense moment they had shared, she remained there, close enough to feel Agatha’s warmth, but restrained enough not to cross any more boundaries without permission.

And that, somehow, was even more dangerous.

“You should rest,” Rio said, her voice lower, more controlled, but still carrying a trace of what had happened between them.

Agatha didn’t respond immediately. Her eyes remained fixed on the water, as if searching for some clarity there.

“I’ve rested too much,” she finally replied, her tone firmer than she felt.

Rio’s gaze slid toward her, attentive.

Agatha then stood up slowly, the water running down her body as she reached for the robe nearby. Her movements were more restrained now, as if she were rebuilding a barrier that had been completely broken the night before.

She put on the fabric calmly, tightening the tie at her waist.

“Do we need to go out today?” she asked, without looking directly at Rio. Rio took a second before answering, as if carefully choosing each word.

“Not necessarily.”

Agatha nodded, finally lifting her gaze.

“Then I want to stay,” she said, firmly. “Today… I just want to stay.”

Rio held her gaze for a few seconds, analyzing, measuring, as she did with everything. But this time, there wasn’t enough calculation to organize it.

“Alright,” she finally answered.

Agatha took a deep breath, passing by Rio carefully as she left the bathroom, feeling her body still sensitive, her mind even more so. Each step toward the bedroom felt like an attempt to reorganize herself, to understand where impulse ended… and where consequences began.

Rio remained still for a few moments, alone in the steam that still filled the room. Her eyes closed briefly. And for the first time in a long while, she wasn’t thinking about business, reputation, or control — she was thinking about Agatha, about the children, and about the fact that, for the first time, something in her life could not be treated like a strategic game.

When she stepped out of the bathtub, the world felt different — not simple like before, but inevitable.

On the other side of the suite, Agatha sat on the edge of the bed, her hands resting on her lap, her gaze lost for a few seconds before drifting, almost instinctively, to her own belly. The gesture was subtle.

“What are we going to do…?” she whispered, more to herself than to anyone else. And for the first time since the beginning of everything, it wasn’t an impulsive question — it was real, and it needed an answer.

◆


Rio woke up to a knock on the door. She sat up slowly in bed, Agatha still deeply asleep — her exhaustion had betrayed her easily, so it hadn’t taken long for her to drift off.

The alpha got up, putting on her black robe, and walked toward the door. She opened it slowly and was startled. Alice was standing there. She could see her friend’s gaze slip past her into the room, where Agatha lay asleep in the bed. Rio closed the door behind her.

“— Alice, what are you doing here?” she asked, as if her friend hadn’t just seen a half-naked woman in Rio’s bed — who, by the way, was Agatha.

“Are you kidding me? Do you know what time it is?”

Rio stayed silent, waiting for the answer.

Alice crossed her arms, still incredulous, and pulled out her phone, showing the screen to Rio: 72 missed calls. It was 5:30 PM on Sunday. Agatha and she had arrived Friday morning. Rio grabbed the phone, stunned — she hadn’t even noticed time passing. It was as if she and Agatha had slipped into a dream, an ecstasy that had only been ripped away by Alice’s presence.

“You missed meetings with investors, lunch with the mayor, and several other commitments,” she said, still shocked.

“I… I don’t know what happened, I…” Rio stammered, gripping her robe.

Alice sighed and guided the alpha toward the elevator.

“You need a drink,” she said, stepping in and pulling Rio along.

They reached the lobby quickly and headed to the art deco–style bar inside the hotel restaurant. Rio sat down, and Alice sat beside her. She raised her hand to the bartender, ordering two shots of whiskey, which were served immediately.

Rio took a sip, feeling the warm bitterness slide down her throat. Alice did the same, waiting for Rio to start talking.

“I lost myself. I… I don’t know why she’s so inevitable to me,” she said, swirling the glass, the ice clinking against it. “I always resisted, and I always managed.”

“Rio, she’s pregnant with your child. It’s natural you’d feel this way,” she said, taking another drink. Rio relaxed her shoulders.

“Children,” she said, handing over her phone with the ultrasound image.

Alice choked slightly when she saw it.

“TWO? They’re twins?” she asked, still in disbelief. “Looks like my plan to end Abuela’s bloodline backfired,” she said, finishing the amber liquid in one go.

“But it’s not just the pregnancy. I’m drawn to her because of her scent! I fucked her without even fully remembering her face. I wanted her like I’ve never wanted anyone before.”

Alice let out a low laugh and finished her own drink.

“Well, logically speaking, it’s probably your alpha biology — you were attracted to her eggs.”

“What?” Rio made a face of shock.

“Remember that thing about our grandparents being drawn to scent back in the day?”

“Vaguely,” Rio shrugged. “What about it?”

“My mom did a study on it in college. Back then, omegas lived more restricted lives, at home and within their communities. That made it easier for an alpha to find an omega whose eggs had higher compatibility with that alpha’s sperm. That’s where that ‘connection’ comes from. Basically, your body is telling you Agatha’s body is ideal for carrying your offspring — healthier, stronger, more agile.”

Rio nodded, then shrugged.

“And what’s the other possibility?” she asked.

Alice smirked.

“You’re in love with Agatha Harkness, the seductive siren of Ibiza.”

“Idiot,” Rio rolled her eyes.

Alice shrugged.

“So what’s the problem? Drop everything and take her. Be with her.”

“I would never put Agatha into my life — not with my grandmother in it.”

“What?” Alice looked surprised.

“My grandmother would make Agatha’s life hell every single day. And honestly, Alice, I’m afraid — afraid of what she might do, what she might plan to hurt Agatha. And you know she’s capable of it.”

“Rio, you can’t run from your grandmother forever.”

“Alice, I can’t give Agatha the life she deserves until I finish inheriting the entire company. I need everything under my name. Only then can I decide for myself.”

“And you plan to kill your grandmother?” Alice asked, with irony.

Rio let out a dry laugh.

“As tempting as that sounds, I made a deal with her.”

“What kind of deal?” Alice asked, intrigued.

“If I marry Jessica, the company becomes entirely mine. It’s written in my other Abuela’s will.”

“You need to explain that. What do you mean?”

Rio sighed, raising her glass for the bartender, who quickly refilled it. She took a sip, gathering breath for the story that followed.

“You know my grandmother is an alpha and married my other grandmother, who was an omega — Abuela Claire.”

“Yeah, I know. So?”

“The company originally belonged to Claire’s family. It was small, but profitable. Claire was an omega, and by my great-grandfather’s rules, only an alpha could inherit the company. So Rita got it and expanded it. When my mother was born, Claire died from childbirth complications. And since my mother was born an omega, she has no right to inherit the company.”

“So… that makes you Claire’s direct heir.”

Rio nodded.

“Yes. The company never truly belonged to Rita. She’s just the ‘regent monarch’ until I can take over. So we made a deal — I marry Jessica, and she signs the documents transferring everything to me.”

“Why Jessica?” Alice asked. Rio shrugged.

“To keep the old rich circle intact. I accepted Jessica because I know she can’t have children — and I didn’t want them… well, not with her.”

“And where does Agatha fit into this?” Alice asked.

Rio sighed.

“I’m going to fix everything. I just need time. I need Agatha to tell me about the pregnancy so I can understand where we stand after that. But I can’t let Abuela even consider that possibility.”

Alice nodded, understanding.

“You know you could take this to court, right?”

Rio nodded.

“I do. But it’s hard when the person you’d be suing is friends with every judge in the state.”

“Fuck.” Alice finished her drink. “And are you planning to stay with Agatha?”

Rio clicked her tongue.

“Alice, honestly, if I could, I’d put a wedding dress on Agatha right now and marry her right here in this bar.” Rio looked away immediately after, as if she had said too much — as if admitting it out loud made everything even more impossible.

“But I can’t,” she added, quieter. “Not now.”

Alice didn’t answer right away. She just watched her for a few seconds, as if seeing past the controlled posture, past the flawless reputation.

“Then you already know what you want,” she said finally, calmly.

Rio didn’t answer — but this time, she didn’t deny it either.

 

Chapter 9: The Other Woman

 

 

 

┕──━──━─┑◆┍─━──━──┙

 

Agatha sighed as she looked at the monitor in front of her, with graphs and spreadsheets blinking over the company’s financial activity. As always, Rio Vidal had closed the month in the green.

Agatha reached for her fruit smoothie, ready to take a sip, when the elevator doors opened.

The omega frowned. Normally, no one came up to that floor without her knowing—after all, she was the one who scheduled every appointment for anyone entering that level. Agatha stood up, but she didn’t need to move to see who it was.

Rita had just walked in as if the entire building belonged to her. She wore tailored black trousers, a boot-cut blouse, and an absurdly expensive fur coat—likely the product of the worst kind of cruelty someone could inflict on an animal.

The woman stopped in front of Agatha’s desk and lifted her sunglasses. She had the same eyes as Rio, but unlike hers, they were as cold as an iceberg.

“I’m going to speak with my granddaughter,” she said, tossing her purse onto Agatha, who caught it quickly. The scent of her pheromones was absolutely nauseating, forming a slow knot of pressure deep inside Agatha’s core.

“She’s in a meeting with Chinese investors. I can let her know you’re here,” Agatha replied, professional.

Rita shrugged and dropped her parasol onto the desk.

“I can wait. That’s good—because I can have a conversation with you.”

Agatha’s eyes widened slightly.

“W-with me?” she asked, a bit nervous.

Rita sighed impatiently.

“Sit. I don’t like talking while looking up.”

Agatha sat, adjusting her blazer slightly as if trying to protect the lives growing in her womb.

“You may speak.”

Rita leaned back in the chair, resting her hands on the cane she carried, looking around for a moment before finally speaking.

“I want to know if you’ve heard anything, if you’ve noticed anything different about Rio.”

The question made Agatha’s spine go cold. She tightened her grip on the armrest.

“Different how?” she asked.

Rita laughed with disdain.

“Don’t play the fool. Rio basically postponed the wedding to next year—a wedding she was fully committed to—and suddenly everything disappears? Something changed, don’t you think?”

Agatha swallowed hard and took a breath, looking directly into Rita’s cold eyes.

“Get to the point. What do you want to know?” she asked, with a courage that even surprised her.

“You’re my granddaughter’s secretary. You schedule everything—her entire agenda. I want to know if she has a lover. I want to know if there’s another woman in her life.”

Checkmate.

Rita Vidal had won that round.

Agatha leaned back in the chair, folding her hands in her lap, using every ounce of deception she had learned lying to her mother to skip church on Sundays.

“I have no idea. I handle the professional side. Her personal life—Mrs. Vidal doesn’t allow me access. You know how private she is about her social life.”

Rita narrowed her eyes, and before she could respond, Rio’s voice echoed, bringing relief to Agatha’s chest. The omega noticed how Rio’s posture shifted—whenever her grandmother was present, Rio seemed just a little less secure.

“Abuela, I wasn’t expecting you here.”

Rita stood, looking at Rio, who quickly—like she had rehearsed it her entire life—kissed her grandmother’s hand. A gesture that would normally signify affection and devotion, but only stirred pity in Agatha.

“I’m surprised you’re surprised to see me here, given the recent… developments.”

Rio sighed, as if she had expected this, and asked Rita to follow her to the office. Rita walked ahead, once again acting like she owned the place. Rio turned quickly to Agatha.

“Did she do anything to you? Are you okay?” she asked softly, like a child hiding a secret.

“I’m fine. You should go.”

Rio nodded quickly and left.

It didn’t take long for Rio to enter the office. Rita stood there, looking around, touching the edge of the desk as if recalling a past still very present in her mind. Rio walked around the desk and sat in her chair.

“So, I assume you didn’t come all the way here to teach me a meatloaf recipe for Thanksgiving,” Rio said, her tone laced with irony, though her posture remained controlled.

Rita sat down calmly, folding her hands over the cane in front of her, perfectly at ease in the confrontation.

“You’re far too witty for someone who isn’t holding up her end of the agreement,” she replied coldly.

“What is this about postponing the wedding? Have you lost the little sense you still had in that empty head of yours?”

Rio let out a restrained sigh and leaned back, her gaze hardening.

“The agreement was that I would marry… not necessarily Jessica.”

Rita laughed immediately, low and full of contempt.

“And who do you intend to marry?” she asked, lifting her chin slightly.

“Jessica has a name. She has a reputation. She’s exactly what an alpha needs in an omega… not those sluts you sleep with.”

Rio’s jaw tightened, her lips pressed together as the poison of those words spread through the room. Still, she took a breath before responding, restraining the reaction that wanted to break free.

Rita continued, leaving no room for argument.

“You signed that contract with me. Marry her, and all of this is yours.” Her gaze swept the room, as if everything there proved what was at stake.

“Waiting is foolish. Everything you’ve ever wanted is within your reach… you just have to say yes.”

For a moment, silence settled—heavy.

“And what about what I feel?” Rio asked, quieter but firm. “Where does that fit in?”

Rita rolled her eyes, impatient.

“Love doesn’t move the world. Money does,” she replied without hesitation.

“And over time, you might even come to like her. Marriage, when used correctly, triples wealth, expands power. Think about everything you can have, everything you can live.”

She tilted her head slightly.

“Let go of this romantic fantasy and fulfill what was agreed.”

Rio remained silent for a few seconds, her gaze fixed on her grandmother, absorbing every word. Then, almost involuntarily, her eyes dropped to the necklace Rita wore.

The same as always. A thin gold chain with a rounded ruby at the tip—discreet, yet impossible to ignore. Rio had never seen her without it. Not once.

And she knew exactly why.

“I doubt she would support this,” Rio said at last, low but clear.

Rita stopped immediately.

“Who?” she asked, turning stiffly.

Without breaking eye contact, Rita pressed a button on the desk, summoning someone outside with an automatic gesture—but her attention was entirely on Rio now.

Rio held her gaze for a second longer, deliberately.

Then she pushed forward.

“Abuela Claire,” she said precisely. “She would be against this.”

The effect was immediate.

Rita froze for a second before turning fully, her face hardening.

Rio was already standing, walking toward her—not hurried, but not retreating either.

“Watch your mouth,” Rita said, her voice low and threatening. “You have no right to speak of her. She is not worthy of coming out of that mouth of yours, Rhiannon.”

But Rio didn’t stop. There was something sharper now, more personal.

“You know she would hate you,” she continued firmly. “Hate all of this… everything you do. She would never forgive you.”

That was the final blow.

Rita didn’t respond with words.

The sound of the slap cut through the air before Rio could even react, the sharp impact turning her face to the side. For a second, everything went completely silent.

Then, almost at the same time, both of them looked at the door.

Agatha stood there—motionless, eyes wide, incredulous.

Rita’s purse was still caught in her fingers, as if forgotten mid-motion, as if she had arrived at the worst possible moment—or the only moment when the truth stopped being whispered and became impossible to ignore.

Rita remained still for a second longer, her gaze still burning with restrained fury, before simply turning away.

The movement was abrupt enough to cut off any continuation of the argument. In a few steps, she reached the door and, as she passed Agatha, snatched the purse from her hands without even looking at her.

The gesture was sharp.

As if Agatha were nothing more than another object in the way.

Without another word, Rita left, her firm footsteps echoing down the corridor until they disappeared, leaving behind a heavy, suffocating silence.

Agatha remained still for a moment, trying to process what she had just witnessed, before her gaze shifted to Rio.

Rio was leaning against the desk, her body slightly tilted back, one hand still pressed against her cheek, as if trying to contain the sting still pulsing there.

Her expression was… different. Not exactly pain.

Something deeper. Harder to name.

Agatha took a step forward, hesitant at first, but concern won out.

“Are you okay?”

Rio nodded almost automatically, still not fully removing her hand.

“I am.”

Agatha didn’t press with words. Instead, she glanced around briefly until she spotted the ice bucket on the side table.

She picked up a few cubes, wrapping them quickly in a clean cloth before approaching again.

This time, closer.

Rio lowered her hand slowly, allowing the gesture.

Agatha pressed the ice gently against her cheek, her touch light—almost too careful, as if afraid of causing more harm than help.

Rio let out a soft sigh at the cold, her eyes closing briefly.

Her hand lifted almost reflexively, touching Agatha’s with a gentleness that didn’t pull away—just… sustaining the contact.

“I think I went too far,” she murmured, her voice lower, less certain than usual. There was a brief pause, then, with a faintly ironic breath: “Well… she deserved it.”

Agatha nodded slightly, not arguing, still holding the ice in place—but there was something different in her expression.

The concern was evident… and at the same time, strangely new, as if she herself wasn’t used to feeling it like that. Rio remained still, saying nothing more.

She just watched, her eyes fixed on Agatha, following every small movement, every silent act of care, every second she stayed there—too close, too gentle.

◆


 “Please, Lorna… I’m begging you.” Rio’s voice came out lower than usual, carrying an urgency that didn’t match the woman who rarely asked anything of anyone.

She clasped her hands together for a brief second, the gesture automatic and immediately uncomfortable. It was humiliating on a quiet, intimate level. Still, she didn’t back down.

Lorna looked at her for a few seconds, clearly assessing how far this was going.

“Vidal, it’s already absurd enough that you used your influence to get access to Agatha’s exams,” she said firmly, crossing her arms.

“I can’t allow you to be in the room without her prior authorization.” The doctor tilted her head slightly, her eyes narrowing. “And honestly… why don’t you just say you’re the baby’s other mother?”

Rio closed her eyes for a moment, running a hand over her face as if trying to reorganize her thoughts before answering.

“She told Alice she doesn’t want to know who the other mother is.” The confession came out heavier than she expected. “I… I’m afraid she’ll leave if she finds out now.”

Her voice faltered for the briefest second—but it was enough.

“And I don’t want that. I don’t want to scare her away. I want her to… trust me.”

Lorna stayed silent for a moment, absorbing that with a more analytical than emotional gaze.
 “You could get yourself into serious trouble with this,” she replied at last. “Trust isn’t built on lies like that.”

Rio lifted her gaze, steady but not defensive.

“It’s not a lie,” she said, more firmly this time. “I just… want her to come to me on her own. I want her to trust me enough to tell me. That’s all.”

The silence that followed stretched longer.

Lorna let out a quiet sigh, like someone who knew exactly she was about to give in—even without fully agreeing.

“Fine,” she said at last.

She turned and walked to a discreet door in the corner of the room, almost unnoticeable at first glance. When she opened it, she turned on the light inside, revealing a narrow, clean space with a large glass surface covering the wall ahead.

“This is an observation room,” she explained, stepping in first.
 “For anyone on the outside, it’s just a regular mirror. But from here…” she made a small gesture with her hand, “…you can see everything.”

Rio stepped closer slowly, her eyes already adjusting as she looked through the glass.

“My students use this space to observe exams during external classes,” Lorna continued.
 “You can stay here.”

Then she turned her head, looking at Rio more seriously.
 “But you need to remain completely silent.”

Rio nodded without hesitation.

“Thank you, Lorna.”

The doctor held her gaze for one more second, as if she wanted to say something else… but gave up. She simply stepped out, closing the door behind her with a soft click.

Outside, Lorna exhaled slowly, running a hand through her hair, already aware this wouldn’t end simply.
 Inside, Rio remained still. Alone. Eyes fixed on the glass, waiting.

◆

The waiting room was bright—too quiet for the kind of conversation happening in that specific corner. Magazines neatly arranged, chairs aligned, the low hum of constant air conditioning… everything controlled.

Except for the table where Agatha sat.

Wanda and Jen were leaning over an open pregnancy magazine on their laps, clearly more interested than they should be… and dangerously entertained.

“No, no, no,” Jen said, trying to hold back laughter as she pointed at a section on the page.
 “Read this out loud.”

Wanda was already laughing before she even started.
 “‘In more advanced cases, the weight of the pregnancy may cause… rectal prolapse.’” She stopped, covering her mouth, eyes widening before bursting into laughter. “AGATHA—”

Agatha froze.
 “What?”

“Your—” Wanda tried to speak, but laughter came first. “Your ass can just… fall out!”

“MY WHAT?!” Agatha practically shouted, snatching the magazine from their hands in panic, her eyes scanning the text like it was a death sentence.

“NO. No, no, no—this isn’t real, this can’t be real,” she muttered, already spiraling.

“Oh my God, I’m going to lose control of my own body, my ass is going to fall out, I’ll just be walking and—” Jen was nearly crying from laughter.

“Imagine,” she said between laughs, “you in the middle of the hospital, super serious, and suddenly—”

“JEN!” Agatha shot her a murderous look, completely horrified. “This is not funny!”

Wanda took a deep breath, trying to compose herself… failing miserably.

“Look on the bright side,” she said, still laughing. “We’d still love you anyway.”

“Absolutely,” Jen added, wiping a tear from the corner of her eye. “It’d be a great story to tell your kids.”

Agatha stared at them for a few seconds in absolute disbelief.
 “I don’t know why you two are my babies’ aunts,” she said finally, crossing her arms, offended. “Seriously. You’re horrible.”

“We’re amazing,” Wanda corrected with a satisfied smile.

“And very present,” Jen added.

“You’re a nightmare,” Agatha muttered, still eyeing the magazine suspiciously as if it might attack her again. That’s when a professional voice cut through the moment.

“Agatha Harkness?”

All three turned at once.

A nurse stood by the door, holding a clipboard, smiling politely.

“The doctor is ready for you.” Agatha took a breath, still a little shaken, and stood carefully.

“Right…” she murmured, adjusting her bag over her shoulder.

Wanda and Jen stood right after her, exchanging knowing looks, clearly holding back more jokes.

Agatha shot them one last warning glance.

“Don’t you dare bring that up in there.”

“We promise nothing,” Jen replied, far too innocent to be trusted.

“Absolutely nothing,” Wanda reinforced.

Agatha rolled her eyes and started walking.

Lorna greeted them with an open smile as soon as the door closed behind them, her expression light in contrast to the impeccable clinical setting.

“So nice to see you three together like this,” she commented, genuinely cheerful.

Agatha sighed and dropped into the chair in front of the desk, crossing her arms, her irritation still visible.

“Don’t even ask, doctor.”

Behind her, Wanda and Jen were already laughing quietly as they settled onto the small couch, exchanging looks that made it clear the story was still fresh.

Lorna raised an eyebrow slightly, curiosity growing.

“I feel like you want to ask me something.” Agatha didn’t hesitate.

“What are the chances of my ass falling out?”

The silence lasted half a second. Then Wanda and Jen completely lost it again, laughter exploding without restraint.

Lorna blinked, clearly processing the question—until understanding hit, followed by a small contained smile.

“Oh… you’re talking about rectal prolapse?” Agatha nodded quickly, almost desperate.

“Yes. That. That thing.” Lorna let out a soft laugh, shaking her head.

“It’s actually quite rare. Not common, even in multiple pregnancies.” She stepped closer, adopting a more professional but still gentle tone.

“That’s why it’s important to maintain some light physical activity during pregnancy. Things like regular walking and water aerobics help a lot without putting too much strain on your body.”

She made a calm gesture with her hand.

“But you can relax. It’s not something you need to worry about.”

Wanda and Jen were still laughing in the background, trying to recover, and Agatha turned to them, narrowing her eyes.

“Idiots.”

“Sorry,” Jen said, clearly not sorry at all.

“Not even a little,” Wanda added, still smiling. Lorna then began the exams naturally, wrapping the blood pressure cuff around Agatha’s arm, inflating it as she watched the display carefully.

“Let’s see how you’re doing…” she murmured, then released the pressure and mentally noted the values.

Next came the glucose test—quick and efficient.

“Well… everything looks fine for now,” she said with a small nod. “But we need to keep an eye on your sugar levels. Gestational diabetes is quite common in twin pregnancies.”

Agatha nodded, absorbing that, already about to say something…

When a sharp noise echoed from the next room. Something falling. It was brief—but loud enough to catch attention.

Lorna reacted a little too quickly.

“Oh, don’t worry,” she said immediately, with a smile just a bit too calm to be entirely natural. “The room next door is under construction. There’s always some noise.”

Agatha frowned slightly, looking toward the wall for a second longer than necessary.

There was something—a strange feeling.  And then it came, a familiar scent. Subtle, almost nonexistent… but there.

Rio. Agatha blinked, looking away, trying to shake the thought. Must be in my head. Lorna tapped the examination table lightly, drawing her attention back.

“Please lie down.”

Agatha stood and walked over carefully, climbing onto the table as Wanda and Jen approached, now more attentive than playful.

She lifted her shirt slowly, revealing a belly that was no longer the same as weeks ago—rounder, more defined, a soft curve that was beginning to clearly show what before had only been a suggestion.

Lorna observed with professional attention, positioning herself beside her.

“You’re entering your sixteenth week,” she explained. “Approaching the fourth month. In twin pregnancies, these changes become visible a bit earlier.”

She prepared the equipment with precise movements, then looked at Agatha.

“If you want… we can try to identify the babies’ sex.” Agatha stayed silent for a few seconds, her gaze distant as she thought.

Then she took a breath.

“I want to be surprised.” Lorna smiled slightly.

 “Are you sure?” Agatha nodded.

 “I don’t care about the gender. I just… want them to be okay.” Lorna tilted her head, understanding.

 “Alright.”

And then she began the exam, the device gliding over Agatha’s skin as the screen came to life. The sound of gel spreading was followed by the smooth movement of the transducer, and within seconds the screen filled with delicate but unmistakable shapes—for those who knew what to look for.

Lorna leaned slightly, adjusting the angle with precision.

“Here…” she said, her voice softer now, almost reverent.

“See?” Agatha held her breath without realizing, eyes locked on the screen.

Lorna pointed carefully. “This one is…” she paused, adjusting slightly, “…and this is the other.”

Wanda brought a hand to her mouth, completely overwhelmed by the moment, while Jen leaned forward, eyes wide, absorbing every detail like she was trying to memorize it.

“Oh my God…” Jen whispered.

Agatha didn’t say anything.

She couldn’t.

Her eyes shone, filling with tears, fixed on that image that suddenly stopped being abstract. It wasn’t an idea anymore. Not a “maybe.”

They were there.

Real. Small. Moving. Lorna continued, now more technical again, but still gentle.

“They’re a good size for the gestational age… development within expectations…” she paused, adjusting again.

“Here you can see the outline better… and this movement—” she pointed with a small smile, “—those are early reflexes.”

Agatha let out a small, shaky laugh, almost a disguised sob.

 “They’re… moving.” “They are,” Lorna confirmed.

Then, with a precise motion, she turned on the audio. And the sound filled the room, fast and strong, a heartbeat impossible to ignore.

Then another.

Two rhythms.

Two hearts.

Agatha brought a hand to her mouth, her eyes completely filling now, no longer trying to hold back. Wanda couldn’t resist, letting a tear fall while laughing softly at the same time, completely overwhelmed.
