“I can’t believe this…” Jen wiped her face quickly, not even realizing when she had started crying.
 “There are two… there really are two.”

Agatha just nodded, unable to speak, her other hand instinctively resting over her own belly, as if trying to close the distance between her and what she was seeing.

On the other side of the glass, Rio didn’t move. Her hands were pressed against the cold surface, fingers slightly tense, as if that was the only thing keeping her body from stepping forward.

Her eyes were fixed on the screen, absorbing everything—every detail, every movement—but it was the sound that broke her.

The first heartbeat made something inside her falter.

The second… shattered whatever remained of her control.

The air seemed to fail for a second, caught abruptly in her chest, while her eyes stayed locked on the image of those two small bodies that existed… because of her.

Because of her and Agatha.

A tear slipped down before she could stop it. Silent and warm, tracing a path along her skin without Rio even blinking.

She didn’t try to wipe it away, didn’t try to hide what it meant—much less rationalize what she was feeling. For the first time in a long while, there was no logic strong enough to contain what was moving through her.

All that existed was the steady rhythm of those hearts, the living image before her eyes, and the crushing certainty that it was real.

And regardless of any plan, strategy, or fear she still tried to hold onto, Rio Vidal was already far too involved to simply walk away.

The exam came to an end gradually, as if no one there really wanted that moment to be over. Lorna removed the device carefully, handing Agatha a towel so she could wipe the still-cold gel from her skin, while her expression slowly returned to its usual professionalism—though the shine in her eyes betrayed that she had been touched by it too.

“Everything is progressing very well,” she said, organizing a few papers on the counter. “But I’m going to give you some important guidelines.”

Agatha adjusted herself on the exam table, still a little dazed, while Wanda and Jen remained nearby, quieter now.

“Keep up with light activities like walking and water aerobics—they’ll help a lot with circulation and comfort throughout the pregnancy,” Lorna explained calmly, writing things down. “And I’ll recommend some specific supplements to support the babies’ lung development. Nothing unusual, but important—especially in a twin pregnancy.”

Agatha nodded, taking it all in more seriously now.
 “Alright.”

Lorna smiled slightly.
 “If you experience any unusual symptoms, excessive pain, or any changes in your general condition, you contact me immediately.”

“I will.”

A few minutes later, with everything finished, the three of them left the office, their voices fading down the hallway in a lighter tone, slowly returning to their earlier energy.

On the other side of the glass, silence returned.

And only then did Rio realize how tense she had been. The air left her lungs all at once in a long, deep exhale, as if she hadn’t truly breathed the entire time.

Her shoulders relaxed slightly—but the impact… remained.

The door behind her opened with a soft click, and Lorna stepped in, watching her for a moment before closing it again.

“Are you okay?” she asked, more careful now.

Rio nodded, lightly running a hand over her face, still regaining her composure.
 “I’m… fine.” A small pause. “I just got a little emotional.”

Lorna watched her in silence for a moment, as if weighing something internally.

Then she sighed.

“This goes against several medical rules…” she began, already walking toward the desk.

Rio frowned slightly, not understanding. Lorna opened a small drawer, took something out, and turned back to her.

“But…” she extended her hand, “I think you should have this.”

Rio looked, surprised.

It was an ultrasound photo.

She took it carefully, almost too slowly, as if afraid it might disappear if she moved too fast.

And for the first time in a long time, she smiled.

A different smile. Lighter.

Almost… childlike.

Like someone who had just been given something too precious to measure.

“Thank you, Lorna,” she said, her voice softer, but filled with rare sincerity.

Lorna just nodded, watching quietly.

Rio stayed there for another second, her eyes fixed on the image, as if she needed to make sure it was still real.

◆

Night had fully settled in, wrapping Rio’s apartment in a dense, comfortable silence, broken only by the distant sound of the city filtered through the tall windows. The lighting was low, soft, casting long shadows across the elegant space—but in that moment, none of it really mattered.

Rio was sitting on the couch, her body slightly leaning forward, elbows resting on her legs, completely absorbed.

The photo was between her fingers.

Small. Simple.

But to her—impossible to ignore.

The ultrasound.

Her eyes traced the image over and over again, as if each time revealed something new. The still-undefined contours, the forms too small—and yet… so clear to someone like her.

Two bodies.

Two hearts.

Two lives that existed beyond any control she had ever held.

Time passed without her noticing.

Minutes.

Maybe hours.

Until the sound of the doorbell cut through the space, breaking the silence like a snap.

Rio blinked, as if returning from very far away.

She frowned slightly, confused for a second, before standing. She held the photo for just a moment longer, her fingers pressing the paper carefully, as if reluctant to let it go.

Then she tucked it into her wallet.

And walked to the door.

When she opened it, the surprise was immediate.

“Hi…”

Jessica stood there, with the same impeccable posture as always, but the smile that appeared was more restrained—almost cautious.

“Hi.”

There was a brief silence before she tilted her head slightly.

“Can I come in?”

Rio nodded, opening the door wider.
 “Of course.”

Jessica stepped past her, her familiar perfume filling the space as she walked inside like someone who already knew the place. When she turned back, she approached Rio and placed a quick kiss on her lips—too brief to carry any real intimacy.

Automatic.

Polite.

“You disappeared,” she commented, looking around before turning back to face her.

Rio closed the door behind her, keeping a certain distance.

“Work…” she replied, her voice controlled. “I ended up traveling to Chicago.”

Jessica nodded, walking to the couch and sitting down gracefully.

“I saw some photos.”

Rio watched her for a moment before approaching as well, letting out a quiet sigh as she sat down.

“We need to talk,” Jessica said, direct but not aggressive.

Rio nodded slightly.
 “Yeah… we do.”

The silence that followed wasn’t comfortable.

Jessica rested her hands on her lap, fingers interlacing for a moment before she asked:

“What happened, Rio?” Her gaze was firm now. “Did I do something?”

Rio shook her head immediately.
 “No. You didn’t do anything.”

She paused briefly, organizing her thoughts.

“I just…” she took a breath. “I’m unsure.”

Jessica frowned slightly.
 “Unsure?”

“About my feelings.”

The words fell between them with more weight than the tone suggested.

Jessica looked away for a moment, absorbing it, then let out a quiet sigh.

“I really like you, Rio…” she said, softer now. “I wanted to marry you.” Rio kept her eyes fixed on her, silent.

"I don’t know if I’ll have more time."

This time, it was Rio who reacted. Her body leaned slightly forward, surprise evident on her face.

"What do you mean?" she asked, her voice lower now, but filled with attention. "Why?"

Jessica let out a long breath, as if she needed to gather courage before continuing.

"Life… plays tricks we don’t expect."

Her tone wasn’t dramatic, but there was something broken there—something that didn’t match the woman Rio had always known as so controlled. Without saying anything else, Jessica opened her purse with slower movements than usual and pulled out an envelope, extending it toward Rio.

Rio took it almost immediately.

The paper made a soft sound as it was opened, and her eyes began scanning the lines with the precision of someone used to reading reports, diagnoses, technical terms. At first, it was automatic.

Until it wasn’t.

Her body stiffened.

Her eyes stopped.

And everything inside her seemed to freeze in that same instant.

"You have… cancer?"

Her voice came out lower than usual, filled with disbelief.

Jessica nodded, a small movement of her head that seemed heavier than it should.

"Yes… stage 4."

The silence that followed was brutal.

The information hit Rio directly, leaving no room for defense, and something heavy settled in her chest—a confusing mix of shock and a guilt she couldn’t even fully understand, but that was still there.

Tears began to stream down Jessica’s face without any control.

And Rio’s instinct came before any thought. She stepped closer and pulled her into an embrace. Strong. Firm. As if she could hold everything together.

Jessica collapsed against her, her body trembling slightly.

"I’m scared…" her voice broke, muffled against Rio’s shoulder. "I don’t know what to do… I don’t have…" she faltered, air failing her, "I don’t have anything to do."

Rio tightened the embrace, one hand moving up to cradle her head gently.

"Hey…" she murmured, softer than usual. "You’re not alone in this."

She pulled back just enough to look into her eyes.

"I’ll be with you, Jessica. Whatever decision you make. If you want to seek treatment… I’ll be by your side."

Jessica gave a small smile through her tears, but there was sadness in it.

"I don’t know…" she whispered. "After stage 4… breast cancer… there’s not much to do."

Rio held her gaze, steady.

"The choice is yours," she said calmly. "But we can look for help. The best doctor there is, if you want. We’ll explore every option."

Jessica took a deep breath, trying to compose herself, and nodded slowly.

"You’re very… kind, Rio."

That made something tighten inside her, but she didn’t respond.

"I’ll think about it," Jessica added, wiping her face.

Rio was still holding her hands, their fingers intertwined almost automatically, as if letting go in that moment would be wrong.

"Do you want to stay here tonight?" she asked, more quietly. Jessica shook her head gently.

"No… my mother wants to see me. She already knows…" her voice faltered for a second, "actually, the whole family knows. Everyone is… devastated."

Rio nodded, understanding.

"If you need, I can go."

Jessica shook her head again.

"No need… but…" she hesitated, a small smile appearing, "we could have lunch at that restaurant I like."

Rio nodded.

"Of course."

The silence returned, calmer now, though still heavy. Rio stood up and walked her to the door. She opened it, and Jessica paused for a moment before leaving. She turned slightly, stepped closer, and placed a chaste kiss on Rio’s lips.

Quick. Soft.

Then she left, and the door closed with a quiet click, and the apartment fell back into silence.

But this time… it felt much heavier.

◆

Jessica raised her hand on the sidewalk, the gesture firm despite the tears already dried on her face. A taxi pulled up almost immediately, and she got in without looking back.

"To the hotel," she said, giving the address of one of New York’s most famous ones, her voice already composed, controlled.

The car drove off.

During the ride, Jessica remained silent, her eyes fixed on the city passing by the windows like a blur of lights and motion. Her reflection in the glass showed a different face from the one that had left Rio’s apartment minutes earlier. There was no more exposed fragility there.

The taxi stopped in front of the hotel’s imposing entrance, where the illuminated façade reflected luxury in every detail—tall columns, immaculate glass revolving doors, staff aligned with near-choreographed precision. A valet was already approaching, and the doorman opened the door with deference.

Jessica stepped out gracefully, adjusting her coat as if nothing were out of place.

Inside, the environment was even more grand.

The lobby opened into polished marble, crystal chandeliers casting golden light across the space, floral arrangements perfectly placed at strategic points, and a sophisticated silence broken only by quiet conversations and the distant sound of footsteps softened by thick carpets.

Jessica walked to the reception desk without hesitation.

"I’m here to see the guest in the presidential suite," she said directly.

The receptionist looked up, assessing her for a brief second—just enough to recognize who she was… or to understand that no questions should be asked.

A professional smile appeared immediately.

"Of course, ma’am. You may go up."

Jessica nodded slightly and stepped away.

The private elevator was further back, isolated from the main flow. The doors opened as soon as she approached, and she stepped in, pressing the top floor without needing confirmation.

The ride up was quick.

When the doors opened again, the atmosphere changed. The floor was practically private, the carpet even thicker, the lighting lower, creating an almost untouchable environment.

Two guards stood by the suite door.

They recognized her immediately.

Without a word, they stepped aside.

Jessica passed them and entered.

The presidential suite was vast, exquisitely sophisticated—impeccably designed furniture, discreet artwork, wide windows revealing the city outside like a distant and irrelevant backdrop compared to what was happening inside.

An attendant approached with perfect posture.

"I’ll inform her."

Jessica simply nodded.

She walked to one of the sofas and sat down calmly, crossing her legs, her hands resting on her lap. The room was quiet, but heavy with expectation.

A few minutes later, footsteps echoed through the space.

And then Rita entered the room, unhurried. With her usual calculated calm, her cane tapping lightly against the floor with each step, marking a rhythm that felt more like a warning than a necessity.

Her eyes met Jessica’s almost immediately, analyzing, measuring, as if every detail of her face was an answer even before words were spoken.

"So?" she asked, without preamble.

Jessica exhaled slowly, resting her hands on her lap to keep her posture steady.

"I told her."

Rita tilted her head slightly, interested.

"And how did she react?"

There was a brief pause.

"She was… devastated."

The silence that followed was short, but enough.

Then Rita smiled.

Satisfied.

"I told you." Her voice came soft, almost proud. "My granddaughter may be stubborn, but she has a big heart." She took a few steps around the room, calm. "And you… are bringing her back. Very good."

Jessica looked away for a moment, the confidence she had maintained until then wavering slightly.

"What if she finds out, Rita?" she asked, more quietly now. "What if she knows?"

Rita stopped.

She turned slowly to face her, the smile fading just enough to give way to something colder.

"She won’t find out."

The answer came too easily.

"You will do exactly what I told you." Rita resumed walking, approaching calmly. "You’ll go to the doctor I recommended. When Rio questions it, he’ll have a flawless, irrefutable history." A faint smile returned to her lips. "You’ll do the ‘treatment’ from home… and, magically, after the wedding… you’ll present an impressive recovery. A rare case. Revolutionary."

Jessica nodded slowly, but there was hesitation there.

"I… don’t know."

Rita stopped in front of her, resting her hands on the cane, leaning slightly forward as if to ensure every word was absorbed.

"It’s too late not to know."

Her tone didn’t rise, but its weight did.

"You know the price of entering one of the richest families in this country…" she continued, eyes fixed, "and, above all, of having me as an ally."

Jessica swallowed hard, holding her gaze.

"I do." A small pause. "And I’m grateful for our… friendship."

Rita smiled again, satisfied with the answer.

"With you in my granddaughter’s life, I don’t need to worry." Her voice returned to an almost affectionate tone, but something sharp remained underneath. "You will follow everything to the letter." She reached out, lightly touching Jessica’s chin, lifting it slightly. "And control…" she murmured, "remains mine."

Jessica didn’t move.

"The two of us, side by side…" Rita continued, a cold glint in her eyes, "will keep Rio from ruining her own life."

Rita turned around with her usual calm, as if the previous conversation had been just another detail resolved within a perfectly controlled routine.

She walked to the suite’s bar, where rare bottles rested arranged with precision, and chose one without even looking at the label—as if she already knew exactly what she was picking.

The soft sound of the cork being removed broke the silence.

She poured two glasses carefully, the golden liquid filling the crystal with almost ceremonial elegance. Then she returned to Jessica and handed her one of the glasses.

"What shall we toast to?" she asked, with a slight arch of her brow.

Jessica held the glass, still silent, watching the champagne shimmer for a moment longer than necessary.

Rita didn’t wait.

She raised her own glass, her cold eyes fixed on her.

"Well…" she began, with a subtle smile, "to my intelligence in not letting my lineage end because of my granddaughter’s whims."

A brief pause.

"And you…" she continued, slightly tilting her glass toward Jessica, "to your fake cancer."

Her smile didn’t falter.

"What do you think?" Jessica held her gaze for a moment, then nodded. The glasses clinked with a delicate sound, almost too beautiful for what it truly meant. She brought the crystal to her lips and drank; the liquid went down smooth, sophisticated, perfectly balanced—and completely bitter in her throat.

Rita watched, satisfied.

"You’re going to do very well," she said calmly, as if she could already see the outcome long before it happened. "Rio has always been predictable when it comes to feelings… you just need to know where to apply pressure."

Jessica didn’t respond right away.

Her fingers tightened slightly around the glass.

"And if… this gets out of control?" she asked, more quietly now, almost to herself.

Rita let out a short, humorless laugh.

"It won’t." She stepped closer, close enough that her presence was impossible to ignore.

"Because I won’t allow it."

Jessica’s gaze lifted slowly, and in that moment, it became clear that this wasn’t just a plan. It was a game—and they were already in it until the end.

Rita slowly swirled the glass between her fingers, watching the golden liquid dance against the crystal as if it were the only thing worthy of her attention for a brief moment. When she spoke again, her voice was calm.

"And besides…" she began, lifting her eyes to Jessica, "being married to my granddaughter will be excellent."

She took a small sip before continuing.

"Rio is attractive, tall, intelligent…" a faint smile appeared, laced with something between pride and superiority, "very much like me at her age."

The pause was subtle, calculated.

"With the difference that I have always been smarter."

Jessica remained silent, absorbing it.

Rita tilted her head slightly, studying her once more, as if evaluating a piece she had already decided to use.

"So don’t concern yourself with trivialities like love," she finished, with a light disdain that was almost too elegant for what it truly meant.

She stepped a little closer, enough that every word carried even more weight.

"What comes after…" she murmured, her eyes fixed on Jessica’s, "is far better."

The silence that settled wasn’t comfortable—it was unbearably dense, heavy with promises that had nothing romantic about them, only power, control, and permanence.

Jessica tightened her grip slightly on the glass, feeling the cold against her skin, and still, she nodded.

◆

The morning had begun far too quiet for Agatha’s mind.

Sitting in front of the computer at the office, her fingers hovered over the touchpad while her eyes scanned yet another page of apartments that all started to look the same after a while—well-lit photos, overly optimistic descriptions, promises of comfort that never came with the difficult parts attached.

Two bedrooms. Always two.

She had been searching for days, in broken intervals, hidden between one task and another, as if the simple act of looking was already something that shouldn’t be seen. Not by Wanda. Not by Jen.

They had said she could stay. They had insisted, actually.

But Agatha knew she couldn’t.

She couldn’t turn their lives into chaos over something that… she still didn’t quite know how to name. A mistake, maybe. Or a consequence. Or simply something that happened too fast to be avoided.

Her eyes dropped to an open spreadsheet beside it.

She had organized herself, done the math, saved money.

Enough to cover a few months of rent—at least until the babies were six months old.

Enough to buy time. Time to find a routine, a nanny, maybe a daycare… some way to make this work without depending on anyone.

Without weighing on anyone’s life.

She let out a slow breath, running a hand over her face, trying to focus.

But the train of thought broke the moment the elevator doors opened—too early.

Agatha looked up automatically.

And found Rio.

For a second—short, but dense—they just looked at each other.

Agatha reacted first, tugging the hem of her sweater down in an immediate reflex, even though it was already large enough to hide anything.

Rio noticed.

Of course she did.

But she didn’t comment.

She simply walked toward her, posture firm, but there was something… different. Less controlled than usual, subtle enough that anyone else might have missed it.

"Can we talk?" she asked, her voice low and direct.

Agatha nodded almost immediately.

"Of course."

She stood, adjusting the chair back into place as she followed Rio into the room beside them. The distance was short, but filled with a silence that felt heavier than it should for something so simple.

As soon as they stepped in, Rio gave a small nod.

"Close the door."

Agatha did.

The soft click of the door closing seemed to cut off the world outside.

Rio exhaled slowly and leaned back against the desk, crossing her arms for a moment before uncrossing them again, as if she didn’t quite know what to do with her hands.

Agatha noticed.

"Are you okay?" she asked, taking a small step forward, concern slipping out before she could filter it.

Rio lifted her gaze to her.

She was still leaning against the desk, but something about the way she held herself was different—less steady, as if the control she always maintained was, for the first time, taking real effort.

She took a breath.

"Jessica has cancer… terminal stage."

The words weren’t loud, weren’t dramatic, but they fell with brutal weight.

Agatha froze.

The world seemed to lag slightly, as if the sound had arrived before the meaning. Her eyes blinked once, twice, trying to place it somewhere logical in her mind.

"I’m sorry…" she said finally, her voice softer, sincere.

And she was. Truly.

But something inside her also tightened—a silent tension that had no name, only presence.

Rio took a step forward.

Then another.

"That’s why I think…" she began, hesitating for the first time, "that we need to take a break. I didn’t want to, I—"

"Mrs. Vidal," Agatha interrupted, with a calm that didn’t feel natural, "you don’t owe me any explanation."

Her eyes lifted, steady.

"She’s your fiancée, isn’t she?"

That landed.

Rio stilled for a second, as if the sentence had struck exactly where it shouldn’t.

"Agatha…" her voice softened, "you can call me Rio. Really."

She stepped closer, closing the distance between them.

"We went through something… too intense for me to pretend it didn’t happen. I have no intention of discarding you. Quite the opposite."

Agatha held her gaze.

Then said, almost dry:

"So I’m in the waiting room?"

The silence turned heavy.

"No," Rio replied immediately. "You’re not in any waiting room, Agatha, I just need to… I—"

Agatha took a breath, looking away for a second before returning her gaze.

"Rio, let’s be honest." Her voice wasn’t loud, but it was firm. "This would never work. This fairytale… it’s not for us. We come from completely different worlds."

A faint, humorless smile appeared.

"And I understand. I really do. It was good… but if you want to go on—"

"No," Rio cut in, stepping closer quickly.

Her hand wrapped around Agatha’s arm, firm and warm.

"Listen to me."

Agatha stopped.

Rio was too close now, her eyes locked onto hers with an intensity that left no room to escape.

"I need to stay. I need to fix this." Her voice lowered, heavy. "I can’t abandon her… not now."

A pause—shorter, sharper.

"But I want you." Her tone faltered, just slightly. "I want you more than anything."

Agatha let out a soft, humorless laugh.

"I’ve heard that many times."

"No!" Rio answered too quickly. "I’ve wanted other people before, yes, but this… this isn’t because of—"

She stopped.

Agatha narrowed her eyes.

"Because of…?"

Rio ran a hand over her face, frustrated with herself.

"It’s not about a challenge. It’s not impulsive." She looked back at Agatha. "I feel… different with you. I can’t explain it. And I don’t want to just pretend it doesn’t exist. I just… need to deal with this situation."

Agatha stayed silent for a moment, weighing it.

Then nodded slowly.

"I was born alone, Rio." Her voice was quieter now, but firm. "I left home at sixteen. I know how to be alone."

It wasn’t dramatic. It was a fact.

Rio’s jaw tightened, her gaze softening for a brief second.

Her hand slid from Agatha’s arm to her waist, pulling her slightly closer, almost instinctively.

"But I don’t want you to be alone."

Agatha closed her eyes for a moment and took a breath. When she opened them, something harder was there.

"You need to stop living in a fairytale."

Her voice wasn’t cruel, but it was firm.

She gently pulled away, without hesitation. Rio’s touch disappeared—and with it, something even harder to name.

Agatha turned her back before she could change her mind and walked to the door, opening it and leaving. The click behind her sounded louder than it should have.

Outside, the air felt different—colder, drier.

Or maybe it was just her trying not to feel what was rising inside.

Agatha walked quickly down the hallway, her steps echoing softly against the floor, trying to keep control, trying to keep her breathing steady.

Failing.

When she reached the elevator, she pressed the button too hard. The display took too long.

She ran a hand over her face—but the tears were already there, slipping out without permission, falling fast, warm, impossible to stop.

Agatha let out a broken, almost soundless laugh.

"Great…" she murmured, her voice faltering.

The elevator doors finally opened, and she stepped inside like she was escaping.

Because in a way… she was.

The air outside hit her face like a shock. She went down the building steps almost on autopilot, her pace quick, uneven, as if her body was trying to keep up with something her mind still couldn’t organize.

As soon as she reached the sidewalk, she didn’t stop.

Didn’t think.

She just kept walking, blending into the flow of people moving back and forth, unaware of anything beyond their own routines.

The tears kept falling.

Silent. Constant.

She tried to wipe one away, then another, brushing her face impatiently—but it was useless. The more she tried to hold it in, the more came—as if her body had decided for her that this needed to come out, whether she liked it or not.

Her eyes burned, her breathing faltered slightly along the way.

And still, she kept walking. It was in the middle of that path—vision blurred, attention completely fractured—that it happened.

Agatha turned a corner too quickly—and collided into someone hard enough to send everything scattering across the ground. Folders and papers hit the pavement, a sharp sound echoing between them.

"Shit," she blurted automatically, her voice still unsteady. "I’m sorry, I didn’t see—"

She was already crouching down, rushing to gather the documents scattered across the sidewalk, her fingers clumsy, picking up pages out of order, not even really looking at what she was holding.

"It’s fine," the other person said, with surprising calm.

Agatha nodded, still focused on collecting everything, until she handed one of the folders back… and finally looked up.

"Miss Harkness."

Melina Vostokoff was standing there, impeccable as always.

Her blue eyes were far too attentive for someone who had just been bumped into, studying Agatha with an interest that was anything but casual.

Melina crouched down as well, picking up the last of the papers with organized, precise movements, then stood first, extending a hand to help her up. Agatha hesitated for a second, but accepted.

"I’m sorry… I wasn’t looking where I was going, I—"

"You’re crying?"

The question came directly, without hesitation. Agatha blinked quickly, looking away almost immediately, brushing a hand over her face as if she could erase the obvious.

"No… I…" her voice faltered slightly, but she tried to hold it together, "I just have allergies."

Melina smiled.

"Right…" she said, stretching the word slightly, clearly not buying the explanation, "allergies."

She adjusted the folders against her body and tilted her head toward the corner.

"So…" she continued lightly, "we can go to a café right there, and you can tell me more about this allergy."

Agatha exhaled slowly, her gaze still avoiding hers for a moment, too tired to maintain any convincing defense.

Too tired to keep walking aimlessly.

She nodded.

"Okay."

Melina smiled faintly, satisfied enough. And side by side, the two of them began walking down the sidewalk, moving away from the chaos that still seemed to pulse inside Agatha—even as she tried, with every step, to pretend it didn’t.



Chapter 10: The One That Got Away

 

 

 

┕──━──━─┑◆┍─━──━──┙

 

Ever since Agatha could remember, loneliness was never a choice—it was always a fact. It had been there from very early on, even when she was still a child, trailing behind her mother in the December cold, handing out pamphlets for the Westview church from door to door.

Or when she sat at the dinner table at home with her mother, her father, and her brother Jackson. She learned that silence in her home was her greatest protection, and she hid everything.

She didn’t tell her mother when that strange man on the street harassed her, nor when her shoes were too small for her feet, nor when her brother Jackson made her hide marijuana inside her favorite stuffed animal.

And now she couldn’t even bring herself to tell her friends that she intended to move out. Agatha had always expected loneliness, but she didn’t know what to do when she wasn’t exactly alone.

“Are you okay?” Melina asked, pushing the cup of tea toward Agatha. They had stepped into the café on the corner, which luckily was empty, the smell of corn cake filling the air.

“It was just a hard day,” Agatha replied, taking a sip of the tea.

“Well,” the alpha said, tracing her fingers along the rim of her coffee cup, “it didn’t look like just a hard day.”

Agatha took a deep breath, setting the still-steaming cup onto the saucer. The taste of chamomile spread across her tongue, making her sigh.

“You were right. I’m one of Vidal’s girls.” Melina nodded, as if confirming something she already knew.

“And… I don’t even know why I’m telling you this.” Agatha rubbed her face, breathing deeply, and Melina reached out, placing her hand over hers.

“You can vent if you want. I’m here.”

Agatha regained a bit of her composure and looked into Melina’s deep blue eyes, studying the woman’s face in front of her.

“Are you interested in me? You don’t owe me anything—so why the interest in listening?” she asked carefully.

“Well, yes, I am interested in you. Ever since I saw you at that event, your face hasn’t left my mind.”

“I… appreciate it, but we can’t have anything. Not now.” Melina raised her eyebrows. Agatha thanked her for the tea and stood up, grabbing her bag.

“Hey, wait!” Agatha stepped out, and Melina followed, catching her hand. The omega turned back like she had been caught off guard.

“Why? You don’t want to go out with me?”

“I’m pregnant,” she said firmly. Melina froze for a few seconds, genuinely stunned, looking Agatha up and down. “She… is—”

“No! She doesn’t even know I’m pregnant. Well, this distance will be good. Soon my body would start changing in ways that would be impossible not to notice.”

Melina stepped closer and pressed a light kiss to Agatha’s cheek.

“I’m not stuck in the past,” she said with a small smile. “I understand you’re pregnant, but that doesn’t matter to me—not in a relationship, if that’s something you want. I’ll be here if you ever want to revisit this conversation.”

Agatha sighed and nodded.

“Thank you for listening. I… I have to get back to work, but I appreciate the tea. Really.”

Melina nodded, turning back toward the café. Agatha took a deep breath, still shaken by the emotions that had overtaken her just minutes before. The company building wasn’t far from the café, so she walked a short distance before entering the lobby.

Agatha stepped into the elevator and pressed the top floor button without thinking much. She searched her pockets for her phone, only to realize she had left everything behind when she ran out, fleeing from Vidal. Part of her felt genuine embarrassment; the other part was just… silent.

She stepped out onto the floor and found Rio standing by her desk, just finishing hanging up the phone Agatha usually answered.

Rio turned, exhaling softly for a moment, as if she had truly been worried that Agatha had left everything behind.

“You didn’t have to answer it. I just went to get some air.”

Rio nodded. “I know, but the phone wouldn’t stop ringing. I think you should get back to it. A man was asking for you—I think your brother, Jackson Harkness.”

“What? That little idiot must’ve gotten himself into trouble.” Agatha said, grabbing her phone. She unlocked the screen, but froze when she saw 14 missed calls from Jackson.

Jackson only called repeatedly when he was in trouble. That’s how Agatha had gotten her older brother out of jail more than once.

“Hello, Jackson? What did you—”

“Dad’s dead.”

Her brother’s voice cut through the line. Agatha stopped. Every thought she had in that moment was severed by an invisible blade.

“W-what?” Agatha stammered. “What happened?”

On the other end of the line came the sound of her brother sighing—and the familiar drag of his cigarette.

“My mom got home and found him dead in that room where he used to build those stupid ships inside bottles. The coroner said it was a massive heart attack—he didn’t even feel it.”

Agatha sat down in one of the reception chairs. Rio watched her closely, concern evident in her gaze.

“Evanora?” Agatha asked.

“She’s not saying anything. Aunt Myrtle is handling everything. You need to come, sis.”

“Yeah, I will. I’ll buy a bus ticket today.” Agatha gripped her necklace tightly. The call ended without any goodbyes.

Rio kept watching her. The omega took a deep breath.

“My dad died,” she said, definitively.

“I’m sorry, Agatha,” Rio said, sitting down beside her.

“Were you close?”

Agatha didn’t quite know how to explain her feelings. Her father had always been a quiet man. He avoided arguments, stayed out of the fights between Agatha and Evanora, but he and Agatha knew how to share silence.

She liked coming home from school and finding her father, who had just parked the delivery truck he worked with.

They would go inside, she’d heat up a low-quality meal, and the two of them would watch reruns of old baseball games. It was something that belonged only to them.

“Enough. He… was a good person.”

Rio nodded, lightly touching Agatha’s hand. The omega took a deep breath, trying to regain control of everything.

 

◆

 

“You know you didn’t have to go alone,” Wanda said, driving toward the bus station.

“I’ll be fine, really. And the house is going to be so full… I don’t even want to think about it right now.”

The redhead sighed, parking the car, still wearing a displeased expression. Agatha threw herself into her arms, hugging her and placing a quick kiss on her cheek.

“Take care,” Wanda said with a sigh.

“Don’t worry, I’m not planning on getting pregnant.”

Wanda laughed, rolling her eyes.

“Idiot.”

She pressed the button to unlock the doors. The omega stepped out, carrying her backpack and holding a small travel bag.

“I’ll let you know when I get there,” Agatha said, waving as she walked into the station.

The bus station was busy. It was eight at night, and people moved back and forth, heading to their terminals, buying tickets, boarding buses.

Westview was a small town of around fifty thousand people, the kind that seemed pulled straight from old 1950s American dream advertisements. Identical suburbs with no identity, omegas and alphas confined to their socially assigned roles—boring.

But it still had its beauty in certain things. Agatha liked going to the library and to the park when the sunlight was pleasant.

Everything else, though, she could never accept. That’s why she earned a scholarship to study in New York. She and Wanda left that conformist suburban dream behind.

Agatha boarded the bus after the station employee scanned her ticket. She walked to the back, placing her bags in the overhead compartment. She had chosen a seat closer to the bathroom, since her bladder was starting to become a problem as the babies grew in her womb.

“Good evening,” Agatha said, sitting down next to a woman in her early sixties, wrapped in her coat, her hair streaked with gray.

“Good evening, dear.”

Agatha leaned back in her seat. The woman studied her curiously.

“It’s unusual for a young woman to pick a seat so close to the bathroom. Usually you all prefer the front, where there’s Wi-Fi.”

Agatha let out a quiet laugh, opening her coat slightly and rubbing her belly.

“Not when young women are reckless and get pregnant by complete strangers.” The older omega widened her eyes.

“Oh, dear Lord, I didn’t mean to be intrusive,” she said, embarrassed.

Agatha shrugged.

“Don’t worry. Really.”

The woman rummaged through her bag and pulled out a plastic container. As soon as she opened it, the smell of freshly baked pastries filled the air.

“Here, for you.”

She gently handed one to Agatha. She could see raspberry jam oozing through the cracks of the golden, fluffy dough. To hell with the “don’t accept food from strangers” rule—Agatha devoured it in one bite.

“Oh my God, thank you,” she said between bites. “I was so hungry, I didn’t even have time to grab a snack.”

“I always make extra pastries for trips.”

Agatha covered her mouth as she swallowed the last bite.

“Agatha, nice to meet you,” she said.

The older woman smiled slightly.

“Lilia.” Agatha nodded. “Lilia, nice to meet you.”

She settled into her seat.

“So, what are you going to do in Westview, girl? Seriously, that place is the end of the world.”

Agatha gave a small, strained smile.

“I’m going to my father’s funeral.”

Lilia froze, visibly uncomfortable.

“Good Lord, I really should keep my mouth shut.”

Agatha laughed softly at the situation.

“It’s okay. And you? What are you doing in that middle-of-nowhere place?”

Lilia shrugged.

“I’m finishing the sale of my last property in that quiet town. I’m going to sign the papers to sell a flower shop that belonged to my brother. He passed away last year, and the place’s been sitting empty. I became his legal representative after he died.”

“My condolences,” Agatha said.

Lilia shrugged again.

“I hated him.”

Agatha almost laughed.

“You can laugh. He was trash. Getting rid of this means I can finally live in peace and focus on my life in New York, far away from his memory,” Lilia said, gesturing with her hand.

“What do you do in New York?” Agatha asked.

“I own a few properties—apartments, to be exact—that I inherited from my father,” Lilia said, waving her hand as if sketching an imaginary map. “Near Park Slope and Williamsburg. I also have a bookstore, so that’s basically my life.”

“Good to know. I’ve been looking for rentals around there. I live with two friends, but with the pregnancy, I need a place for me and the babies.”

“Babies?” Lilia widened her eyes. “Girl, you really screwed yourself.”

Agatha laughed.

“I know. But I can’t bring that chaos into my friends’ lives. I know how to take care of myself, and I’ll learn. So I need to leave quietly—move things little by little and drop the bomb when it’s all done.”

Agatha shrugged.

“So a place near where I live would help. Especially if it’s not insanely expensive—I’d love your number.”

Lilia whistled.

“Have you considered the possibility that they wouldn’t mind?”

Agatha nodded.

“Yes, but I can’t take away their lives—dating, parties—not when I was the irresponsible one.”

“Being irresponsible is stealing from a store or dealing drugs. You were just an omega who got pregnant. Don’t be so harsh on yourself, young lady.”

Agatha felt something soften inside her at Lilia’s words. The woman opened her phone case and pulled up a real estate app.

“Where do you live?” Lilia asked, putting on her glasses.

“184 Wythe Avenue,” Agatha replied immediately.

Lilia turned the screen toward her. Agatha squinted—the brightness was almost offensive.

“I’ve got this one. A few blocks from where you live, right above my apartment—which means you’d be my direct tenant. And before you say anything, no, I won’t hear the crying, and the walls are reinforced.”

Agatha laughed. Lilia had an adorably ironic, slightly irritating way about her.

“How much is the rent?” Agatha asked.

“Eight hundred ninety dollars.” Agatha let out a disbelieving laugh.

“Impossible. I pay that monthly splitting with my friends, and that’s for a good two-bedroom apartment.”

“Eight hundred ninety dollars, young lady. Take it or leave it.” Lilia winked.

“Why would you do that?” Agatha asked.

“I know this apartment costs at least a thousand dollars.”

“I’ve been in your place. I know what the world is like for omegas who aren’t bonded to an alpha. I know how we’re judged. And if this is a way I can help you, then I’m happy to do it.”

Agatha felt the tears threatening to spill, but she held back everything that was trying to pour out of her.

“Let’s talk. I don’t even know how to thank you, seriously.”

“Just be well, and promise me you’ll get through this. You’re strong—you can’t lose yourself or punish yourself for this. And when the little ones are born, use heat suppressants.”

Agatha laughed, just like Lilia.

The bus started moving soon after, the engine vibrating beneath their feet as the city slowly faded away, light by light, until it became nothing more than a distant blur beyond the window. Agatha rested her head against the cold glass, her arms crossed over her still subtle—but impossible to ignore—belly.

For the first time in a long while, the silence didn’t feel empty.

It carried everything: fear, grief, guilt… and something harder to name. Something that insisted on existing even when everything around her was falling apart.

Maybe it was hope. Or maybe it was just the raw instinct to keep going.

Agatha closed her eyes for a few seconds, feeling the steady sway of the road, breathing deeply as if she could organize inside herself everything that was out of control. New York was no longer there. Rio wasn’t there. None of it was.

And still… all of it was with her.

When she opened her eyes again, she wasn’t crying anymore. The loneliness was still there—steady, familiar—but this time, it wasn’t the only thing accompanying her.

 

◆

 

Rio remained beside the reclining chair, her eyes fixed on Jessica, connected to tubes, to the IV drip that fell with an almost irritating precision. The room was too quiet, too clinical—and yet, there was something there that unsettled her in a way she couldn’t quite name. Jessica sighed, turning her face slightly toward her.

“Are you okay?” she asked, her voice weaker than usual. “You seem… distant.”

Rio blinked a few times, as if returning from somewhere far away.

“I am. I just… got distracted.”

But it wasn't a distraction. It was Agatha. It was always Agatha.

She looked away, as if she could hide that even from herself, then stepped a little closer.

“And you? Are you feeling okay?”

Jessica made a slight face.

“Just a headache. Nothing serious.”

Rio frowned immediately, already tense.

“Do you want me to call a nurse?”

“No,” Jessica replied quickly, forcing a small smile. “Just… could you go to the cafeteria for me? A yogurt, maybe a granola bar.”

Rio hesitated for a second, as if she didn’t want to leave—or maybe as if she wanted to leave more than she should.

“Of course. I’ll be right back.”

She stood, adjusting her coat almost automatically, and left the room. As soon as the door closed behind her, the air seemed to change. Heavier. More real.

The hallway was long, overly bright, and the sound of her own footsteps echoed with uncomfortable clarity. Rio ran a hand over her face, breathing deeply, trying to organize the confusion spreading inside her like poison.

Guilt came first. The guilt of being there. The guilt of thinking about someone else while holding the hand of someone who believed she was dying.

The guilt of knowing exactly who she was thinking about.

Agatha. The name crossed her mind with almost physical force.

When she stepped into the elevator, the metallic silence wrapped around her instantly. The doors closed with a soft click, and she finally allowed her shoulders to drop slightly. Her fingers slipped into her pocket, pulling out her phone as if it were an unavoidable reflex.

The screen lit up.

And there it was.

The conversation.

Rio stared at it for a few seconds too long, her thumb hovering over the keyboard. Then, finally, she started typing.

“We need to talk, I didn’t mean to hurt you, I…” 

She read the message once. Twice. Three times.

The cursor blinked at the end of the sentence, insistent. Rio held her breath, as if it were something far greater than just a few words, and then erased it, letter by letter, until nothing was left. The screen returned to emptiness, and still, it felt heavier than before.

Rio, contrary to what she wanted, simply wrote something brief: “Did you arrive safely?”

The alpha bought the snacks and sat at one of the tables in the corner of the cafeteria. She pulled her phone from her pocket again and opened Alice’s message—the last one had been sent just a few minutes ago: “I didn’t see any posts.”

The night before, Rio had almost begged Alice to keep an eye on Agatha’s profile, which had gone private a week ago—understandably so, given the pregnancy.

The alpha had agreed reluctantly, after Rio started lightly blackmailing her about exposing all of Alice’s past girlfriends.

Vidal sighed, still tired, restless from the lack of news, while also struggling with her own guilt over an intrusive, betraying thought—one that deeply wounded the core of the good conduct she believed she had.

 

◆

 

Agatha got off the bus at the Westview station at six-thirty in the morning. She said a quick goodbye to Lilia, who mentioned that if Agatha wanted, they could meet for coffee sometime. Lilia Calderu had fit into the omega’s life like a glove.

The brunette walked to the taxi stand and quickly waved one down. An old cab, its sides worn by time, the Westview Taxi logo peeling off, stopped in front of her. Agatha opened the door and slid inside, trying to escape the biting cold of that November morning.

“2804 Sherwood Drive, corner of Maple, please,” Agatha said, wrapping herself tighter in her long coat.

The driver didn’t respond, just started driving—common enough, since Westview didn’t have many places to get lost in.

The omega sighed, leaning back as her eyes drifted over the suburban landscape slowly unfolding in its quiet longevity. Everything looked the same, as if Westview were trapped in a spell that kept time from moving the way it did everywhere else.

The taxi didn’t take long to stop in front of Agatha’s house. She handed over a few dollars and stepped out. The cold outside was even harsher than at the station. She clutched her bag and walked toward the front door of her childhood home—the same cheap suburban place she remembered.

Before she could open it, the door swung open from the inside. Myrtle, her father’s sister, appeared.

“Agatha, I’m so glad you’re here.”

The omega gave a small, sad smile. Agatha sighed, and the two hugged briefly.

“How are things? I got the first ticket I could when Jackson called me.”

The older omega sighed, taking a drag from her cigarette.

“The family’s sad. Your mother… well, she’s at church all the time, barely says anything. Your brother and I are trying to handle the funeral,” she exhaled. “But you know how Jack is. He doesn’t take anything seriously.”

“Yeah, I already understand what my role is here. And part of his irresponsibility is Evanora’s fault.”

Myrtle nodded.

Agatha stepped inside the house. Everything was the same—the quiet, oppressive suburban décor. Brown-toned living rooms, a kitchen with yellowed tiles, photos of a happy family above the fireplace, and an old plasma TV that refused to pick up anything but local channels.

She picked up a photo from the mantle and looked at it. She and her father were at one of Jackson’s baseball games. He wore the red Westview team shirt, and Agatha wore a summer dress with a red ribbon. She was eight years old.

The pull into memory was interrupted by Evanora’s arrival. She came in accompanied by two other fervent church members.

“Agatha.”

Evanora sighed—not out of happiness to see her, but because she knew that from that moment on, Agatha would take on responsibility without question. And for Agatha, that was nothing new.

They exchanged a cold, brief hug.

“How did this happen?” Agatha asked.

Evanora sighed.

“Your father was old and didn’t take care of himself. Well, there you have it—the result. Me having to deal with his mess even after his death.”

Agatha found it ironic. For someone who used the Bible like punctuation, Evanora was exactly the kind of believer who refused to follow what the “holy scripture” actually said. “Love thy neighbor,” “care for your loved ones until death”—those things weren’t written in Evanora Harkness’s Bible. 

“Use it as a weapon against your daughter?” That definitely was.

“Well, you don’t need to worry. I called some providers on the bus and already secured part of Dad’s insurance money. Everything will be covered. Tomorrow we’ll go choose the coffin.”

Evanora sighed in relief.

“Good that you’re doing your part,” she said, lightly touching Agatha’s shoulder, looking her over. “God, you’ve gained weight.”

“Ah, your mother told me you’re pregnant,” Myrtle interrupted. Agatha rolled her eyes. “I imagine the whole town already knows.”

“No, you’re not going to ruin my reputation. Only Myrtle knows, and my two dear sisters from the church. People will only find out when you bring that Chinese Alpha who tried to humiliate me at the mall. Honestly, humiliating your own mother-in-law. By the way, where is she?” Evanora looked at Agatha as if searching for Alice.

“Business trip. She had to go.”

Evanora sniffed like a dog. Myrtle placed a hand on her shoulder. “Your daughter is wearing designer clothes. I doubt she’s lying. Let’s focus on what matters now, alright?”

Agatha took a deep breath. She and Aunt Myrtle exchanged a knowing look, and Evanora headed to the kitchen with her two friends beside her.

“Thanks for that,” Agatha said.

“You’re an adult omega. You don’t have to explain yourself to your mother.”

“Where’s Jackson?” Agatha asked.

Myrtle laughed. “Where else? In that filthy trailer out back.”

“Figured.” Agatha walked to the sliding door that separated the kitchen from the yard. She stepped outside and took a few steps toward Jackson’s trailer. As if sensing her, Jackson came out.

The alpha, as always, had stubble on his face, heavier than the last time she’d seen him. His brown, slightly blond hair was messy.

“Little sis!” he said, approaching and pulling Agatha into a hug. She hugged him back awkwardly. Jackson always smelled like cigarettes and something citrusy.

“Hey. Still getting into trouble?” Agatha asked, pulling back enough to look at him.

“Always. Want a hit?” he said, gesturing to the joint.

“No, I’m good,” Agatha replied.

Jackson raised an eyebrow. “What, you went soft?”

Agatha rolled her eyes and opened her coat, revealing her belly. Jackson’s mouth dropped open and then shut quickly, stunned, as if Agatha had just grown a third head.

“Holy shit!” he said, pulling her into another hug. This time Agatha laughed, genuinely relieved by her idiot younger brother’s real happiness. He lifted her off the ground effortlessly, and she laughed for a few seconds.

“I’m gonna be an uncle. This is awesome,” he said, setting her down.

“I’m never letting you hold my babies until they’re four.”

Jackson rolled his eyes. “It’s not like I’d drop them. Babies? Plural?”

Agatha laughed. “Yeah.”

Jackson clapped his hands, celebrating, genuinely happy.

Agatha had always had mixed feelings about Jackson being in her life. Part of her wanted to forget her past entirely, and another part wanted him present.

Jackson was only two years older than Agatha and had always been the problem child. Not that Agatha was a saint, but Jackson was much worse.

Between minor arrests for theft and vandalism, he had grown older. She knew he lived a life she considered absolutely miserable, but he seemed happy living off odd jobs—fixing cars, working security, which was easy given his height, or making drinks at shady bars.

The way her brother lived as if there was nothing wrong with being thirty and living in their mother’s backyard was both frustrating and, in a strange way, fascinating.

“Well, I’m going inside. I need to call the funeral home.”

The alpha nodded. “I’m going to take some of Dad’s things to storage. Mom wants to get rid of everything, so if you want anything, check his office.”

“Alright.”

Agatha turned, went inside, grabbed her bags, and headed upstairs. She pushed open the door to her old room and stepped in, setting everything down.

The space was unchanged—violet walls peeling with time, posters of dreams that now felt distant. An old white computer and a bookshelf filled with works that once defined her, now feeling impersonal.

She paused, lightly touching the books that once described her so well but now felt like a distortion of a version of herself that no longer existed.

Agatha sat on the bed, picking up her phone after hours of travel. There was a message from Rio, asking if she had arrived safely. Agatha felt warmth in her chest, but also the bitterness of being “kept waiting in the other room.”

“I’m fine, thanks for asking,” she replied, quickly and coldly.

Rio responded just as quickly, offering condolences and telling her to rest.

◆

 

Rio arrived home, taking off her coat and letting out a long sigh. Jessica had stayed at her apartment and said her mother would be visiting. Rio understood, as always.

The alpha hung up her dark coat and immediately grabbed her phone. Agatha had replied. A small sense of relief eased her anxiety knowing Agatha had arrived safely. But part of her alpha instincts was deeply unsettled being away from her pups—and from the one her subconscious already wanted to claim as her omega.

Rio went upstairs to her room, stepped into the shower for a quick rinse, then changed into her usual hoodie. She sat on the bed with her phone and tablet, opening a new document she had received from the hospital.

Agatha was approaching her fifth month, which meant she would soon quit her job. Rio would lose all contact with her—and be definitively cut off from her pups.

The thought disturbed her enough to make her close her eyes for a moment, only to be pulled back by a notification from Alice. Rio quickly grabbed her phone. It was one of those funeral posts—time, place, all the details. Below it, a short message from Alice:

“You owe me.”

Rio definitely agreed.

◆

 

The next morning in Westview was colder than the last.

Agatha woke before sunrise—5:10 a.m.—the sky still dark, as if the sun had been covered by a thick wool blanket.

She stood in front of the mirror, finishing fastening a compression band around her belly. It wasn’t tight, but it subtly shaped her body—enough that, under one of her heavy coats, the pregnancy would go unnoticed.

She finished adjusting it and pulled on a black sweater that covered her completely.

Perfect. It was the closest thing to anonymity she could manage right now.

The omega took a deep breath as she sat on her bed, stretching her feet upward slightly. They were swollen—a clear sign of the pregnancy—and she had to lift them a bit now just to see them fully, since part of her belly already blocked the view.

A knock sounded at the door. Agatha slipped her feet into her cold-weather boots and walked over to open it.

Evanora stood there, dressed entirely in black. She looked Agatha up and down, silently approving what she saw.

“If you dressed like this more often, you wouldn’t be in your current situation.”

Agatha rolled her eyes, grabbing her bag and coat.

“I already spoke to the funeral home. We just need to choose a casket. Please, Mom, don’t make a scene.”

Evanora lifted her chin as if something beneath it smelled foul. “I know what’s best for your father.”

Agatha held it in—almost spoke—but let it go. If she had known, maybe he would still be alive.

Evanora turned and headed downstairs, calling for Jackson and Myrtle. Agatha walked to the end of the hallway, taking a deep breath as she approached the one room she had avoided the entire time she’d been there.

She slowly pushed the door open.

Her father’s office was the same—almost completely dark, lit only by faint sunlight filtering through dusty blinds, illuminating floating specks of dust in the air.

Agatha stepped closer to one of the boats her father used to build. He had taken up the hobby of crafting miniature ships inside bottles to overcome his alcoholism—around the same time Agatha was born.

She walked over to the bottle holding her favorite one. It was small and purple, with green details along the side. Painted on it were the words: “S.S Eternidade.”

Agatha picked it up quickly and left the room. She stopped briefly by her old bedroom, carefully placed the bottle inside her bag, and went downstairs.

She found her brother, aunt, and mother getting ready to leave. Jackson gave her a good morning smile; she simply nodded.

“Let’s go, we need to get there and take care of this,” Evanora said, heading outside. Jackson grabbed the car keys and followed her.

She stopped by the passenger door and reached for it, but Jackson stopped her.

“What are you doing?” Evanora asked, shocked.

“Aggs is sitting in the front. The back seat’s uncomfortable for her.”

“Jackson, you don’t have to,” Agatha sighed, trying to avoid conflict.

“This is absurd. I’m your mother.”

Jackson shrugged. “And Agatha’s pregnant. You’ll be fine in the back.”

Evanora frowned, and Agatha gave a small smile as she walked to the truck. Before getting in, she kissed her brother quickly on the cheek and took the seat.

Myrtle found the whole thing amusing and sat in the back while Evanora complained about the disrespect of today’s youth.

Jackson drove in silence, and Agatha relaxed a little when he turned on the radio. It was strange having her younger brother releasing protective pheromones, when she had always been the one doing that for him.

“I think he would’ve liked a blue casket,” Jackson said.

Evanora rolled her eyes in the rearview mirror. “Your father will not have a blue casket.”

Agatha laughed.

“My brother loved blue. It’s something to consider, Evanora,” Myrtle added.

Evanora put on her sunglasses and crossed her arms.

Jackson parked in front of the funeral home. The building was typical of a small town—no more than two floors, with a sign out front reading “Rest in Peace Funeral Home.” Ironic.

They entered and were quickly greeted by the funeral agent, who had clearly been expecting them. Evanora took control of everything, not allowing anyone else a voice in the decision-making. Honestly, Agatha was almost grateful—she had no desire to compete with her mother over who was suffering more.

The omega wandered through the place, looking at caskets and discounted floral wreaths scattered throughout the bleak space.

She passed by a slightly open door, and her gaze slipped inside.

On the table lay her father—or the empty shell that used to be him.

Almost without realizing it, Agatha pushed the door open and stepped inside.

The room was unbearably cold. Perfect—that was ideal.

She walked slowly toward the body. Pale. Lifeless.

And little by little, it began to sink in.

Her children would never know their grandfather. They would never have that bond. Something had been taken from them before she could even try to protect it—their chance to form a memory with him.

Tears streamed down her face now, heavier, uncontrollable.

And in the middle of it all, the only person she wanted to hold her was Rio.

She wanted to disappear into the warmth of Rio Vidal’s arms—and she didn’t even know why.

Agatha turned and left, stepping outside into the freezing Westview air. She pulled out her phone, unlocked it, opened Rio’s contact, and typed without thinking:

“I need you. I don’t know why, but I need you.”

Agatha felt like the most selfish bitch in the world. Rio’s fiancée was fighting for her life at that very moment, and still, she wanted Rio entirely for herself—without caring about consequences, without caring about morality. She just wanted the alpha, desperately and unconditionally, wrapped around her.

She sat down on a bench outside, burying her face in her hands and allowing herself to cry, feeling the warmth of her tears against the biting cold air.

“Agatha?”

She lifted her face and sighed.

Lilia—the woman she had met on the bus—was there, holding a box of old plant pots.

“Lilia, hi…” Agatha said, embarrassed to be caught in such a vulnerable moment.

“Girl, they say you run into people easily in this middle-of-nowhere place.”

Agatha let out a small laugh.

“Are you okay?” the older omega asked carefully.

“I think I had a small fainting spell. We’re picking out my dad’s casket, and I saw the body and…”

“Girl, you’re grieving. That’s not fainting—it’s grief.”

Agatha nodded.

“Are you done in there?”

Agatha shrugged, standing up and pulling her coat tighter around her neck as another cold gust swept through.

“My mom’s not going to let anyone choose anything else, so I guess I’m free.”

“Come on, there’s a great café on the corner. Perfect with a shot of rum—well, for me.”

The younger omega let out a soft laugh as they walked side by side.

“Well, thanks for the reminder.”

“You’re welcome, dear.”

The two of them walked a few meters to the café. Lilia walked in as if she owned the place, greeting the staff and dropping the box on top of a table. Agatha sat down, taking a deep breath and removing her coat as she felt the pleasant warmth of the heater.

“One coffee with rum for me,” Lilia ordered.
“I’ll have a decaf, please.”

The waiter quickly wrote it down and left.

“You’re going to be a good mother. You’re already drinking this awful thing just to keep them healthier.”

“Thank you. It’s just my way of making sure they’re not as anxious as their mother.”

The drinks arrived, and the omegas clinked their cups. Agatha sighed as the warmth spread through her mouth. Lilia watched her reaction.

“Alright, tell me your story.”

Agatha shrugged.

“I got pregnant in Ibiza. I got drunk, took a few things, and woke up in an extremely luxurious hotel with a ‘good morning’ note, without ever seeing anyone’s face.”

Lilia whistled.

“At least you had good taste, even drunk,” the older woman laughed with a hint of disdain. “I knew he was a bastard and insisted anyway.”

Agatha gave a faint smile.

“So I went back to New York, found out I was pregnant and—”

Lilia raised an eyebrow.

“Don’t tell me you didn’t try to find the alpha responsible.”

Agatha looked slightly guilty.

“Why?”

The younger omega took a deep breath.

“I don’t want to live up to anyone’s expectations. And I don’t want to feel rejection.”

“Agatha, looking for that alpha is claiming your right to share this. You didn’t make those babies alone, girl.”

Agatha nodded, taking another sip of her coffee.

“I know… but the story gets interesting now.”

She traced a finger along the rim of her porcelain cup. Lilia sighed, already expecting more.

“I’m in an extramarital relationship with my boss, who’s engaged.”

Lilia let out a low laugh, almost a murmur, and then started clapping.

“Congratulations. Sounds like a complete disaster. You checked every box.”

Agatha gave a mock bow.

“I know. I deserve that award.”

Lilia leaned back in her chair, crossing her arms.

“And she doesn’t care that you’re pregnant?”

“She doesn’t know.”

This time Lilia laughed for real.

“What’s so funny?”

“My dear, you know that’s impossible, right?”

“What?”

Agatha genuinely frowned.

“Are you serious?”

The older omega raised an eyebrow. Agatha didn’t change her expression.

“Agatha, it’s impossible for an alpha not to know when an omega around them is carrying pups. She might not have noticed at the beginning, but now? You definitely smell like pups.”

Lilia lifted her hand like a teacher explaining something simple.

“Any alpha within a kilometer would know there’s an omega nearby expecting.”

“My brother didn’t know.”

Lilia laughed again.

“If your brother is that ‘charming’ one I saw through the window, that makes sense. Alphas who use substances and drink have a harder time noticing at first. But give it a few hours, and he’d figure it out.”

Agatha froze.

“If… she knows, why hasn’t she fired me? The company has strict policies about pregnancy.”

Lilia smiled knowingly as she finished her coffee.

“Well… I’ll let you figure that one out.”

Agatha stayed silent for a few seconds, her gaze lost in the now-empty cup. The information didn’t just make sense—it connected pieces she hadn’t even realized were missing.

The way Rio watched her sometimes. The way she got closer… and then pulled away. The control. The tension.

“So she knows…” Agatha murmured, more to herself than to Lilia.

The older woman shrugged, resting her chin on her hand, studying her with almost clinical interest.

“Either she knows… or she suspects enough not to ignore it. And if she hasn’t done anything yet…” she tilted her head slightly, “that says more about her than it does about you.”

Agatha swallowed hard, something tightening in her chest. Because for the first time, that possibility stopped being comforting.

It wasn’t “she doesn’t know” anymore.

It was “she knows… and stayed.”

And that was far more dangerous.

Agatha looked away, running a hand over her face, trying to reorganize thoughts that were now racing faster than she could keep up with.

“I need to go back,” she said at last, her voice quieter but steady.

Lilia nodded, not insisting.

“Go. But don’t run from this conversation forever, girl.”

Agatha let out a small breath through her nose, grabbing her coat.

“I never run,” she replied, almost automatically.

But as she stood and walked toward the door, she knew… this time, she might be.

◆

 

Morning came quickly for Agatha that day. She didn’t know if it was relief or torment, but she did know her bags were already packed, ready to be taken to the cemetery reception. She had a plan.

Go to her father’s funeral, and as soon as it was over, grab her things and leave.

Deep down, she knew Evanora would be grateful—and so would she. The whole family knew Agatha was the black sheep.

It wouldn’t take long before everyone found out about the “little secret” growing in her womb.

The omega got up and walked to her bedroom bathroom, closing the door behind her. She removed her sleep clothes and stepped into the bathtub she had filled minutes earlier.

The hot water wrapped around her body, making her sigh. Her belly slowly began to rise above the surface, making it clear that her encounters with Rio would become difficult anyway.

But the thought Lilia had planted refused to leave her mind.

Did Rio know?

It could definitely be true. Lilia seemed like the kind of omega who had lived long enough not to say something without reason.

After a few minutes, Agatha finished her bath. She wrapped herself in her old teenage robe, which no longer closed properly because of her belly.

And then she froze,  the message. She had sent it to Rio in a moment of desperation—and completely forgotten about it.

Agatha crossed the room quickly, reaching the dresser where her phone was charging, unlocking it in a rush.

She held her breath when she saw that the message hadn’t even been delivered to Rio. Strange, but undeniably a blessing, she deleted it before the second checkmark could ever appear.

Agatha let out a breath of relief, setting her phone aside as she went to get dressed. She didn’t put much effort into it—just slipped into one of her black pants with extra elastic, fastened the binder over her stomach, and pulled it tight. Over everything, she wore a dark purple sweater that would blend well beneath her large winter coat.

She adjusted the coat over her shoulders, tugging at the sleeves with almost automatic care, as if each movement helped her maintain control. Her fingers were still slightly damp from the shower, now cold, and she rubbed them discreetly before picking up the bags from the bedroom floor.

She paused for a second before leaving.

Her gaze swept across the space one last time—the room, the walls, the remnants of who she used to be there. There wasn’t enough attachment to make her stay, but there was enough memory to weigh on her.

Then she left.

Walking down the stairs with her bags already in hand, the sound of her steps echoing through the narrow hallway, Agatha found the three of them in the living room. Myrtle was adjusting her coat, Jackson was fiddling with the car keys as usual, impatient, and Evanora… was simply watching.

Always watching.

The woman’s gaze dropped straight to the bags in Agatha’s hands.

“So you’re already running off?” she asked, her tone sharp, almost bored.

Agatha didn’t look away.

“I’m leaving straight after the funeral,” she replied simply, setting the bags near the door. “Better to have everything ready so I don’t waste time.”

Evanora let out a nasal sigh, crossing her arms.

“Great. That way the family won’t have to deal with… certain embarrassments.”

Silence fell for a moment.

Jackson rolled his eyes, clearly irritated, but said nothing. Myrtle shot Agatha a quick look, almost a silent plea for her not to pick that fight.

Agatha just nodded.

“Glad to make things easier.”

And that… seemed to bother Evanora more than any confrontation could have. Because there was no reaction. No attack. Nothing for her to use.

Agatha picked up her bags again and walked to the door before anything else could be said. The cold morning air hit her face the moment she stepped outside, sharp enough to fully wake her.

The sky still carried that dull, heavy tone, as if the day itself wasn’t entirely willing to begin. She walked to the car, placing her bags in the trunk without ceremony.

Agatha got in, pulling the seatbelt firmly across her chest as she stared out the window. The house faded behind them as Jackson started the engine, shrinking in the rearview mirror until it became just another among many in that suffocating suburb.

And even with everything that still awaited her that day, there was a strange sense of a line being crossed inside her. She definitely wanted her brother in her life in some way—maybe her aunt too. But Evanora?

Definitely not.

The drive to the cemetery was filled with a dense silence, the kind that doesn’t need words to feel heavy. The radio stayed on at a low volume, some local station playing old songs Agatha remembered listening to with her father—ironically, or perhaps cruelly fitting.

Jackson drove with unusual focus, his fingers lightly tapping the steering wheel as if he needed something to occupy them. Myrtle stared out the window, quiet, while Evanora sat stiffly in the back seat, as if she were heading to just another obligation—not her husband’s funeral.

Agatha just looked outside.

The streets of Westview passed slowly, far too familiar, each corner carrying a memory she had never asked to revisit.

When the car finally slowed, the scenery changed.

The cemetery stretched before them like an old painting—perfectly trimmed lawns, symmetrical rows of pale headstones, tall trees swaying gently in the cold morning wind. One of those classic cemeteries, almost too beautiful for what it represented.

People were already gathered.

Familiar faces—some aged, others exactly as she remembered. Old coworkers of her father, neighbors, church members. Low murmurs filled the air, occasionally interrupted by embraces and greetings heavy with false solemnity.

Jackson parked.

For a moment, no one moved.

Then, as if following an invisible command, they all stepped out of the car. Agatha grabbed her things automatically, but this time left the bags in the trunk.

She walked, step by step, each one heavier than the last. The stares began to fall on her—curiosity, judgment, pity. She felt them all, even without looking directly at anyone.

She saw it, the coffin.

Positioned beneath a white tent, surrounded by flowers she knew hadn’t been chosen by him. Nothing there resembled the man she knew—everything was too clean, too organized, too silent.

Agatha stopped.

The air seemed to vanish for a second. Jackson passed by her, placing a hand lightly on her shoulder—a quick gesture, but firm enough to say I’m here.

And then she kept going.

Getting closer.

Every detail became sharper as she approached—the inner fabric of the coffin, the subtle shine of the wood, the perfectly arranged flowers.

And at the center of it all…

Him. Her father. Arranged in a way he would never have chosen in life. His hair combed, his expression too neutral, as if someone had tried to erase every trace of who he really was.

Agatha stopped beside him.

Her fingers trembled slightly as they rested on the edge of the coffin. For a moment, nothing came. No tears. No sound. Just silence.

But then reality hit, brutally.

He wasn’t going to open his eyes. He wasn’t going to make a quiet comment about the weather, wasn’t going to ask if she had eaten. He simply… wouldn’t exist anymore.

The air left her chest in a broken motion, almost a trapped sob. Her eyes began to burn.

And then it came—slow at first. One tear. Then another.

Agatha lifted a hand to her face.

“You could have waited…” her voice came out low, broken, almost inaudible. “Just a little longer…”

She let out a weak laugh, completely humorless, the sound dying halfway through.

“I… I was going to tell you.” Her hand slowly dropped to her own stomach, an instinctive gesture.

“You would’ve liked them…” she whispered, her voice truly failing now. “They’re… they’re your grandchildren.”

The silence around her seemed to grow, as if the world itself had chosen to respect that moment.

But there was no answer. There never would be.

Agatha closed her eyes for a second, trying to compose herself, but the weight in her chest only grew heavier.

It was everything left unsaid. Everything that never happened.

Everything that would never have the chance to exist.

When she opened her eyes again, they were red, damp—but there was something else there now.

A raw, painful acceptance.

The world around her felt distant, muffled, as if someone had placed glass between her and everything else. The officiant’s words continued—solemn, predictable, empty—passing through her without truly reaching.

But then—

A dense, familiar scent filled Agatha’s senses. Warm. Protective. So out of place in that cold environment that for a moment she thought she had imagined it.

Her breath faltered, short, and her hand rose instinctively to her belly, fingers pressing lightly as if trying to anchor something within herself.

It didn’t make sense.

Not there. Not at that moment.

Her eyes moved quickly, almost restless. People were too busy—some crying, others murmuring condolences, others simply fulfilling the social obligation of being present.

No one seemed to notice.

No one except her.

Agatha took a step back, her heart racing unevenly, as if she were about to cross an invisible line.

And then she saw—

Further away, near one of the large trees surrounding the cemetery—

Rio.

Dressed in black, impeccable as always. Dark glasses hiding her eyes. Her body still in an almost unnatural stillness.

Far enough not to be part of the scene, but close enough to have never really been absent.

Agatha blinked, as if it might disappear.

The sound of wood closing echoed in the background—the coffin being sealed. Final. People began to move, to approach the family, to say words that meant nothing.

Agatha answered a few, heard none of them. The only thing that existed now… was her.

And Rio.

And when she managed to pull herself free, she walked—quick steps, almost automatic, as if her body knew the way before her mind could accept it.

Agatha stopped just a few steps away, still not quite believing it. Rio’s hand rose slowly, removing her dark glasses.

“How…” Agatha’s voice came out weaker than she expected. “What are you doing here?”

Rio didn’t answer right away. She simply stepped closer.

Her hand lifted, touching Agatha’s face with a softness almost absurd for everything she was. Her thumb brushed gently over the cold skin, wiping away the trace of a tear Agatha hadn’t even realized was still there.

“I couldn’t leave you alone,” she said, her voice low, steady—too intimate for that place. “Not today.”

Agatha felt the air catch again, her eyes searching for some explanation, any logic that could hold that moment together.

“How did you know?”

A brief silence passed between them.

“I knew you’d be here,” Rio replied, without looking away. “And this city isn’t big enough to hide you from me.”

Agatha let out a faint, almost soundless laugh, completely incredulous, her eyes still damp, now mixed with something she couldn’t name.

Anger.

Relief.

Desire.

Pain.

All at once. 

“You shouldn’t be here…” she murmured—but there was no strength in it.

Because, deep down… she was exactly where Agatha wanted her to be.

Rio didn’t step back. Her hand slowly slid from Agatha’s face to the side of her neck—firm enough to hold, gentle enough not to trap.

“And yet, I am.”

The world around them kept moving—people walking, voices, life flowing. But between the two of them… nothing existed beyond that narrow space, heavy and inevitable.

Jackson appeared before the moment between them could fully settle.

“Agatha?” His voice came firm, edged with alertness. He approached quickly, his eyes going straight to Rio—assessing, measuring, suspicious. “Are you okay? Is this woman bothering you?”

The air shifted. It wasn’t subtle, nor discreet. His pheromones spread first—dense, protective, almost aggressive—taking up space as if it were his right. As if he were marking territory. As if he were claiming something.

Rio felt it instantly.

Her reaction was immediate.

Incredulous. Offended.
