Something inside her tightened in an almost instinctive, primitive way—an impulse that bypassed reason entirely. That alpha… that complete stranger… daring to place himself between her and—

Mine, the word came to her mind before she could stop it.

Her omega, her  pups.

Rio’s jaw clenched. She stepped forward, her body positioning itself without permission.

“Don’t interfere,” she said, cold, direct, cutting.

Jackson stiffened immediately.

“Excuse me?” he shot back, his posture shifting, his chest subtly expanding. “You’re talking to my sister.”

The air between them grew heavy enough to suffocate.

“I said don’t interfere,” Rio repeated, lower this time, but far more dangerous.

“And I said—”

“Enough.” Agatha’s voice cut through both of them.

She stepped between them before it could escalate, one hand raised subtly, her body firm despite the internal tremor.

“It’s fine,” she said, looking first at Jackson.

He frowned, still suspicious, his eyes flicking between the two.

“Are you sure?” he asked, lower now, but still tense.

Agatha nodded.

“I am. She’s not bothering me.”

A pause.

“She’s just… a friend. She came to give me a ride.”

The word lingered in the air.

Friend.

Rio felt as if she had been pushed a step back without even being touched. Jackson studied them again, clearly not fully buying it—but trusting her.

In the end, it had always been like that. He relaxed slowly, his shoulders easing.

“Alright…” he muttered, still glancing at Rio as if committing her face to memory.

Then he turned back to Agatha, pulling her into a quick, strong, genuine hug.

“If anything happens, call me, okay?”

Agatha nodded against his chest.

“I will.”

He stepped back, running a hand over the back of his neck.

“Your bags are already in the truck bed,” he said, nodding toward it. “You can grab them whenever you want.”

Agatha gave a small smile.

“Thank you.”

Jackson cast one last long, suspicious look at Rio before finally turning and leaving, his firm steps carrying him away across the cemetery.

Rio remained rigid, her gaze fixed in the direction he had gone, as if still processing the audacity. The nerve. The mere fact that he had been there, interfering.

Agatha turned to her.

Rio let out a restrained sigh, running a hand over her face before finally looking at her.

“Sorry,” she said, quieter now, the tension still evident in her voice. “I shouldn’t have—”

“You shouldn’t have.” Agatha didn’t raise her voice. She didn’t need to. She simply turned and started walking.

Rio hesitated for less than a second before following.

“Agatha—”

She didn’t stop immediately—only when they reached the truck. The bags were there, just as Jackson had said. The cold metal reflected the pale morning light, and the world around them felt too distant.

Agatha stopped.

She stood there in silence for a few seconds, her back turned, breathing deeply, holding herself together—until she couldn’t anymore.

She turned around, eyes locked onto Rio, too close now for any comfortable escape.

“Be honest,” she said, her voice firm, carrying something that had been building for far too long. “At least once in your life.”

Rio frowned slightly, still trying to follow the sudden shift.

“What are you talking about—”

“Did you already know?” Agatha cut in, stepping forward. “Did you know I was pregnant?”

Rio didn’t answer immediately—and that alone was already an answer.

Her eyes shifted for the briefest second, almost imperceptible—but Agatha saw it. She always did. That was the problem.

Agatha let out a humorless laugh, shaking her head as she crossed her arms over her body, as if she needed to hold herself together.

“Of course…” she murmured, her voice low but steady. “Of course you knew.”

Rio stepped forward.

“Agatha, I—”

“Since when?” she cut in, lifting her gaze. There was no yelling. There was something worse. Control. “Since when have you known?”

Rio hesitated. And this time, she couldn’t hide it.

“For a while.”

That hit Agatha like a blunt impact.

She laughed again—but now there was something sharper in it, something that burned.

“‘For a while’…” she repeated, as if tasting the words. “Do you have any idea what that means to me?”

Rio opened her mouth, but no answer came fast enough.

And that only made it worse.

“You knew…” Agatha took a step back, as if the space between them had suddenly become necessary to breathe. “You knew, and still you stayed there… looking at me, touching me, wanting me as if I were—”

She stopped, swallowing hard.

“—as if I weren’t carrying another alpha’s baby inside me.”

Rio’s eyes darkened, her expression shifting from something restrained to something more raw, more instinctive.

“I never looked at you ‘despite that’,” her voice came out lower, firm. “I looked because of that too.”

Agatha froze.

“Don’t do that,” she said quickly, almost defensive. “Don’t try to turn this into something beautiful now.”

“It’s not now.” Rio took another step forward, closing the distance again. “It never was.”

The silence between them grew heavy, dense, filled with everything that couldn’t fit into words.

Agatha took a deep breath, looking away for a moment, running a hand over her face.

“Then why didn’t you say anything?” she asked, softer now. “Why pretend you didn’t know?”

Rio took her time.

This time, not out of hesitation… but because she was choosing each word carefully.

“Because the moment I said it out loud…” she began, eyes fixed on her, “…it would stop being just yours.”

Agatha frowned slightly.

“I didn’t have that right.”

The answer came calm. Simple. And still, devastating.

“You already had too much being taken from you,” Rio continued. “Control, choice… the direction of your own life. I wasn’t going to be another person doing that.”

Agatha fell silent.

The cold wind passed between them, lifting their coats slightly, but neither of them moved.

“So you decided for me anyway,” Agatha shot back, but without the same strength as before. Quietly.

Rio absorbed the blow and nodded faintly.

“Yes.” No defense. No attempt to justify herself beyond that.

That disarmed more than any argument could have. The omega let out a slow breath, looking down for a second before facing Rio again.

“And you’re not going to fire me?”

Rio sighed.

“If you want to leave the company, that’s your right, but no, I’m not going to fire you.”

Especially because I know your children are mine, Rio thought, feeling the words reach her mouth — but she didn’t have the strength to say them.

“I know you’re pregnant,” her voice softened, but didn’t lose its firmness. “I know you’re scared, that you’re trying to do everything on your own because you think you have to.”

The alpha took another step, now very close.

“And even so…” she added, lower, “…I still want you.”

Agatha held her breath, and the world around them seemed too distant, everything muffled by the small space between them.

“That doesn’t make anything easier,” Agatha murmured.

“I know.”

“It actually makes everything worse.”

“I know.” And still, Rio didn’t step back.

Agatha closed her eyes for a second, as if it was too much… as if it was exactly what she wanted to avoid. When she opened them again, something had changed.

“You should be with her,” she said quietly.

“She knows she’s dying.” The sentence fell between them like a cruel reminder of reality.

“And you think that stops me from being here?” Rio asked in a whisper.

Agatha didn’t answer, because she knew the answer — and maybe that was exactly what scared her the most.

Rio didn’t say anything else.

And honestly, she didn’t need to.

She simply moved.

She walked around the truck with firm, almost rigid steps, as if channeling everything she hadn’t said into action.

Without asking, without giving space for refusal.

In a simple — and absurdly revealing — gesture, she picked up all of Agatha’s bags with one hand, as if they weighed nothing.

Care… distorted, maybe. But still care.

Agatha stood still, watching.

It was like watching something happen from outside her own body. As if everything had lost a bit of clarity, and at the same time, every detail was too sharp.

Rio walked to the car, unlocked the trunk remotely with a dry click. It opened smoothly, and she placed everything inside with speed and precision — without hesitation.

As if she had already decided for both of them. As if it was inevitable.

Agatha still hadn’t moved, her eyes fixed on her.

Trying to understand, trying to keep up, fighting not to feel.

Rio closed the trunk and walked around the car again, this time going straight to the passenger door. She opened it, holding it, and for a second — just one — her eyes met Agatha’s.

There was something there. Something that didn’t fit into words.

“Get in,” she said softly. Not an order. But not a request either.

Agatha didn’t argue. She didn’t have the energy for it.

She approached slowly, almost automatically, and got into the car. The cold seat against her body, the familiar scent surrounding her instantly — leather, subtle perfume… and that. That note that was only Rio.

The door was closed carefully.

The alpha walked around to the other side and got into the driver’s seat, her movements precise, practiced. Without looking directly at Agatha, she pressed the ignition button.

The car responded immediately, the engine coming to life with a low, controlled hum.

Agatha pulled the seatbelt across her body, fastening it as if she needed that to hold herself together.

Her fingers were still slightly trembling, but she didn’t comment on it.

She didn’t look, didn’t say anything. Neither did the alpha.

Rio simply put the car in motion, leaving the cemetery behind slowly, disappearing in the rearview mirror like everything in that town seemed to do — without drama, without resistance, just… staying behind.

 

◆

 

After a few hours of silence on the road, the constant sound of the tires against the asphalt had become almost hypnotic background noise. Trees passed by in green and brown blurs, the sky began to warm as the day went on, and still, inside the car, everything felt suspended.

Rio kept her hands steady on the wheel, her eyes on the road, but her mind… her mind was on every unspoken word between them.

Until she broke it.

“Let’s stop,” she said simply, without looking away. “You’re hungry.”

Agatha didn’t turn immediately. She kept staring out the window for a few more seconds, as if considering ignoring it completely.

But she was. She was hungry — her stomach tightening in soft, insistent waves, and she knew it wasn’t just hers.

“I’m not,” she replied firmly.

Rio didn’t look at her, but she felt it — deep in her chest, in that raw, irritating instinct she could no longer ignore. It wasn’t just perception. It wasn’t just care. It was their children telling her they needed to eat.

Rio didn’t argue.

She just signaled and turned the wheel, pulling into the parking lot of a roadside restaurant up ahead.

It was one of those classic places, almost straight out of an old movie. A neon sign flickered in red and blue, some letters failing, announcing “DINER” alongside a small “MOTEL.”

The paint outside was faded, old cars parked haphazardly, a noisy ice machine by the side.

Agatha sighed, running a hand over her face.

“I don’t want to delay the trip.”

Rio turned off the car, finally looking at her.

“The trip can wait. You can’t go without eating.”

It wasn’t authoritarian — but it definitely wasn’t a request either.

Agatha held her gaze for a few seconds… then looked away, exhaling slowly in silent defeat.

“Fine.”

They got out of the car almost at the same time. The outside air was a bit warmer now, but still carried that smell of road, dust, and fuel. They walked side by side to the entrance, in silence, as if any word could reignite everything.

When they pushed the door open, a small bell chimed above them.

The place was simple, but cozy.

Worn red leather booths, formica tables, a long counter with chrome swivel stools. An old fan turned lazily on the ceiling while soft music — something with a Latin influence — played in the background.

And there was something else. A different smell — spices, cumin, pepper, meat. A kind of improvised fusion between diner and Mexican food.

Agatha felt it, and against her will, something inside her eased, almost happy. Her stomach reacted immediately, traitorous, craving something too specific to ignore.

Burritos — shredded meat with cheese, tomatoes, and beans. She didn’t even notice the small sigh that escaped her.

They chose a table at the back and sat across from each other.

Agatha picked up the menu, scanning it, though she already knew what she wanted. She sighed, then looked up.

“How did you know I was hungry?”

Rio shrugged simply, resting her arm on the table.

“Where you were, there wasn’t any food.” A short pause. “I just assumed.”

Agatha narrowed her eyes slightly, as if evaluating the answer, deciding whether she believed it.

But she didn’t press it. Not this time.

“Mm.” She went back to the menu, but the corner of her mouth almost lifted.

“Well… you won this round.”

Rio tilted her head slightly, a subtle glint appearing in her eyes.

“I usually win.”

Agatha let out a small sound through her nose, without looking at her.

Time passed strangely after that, as if the world had decided to give them a quiet truce.

The food arrived — full, hot — and for the first time since leaving Westview, Agatha actually ate. Not out of obligation. Not out of impulse. But because she wanted to.

And Rio watched everything in silence.

The way Agatha held the fork more calmly after the first rushed bites. The way her shoulders gradually relaxed, as if each piece of food lifted some of the weight she’d been carrying since the day before.

When they finished, the silence returned — but it wasn’t the same.

Agatha wiped her lips, taking a deep breath, her eyes dropping briefly to the table before lifting again.

“We’ve stopped here before…” she said, her voice lower now, but steady.

Rio tilted her head slightly.

“Have we?”

Agatha nodded.

“With my dad.” A short pause. “He used to stop here when we were coming back from the lake.”

Rio didn’t interrupt. She just listened.

“The lake?” she asked softly.

Agatha let out a small breath, almost a laugh.

“He used to take me and Jackson fishing…” She rested her elbow on the table, running her fingers through her hair, slightly messing it up. “Not always. But when it happened… it was good.”

Her eyes drifted for a second, as if seeing something that wasn’t there anymore.

“And the best part was that Evanora hated it,” she continued, with a small, crooked smile. “So it was just the three of us. No yelling. No judgment. Just… silence.”

Rio watched every detail — the way Agatha’s face softened when she spoke about it. The rare, almost fragile lightness there.

And that made something inside her tighten.

Because it was beautiful. Because it was something that no longer existed.

Rio smiled faintly, without irony, without games.

“Sounds like they were good moments.”

Agatha nodded slowly.

“They were.”

Silence returned once more.

Agatha ran a hand through her hair again, more slowly this time, as if returning to the present. Then she looked at Rio.

And there was something different there now. Something warmer, rawer — and definitely more dangerous.

“Come with me,” she said.

Agatha was already standing before Rio could respond. Rio didn’t question it — she simply stood up too, because she always did when the command came from her.

Agatha held out her hand, and this time, there was no hesitation. Rio’s fingers wrapped around hers naturally.

And the omega began to walk, gently pulling her toward the back of the restaurant, moving down the narrow hallway that led outside, away from the tables, away from people, away from any eyes that could interrupt whatever was about to happen.

Agatha stepped into the bathroom still holding Rio’s hand, pulling her in without giving her space to hesitate. The door shut on its own behind them with a dull, heavy thud, sealing off the world outside.

And then she didn’t wait.

Agatha’s hands came up quickly, firmly, cupping Rio’s face as if she needed to hold her there, as if any second of delay would be too much. And she kissed her—intensely.

A control Rio didn’t expect—not in that state, not after everything.

But she didn’t refuse it either.

Rio’s hands immediately dropped to Agatha’s back, firm, pulling her closer as the kiss deepened, gaining weight, heat, urgency. Their bodies fit together with barely any space between them, as if that contact were inevitable, necessary.

Agatha yielded to the touch, but didn’t retreat. On the contrary—she pushed forward.

Her breathing already faltering between movements, her lips moving with haste, with hunger, with something that went beyond desire.

Rio reacted instinctively, her hand sliding up along the side of Agatha’s body, firm at her waist, guiding, holding. Then she pushed her lightly against the cold bathroom wall, the impact soft, but enough to pin her there.

The kiss broke for a second, just long enough for them to breathe.

Their lips still too close, warm air mingling between them, a thin thread of saliva still connecting them for a brief moment before it snapped.

Neither of them pulled away—not even an inch. Rio’s forehead was almost touching Agatha’s, and both of their breaths were heavy, uneven.

Rio slid her hand down between their bodies and undid the button of Agatha’s dark pants. The omega, in turn, used her legs to push the clothing down to the floor, along with her underwear.

Driven by desire, the alpha lifted the omega off the ground, wrapping Agatha’s legs around her waist and using the weight of her body to hold her against the wall. Their eyes met, pupils blown wide, swallowing the blue that usually filled much of Agatha’s gaze.

The omega brushed her nose against the alpha’s, who let out a soft breath, tugging her sweatpants down just enough to free her throbbing member from the fabric.

“What if someone hears?” Rio whispered, her voice almost brushing against Agatha’s lips. “I’ll be quiet.”

Rio slid her cock between Agatha’s wet, swollen folds. The omega let out a low moan as Rio began to move slowly, their bodies falling into a synchronized rhythm.

Agatha’s body pressed and rubbed against Rio’s with each thrust, as her alpha’s cock pushed into the tight walls of her core.

Rio kept her pace slow and steady, unhurried, as if time and place had bent entirely to their will.

“Mm… keep going like that…” Agatha’s hand cupped one side of Rio’s face; she wanted to see that unfocused look directed at her. The alpha understood, locking eyes with Agatha as her cock pushed deeper, driving her core closer and closer to its limit.

Rio’s hips grew firmer in their rhythm. Agatha shut her eyes, clenching a fist and pressing it against Rio’s chest.

Their breathing mingled, and the alpha realized she was close to knotting her—she knew that knotting an omega meant binding them in a way that couldn’t be undone.

“Agatha… mm…” the alpha groaned, low and unsteady, resting her forehead against Agatha’s, no longer able to keep her eyes open.

The omega felt the base of Rio’s cock swell and gasped, her body tightening instinctively, her pussy trying to clamp down around the full length of her alpha’s cock.

Agatha breathed hard as the orgasm surged through her, gripping Rio’s cock tightly. The alpha moaned louder than she intended, spilling her thick, hot release, which began to slide out of Agatha’s wet, still pulsing pussy.

Their bodies trembled in the aftermath. Rio regained some clarity and slipped out before the knot could fully swell, before they were forced to stay far longer than was safe in that bathroom.

Rio captured Agatha’s lips in a deep, intense kiss, their tongues meeting as if that kiss was necessary to realign them both. The alpha lowered the brunette carefully back to the ground and, without a word, pulled her dark pants back up.

Agatha let out a soft moan as the fabric brushed against her still-wet pussy. Rio noticed—and her cock hardened again.

“Fuck… we’re never getting out of here,” she murmured against Agatha’s lips, sliding her hand inside her pants, rubbing over the omega’s wet pussy.

Rio took a deep breath, regaining control, then withdrew her hand and licked her fingers, looking directly at Agatha, who exhaled softly.

They shared a quick kiss—one that said far more than either of them could put into words.

 

Chapter 11: Cards on Table

Chapter Notes

I'm back early! (especially since this chapter is basically a continuation of chapter 10, so I'm giving you two chapters in a row, DON'T GET USED TO IT LOL) And I brought a very romantic and hormonal Aggie in this chapter, I hope you like it!

This is my Twitter if anyone wants to follow me :) @lovettblack

 

 

 

┕──━──━─┑◆┍─━──━──┙

 

Rio let out a low sigh, bringing the glass to her lips and finishing the rest of the wine in a single controlled gulp. 

The liquid went down warm, but not enough to dissolve the discomfort that remained lodged somewhere between her chest and her throat, her fingers still rotated the stem of the empty glass for a few seconds, as if searching for some kind of distraction there that never came.

Across from her, Alice chewed the last piece of her filet calmly, savoring every detail of the expensive dish as if nothing in the world were out of place.

There was something almost irritating in that lightness, in that ability to exist without the constant weight Rio had been carrying over the past few days.

The restaurant wrapped around them with its carefully constructed atmosphere.

The low, golden lighting traced soft contours across faces and hid imperfections no one there wanted to see. The tables were spaced far enough apart to guarantee privacy, yet close enough that the place never lost its sense of shared exclusivity.

It was a place made for alphas, you could feel it before you even realized it.

Expensive perfumes dominated the air, woody and intense notes blending with the scent of grilled meat and aged wine.

The men and women there wore impeccable cuts, fabrics that fell perfectly over bodies that knew exactly the space they occupied. No one needed to prove power there. It simply… existed.

And beneath that polished surface, there was another layer.

Omega companions, men and women, moved between the tables with elegance, easily blending in with regular customers until small details gave away their role.

A touch just a bit too brief on the shoulder, a smile held just long enough, a look that evaluated before offering itself. Nothing explicit. Nothing vulgar. Just… understood.

Rio knew that game well—she had played it before, being part of that world where everything was easily accessible, as long as you knew how to look.

But that had been left behind. In the past few weeks, any attempt at approach was dismissed before it even began.

A glance was enough. A minimal gesture, and people understood. Agatha had become Rio’s north, and she simply no longer had any interest in that world that once felt so routine.

Alice wiped her lips with the napkin, leaning back in her chair as she watched Rio with a crooked, curious smile.

“You’ve been boring lately.”

Rio didn’t respond immediately. Her eyes swept across the room once more, as if searching for something that clearly wasn’t there.

“Evolving, maybe,” she said at last, her voice low, controlled.

Alice let out a short, disbelieving laugh, tilting her head slightly.

“Oh, of course. Evolution. That has a name.”

Rio lifted her gaze then. “Don’t start.”

Alice gave a small smile, reaching for the wine bottle naturally. The reddish liquid flowed back into their glasses, filling them. 

Alice placed the bottle back in its holder carefully and then turned her attention back to Rio, now more focused.

“Three weeks,” she said, lightly spinning the glass between her fingers. “Three weeks since she pinned you against the wall and made you confess your first lie. How are you feeling?”

Rio rolled her eyes, settling more comfortably into her chair, but she didn’t completely look away.

“It’s not like it was a lie,” she replied, dry. “That was my plan. Things just… happened sooner than expected.”

Alice nodded slowly, clearly satisfied with the answer, as if she were only confirming something she already knew.

“Of course,” she murmured. “There’s always a plan.”

She brought the glass to her lips, taking a small sip before continuing.

“Well… now Agatha knows that you know about the pregnancy,” she tilted her head, watching her. “What’s the next step?”

Rio let out a low sigh, running her thumb along the rim of the glass.

“I want to get closer to her about it,” she said after a second. “But it’s going to be gradual.”

Alice raised an eyebrow.

“Don’t take too long,” she replied, direct. “It’s important for you to be part of these processes.”

Rio nodded, looking away for a moment.

“I know.”

The silence that followed wasn’t long, but it was enough for Alice to notice the shift. She leaned slightly forward, studying Rio’s face more carefully.

“What is it?”

Rio took a moment to respond. Her fingers tightened slightly around the stem of the glass, and she exhaled slowly, as if this was harder than it should be.

“I have… a thought,” she began, her voice lower now. “And I feel like shit for thinking this way.”

“It’s about Jessica…” Rio started, her voice quieter, more restrained than usual. She looked away for a moment, as if organizing it in her own head was harder than saying it out loud.

“I don’t want it to sound like I’m wishing she’d get sick or anything like that, but… fuck…”

She let the air out slowly, running a hand over her face before continuing.

“Your grandmother, remember when she went through chemotherapy?”

Alice stiffened slightly in her chair, her body reacting before her mind did.

“It was awful…” she replied, almost automatically, a faint shiver running down her arms.

Rio nodded, resting her elbow on the table.

“Alice… it’s been three weeks,” she said, now looking directly at her. “And Jessica is still… the same. No effects, no hair loss, nothing.”

Alice frowned, thoughtful, swirling the wine in her glass.

“Maybe she’s resistant, Rio,” she said after a moment. “It varies from person to person.”

Rio held her gaze for a few more seconds, as if she wanted to fully believe that—but she didn’t.

Still, she sighed.

“Yeah… you’re probably right.”

She brought the glass to her lips, but didn’t drink immediately.

“We haven’t even…” she began, hesitating for the first time. “We haven’t spent another night together.”

“That’s strange.” Alice nodded slowly, without judgment this time. “Your relationship is pretty messy right now.”

Rio agreed with a small nod, finally taking a sip of the wine. It tasted more bitter than before.

“Okay…” Alice let out a heavier sigh, running a hand through her hair as if trying to push the thought away before even saying it.

“Don’t start thinking like that…” she began, but stopped halfway through. She made a slight grimace, annoyed at herself. “Fuck, what I’m about to say…”

Rio looked up immediately, firm.

“Spit it out.”

Alice hesitated for a second, her fingers still wrapped around the stem of the glass, spinning the wine without really paying attention to it. Then she exhaled all at once.

“Don’t you think it’s strange?” she said, quieter now. “It’s almost… convenient that…”

She stopped, shaking her head, as if it was too late to continue.

“Forget it.”

But Rio didn’t let it go. Her gaze hardened slightly, her expression closing off as she finished it effortlessly:

“That she got sick exactly when I wanted to end everything.”

The silence fell heavy between them. Alice didn’t deny it—she just nodded, slowly.

Rio returned to the company shortly after lunch, her heels echoing softly through the silent corridor of the executive floor. The environment was as always—organized, impeccable, and cold—but something was different now. Or maybe it was just her.

When she crossed the reception, she saw Agatha.

Sitting at her desk, focused, flipping through a thick file she had taken from her bag.

Her fingers moved carefully between the papers, but there was a subtle tension in the way she held everything, as if that control was the only thing keeping the rest in place.

Rio slowed her pace and approached gradually.

“How are you?”

Agatha didn’t look up immediately.

“Normal,” she replied, simple, almost automatic.

She turned another page, but this time she closed the file right after, as if she had already seen enough.

“I’ll need to leave soon,” she added, grabbing her bag. “I have an appointment.”

Rio frowned slightly.

“You’re going alone?”

Agatha let out a small sigh, finally lifting her gaze.

“Yes,” she said, direct. “I’m used to it.”

That bothered Rio more than it should have. She hesitated for a second, but didn’t back down.

“I can go with you,” she said, firm, no hesitation.

Agatha raised an eyebrow, crossing her arms for a moment.

“Why?” The question wasn’t curious—it was accusatory. “You already knew I was pregnant,” she continued, her tone sharper now. “And you didn’t think it was necessary to tell me.”

Rio held her gaze this time, not looking away.

“I know you’re upset with me,” she said more carefully. “And you have every reason to be.”

She took a small step forward.

“But I want to… fix things. At least try to do this right.”

The silence between them lasted a few seconds. Agatha watched her, evaluating, as if measuring every word, every intention. Then she stood up and grabbed her bag with a decisive motion.

“You can come,” she said finally. “But don’t ask too many questions.”

Rio didn’t smile, but inside she lit up like a child.

“Alright.”

And it was more than alright.

◆

Agatha walked into the reception and Rio followed right behind. The brunette rummaged through her bag looking for documents that had gotten lost among prenatal vitamin bottles and her makeup bag, and without really noticing, she tossed her coat and bag into Rio’s hands, who held them immediately.

Agatha grabbed her documents and handed them to the receptionist, slightly out of breath.

“I told you that way was confusing,” Agatha muttered while waiting for the receptionist to check her file.

“You suggested it,” Rio shot back. “I said if we took the main road we’d get here faster.”

The omega pouted, rolling her eyes.

“I agreed to bring you if you behaved.”

Rio gave a small smile. It was impossible that she was actually feeling comfortable in that ridiculous, casual scene with Agatha.

“Well, I’ll stay quiet from now on.”

Agatha rolled her eyes, taking her bag and coat from Rio’s hands and walking toward the elevator. How fast could a woman pregnant with twins even walk?

Rio picked up her pace, catching up with Agatha and stepping into the elevator before the doors fully closed.

The alpha took a deep breath. She felt different—almost pathetic—but at the same time completely overwhelmed with happiness just from being there.

They stepped out of the elevator, and Rio’s gaze immediately met Lorna’s, who was coming out of the room holding a clipboard. Lorna took in the scene quickly, and the pure panic in her eyes gave Rio away.

Please, this is working, please don’t say anything.

“Miss Harkness,” Lorna greeted. “Doctor, sorry for the delay, I had a terrible driver.”

Rio nodded awkwardly. Agatha’s eyes moved from one to the other.

“Clearly you two know each other, I assume.”

“Yes, it’s actually interesting to see you both here together. I know Mrs. Vidal because of my daughter Alice,” the doctor explained.

Rio let out a quiet breath. Dr. Lorna didn’t just know her—she had known her long enough to remember how tall Rio had been when they first met, and she certainly hadn’t even reached half of Lorna’s hip at the time.

“Agatha, I left the gown for you inside. I’ll be right in, okay?”

Agatha nodded and went in. The omega picked up the gown that had been packaged in a bag and headed to the changing room, closing the door behind her.

Rio let out a breath, and Lorna shook her head from side to side.

“What are you doing here?” she asked in a whisper, glancing at the still-closed dressing room door.

“She found out that I know, she found out I know about the pregnancy.” The alpha ran a hand back through her hair, still slightly dazed.

“Rio, you need to tell her everything.”

“I will, but… I don’t want to push her away, not now. She let me come with her, she didn’t take that away from me even being upset with me.”

The alpha paused, catching her breath a little, still extremely overwhelmed by Agatha’s scent, mixed among so many other pregnant omegas around them.

“Take a deep breath, it’s normal. You’re in an environment with many omegas and their alphas nearby, it’s normal to feel like an intruder.”

Intruder. That felt like a knife slowly twisting in her abdomen.

“What?” Rio asked, this time looking toward the dressing room door. Lorna raised an eyebrow.

“Did you knot Agatha?”

Rio flushed completely. Even though Lorna was a professional doctor, it was still strange to talk about her sex life with someone who had known her since she was a pup.

“And… what if I did?” Rio stammered.

◆

3 weeks ago.

Agatha took a deep breath as she stepped out of the bathroom, pushing the door harder than necessary, as if she needed that small impact to anchor herself back to reality.

The air in the restaurant felt different now—heavier, mixed with the smell of stale coffee, fried food, and floors recently cleaned with cheap products.

The fluorescent lights buzzed overhead, casting a cold glow over everything, clashing with the worn red of the cracked leather seats.

She was still dazed.

The last “interaction” with Rio spun in her head like a persistent echo, hard to organize, impossible to ignore. Only after taking a couple of steps forward did she realize she was still holding her hand.

Rio walked beside her, too close.

Close enough for anyone paying attention to notice. Close enough to make Agatha wonder, for one uncomfortable second, if anyone there knew, if anyone had seen.

The few customers scattered around the place seemed oblivious, focused on their meals or staring blankly at the TV hanging in the corner. Still, Agatha felt heat rise up her neck.

She let go of Rio’s hand slowly, almost without noticing the gesture.

She opened her mouth to say something, anything that would normalize the moment—but no sound came out.

A thunderclap cut through the air.

The windows trembled slightly, and then the rain started—not as a drizzle, but like a solid wall of water crashing down outside.

“What the fuck!” Rio muttered, already moving toward the window.

She placed a hand against the glass, leaning in to try to see better.

Water streamed down in thick cascades, completely distorting the view of the parking lot. Her car was nothing more than a dark blur between reflections and motion.

For a second, Rio stayed there, silently analyzing the situation.

Then she turned to the omega.

Agatha was already wrapping herself tighter in her coat, pulling the fabric closer to her body, shoulders slightly tense, the clear expression of someone trying to decide her next move without admitting she had no control over the situation.

Their eyes met, neither of them needed to say much. A few minutes later, they were at the reception of the motel next to the restaurant.

The change of environment was almost comical. The smell of food was replaced by cheap disinfectant and old, slightly damp carpet.

A noisy fan spun lazily on the ceiling, and a small television behind the counter played some random program at a volume too low to understand.

The place wasn’t the kind of environment Rio frequented, but it wasn’t a complete disaster either, just too simple.

“We need a room,” Rio said directly, placing her credit card on the counter with a firm gesture. “Just until tomorrow morning.”

The receptionist, a woman with faded red hair and a disinterested expression, slowly looked up.

She scanned Rio from head to toe without any effort to hide her judgment, then let out a short whistle.

“If you’re lucky, this rain will stop before dawn.”

The tone wasn’t kind. Not even a little.

Rio blinked, surprised, her jaw tightening for a second. There was a trace of offense there—not because of what was said, but because of how it was said.

Agatha, beside her, exhaled slowly and stepped up to the counter, resting her elbows on it, leaning forward slightly as if slipping into a role she knew very well.

“Alright, sweetheart…” she said, tossing a strand of hair back with a calculated motion, her rural accent slipping in with theatrical ease. “We want the best room in this dump.”

She took the card from Rio’s hand without asking and handed it to the receptionist with a small crooked smile.

“And no bedbugs, thanks.”

The woman raised an eyebrow but took the card. She ran it through the machine without hurry, watching Agatha more than the screen, then handed the card back—not to Agatha, but to Rio.

Rio still seemed to be processing the scene she had just witnessed. The key came right after, slid across the counter toward Agatha.

“Stairs to the left.”

“Much appreciated. Rio, tip her,” the omega said, taking the key with a brief nod and already turning toward the stairs as if the place were familiar territory.

Rio took half a second longer to react, still caught in what had just happened. She pulled out some cash and placed it on the counter before following her.

“What was that back there?” she asked as soon as they closed the room door behind them.

The sound of rain was even louder inside, pounding violently against the window, filling the silence between words.

The room was simple—old wooden furniture, slightly worn, a single double bed occupying the center of the space like a widow forgotten in time. 

The red bedspread contrasted with the cream sheets, creating an aesthetic that tried to be charming but instead immediately gave off a “sleep and shut up” vibe.

In the corner, an old television rested on a stand, turned off. The air carried a faint smell of dust and stored fabric.

Agatha walked in first, leaving the key on the small side table before sitting on the edge of the bed.

She took off her shoes with an audible sigh, her feet finally free, revealing the exhaustion she had been ignoring for hours.

“You’re not in the big city, honey,” she replied, without looking directly at Rio, lightly massaging her own foot before stretching it. “People aren’t going to be nice just because you have money.”

Rio crossed her arms, shifting her weight onto one leg, observing the room with a critical eye.

“Well… they should be. I’m paying for this place.”

Agatha let out a low murmur, almost a contained laugh.

“Welcome to the real world, Mrs. Alpha,” she said, tossing her hair back as she ran her hand over the red sheet, feeling the rough texture under her fingers.

“Here, if you don’t say you want your sandwich without pickles… you’re gonna have to eat it with pickles. And still say thank you. And sometimes even if you ask for no pickles, they’ll still put the damn pickles in it.”

“The places I go to already know what I like,” Rio said, her tone low, as if it were too simple a truth to be questioned.

Agatha let out a tired sigh, partially ignoring the comment as she lifted her feet onto the bed, resting them carefully on the mattress.

“Damn…” she murmured, running her hand over her ankle. “My feet are swollen… and I don’t have my compression socks… my vitamins are still in the car.”

Rio frowned slightly, bothered, and didn’t respond immediately—she just watched.

She absorbed every detail—the discomfort, the slight weight in Agatha’s breathing, the way she avoided looking fragile even when she clearly was.

“I’ll ask for more towels,” she said then, already moving toward the door before any objection could come.

Agatha barely had time to react before Rio stepped out.

The hallway was colder than the room, and the sound of the rain felt even more aggressive out there. As she went down the stairs, the noise of water hitting the roof and pavement became almost deafening.

She paused for a second in front of the glass door and took a deep breath, then unlocked the car from a distance.

The next second, she opened the door and stepped outside.

The rain came down hard on her in the first step, quickly soaking her coat, her hair, her skin. Rio moved forward anyway, in long, firm strides, crossing the parking lot as if it were just another irrelevant obstacle.

It wasn’t—but it didn’t matter.

She opened the trunk, quickly grabbing Agatha’s bags and a smaller one, closing everything fast before heading back.

Water ran down her face, her hands, her clothes with every movement, and her clothes became a second skin from how soaked they were.

When the alpha got back inside, she was completely drenched. She climbed the stairs without stopping and opened the room door.

Agatha looked up immediately.

“Oh my God… you went out there?”

There was surprise in her voice, but also something els, Rio didn’t answer, just walked in, dropping the bags on the floor, still dripping.

Agatha was already on her feet. She went to the closet, opened it, and found folded towels that had clearly been there the whole time. She grabbed one without hesitation and walked back to the alpha.

“I knew if I said I was going, you’d say no,” Rio said, her voice low, too calm for someone in that situation.

“Of course I would,” Agatha replied, wrapping the towel around her carefully.

“I don’t want you getting sick.”

Rio held her gaze for a second, closer now.

“I don’t want you to be uncomfortable.”

The silence that followed wasn’t empty. Agatha started drying her slowly.

First her hair, pressing the towel gently, absorbing the water still dripping. Then down to her face, her neck, her shoulders, the wet fabric clinging to Rio’s body.

The touch was careful. Intimate.

“Go take a hot shower,” Agatha said, her voice lower now, already moving before any response.

“Yes, ma’am.”

She stepped closer, her fingers finding the buttons of Rio’s shirt with a natural ease that didn’t ask permission. She undid them one by one, revealing skin still damp from the rain, cold to the touch.

The fabric gave way, and Agatha slid the shirt off her shoulders carefully, avoiding sudden movements, as if the simple gesture carried more weight than it should.

Then she turned, spreading the piece over the heater, adjusting it so it would dry better. When she looked back, Rio was already wrapped only in the towel.

The contrast was inevitable, the still-wet body, the light fabric clinging in places, dark hair falling heavy over her shoulders.

But something in Agatha made her find the scene almost adorable. Rio looked like a big, lost pup, standing there wrapped in a towel, just waiting for someone to tell her to go shower.

“You can go.”

Then Rio moved, walking toward the bathroom in silence, her steps firm, but slower than usual, as if she were too aware of every inch of space between them.

Agatha started going through her bag, still feeling the echo of everything that had happened pulsing under her skin. Between papers, clothes, and small objects, she noticed something that wasn’t hers—a smaller, discreet bag, with a change of clothes that clearly belonged to the alpha.

She vaguely remembered Rio saying she always kept spare clothes in her car for extraordinary situations. She paused for a second, fingers resting on the fabric.

Then she set it aside, letting out a low sigh as she sat on the edge of the bed. The room felt quieter now, filled only with the constant sound of the shower and the rain outside.

Agatha ran a hand over her face, trying to organize her thoughts and failing miserably. Curiosity came first, creeping and insistent.

And along with it, something warmer. Harder to ignore.

“Fuck it…” she muttered to herself, getting up and walking to the bathroom.

Her steps were slow and cautious, as if each one was a conscious choice she could abandon at any moment.

She pushed the door open slowly. Steam escaped first—hot and dense, wrapping around her face before she could even see clearly.

Rio stood with her back turned under the water, dark hair clinging to her neck, water running down the firm lines of her body. Her back revealed the natural tension of someone used to control defined muscles, straight posture… and scars.

Old, but still there. Marks that didn’t belong to the flawless image the world saw.

Agatha stopped in the doorway. The air felt heavier in her lungs.

The omega’s gaze moved slowly, taking in every detail Rio never offered in words. Now and then, she could see Rio’s half-hard member resting against her thigh.

A slow heat began forming low in the omega’s belly, spreading outward, mixed with everything—tension, desire, confusion, and need.

Agatha stepped into the bathroom slowly, quietly closing the door behind her. She removed her clothes, leaving them in a pile on the floor, and walked toward the shower.

The omega moved with slow, catlike steps, pulling the curtain aside gently and stepping into the shower behind Rio. She approached slowly, touching the alpha’s back, who shivered at the contact. Agatha traced the scars with care, making Rio turn toward her.

Rio sighed, as if a weight had been lifted off her shoulders. The alpha’s eyes quickly moved over the omega’s body—Agatha’s belly was more noticeable now, which made Rio wonder if she was wearing some kind of binder.

She felt slightly unsettled, so she held Agatha’s face between her hands and kissed her. Agatha sighed, feeling Rio’s warm lips against hers.

The kiss was soft and affectionate. Rio pulled back slightly, sliding her thumbs over Agatha’s face, then down to her chin, and finally to the omega’s slightly parted lips.

Rio’s other hand rubbed Agatha’s back, moving downward toward the younger woman’s ass before squeezing the soft flesh on one side.

“I… I’m glad you’re here,” Agatha confessed, burying her face against Rio’s chest—against the alpha’s small breasts. Rio pressed her nose into the top of Agatha’s head, her hands sliding down over the woman’s full hips before lifting her up.

Agatha gasped in surprise and wrapped her legs around Rio’s waist, her arms looping around her neck.

They deepened the kiss this time, more intense, and the alpha pressed the omega against the wall, making sure she was steady.

Agatha panted between the heated kisses, holding Rio’s face.

“I need you,” she whispered against Rio’s lips, her forehead resting against the older woman’s. “All the fucking time.”

A tear slipped from Agatha’s eye, mixing with the shower water. Rio slid one hand to the back of Agatha’s neck while using the other to hold her.

“I’m here, I’m here,” she said, kissing the omega’s tears—her omega. Breathing in her strong scent, the scent of her pups, losing herself more and more.

Agatha let out a loud, surprised moan when Rio’s cock slid between her legs, bringing relief to the ache and the sense of agony she had felt seconds before.

But this time something in her body felt different—her pussy clenched tightly around Rio’s cock, making her let out a nearly animalistic groan.

“Fuck!” Rio started moving her hips back and forth, pushing into the tight, wet walls of Agatha’s pussy, the space seeming to grow tighter and tighter.

Their next kiss was completely messy—Agatha bit Rio’s lower lip, feeling the alpha’s tongue slide against hers.

Agatha broke the kiss to breathe, squeezing her eyes shut as she clutched Rio’s back, unable to stop herself from scratching the alpha’s shoulders. 

Rio moved her hips faster now, and with every motion her pelvis rubbed against Agatha’s pulsing clit, making her body twitch in small spasms.

“Ahm… like that… keep going… like that,” Agatha said, eyes shut tight, clinging to Rio as if she depended on her for everything. 

The alpha groaned, bracing one hand against the wall as she moved her hips harder, Agatha’s body bouncing against her.

The omega’s nails dug into the alpha’s back when the orgasm hit her, but this time, unlike all the others, her pussy closed completely around Rio’s cock, which was swollen and throbbing, denied its release for too long.

Rio used her body to hold Agatha against the wall and moaned loudly, bracing both hands against it as she came hard inside her tight pussy. It felt like every part of her cock was being pulled and held by Agatha’s insides.

The base of Rio’s cock swelled immediately, locking them together in a knot. Agatha gasped as she realized what had just happened.

She had studied “knots” in her sexuality classes at school, but she also knew that the chances of an omega knotting an alpha were extremely low.

Rio recovered slowly, still trembling. She was completely dazed by the knot she had just received from Agatha, the scent of pheromones so strong that she simply held Agatha in her arms while the omega reached out to quickly turn off the shower.

Still disoriented and completely pressed against Agatha, Rio walked them to the bedroom and climbed onto the bed. The omega still had her legs wrapped around Rio’s waist, so they had to stay facing each other, bodies fully pressed together.

“Rio,” Agatha called, holding the alpha’s face. She seemed to be in a state Agatha had never seen before. Rio looked at her completely silent, lips slightly parted, pupils blown wide. Rio Vidal looked like she had just been struck by lightning.

But the feeling was that her body now depended on Agatha even to breathe. The omega, in turn, pulled Rio’s head closer instinctively—she simply felt like she needed to do it.

Rio buried her face in Agatha’s neck, holding her tightly, as if she might disappear at any moment.

“I’m here,” Agatha whispered.

Agatha sighed, feeling Rio’s warm breath against her neck, and slid her hand to the back of the alpha’s head, gently scratching her scalp with her nails.

Rio fell asleep almost instantly. Her heavier breathing against Agatha’s neck became a powerful lull that made the omega drift off to sleep in the alpha’s arms.

 

◆

Agatha opened her eyes after a few hours. Rio was in complete silence, her fingers slowly gliding over her belly. She stayed quiet, watching, waiting to see what would happen next.

Rio startled when she met Agatha’s gaze.

“Sorry…” she said softly, honestly. Normally, Agatha hated when other people touched her belly. People had simply started treating her body like public property. The only ones allowed were Wanda and Jen, but even so, Rio’s touch made her heart race in a way she had never felt before.

“They’re twins,” Agatha whispered. Rio made a subtle expression of surprise.

“And are you happy about that?” she asked quietly.

“I was scared at first, I admit it. I’m still scared most of the time. But part of me is happy they’ll grow up together… and be best friends.”

Rio smiled, brushing a strand of Agatha’s hair back. The only sounds were the rain falling outside and their steady breathing.

“You’re going to be an amazing mother,” Rio said, gently touching Agatha’s nose, tracing her finger along its shape, rubbing the small prominent bone along the bridge.

Agatha had heard she would be an amazing mother from many people before. But hearing it from Rio hit somewhere she couldn’t quite explain.

Tears rolled down Agatha’s face, bringing a look of concern to Rio.

“What is it?” she asked, touching her face. Agatha sniffled, trying to steady her breathing, but failed. Through heavy tears, she only said:

“Shut up and hold me.”

Rio obeyed instantly, wrapping her arms around her. Agatha buried her face in Rio’s neck, who began stroking her back. Slowly, Agatha calmed down. Rio’s scent became an invisible anchor.

“I wish my children had a mother like you,” Agatha whispered. Rio took a deep breath, holding her tighter.

It was simple. She just had to say it. Just say: I’m here, your children are mine, I love them as much as I love you, I want to raise them with you. Thin tears began to slip from Rio’s eyes.

But reason came first. Rio knew that revealing that would put Agatha directly in her grandmother’s crosshairs. And she couldn’t—she couldn’t put Agatha in danger.

“I wish that too,” Rio whispered, very softly, answered only by the heavy sound of Agatha falling asleep in her arms—completely surrendered in a way that was deeply rewarding… and a little painful.

◆
Present day

 

Lorna took a deep breath, rubbing her forehead hard, as if trying to organize too many thoughts at once.

Before saying anything, she glanced quickly toward the bathroom door, making sure Agatha was still inside. Only then did she look back at Rio.

“You tied her.”

The words came out dry, almost disbelieving. Lorna was still rubbing her face, as if repeating it out loud might make it make more sense.

Rio blinked, stunned.

“I… I…” she hesitated, her throat tightening for a moment. “I tied… I tied her.”

It sounded like a confession. Not of something planned, but something that just… happened. Something she hadn’t even realized she was doing until it was already too late.

Lorna looked at her, now completely serious.

“Rio, tying a pregnant omega who’s carrying your children binds you completely to her and to the pups. Directly.”

Rio frowned, tension building in her shoulders.

“And what does that imply?”

The question came out lower, tighter than she intended. Lorna inhaled slowly, holding her gaze.

“That your presence now directly impacts the health of the pups. You created a bond. That means you need to be around most of the time.”

“In other words… you’re going to have to tell her.”

Rio opened her mouth, ready to respond, but the bathroom door opened at that exact moment.

Agatha walked out, already wearing the hospital gown, adjusting the fabric over her body. Lorna gave Rio one last meaningful look, a silent “we’ll continue this later”, before completely shifting her posture.

“Let’s go,” she said, already moving with professional ease.

Rio entered the room right after, her steps more restrained now. She sat beside the examination bed as Agatha carefully climbed onto it, still tense.

She had seen one of Agatha’s ultrasounds before. But this, being there, close and watching, it was different.

Agatha lifted the gown, taking a deep breath. The gesture was simple, but carried vulnerability. Rio, almost without thinking, lightly touched her arm.

Agatha relaxed.

Lorna applied the cold gel to her belly, and Agatha tensed slightly at the sensation. Then the transducer moved over her rounded abdomen, steady and precise.

It didn’t take long. The images appeared on the screen, two sacs, just as expected.

Lorna let out a small, satisfied sound, automatic and that alone eased something inside Agatha.

“As you can see, they’ve gained quite a bit of weight,” Lorna explained, adjusting the machine. “Their cervical spines are already formed… and here—” she pointed, “their little feet.”

Agatha smiled, and tears came before she even realized it. Without noticing, she squeezed Rio’s hand and Rio didn’t pull away.

Lorna pressed a few buttons, and then the sound of two heartbeats filled the room.

Rio felt the impact of it physically. A single tear slipped down her face, silent and unnoticed.

“The heart rates are excellent,” Lorna continued.

“They’ve gained weight. One is slightly bigger than the other, but that’s not a risk.”

Agatha frowned.

“What do you mean?”

“They’re fraternal twins,” Lorna explained calmly.

“They’re not identical. The first one seems a bit bigger… could be a boy, but that’s still just a hypothesis.”

She gave a small, light wink. Agatha exhaled, relieved.

“But we’re going to monitor the second twin more closely,” Lorna continued. “I’ll prescribe supplements to help with weight gain.”

Agatha nodded, but her gaze wavered for a moment.

“These medications… how much do they cost?”

“Around 300 dollars.” Before Agatha could react, Rio spoke:

“Alright.” Just like that.

But inside, something twisted. That was… nothing to her. And yet, clearly now it wasn’t nothing for Agatha.

“We’re going to increase the frequency of your appointments,” Lorna continued. “The sixth month is critical in twin pregnancies. We need strict monitoring.”

“Okay…” Agatha murmured, squeezing Rio’s hand tighter.

“Have you been feeling them move?”

“A bit more lately.”

“That’s expected. They start reacting to sounds, moving more…” Lorna gave a small smile. “And now you need to go on complete bed rest.”

“But—”

“No problem,” Rio cut in.

Agatha turned her head toward her, eyes narrowing. How dare you. She thought, though she didn’t say it. Her expression made it clear enough.

And yet, something warm sparked beneath the irritation. Agatha got up soon after, heading toward the bathroom to change, her movements sharper than necessary.

As soon as the door closed, Rio let out a breath, sinking slightly into the chair.

“About what I was saying…” Lorna resumed, returning to her previous tone.

Rio lifted her gaze.

“A pregnant omega can only tie an alpha whose DNA is compatible with the pups. It’s biological.” Lorna crossed her arms. “That puts you even more connected to them. So… do it right.”

Rio nodded, and this time, without hesitation, stood up, picking up Agatha’s bag.

“I’ll fix everything.”

Lorna simply nodded, satisfied, when Agatha came out of the bathroom, already dressed. The mood had changed.

Rio was silent again, and Agatha took the bag and coat from her hands without looking at her, murmured a brief thank you to Lorna, and walked out without waiting. Rio followed, quickening her pace until she caught up with her.

“Are you mad at me?”

Agatha stopped and turned around. Her face was red, her eyes damp, her hands clenched. Rio froze for a second.

“Why didn’t you let me speak?” her voice came out controlled, but heavy. “When Lorna talked about rest?”

“Because I’m going to put you on leave,” Rio replied, direct. “Do you really think I’m going to let you keep working after this?”

“Rio…” Agatha took a deep breath, trying to stay firm. “I need my salary, I—”

“Do you think I’m that petty?”

Rio’s tone cut through the air, serious and offended. Agatha froze.

“Well…” she hesitated. “It’s your company that has a clause preventing pregnant omegas from working.”

Rio looked away, her jaw tightening.

“Those clauses existed before me,” she said, lower but firm. 

“I can’t change everything at once. But I would never leave you without support.” Rio paused more heavily now, looking at Agatha.

“Do you really think I don’t care about you?”

That hung in the air. Agatha didn’t answer, but for a second she saw a quick, restrained tear. Rio turned her face away, as if denying even that.

Without saying anything else, she took the bag from Agatha’s hands and started walking ahead with firm, hard steps, as  if trying to compensate for something she couldn’t name.

Rio Vidal didn’t let Agatha carry anything. Not the weight of things, much less the weight of the moment.

And even so, something tightened in Agatha’s chest, something close to guilt.

The ride back to Agatha’s apartment was silent. She glanced at Rio a few times, who kept her eyes on the road. With every moment inside that car, Agatha felt like she had made a huge mistake.

Rio parked the car slowly, aligning it perfectly in the spot as if she needed that small control. The engine turned off, and the silence became even more noticeable.

Agatha held her bag tightly in her lap before looking at her.

“Thanks for coming with me.”

Rio only nodded. She didn’t say a word, didn’t look back. That hurt more than any sharp reply.

Agatha took a deep breath, opening the door and stepping out.

The air outside felt colder than it should have or maybe it was just her. She closed the door carefully and started walking toward the building entrance without looking back.

She didn’t have the courage.

She only realized the car had left when she felt the shift of air behind her, the sound of the engine fading away, and for some reason, that left a strange emptiness.

Agatha entered the building and went up the stairs in silence, her steps echoing louder than usual. When she opened the apartment door, she found Wanda sitting at the counter, focused on papers, and Jen with her back turned, washing dishes.

The sound of the door made both of them look at the same time.

Agatha hung her coat up too slowly, as if trying to buy time, but the expression on her face already gave everything away: confusion, tension… and something neither of them could immediately identify.

“What is it? You look like you’ve seen a ghost,” Wanda asked, narrowing her eyes.

Agatha didn’t answer right away. She walked to the fridge, grabbed a glass, and poured water, drinking in slow sips, as if she needed to prepare her body to speak.

And then she said it, without warning.

“Rio came with me to the appointment today.”

The sound of glass shattering was immediate.

Jen dropped the glass from her hands. It hit the sink with a sharp crack. All three of them startled—even Jen.

“Sorry!” she said quickly, looking at her hand.

But it was too late. The cut appeared almost instantly. Agatha moved toward her in two steps, taking her hand carefully.

“You cut yourself, Jen, what the hell!”

Wanda was already getting up, going to grab the first aid kit without even being asked.

“You literally dropped a bomb in the kitchen,” Jen muttered, still a bit dazed. “We knew you were seeing your boss, but… this? A medical appointment?”

Wanda came back with the kit, opening it on the counter. Agatha grabbed gauze and antiseptic, focusing on what she was doing.

“It’s going to sting,” she warned, before pouring the liquid over the cut.

“FUCK!” Jen pulled her hand instinctively.

Agatha shrugged, holding it firmly.

“I warned you.”

With Wanda’s help, she finished cleaning it and carefully bandaged it with precise movements—as if taking care of others was easier than dealing with what was happening inside her.

“Thanks,” Jen said, taking a deep breath and sitting on one of the counter stools.

“How was that?” Wanda asked.

Agatha inhaled deeply.

“I think after my dad’s funeral we got closer, more than before… before it was just sex, but…”

Agatha drank the rest of the water and continued.

“She offered to go. She was really sweet.” The omega gestured with her hand, as if trying to recall something that was already very present in her mind.

“So, Lorna said I’m getting close to the sixth month, and that I need rest because it’s an important month. A lot of premature births happen… and Rio stepped in and said ‘alright’ like she could fix everything and run my life.”

Wanda and Jen exchanged a look.

“Okay, and what’s the whole story?” Jen asked.

“I think I was kind of stupid with her,” Agatha said. “I started saying I couldn’t stop working because I need the money, and she said… ‘do you really think I’m that petty?’”

“Yikes,” Wanda said, taking a sip of her wine.

“Yeah,” Agatha said. “And now I feel guilty. She really got hurt. I think she even cried, guys. Seriously.”

Jen stood up abruptly, going to the fridge and throwing a few packages away.

“We need to take the trash out. Wanda, come on?” Jen asked.

“We’re in the middle of a conversation,” Wanda raised an eyebrow. “Why do you want to take the trash out now?”

“You’re acting weird, Jen,” Agatha said while peeling a banana to eat.

“Agatha, honestly, I think you should relax and talk to Rio. You tend to isolate yourself even when you don’t need to. Maybe she was just genuinely worried about you. But we’ve talked about this before and you choose not to listen.”

Wanda stayed quiet, noticing the tension settling in the room.

“Noted, Jen,” Agatha said, getting up, still annoyed by the last comment, and went to her room, closing the door.

“What the hell was that, Jen?” Wanda asked, incredulous.

“Trash. Now,” Jen said, grabbing the garbage bag and heading down the stairs. Wanda grumbled and grabbed the second bag. The two of them started going down the building stairs.

The two of them stepped outside, greeted by the cold night. Jen walked around the building, dropping the trash bag on the ground, pacing back and forth.

“What the hell was that, Jen?” Wanda asked again. “We can’t freak out like that. We need her to trust us.”

Jen turned to look at Wanda, clenching her fists, and then said what she had been keeping to herself for months.

“Rio is the other mother of Agatha’s children.”

Wanda froze, processing the information.

“How?” she asked, as if she hadn’t heard it properly.

“She… she’s the mother,” Jen whispered now, embarrassed by the weight of the secret.

“Jennifer, since when do you know this?”

Jen took a deep breath, looking up, incredulous with herself.

“Three months.”

“Fuck!” Wanda paced back and forth, rubbing her face. “And why exactly doesn’t Agatha know this?”

Jen leaned against the wall.

“I found out by accident. At Alice’s house. Rio showed up there. I overheard her conversations in the hallway, and she said she knew Agatha was pregnant—and that she was the mother of those children.”

“You didn’t answer my question,” Wanda said, crossing her arms.

“Alice asked me not to tell. According to her, it was for Agatha’s safety. Rio never approved the idea of having kids, and it also seems like she has some issue with that sick family of hers, and that this could make Agatha a target for something.”

Wanda listened carefully.

“I swear to you, Wanda, Alice was terrified when she told me that.” Jen took a deep breath, a few tears running down her face. Wanda leaned beside her, stunned. The two exchanged a look.

“You know I won’t be able to hide this from her, right?”

Jen nodded. “I know. But can we tell her tomorrow? When we get back from work? I don’t think now is the right moment.”

Wanda nodded.

“It won’t go past tomorrow.”

And she turned, walking away. Jen took a deep breath, throwing the two trash bags into the bin, and went back inside.

◆

 

Agatha took a deep breath as she stepped out of the shower and sat on the bed, still wrapped in the purple robe, the towel around her hair absorbing the moisture that kept sliding down her neck.

The room was quiet, but not empty—there was something there, an echo of what had happened earlier.

Jen’s reaction had been, at the very least, strange. Agatha tried not to think too much about it, choosing instead to blame it all on the exhaustion of the day.

It was easier that way.

With a low sigh, she picked up her phone and swiped across the screen until she opened her banking app. The number there made her pause for a moment.

25 thousand dollars. That was what she had saved.

It wasn’t much… but it also wasn’t nothing. It bought time. It bought some kind of security, even if fragile.

Luckily, Rio’s company health insurance was excellent. At least in that, she wasn’t completely lost, and most of the childbirth costs would be covered.

One less problem.

Agatha opened Venmo and transferred the equivalent of three months’ rent to Lília Calderu. She stared at the screen for a few seconds, as if that made everything more real, before sending the receipt.

The reply came quickly—some typical emojis from someone Lilia’s age, and nothing more. Agatha let out a small breath through her nose, dropping the phone beside her.

Her eyes wandered around the room until they landed on the stacked boxes in the corner. All closed, organized too carefully for something she was pretending she wasn’t doing.

She had started packing everything in silence throughout the week, always with the door locked, always avoiding any questions.

She still hadn’t had the courage to sit down with Wanda and Jen.

So she had already decided—tomorrow, when they left for work, she would finish taking her things out of the apartment. And the conversation…

The conversation would be at the end of the day. No escape, no postponing.

Agatha slowly stood up, undoing the knot of her robe and letting the fabric slip off her body. She picked up the bottle of moisturizer from the dresser.

She stopped in front of the mirror and looked at herself for a second too long. Then Agatha brought her hands to her belly, spreading the cream in slow, careful circular motions. The touch was almost automatic… until she felt the kick in response.

Agatha let out a small amused breath.

“Hello… someone decided to wake up.”

She continued the movement, softer now. She knew that, in the end, it might not prevent stretch marks. In her case, it seemed inevitable.

But still, she tried.

She finished, placed the bottle back on the dresser, and put on something comfortable to sleep—a light tank top and pajama pants. The soft fabric brought a small comfort she hadn’t realized she needed.

The omega walked to the bed and lay down, sinking her head into the pillow with a tired sigh, reaching out to the switch beside the nightstand.

During sleep, the discomfort came first as a distant unease, a low, persistent pressure, then—cramps.

Agatha shifted in bed, her body restless, trapped in a dream that made no sense, disconnected images, distorted voices, a constant feeling of loss. As if she were searching for something… or someone… and never finding it.

The emptiness, and that was what woke her.

Agatha opened her eyes suddenly, pulling in air sharply, her chest rising too fast. A few tears were already falling before she even understood why.

Her hand went straight to her belly.

“Hey…” she murmured, still a little breathless, her fingers pressing lightly, as if she could call back some response.

Nothing.

She frowned, rubbing her belly in circular motions, firmer now.

“Hey…” she repeated, her voice lower, more tense.

The silence inside her began to make noise.

Agatha quickly reached out, turning on the bedside lamp. The warm light filled the room, revealing her face already wet, her breathing uneven.

She kept trying, rubbing her belly, waiting for a movement from the babies, and nothing came. Panic hit fast.

“No… no, no…” she whispered, her voice breaking as tears streamed uncontrollably.

Agatha grabbed her phone with trembling hands, unlocking the screen almost on autopilot. She opened random videos, anything—any distraction to silence that rising panic.

But it didn’t help. Lorna’s voice came back, clear in her mind.

“They’ll move more… you’ll feel it.”

Agatha swallowed hard, her heart racing even faster.

“What’s wrong…?” she murmured, pressing her belly again, almost begging for an answer. “Why aren’t you moving…?”

The silence inside her was deafening.

She didn’t think, not for a second. Her fingers were already opening the contact before her mind caught up.

The call went through. Agatha only realized what she had done when it was already ringing.

“Hello? Agatha?” Rio answered quickly.

Her voice was low, soft… but alert. There was movement on the other end, the sound of a door opening, maybe wind, like she had just stepped out of somewhere. A gas station, a convenience store… something like that.

Agatha looked at the clock — it was 2:00 a.m., too late, or too early; honestly, it didn’t matter.

“I…” her voice broke in the middle of the sentence. She pressed her hand against her belly, trying to anchor herself to something.

“I miss you so much…” The words slipped out before she could stop them.

“I need you,” she continued, already crying openly now. “Something’s wrong… the babies… they’re not moving…”

“I’m coming,” the Alpha said without hesitation, without asking anything else. “Take a deep breath, Agatha. I’m already on my way.”

Agatha was still clutching the phone when she managed to breathe a little better between sobs. Her voice came out uneven, tangled in the crying.

“There’s… a key… under the doormat…”

On the other end, Rio didn’t hesitate.

“Okay.”

And she hung up.

Without wasting a second, Rio turned on her heel and went straight back inside the convenience store.

The sound of the door opening echoed sharply in the quiet of the night. She walked straight to the snack aisle, her eyes scanning quickly over the shelves until she found exactly what she was looking for.

Agatha’s favorites, the same ones she used to eat in secret at work, dodging the company’s ridiculous “healthy eating” policy.

Rio grabbed more than necessary.

Candy, some salty snacks, that cherry-flavored Coca-Cola Agatha liked, anything that might help.

She paid at the self-checkout with precise movements and stepped out again, the cold night air hitting her face.

Normally, she would have rushed straight to Agatha, ignored everything else, but something felt off. An instinct that this didn’t seem exactly like a medical emergency.

Still, she didn’t slow down.

The car practically flew through the empty streets, no traffic, no obstacles, just the sound of the engine cutting through the night as the city blurred past in streaks of light.

When she reached the building, Rio stepped out of the car in one fluid motion. The front door was open.

She paused for half a second.

She didn’t like that.

She didn’t like anything about it, not the unlocked door, not the idea of a key hidden under a doormat. The security of that place was… questionable, to say the least.

And that was where Agatha was. Where her pups were.

Rio exhaled through her nose, forcing down the irritation that wasn’t a priority right now.

She crouched, found the key exactly where Agatha had said, and unlocked the door carefully, stepping inside in silence.

As soon as she crossed the entrance, she found Jen standing in the middle of the kitchen, completely still, holding a wooden spoon like it was a weapon.

They stared at each other for a second.

“Fuck” Jen exhaled, her body relaxing all at once.

“Sorry,” Rio said softly, almost a whisper. “Agatha called me. Asked me to come… she wasn’t feeling well.”

Jen ran a hand over her face, still processing the scare, but nodded.

“Okay… okay. It’s fine.” Rio tilted her head slightly.

“Which room?”

Jen pointed down the hallway.

“The middle door.”

Rio nodded back.

“Thank you.” And moved without wasting another second. She stopped in front of the door for the briefest moment, knocking lightly.

“Agatha, I’m coming in.”

She pushed the door open.

The room was lit only by the bedside lamp. Agatha was in the middle of the bed, curled into herself, rubbing her belly over and over like that motion was the only thing keeping her together.

Her face was swollen, her eyes red—she looked devastated.

Rio crossed the room in a few quick steps and sat beside her.

“What is it, my love?”

The question came out soft, but full of care. Agatha tried to answer, but the sob came first.

“Something’s… something’s wrong…” her voice broke. “They’re not moving… it’s been hours…”

Rio placed the bag on the nightstand without taking her eyes off her.

“Can I?” she asked, gesturing to her belly.

Agatha nodded immediately, like she had been waiting for that.

She shifted on the bed, slipping off her shoes with her feet, moving further onto the mattress, making space.

Rio moved closer.

She placed one hand on each side of Agatha’s belly, her fingers spread with gentle firmness, starting slow, steady circular motions.

Rio kept the touch firm and consistent, the movements slow, as if she were trying to reach something beyond the skin.

A few seconds dragged on—then a movement came, subtle at first. A lazy stretch, like one of them was just shifting position… followed by a more defined kick from the other, quick and precise.

Agatha gasped.

Her whole body relaxed at once, as if someone had cut the string that had been holding her tense.

“Ah…” the sound slipped out, somewhere between relief and exhaustion.

She brought her own hand to where she had felt the movement, almost overlapping Rio’s, as if she needed to confirm it was real.

Another movement, and another—this time stronger, both of them moving as if, now that they had been “woken up,” they had decided to make up for the earlier silence.

Rio let out a quiet breath through her nose, the corner of her lips lifting slightly.

“I think we’ve got a pair of very heavy sleepers.”

Agatha let out a weak laugh, tears still on her face—but completely different from before.

“They almost gave me a heart attack…”

As if in response, the babies stirred even more. A longer movement on one side, another kick on the other, creating small ripples visible beneath the skin.

Agatha’s eyes widened slightly, staring at her own belly with a mix of fascination and disbelief.

“Hey… easy…” she murmured, almost laughing, her hand brushing over it.

Rio was still following the movements beneath Agatha’s skin when something in her gave in.

It was quick, a pure instinct.

Before she could think better of it, she leaned in and pressed her lips against the omega’s belly in a soft gesture, placing a few spaced kisses over Agatha’s stretched skin.

The touch didn’t last long—but it was enough.

Rio realized what she had done the very next second. Her body stiffened slightly, and she pulled back, straightening her posture as she ran a hand through her hair, visibly flustered.

“I…” she let out a quiet breath, avoiding Agatha’s gaze for a moment. “Sorry.”

Agatha was still looking at her, surprised, yes… but not uncomfortable.

There was something different in her gaze now. Calmer. More open.

“It’s okay,” she replied, her voice softer than before.

Rio nodded, still a bit stiff, as if she didn’t quite know where to put her hands now. Then she shifted slightly, looking for something safe.

“I brought some things for you to eat,” she said, trying to regain some control, nodding toward the bag on the nightstand.

Agatha gave a small smile.

But before Rio could pull away completely, she reached out and took her hand.

“Rio—”

The alpha stopped and looked back.

“I’m sorry for how I spoke earlier,” Agatha continued, her voice sincere, without defenses.

“I don’t think you’re a bad person… or selfish… or cruel.”

She gave her hand a gentle squeeze.

“I didn’t want you to think that. I know you were just worried about me.”

Rio held her gaze for a few seconds. Something there wavered. She nodded slowly.

“Don’t worry,” she replied, her tone lower. “I’m used to people thinking I’m a bit… difficult.”

Agatha frowned slightly.

“No,” she said firmly, shaking her head. “You don’t have to get used to a lie.”

Rio let out a restrained breath, looking away for a moment, as if that had hit somewhere she usually avoided.

“Yeah… it’s easier this way,” she murmured.

Agatha watched her for a second, then leaned in—the gesture simple and gentle.

She kissed Rio’s cheek softly, lingering just enough for the touch to be truly felt.

When she pulled back, she stayed close.

“You don’t have to live that lie,” Agatha said quietly, still close enough that every word felt more intimate than it should.

Rio held her gaze for a moment, then a faint smile curved her lips.

“I like being tough.”

There was almost a hint of teasing there—but not enough to hide the exhaustion behind it. Agatha slowly lifted her hand, brushing a strand of Rio’s hair away from her face, the gesture careful, almost automatic.

“Being tough is different from scaring everyone.”

Her fingers lingered for a second longer than necessary, lightly touching the alpha’s skin, as if testing how far she could go.

Rio didn’t pull away.

If anything, she softened.

Her eyes gentled in a way that would have gone unnoticed by anyone else, but not by Agatha.

“Honestly…” Rio began, her voice lower now, more sincere than comfortable. “You’re the only person I feel like being less tough with.”
