She let out a small breath, glancing away for a brief second before looking back.

“Actually… I want to spoil you every minute.”

A faint smile appeared, almost shy, as if the confession had caught even her off guard.

“Everything else feels… boring. People annoy me, or they’re incompetent around me… and it stresses me out.”

The honesty came raw, unfiltered. Agatha watched her in silence.

There was something dangerously honest there. Something that slipped past Rio’s usual control—and because of that, it hit deeper than it should.

“You say that like it’s a problem,” Agatha murmured, her lips curving slightly, but her gaze too attentive to be truly light.

Rio tilted her head, studying every detail of her face, as if trying to figure out how far she could go.

“And isn’t it?” her voice dropped again, less defensive now. “I don’t want to talk to anyone who isn’t you. I don’t want to think about anyone who isn’t you. I want to be with you.”

She let out a soft breath, almost a laugh restrained by her own awareness.

“I think Miss Harkness is taking up a considerable part of my thoughts, you know?”

Agatha took a deep breath. The hand still resting on Rio’s face slid slowly down to her arm, maintaining the contact as if it were necessary to hold that moment together.

She shifted slightly on the bed, gently pulling Rio by the hand without even fully realizing it.

“Stay here…” she asked, softer now, almost a tired whisper.

Rio didn’t answer with words. She simply moved, adjusting herself beside Agatha carefully, as if every movement had to respect an invisible boundary… one that had already been crossed.

She didn’t let go of her hand, not for a second. If anything, her thumb continued to move slowly over the omega’s skin, a steady, grounding touch.

Then, unhurried, Rio pulled her closer.

The gesture was firm and careful.

Agatha didn’t resist, she let herself be guided, her body yielding with a kind of ease that said more than words ever could.

She fit against Rio, finding space in her arms as if that place already belonged to her—even if it shouldn’t.

Her head rested against the alpha’s chest, the warmth immediate, grounding.

Rio adjusted her arm around her, holding her completely, her free hand slowly moving up and down Agatha’s back in a continuous caress.

Agatha exhaled slowly, her muscles finally relaxing for real.

Right there, in that embrace, she could hear the rhythm of Rio’s heartbeat and for a moment, that was enough to quiet everything else.

The alpha rested her chin lightly on top of Agatha’s head, closing her eyes for a brief moment while her hand drifted to the younger woman’s belly, who now slept peacefully in her arms.

 

◆

 

Rio opened her eyes slowly when the morning light began to filter through the lilac curtains in Agatha’s room, tinting the space with a soft glow—almost too delicate for everything that existed there.

The warmth was still there, Agatha’s body resting against hers, but something was different.

That’s when she noticed the room wasn’t exactly… alive.

There were cardboard boxes stacked in the corner, some already sealed with tape, others just hastily closed, as if they had been packed at the very edge of time… or courage.

Clothes that once filled the space were no longer there. Small empty gaps spread throughout the room—silent, but obvious.

Rio stayed quiet for a few seconds, taking it all in. Then she carefully slid her hand over Agatha’s shoulder.

The touch was enough to wake her.

Agatha opened her eyes slowly, letting out a small yawn, still wrapped in that lazy state between sleep and wakefulness.

“Good morning…” she murmured, her voice hoarse, low.

“Good morning,” Rio replied, just as softly.

She brushed a strand of Agatha’s hair away from her face, her fingers lingering a little longer than necessary, as if the gesture itself was also a way to think about what to say next.

Her eyes drifted back to the room, to the boxes—and then, finally, she said:

“You’re moving out?”

The question came out quietly. Agatha hesitated for only a second before nodding.

“I am…” she replied, her voice still calm, but now more awake.

“I haven’t told them yet. I’ve been keeping my room locked…” she let out a small sigh, glancing toward the corner filled with boxes.

“The moving truck arrives today. It’s taking everything to the new apartment. It’s close to here… and tonight I’ll talk to them.”

Rio nodded slowly, absorbing every detail as her fingers began tracing slow movements along Agatha’s back—a continuous, silent caress.

“Do you think they’ll be mad?”

Agatha gave a small, humorless smile.

“Yes…” she answered honestly. “But I can’t turn their lives into chaos. They have their own routines… they don’t need to deal with two babies changing everything.”

Rio didn’t respond right away.

She just kept the gentle motion, up and down Agatha’s back with a near-methodical calm, as if organizing thoughts she didn’t want to say out loud.

“You made the right choice,” she said at last, with enough firmness that it didn’t sound like empty reassurance.

Agatha exhaled slowly, her body fully relaxing when Rio leaned in and placed a soft kiss on her forehead.

A few hours later, the apartment already felt awake.

Agatha was sitting at the breakfast table, a bowl of fruit in front of her, eating slowly, still processing everything the day carried. Her hair was tied up messily, her face lighter… but distant.

Wanda was leaning against the counter, scrolling through her phone with a level of focus that didn’t match the early hour. 

Jen, on the other hand, seemed more present and finishing a plate of pancakes, carefully drizzling honey on top.

The atmosphere was ordinary and familiar, the usual monotony, until the bathroom door opened.

Rio appeared, subtly adjusting the sleeve of the suit she was wearing, a piece that clearly wasn’t hers. 

The cut felt almost too classic trying to be modern, an elegant excess that flirted dangerously with tacky… and yet, it still worked on her.

It reminded her of the Big character costume from Sexy and the City, the one Alice had bought and kept showing her repeatedly at work, talking about how she’d wear it to the party to match with Jen.

Wanda froze.

Her eyes widened the exact moment Rio crossed the small living room space as if she already belonged there. Without ceremony, she leaned in and kissed Agatha’s cheek.

“I’m heading out. We’ll talk later, okay?”

Agatha just nodded—far too natural for someone being watched like an inexplicable event. There was even a brief kiss between them, quick, but intimate enough to make it clear that this wasn’t new.

“And Jen… thanks for lending me the outfit.”

Jen blinked a few times before responding, still processing.

“No problem…” she said, nodding slightly. “Luckily, you and Alice are almost the same size.”

Rio nodded, picked up her bag calmly, and left, as if that entrance, and exit—hadn’t caused a small collapse in the room.

The door closed, and Wanda was the first to react.

“What… just happened?”

Jen let out a small nervous laugh, resting her hands on the counter.

“I woke up thinking we were being robbed,” she said, still in disbelief. “But it was just Rio Vidal walking in here at two in the morning like she knew exactly where the key was.”

Both of them turned their attention to the omega. Agatha finished another spoonful of fruit before standing up, picking up the bowl calmly, as if nothing there was urgent.

A small smile appeared.

“Hormonal crisis.”

And she walked away, leaving behind two people who definitely didn’t accept that answer—but also had no idea where to even begin.

 

 

Chapter 12: The Winner Takes All

Chapter Notes

Good reading!

 

 

┕──━──━─┑◆┍─━──━──┙

Agatha let out a soft sigh as she placed a small cardboard box on the kitchen counter of the new apartment, resting her hands for a second against the cold surface as if she needed to ground herself there.

The space still smelled like fresh paint mixed with the faint odor of wood and moving-day dust.

Agatha’s new apartment had a wide living room directly connected to the open kitchen, creating one continuous space where natural light poured easily through the large windows, illuminating the pale floor and the still almost empty rooms.

On the right side, a small hallway led to the two bedrooms — side by side — and to the newly renovated bathroom, whose light tiles and new metal fixtures contrasted with the rest of the older building. It was simple, but functional. And, above all, quiet in a different kind of way.

Rio walked in right after, carrying three boxes at once, her strong arms supporting the weight effortlessly.

She crossed the living room without difficulty and left everything inside one of the bedrooms, organizing the boxes on the floor with more care than seemed necessary. When she came back a few minutes later, she let out a controlled sigh, quickly running a hand over the back of her neck.

Agatha watched that with a small smile before turning toward the sink. She opened the faucet, filled a glass with cold water, and walked back to her, offering it out. Rio took it without hesitation and drank it in a few gulps, as if only then realizing she was thirsty.

“You didn’t have to come. The delivery guys could’ve carried everything upstairs,” Agatha said, carefully taking the glass back.

Rio leaned down slightly, pressing a quick kiss to her cheek before answering, the closeness still carrying something far too natural.

“I wasn’t going to leave you alone with those two strangers.”

The tone was light, but there was something territorial there, subtle and firm at the same time.

Before Agatha could answer, a knock at the open door caught both their attention. Lilia stepped into the apartment with calm movements, her attentive gaze scanning every corner like someone used to evaluating more than appearances alone.

“Good morning,” she said, adjusting her purse on her shoulder while observing the boxes. “Looks like everything’s already here, right?”

Agatha nodded, crossing her arms for a second before uncrossing them.

“Yeah, Rio and the movers already brought everything up.” She made a small gesture with her hand. “Rio, this is Lilia, my landlady… and Lilia, this is Rio, my…”

“Girlfriend,” Rio completed naturally, extending her hand toward Lilia.

The handshake was firm, confident.

Lilia smiled faintly, but her eyes quickly flicked toward Agatha — and there it was. Happiness. Far too obvious to hide.

“Pleasure to meet you, Rio,” she said before pulling out her clipboard. “Agatha, I’m going to check the bathroom to make sure everything’s in order, alright?”

“Alright, thank you.”

Lilia walked past them toward the hallway, the sound of her footsteps echoing softly until disappearing into the back of the apartment.

As soon as they were alone again, Agatha slowly turned toward Rio, leaning one hip against the counter, her arms loosely crossed. Her eyes carried a curious, almost amused glint.

“Girlfriend?” she asked.

Rio smiled, approaching slowly until she stood directly in front of her. The distance disappeared naturally, her body pressing against Agatha’s with an intimacy that didn’t ask permission.

“Yeah… do you like it?” she murmured, lifting a hand to Agatha’s chin and stroking it slowly.

Agatha let out a small sigh, tilting her face lightly into the touch.

“Of course I do… but when exactly did we reach that status?”

Rio made a slight pout, as if genuinely thinking about it, her eyes drifting upward for a second before returning to her.

“I think calling me at two in the morning so I could come to your bedroom…” she started, her voice low and amused, “…and me definitely going and staying with you all night… counts for a lot already.”

She made a subtle gesture with her fingers, like quotation marks in the air.

“Oh, and a few other ‘little things’ too.”

Agatha lightly smacked her arm, but the smile didn’t last long. Her expression gradually changed, becoming more serious, more cautious.

“And Jessica?” she asked quietly.

Rio didn’t look away.

She had already expected that.

“I scheduled a conversation with her,” she answered firmly, without hesitation. “We haven’t really been in a relationship for a long time. I need to be honest with her… and she needs to be honest with herself too. That relationship is over.”

Agatha felt the weight of that immediately. Her shoulders tensed slightly, and her gaze dropped for a second. The guilt came automatically, an old familiar feeling.

Rio noticed instantly.

Carefully, she lifted a hand to Agatha’s face, raising it gently, her thumb brushing softly along her chin.

“It’s not your fault,” she said quietly, holding her gaze. “My relationship with Jessica already had problems before I even met you. It… shouldn’t have started in the first place.”

Agatha nodded, but the tears still came, silent, betraying every attempt at control. Rio didn’t pull away and lowered her hands to Agatha’s shoulders, squeezing lightly.

“I only got into that relationship because it was convenient for my ideals…” she continued, softer now, “…but I never wanted to be with her. We have nothing in common.”

Agatha breathed deeply, feeling that weight begin to dissolve, even if slowly.

“All the conversations we have… all our moments…” Rio continued, her voice quieter, “none of that ever existed with Jessica.”

She carefully took Agatha’s hand and brought it to her lips, kissing the back of it delicately. Then she lingered there for a second longer, discreetly inhaling the scent of her skin mixed with moisturizer.

“You made me see life in a way I’d never seen before…”

Agatha blinked quickly, trying to hold back the tears, with very little success.

Rio leaned in and kissed her lips briefly, softly, wiping away the tear that slid down afterward with her thumb.

“But I want to be honest with you…” she continued, more serious now, “…and I’ll understand if you don’t want me around.”

Agatha frowned slightly, confused.

“What… are you talking about?”

Rio took a deep breath before answering, as if organizing something difficult inside herself.

“I want you, Agatha. More than anything.” Her voice came out lower, heavier. “I get sick at the idea of someone else having you…”

She paused briefly, steadying her breathing.

“But if you want this relationship… you’re going to have to deal, sometimes, with the problem that is my family. Especially my grandmother.”

Her gaze remained firm.

“And I wouldn’t drag you into that without making it clear.”

Agatha nodded slowly, absorbing everything, her chest rising and falling with heavier breaths.

“You don’t have to answer me now,” Rio added, softer.

But Agatha didn’t answer with words.

She simply stepped forward and hugged her tightly, wrapping herself around Rio like she needed that contact to stay whole.

Rio relaxed immediately into the embrace, her arms surrounding Agatha without hesitation, her hands moving slowly up and down her back in comforting motions.

Agatha buried her face against her chest, breathing deeply, her racing heartbeat pounding against Rio’s body, and little by little… she calmed down.

After a few minutes, the two shared a kiss.

“I have to go, but I’ll come back, okay?”

Agatha nodded, wiping her tears away.

“See you later.”

The omega smiled at the alpha, who walked out the door wearing the same usual blazer she wore every day.

◆

Rio crossed the top floor of the company with her eyes fixed on her phone screen, her firm footsteps echoing through the silent hallway.

Weeks ago, she had hired a detective to investigate details that simply didn’t fit into her grandmother’s will, and the more information surfaced, the clearer it became that there was something deeply wrong with that story.

She had recently discovered that the document had been altered shortly before Claire’s death. Originally, the succession had been direct and the position would automatically pass to the next alpha in the bloodline, something consistent with the family’s old rules.

However, a new clause had been inserted: the figure of a “regent,” someone who would assume control until the heir came of age. And the strangest part — this regent didn’t need to be an alpha.

That changed everything.

In practice, it meant that her own mother had been deliberately removed from power by her own mother. Vetoed. Excluded from a position that, under the previous rules, could have belonged to her.

“Rio.”

The voice pulled her back to reality. She looked up, finding Jessica standing in front of her.

“Jessica.”

The woman smiled faintly and stepped closer, leaning in to kiss her, but Rio discreetly turned away — enough not to be rude, yet impossible to ignore. Jessica noticed immediately. The air between them changed.

Before it could turn into something bigger right there, Rio lifted a hand gently and gestured down the hall.

“Come with me.”

She guided her into the office, closing the door behind them. The room was spacious, organized with almost clinical precision, the sofas positioned in front of the coffee table where she usually met with investors. Everything there projected control.

“Rio, what’s going on?” Jessica asked, her voice already carrying tension.

Rio didn’t answer immediately. She walked to the side bar, poured a glass of water, and handed it to her calmly. Jessica took the glass and drank in quick gulps, as if trying to steady herself.

“First… thank you for coming,” Rio said, sitting down.

Jessica didn’t sit immediately.

“Don’t treat me like one of your business deals,” she shot back, her eyes fixed on her. “What’s happening?”

Rio took a deep breath before answering. She placed her own glass on the coffee table, resting her forearms on her knees for a moment, as if arranging the words.

“This doesn’t make sense anymore, Jessica.”

The silence that followed was heavy.

“W… what?” she stammered, even though she knew exactly what this was about.

Rio looked up.

“Our relationship wasn’t the same even before… and now we’re distant. I don’t want to keep doing this. I don’t want to trap you in something that’s already over. You deserve someone who loves you every day, who wants to be with you…”

She paused briefly, firm.

“And that person isn’t me.”

Jessica remained still for several seconds, as if her body hadn’t yet caught up to what her mind already understood.

“You’re… breaking up with me?” she asked, gripping the glass tightly between her fingers.

“Yes,” Rio answered without looking away. “If you want, I can still go with you to your appointments… but we can’t keep pretending this still exists.”

Jessica took a deep breath, but the air didn’t seem to be enough.

“Who is she, Rio?” she asked, wrapping her arms around herself as she stood up.

Tears started falling before she even realized it. Rio stood too, maintaining the distance.

“Please… this isn’t happiness.”

Jessica laughed without humor, slowly stepping closer.

“I changed my entire life for you,” she said, her voice trembling before turning harder. “I did everything for you!”

The shove came right after, her hands striking Rio’s chest. It wasn’t strong, but it carried everything she was failing to control. Rio grabbed her arms firmly, preventing another movement, maintaining control even while Jessica exploded.

“You know, Rio…” she continued, breathless, her eyes red. “The only thing that comforts me is that you think you have a choice.”

Her voice rose, loaded with anger.

“When in reality, everything in your life is already written! Even your defeats are scripted!”

Rio blinked slowly, absorbing the words. There was loss of control there, but also something else. Something that didn’t seem to come only from pain.

Jessica yanked her arms free.

“No one is ever going to love you. Ever,” she said, her voice cracking somewhere between rage and tears. “You know why? Because you’re a weak version of what your grandmother was. Nothing about you is genuine, Rio. Nothing ever will be.”

The silence that followed fell heavy. Rio took a deep breath, her face unreadable, but her eyes attentive.

“Are you done?” she asked in a low, controlled tone. “You’re creating an unpleasant situation. I think you should leave.”

Jessica didn’t answer immediately. The silence lasted only a moment before breaking violently. The movement came loaded with pure rage and loss of control, her hands going straight at Rio with enough force to try and knock her down. Rio reacted fast, catching her wrists in the middle of the impact, supporting both their weight while stepping back to keep her balance.

“Jessica, enough—”

But she wasn’t listening.

She struggled, tried to break free, shoved again, her breathing uneven, her eyes full of a dangerous mixture of pain and fury. In the middle of the struggle, Rio’s shoulder lightly hit the side of the couch, and it was enough. Rio’s wallet slipped from the inside pocket of her suit and fell to the floor.

The dry sound seemed far too loud in that moment. Rio immediately let go of one of Jessica’s hands, lowering herself on instinct to grab it, but Jessica was faster.

She pulled free, bent down, and grabbed the wallet first.

“What’s this?” she asked, already opening it without permission.

“Jessica, give me that.” Rio’s voice came harsher now as she stepped forward.

But it was too late. The small photo was there, the ultrasound from weeks ago that Lorna had given Rio. Jessica froze for a second, her eyes scanning the image in silence, trying to process what she was seeing. Then she slowly lifted her gaze to Rio.

“…what is this?”

The question came out quieter, but far more dangerous.

Rio extended her hand.

“Get out. Now.”

There was no more room for negotiation in her voice. Jessica didn’t give it back immediately. On the contrary, a slow, crooked smile began to form on her face as she calmly closed the wallet with deliberate slowness.

“Oh…” she murmured, as if she had finally found something that made sense.

Rio stepped forward and ripped the wallet from her hand firmly, her jaw locked tight. Jessica simply let go of it without resistance, still smiling, then picked up her purse with movements far too calm for someone who had been completely out of control seconds earlier. 

She wiped away a tear still sliding down her face, slightly smudging her makeup.

“For your sake, Rio…” she said, adjusting the strap on her shoulder, “I hope the whore you got pregnant is a Rockefeller.”

“Or your grandmother will make every single day of her life miserable.”

“GET THE FUCK OUT!” Rio shouted, her voice finally breaking through the control.

The door opened almost immediately. The security guard entered, alert, his eyes moving from one woman to the other.

“Is everything alright, ma’am?”

Rio was breathing heavily now, trying to regain composure.

“Escort Miss Jones out.”

The tone returned to cold, direct control. Jessica laughed quietly, walking past the guard without resistance, as if she had already gotten exactly what she wanted.

The two of them left, and the door closed.

The silence returned, heavy and suffocating. Rio remained motionless for a few seconds, then slowly walked to the couch and let herself collapse onto it, her body finally giving in.

She buried her hands in her hair, lightly tugging at the strands as she lowered her head forward, her eyes squeezed shut.

The office door opened without ceremony.

Alice walked in quickly, her footsteps echoing against the floor as her eyes searched the room for Rio.

The moment she found her collapsed on the couch, still hunched over with her hands tangled in her own hair, she immediately frowned.

“Rio, what the fuck happened here?” Her voice came firm, filled with urgency. “What the hell was that?”

Rio didn’t answer immediately. It took a few seconds before she lifted her face, her eyes still distant, as if she were trying to fully return to that moment.

“She knows…” she murmured, her voice lower than usual.

Alice stepped forward.

“Who?”

“Jessica.” Rio swallowed hard, running a hand over her face. “She knows that… that someone is going to have my child.”

The silence weighed heavily for a second.

“How?” Alice asked, more direct now.

Rio took a deep breath, trying to organize the words.

“I keep a copy of the ultrasound in my wallet…” she said, her jaw tightening. “She saw it.”

Alice closed her eyes for a brief moment, processing far too quickly.

“On the exact day I ended everything…” Rio continued, the frustration now evident in her voice. “She’s going to tell my grandmother.”

Alice stepped closer, grabbing Rio’s shoulders firmly, forcing her to face reality without escape.

“Then you need to tell Agatha. Now.”

Rio blinked, as if the idea were still trying to settle into place.

“Alice—”

“Now, Rio,” she reinforced without softening. “She needs to hear it from you, not from anyone else.”

Rio’s gaze faltered for a second before she nodded.

It was inevitable.

She stood up at once, still somewhat disoriented, grabbing the blazer from the couch and putting it on with automatic movements, as if her body were acting before her mind could catch up.

“Go,” Alice said, already stepping back a little. “I’ll take care of everything here.”

Rio didn’t answer. She simply turned and left the office, her steps faster now, her breathing still heavy. The hallway seemed longer than ever. She pressed the elevator button hard, her finger digging into the metal as if she could force time to move faster.

The doors still took a few seconds to open. Rio stepped inside the elevator and pressed the garage level button on the panel. She reached the garage quickly and walked fast toward the car parked in its usual spot.

She got in quickly, pressing the ignition button and turning the wheel to get out of there. The moment she exited the garage, she was met with heavy rain crashing violently against the hood of the car.

“Fuck.” Rio slammed her hand against the steering wheel. Both the rain and the traffic building because of it were unbelievably inconvenient.

◆

Agatha closed the last backpack in the old bedroom, pulling the zipper all the way shut with a dry sound that seemed to echo more than it should have in the empty space. She stood there for a few seconds, just looking around.

The walls, once filled with life, now seemed too bare. Too silent.

There was nothing left there holding her back.

She took a deep breath.

The omega slipped the backpack strap over one shoulder and left, carefully closing the door behind her as if that gesture were ending more than just a room. She walked into the living room and dropped the backpack onto the couch, her body already starting to feel heavy from the exhaustion accumulated throughout the day.

Without thinking much about it, she headed into the kitchen.

The apartment still carried its familiar smell — old coffee, cleaning products, something faintly sweet coming from forgotten packages — and it tightened her chest for a moment. She grabbed a glass, filled it with water, and drank too quickly, as if trying to swallow down the anxiety beginning to rise with it.

When she returned to the couch, she barely had time to sit properly.

The door burst open.

“Fuck, what a nightmare! I’ve never been stuck in traffic this bad in my life!” Jen came in first, clearly irritated, shrugging the wet coat off her shoulders.

Wanda came right behind her, closing the door with her foot while trying to balance the soaked umbrella.

“You picked the perfect day to take time off,” she commented, dropping the umbrella and walking to the counter, where she left a bag of Chinese food.

Agatha forced a small smile, still holding the glass between her hands.

“Yeah… this rain came out of nowhere.”

Her voice didn’t come out as steady as she wanted it to.

Wanda and Jen were still distracted, taking off their shoes, complaining about the water flooding the streets, completely unaware of the weight beginning to grow in the room.

Agatha took another deep breath.

This time, there was no way to postpone it.

“Girls…” she called, her voice quieter, but enough to catch both of their attention. “We need to talk.”

“Is everything okay?” Wanda asked, her tone already different now, more attentive.

She and Jen exchanged a quick look, one of those silent looks that carry more than any sentence ever could, and Agatha took a deep breath before making a small gesture toward the couch.

“Sit down, please.”

The two obeyed, still confused, but now clearly alert. Jen crossed her arms, leaning back against the couch, while Wanda leaned slightly forward, resting her elbows on her knees.

Agatha remained standing for a second, as if she needed to gather courage, then slowly sat down in the armchair across from them. Her hands intertwined in her lap.

“I…” she started, but her voice failed. She cleared her throat, trying again. “I’m very lucky to have you.”

Wanda frowned slightly.

Agatha continued, her gaze shifting between both of them.

“Seriously. You’re… everything to me. You always have been.”

An uncomfortable silence began to grow.

“Wanda…” she turned her face slightly more toward her friend. “We’ve known each other since we were kids. You know things about me that nobody else does… you’ve been there for every important moment in my life.”

Wanda didn’t answer, but her expression softened for a moment.

“And you, Jen…” Agatha continued, now looking at the other woman, “you found me at the worst possible moment and… you saved me. Truly.”

Jen uncrossed her arms, but said nothing.

“I love both of you. So much. And… I’ll never forget anything we built here.”

“Agatha…” Jen interrupted, her voice firmer now. “What’s going on?”

That was the final push she needed.

Agatha nodded slowly, leaning down to grab the bag beside the couch. From inside it, she pulled out an envelope and placed it on the coffee table, sliding it slightly toward them.

“In here…” she said, keeping her voice controlled, “is my share of the rent for the next three months.”

Wanda frowned immediately.

“Rent? What do you mean, ‘rent’?”

The silence lasted a second. Jen looked at the envelope. Then at the backpack on the couch. Then back at Agatha, and finally said it, directly:

“She’s leaving.”

Wanda let out a short, disbelieving laugh as she was already getting to her feet.

“No… no, this is ridiculous.”

She crossed the living room in quick steps, opened the door to Agatha’s old bedroom—

And stopped.

The emptiness of the room answered for her. Wanda stood there for a second, staring at nothing, as if it were some kind of mistake that could be fixed if she blinked enough times.

Behind her, Jen stood more slowly, her expression hardening.

“Why didn’t you tell us?” she asked, her voice low, but heavy.

Agatha took a deep breath, feeling the weight of every stare on her.

“Because…” she started, choosing her words carefully, “because no matter how much you say it’s not a problem… I know it is.”

She lifted her gaze.

“I don’t have the right to completely change both of your lives just because I’m going to have children.”

Wanda turned her head immediately, still standing in the bedroom doorway, her eyes already shining with frustration.

“You have got to be kidding me,” she said, her voice rising. “You didn’t tell me!”

She walked back into the living room in quick strides.

“We’ve been friends for years, Agatha! Years!”

“I tried to minimize everything as much as possible—”

“And you managed to make everything worse!” Wanda cut her off before she could finish. “You just decide to leave and say nothing? Nothing?!”

The atmosphere turned heavy, far too tense.

Jen, who until then had been more restrained, finally exploded.

“The bare minimum was telling us!” she said, her voice firm, louder now. “We were there for you through the worst and the best moments!”

She took a step forward.

“And now you just shove us aside because you decided you’re going to solve everything alone? Again with this bullshit ‘I can handle everything myself’ syndrome?”

“That’s not it—”

“That’s exactly what it is!” Jen shot back without hesitation.

Agatha shook her head, feeling her chest tighten.

“It’s not…” she repeated, quieter now, trying to stay steady. “I just… don’t want to be a burden to you.”

“No,” Jen cut in immediately, stepping closer, her eyes fixed on Agatha. “You decided that for both of us without even talking to us.”

The silence weighed heavily for a second.

“You based all of this on something that simply doesn’t exist,” she continued, her voice filled with frustration. “No one here asked you to leave.”

Agatha shook her head, already feeling the tension rising again.

“You say that now, but—”

“But nothing, Agatha!” Jen interrupted, raising her voice. “You didn’t even give us the chance to decide!”

“I’m trying not to fuck up your lives!” Agatha shot back, standing too, her control beginning to slip.

“And who said you’re fucking anything up?!” Jen fired back, already out of patience. “That’s all in your head!”

The two of them were standing face-to-face now, the air between them almost palpable with tension.

“I can’t just throw two babies into the middle of your routines!” Agatha insisted, her eyes shining. “It’s not fair!”

“What’s not fair,” Jen snapped, “is you deciding everything alone like we’re not part of your life!”

Agatha opened her mouth to answer, but Jen was faster.

“You have this ridiculous syndrome where you think you have to solve everything alone!”

“That’s not true—”

“Yes, it is!” Jen took another step forward, now clearly out of control. “You’re like fucking Elsa, isolating yourself in an ice castle pretending that solves anything, you can’t even see the truth right in fucking front of you.”

Wanda immediately realized where this was heading.

“Enough.” She stepped between them quickly, one hand stretched toward each side, creating space before things got even worse.

“That’s enough, both of you.”

But the damage had already been done.

Agatha was breathing fast, her chest rising and falling while her eyes burned. Without saying another word, she turned sharply, grabbing the backpack off the couch with a rough movement.

“Agatha—”

But she didn’t stop.

She walked to the door and left, slamming it hard behind her.

The sound echoed through the entire apartment. Wanda ran a hand over her face, slowly exhaling, trying to pull herself together.

“You almost fucked everything up,” she said, still staring at the closed door.

Jen crossed her arms, her chest still rising quickly from the argument.

“I don’t care,” she answered coldly. “She’s not a child anymore.”

Without waiting for a response, she turned and walked to her bedroom, entering and slamming the door right after. Wanda remained alone in the living room, her gaze still fixed on the door Agatha had walked out through.

◆

Agatha entered the apartment completely soaked, the umbrella dripping while she struggled slightly to close it by the entrance.

The door shut behind her with a muffled sound, and the silence she found inside seemed far too loud, deafening.

Agatha swallowed hard, running a hand over her face to wipe away the tears mixed with rainwater. Her chest still rose and fell unevenly, as if her body hadn’t understood that it was already over.

She didn’t stop in the living room.

She headed straight for the bathroom, her steps slightly rushed, as if she needed to escape the heaviness in the air. She pushed the door open and closed it behind her, leaning against the wood for a second before moving again.

Agatha started taking off her clothes with almost automatic movements, the wet fabric clinging to her skin before falling onto the cold floor. There was no care to it, no organization.

She stepped into the shower still breathless.

The hot water took a second to stabilize, but when it came, it hit heavily against her shoulders, running down her body, taking away the cold from the rain… but not the weight of what had happened.

The omega closed her eyes and braced her hands against the wall, her forehead resting against the tiles while the water streamed through her hair, down her face, down her neck.

Her breathing slowly began to steady, but even then, the words from the argument and the fight were still there, trapped inside her mind.

Repeating every second like a private hell.

She let out a trembling sigh, letting the water continue to fall as if, at some point, it would be enough to wash away everything she couldn’t shut off inside herself.

A few minutes later, water was still dripping down Agatha’s body. She turned off the shower, remaining there for a few seconds, forehead still pressed against the tile, trying to steady her breathing, trying to silence her own mind.

Then a sound pulled Agatha back to reality.

The doorbell.

Repeated, insistent, far too loud for the still-empty apartment.

Agatha frowned, immediately feeling her heart accelerate.

“I’m coming!” she tried to say, but her voice came out weak, muffled.

The doorbell didn’t stop.

She hurried out of the bathroom, still wet, pulling the robe tightly around her body and wrapping the towel around her hair with quick, almost clumsy movements.

The sound continued, urgent and repetitive, cutting through the air like an alarm.

Agatha walked quickly to the door, opened it while gripping the digital reader of the electronic handle, and saw Rio standing there, completely drenched.

Rainwater still ran down her face, through the dark strands of hair stuck to her forehead, down her jaw and neck. Her clothes clung to her body, heavy, dark, as if she had crossed the entire storm without once thinking about stopping.

“Fuck… you scared me,” Agatha let out, still breathless, her hand lightly tightening against the wood of the door.

Rio didn’t answer.

For a second, she just stood there, breathing deeply, as if she had run a distance that wasn’t physical.

Then she slowly stepped closer, the alpha’s hand lifting to the omega’s face, touching her skin with a gentleness that completely contrasted with the state she was in.

Agatha swallowed hard.

“Rio… you’re scaring me,” she murmured, her voice quieter now, almost breaking.

Rio blinked, as if returning to herself.

“Sorry…” she said, still somewhat lost, her eyes fixed on Agatha’s face.

Agatha took a deep breath, closing her eyes for a second, unconsciously leaning slightly into the touch. The warmth of her body, still from the shower, contrasting with the coldness of Rio’s skin.

“They’re mine… aren’t they?”

Rio’s voice came out far too low, breaking in the middle, as if she weren’t used to asking, much less fearing the answer.

For a second, Agatha forgot how to breathe.

Because it wasn’t just a question.

It was something Agatha had spent weeks ignoring. It was exactly what Jen had said — the truth that had been right in front of her the entire time, and even then she kept running from the answer that was obvious, that had been sitting right beneath her nose.

The omega opened her mouth, but nothing came out. Her gaze faltered, looking away for a brief second.

“Agatha…”

Her name came out even softer.

That shattered whatever last attempt Agatha had to keep herself together. The air left her lungs in a trembling breath, her body giving an inch forward, as if she were too exhausted to keep carrying all of this alone.

She shook her head faintly, her eyes already completely full.

“I…” Her voice failed. “I think so…”

She gave in, finally stopping running from herself and from the truth, as if admitting it hurt more than any outside confirmation ever could.

Agatha’s shoulders collapsed at the exact same moment, and right after that the sob broke out of her without any control at all, raw and violent and without the slightest attempt to hold it back.

Agatha’s entire body crumbled against Rio’s, her hands clutching the soaked fabric of Rio’s clothes as if it were the only thing stopping her from simply falling apart.

Rio reacted in the same second.

She pulled Agatha against her with force, leaving no space, no distance, wrapping herself around her body as if she were protecting something far bigger than just that moment.

Rio stepped inside the apartment with her almost without realizing it, the door slamming shut behind them while the embrace tightened even more. The contrast was brutal between Agatha still warm from the shower and Rio cold, drenched, rainwater still running down her skin.

Neither of them pulled away.

Agatha cried against Rio’s chest, the sound muffled, uneven, loaded with everything she had been trying to carry alone for far too long.

Rio felt it.

She felt every tremor and every broken breath, every piece of fear Agatha couldn’t put into words.

Rio’s tears came without permission, silent, mixing with the rainwater on her face while she held Agatha even tighter against herself, merging the two of them there, in that exact place, burying her face into Agatha’s hair for a second as if she needed to convince herself that this was real.

◆

The storm outside seemed unwilling to let up. Rain hammered hard against the apartment windows, creating a constant sound that filled every space of silence still left inside.

In bed, the two of them were finally quiet.

Agatha remained completely curled against Rio, her face hidden in her neck, breathing slowly now, though still uneven from time to time, as if her body were still processing everything that had collapsed minutes earlier. Her robe hung half open, forgotten.

Rio, meanwhile, no longer carried the weight of the rain on her body, only traces of it left in her slightly damp hair. She was lying on her side, holding Agatha naturally, as if that fit between them were instinctive and inevitable.

She had removed all her clothes and was wearing only her boxers, feeling the warmth of the omega’s body. 

One of her hands remained firm against Agatha’s back while the other slowly moved over Agatha’s stomach, the alpha’s hand tracing constant, careful circular motions.

Her fingers slightly lessened their pressure when they felt a small movement beneath the skin.

Rio stopped for a second.

Her breathing faltered, almost imperceptibly, then resumed, even slower now, as if she were afraid of losing that feeling.

Agatha answered with a soft sigh against her neck, feeling her body relax a little more, one of her hands lightly gripping the side of Rio’s waist.

“They’re awake…” Rio murmured, her voice low, too close against Agatha’s skin.

Agatha let out a faint breath against Rio’s skin, her face still near her neck, her voice low, almost like a secret shared only between them.

“They always wake up when you’re close…”

Slowly, she pulled back just enough to look at Rio’s face, her eyes still damp, but calmer now.

Rio held her gaze for a moment, as if that required more from her than words. The hand that had been resting on Agatha’s stomach slowly moved upward, passing over her torso until it reached her face. 

Her fingers touched her skin carefully, almost reverently, brushing a damp strand of hair away from her forehead.

“How long have you known?” Agatha asked quietly.

Rio exhaled slowly. There was no more room for omission.

“For a few months…” she began, her voice lower, more honest than usual. “I… started putting the pieces together. And I remembered you in Ibiza.”

She paused briefly, her thumb still brushing lightly against Agatha’s cheek.

“Actually… I never forgot.”

Her eyes drifted for a second, as if revisiting something distant.

“Your scent…” she continued more quietly. “That’s what made me cross an entire party. I didn’t even know what I was looking for until I realized it was you.”

Agatha didn’t look away.

“And… the day I became sure,” Rio continued, “was when you went to the hospital… on Jen’s birthday.”

A brief silence settled between them.

“Alice told me,” she finished.

Agatha blinked in confusion.

“Alice…?”

“Jen told her you were pregnant,” Rio explained calmly. “The day I… remembered you in Ibiza, I called Alice. She was on a date with Jen and ended up bringing her to my apartment.”

Agatha frowned slightly, trying to follow.

“She overheard our conversation…” Rio continued. “She connected the dots. She argued with Alice when she left… and ended up telling her.”

Agatha took a deep breath.

“So… she knew it was you…” she murmured. “And she didn’t tell me.”

Rio nodded slowly, watching every micro reaction on her face.

“Are you angry?” she asked with rare caution.

Agatha took a second before answering.

“No…” she said finally, her voice lower, but steady. “She did the right thing. You were the one who had to come talk to me… but I’m surprised.”

Rio seemed to absorb that, but she still didn’t fully relax.

Agatha continued, something heavier now lingering beneath her words.

“But… after you knew everything… why didn’t you come find me?”

The question stayed between them, with no escape or distraction.

Rio nodded faintly, as if she had already expected it.

“I tried…” she said, almost in a whisper. “But I was a coward.”

Her gaze dropped for a second, something rare, almost uncomfortable.

“Alice told me she asked you about the party list… that you were afraid of knowing, of living up to someone else’s expectations,” she explained. “And I…”

She stopped for a moment, her hand still on Agatha’s face, though now motionless.

“I didn’t want to lose you.” The alpha took a deep breath.

“I didn’t want to scare you away, Agatha.” Her voice came out lower, rougher. “I was afraid to talk to you… and have you simply… leave.”

Agatha breathed deeply, absorbing every word like someone trying to reorganize her entire world from the inside out. The silence between them wasn’t empty — it was dense, heavy with everything still being understood.

Rio took her hand then.

Firmly, as if it were the only stable thing in that moment. She brought Agatha’s hand to her face, kissing her palm slowly, closing her eyes for a second while inhaling her scent as if she needed it to stay standing.

Dependent.

More than she would ever admit in any other context.

“I don’t want to lose you…” her voice came out low, breaking halfway through. “I don’t want to lose our cubs…”

Agatha swallowed hard, her chest tightening.

“Then why tell me now, Rio…?”

Rio exhaled slowly, still holding her hand.

“Jessica found out,” she said bluntly. “And… soon… my grandmother might find out too.”

Agatha’s body stiffened slightly.

“And how will she know it’s me?”

Rio slowly shook her head, a short humorless laugh escaping her.

“She’ll find out…” she murmured. “She always finds out.”

And then the tears came without warning, running down Rio’s face in a raw, almost unsettling way.

“I need to protect you…” her voice broke. “I need to…”

This time Agatha didn’t hesitate.

She sat up in bed, pulling Rio closer, guiding her body onto her lap with a firmness that left no room for retreat.

“I’m not afraid of that old woman.”

Rio let herself be pulled in, but her gaze remained restless.

“But I am,” she answered almost immediately. “Because if you don’t give in… she’ll interfere in your life. And I…” her breathing failed for a second, “I can’t live knowing she’s trying to hurt you. I’ll lose my mind if she does anything to you.”

Agatha frowned, trying to grasp the scale of it.

“What could she even do…?”

Rio looked away.

“I don’t know…” she admitted. “Legally… a lot of things.”

Agatha felt her heart speed up.

“She’s going to try to take my babies away from me?”

Rio stood up immediately, as if the question had triggered something.

“Rio…” Agatha insisted, firmer now. “Answer me.”

The tension in the air was almost tangible.

“You weren’t afraid of me leaving for no reason…” Agatha’s voice lowered, more serious now. “So tell me.”

Rio stood with her back turned for a moment, breathing deeply, trying to organize something she had clearly never said out loud like this before.

Then she turned around, her eyes red from crying.

“I didn’t want children before…” she began, her voice low but steady. “Because I wanted to end her bloodline.”

“But…” she took a deep breath, dragging a hand over her face, “every Vidal child… by law… is born belonging to the Vidal family.”

Agatha stayed silent, processing.

“As if they were… property,” Rio continued, her voice heavy with bitterness. “An object.”

She laughed, but there was nothing light in it.

“Legally… there are places I’m not even allowed to go because of my last name. I can’t choose where to study… where to live…”

Her eyes returned to Agatha’s, intense, almost painful.

“I’m a product of this industry.”

The pause that followed was short.

“I was never a person,” she finished more quietly. “I was always something that belonged to a family dynamic, someone who had to continue everything.”

Rio calmed herself as she walked back to the bed and sat beside Agatha.

“And I can’t let the same thing happen to them,” she said gently, touching Agatha’s stomach. “And I won’t give them the same fate I had.”

Agatha took a deep breath, holding one of Rio’s hands. The alpha pulled her into an embrace, breathing deeply.

Despite everything, Agatha had never felt so protected by anyone. Rio being willing to fight for their cubs before they had even been born felt almost unreal.

“And do you have any idea?” Agatha asked.

“Yes… I hired a detective to investigate some documents about my family. He found a few things I already knew… but there’s something strange in my grandmother Claire’s will…”

“What do you mean? Claire?”

“Yes. Rita inherited the Vidal companies through marriage. She isn’t a blood-born Vidal… anyway, my Abuela Claire died giving birth to my mother, and that changed Rita forever.”

Agatha nodded, still listening silently.

“But the detective found two versions of the will. One said the family power would pass directly to the next living descendant — meaning my mother… and another saying an alpha regent was necessary to control the company and finances…”

Rio’s jaw tightened slightly.

“It was changed two days before my grandmother died.”

Agatha blinked.

“Why would your grandmother change her own will like that? That’s a drastic change… did she already know her life was at risk?”

Rio sighed.

“According to the medical reports the detective found… Grandma had a normal pregnancy. No risk.”

Agatha covered her mouth in disbelief.

“You think Rita killed her own wife?” she asked quietly, like someone sharing a secret.

“I don’t know… I don’t think so… Abuela Claire was a forbidden subject. Not even my mother talked about her… not that she had much to say, considering… she never even met her.”

“And how safe is it for you to investigate this, Rio?”

“I don’t know… but I don’t care. I want to protect our children.”

Agatha nodded.

“Your safety matters too… I don’t want them growing up without you.”

“Really?”

“Of course…”

Rio’s smile didn’t fully come at first. It started small, almost unaccustomed, as if she still didn’t quite know what to do with something as simple as being wanted there.

Even so, the smile stayed.

“Of course…” Agatha repeated, quieter now, closer.

Rio tilted her face slightly, watching Agatha for a few seconds with almost reverent attention, as if trying to memorize every detail of her in that exact moment. The way her hair was still slightly damp, the exhausted shine in her eyes, the way her hand remained over her own stomach instinctively.

Then Rio moved, unhurried, wrapping Agatha in another embrace, this one slower, more conscious, pulling her against herself while closing her eyes for a brief second.

Rio’s hands slid up Agatha’s back in a firm, protective gesture, almost possessive — not in the sense of dominance, but in the sense of care.

Her hands moved carefully over Agatha’s body until they reached her stomach again, gently pulling aside the already open fabric of the robe. Her gaze softened in a way that was almost unrecognizable, far removed from everything she showed the world.

Rio leaned down and left slow kisses against Agatha’s stomach, lingering and tender.

Rio’s body relaxed in an almost imperceptible, but deep way. Her forehead rested against Agatha’s stomach, and a small smile — real, vulnerable — appeared without her realizing it.

“I’m going to protect you,” she murmured softly, her voice nearly breaking at the end. “No matter what it costs.”

Rio slowly rose, still far too close, as if any inch of distance would be a mistake. Her fingers found Agatha’s hand naturally, intertwining with it before bringing it to her lips. The kiss came soft at first… almost reverent.

Agatha sighed.

This time there was no doubt about what she felt — the heat between them wasn’t only emotional. It was firm, growing, pulsing low in her belly like an inevitable response to Rio’s presence.

“So beautiful…” Agatha’s voice came out low, heavy, while her free fingers slid up to Rio’s chin. “Completely surrendered to me…”

“All yours…” Rio answered in a whisper that sounded more like a confession than a sentence.

Agatha tightened her grip on Rio’s chin then, tilting her face into the right position before kissing her.

Rio shivered all over.

Her hands automatically slid up the sides of Agatha’s body, firm, but without breaking the rhythm Agatha dictated. When the kiss finally broke, it was only for air, and even then only for the briefest second.

Agatha’s hand still held Rio’s chin, whose pupils were now completely blown wide. Rio’s nose brushed against Agatha’s fingertips, still searching greedily for her scent.

“Be a good boy… and satisfy me.”

◆

Morning light filtered through the pale curtains of the new apartment in soft golden tones, completely different from the brutal storm of the night before.

The bedroom still carried their scent mixed into the tangled sheets and rain, pheromones, and Rio’s woody perfume soaked into the pillow.

Agatha woke slowly, her entire body felt heavy, loose, deliciously exhausted.

She let out a small sigh, stretching lazily against the mattress while the sheet slid across her warm skin. Her rounded belly moved with the gesture, the babies restless as if they’d already been awake for a long time.

For a few seconds she simply stayed there, still lost somewhere between sleep and reality, feeling the comfortable silence of the apartment.

Then she reached toward the other side of the bed, empty. Agatha opened her eyes a little more, frowning sleepily. Her fingers touched only the lingering warmth where Rio should have been.

She sat up slowly, pulling the sheet against her naked body before resting one hand beneath her belly, still trying to fully wake up.

The bedroom looked different in daylight. The stacked moving boxes in the corner, the dresser still partially empty, the smell of fresh paint blending into the room.

Everything was new.

Agatha ran her fingers through her messy hair and then heard sounds coming from the kitchen.

Cabinet doors opening. Something being shut.

She stood slowly, wrapping the sheet around herself before giving up halfway and simply grabbing the robe tossed near the bed. She slipped it on without hurry, still sleepy, walking barefoot down the hallway.

When she entered the kitchen, she found Rio standing with her back turned, opening one of the upper cabinets with an expression of deep concentration, as if she were trying to solve an impossible mystery.

The alpha wore only a pair of dark sweatpants hanging low on her hips and a simple black T-shirt that probably belonged to Agatha, stretched too tightly across her broad shoulders.

Agatha leaned against the doorway, watching the scene for a few seconds before speaking, her voice rough with sleep.

“Good morning.”

Rio turned immediately and smiled — not the elegant, controlled smile she offered the world, but a real one, warm and almost youthful.

“Good morning,” she answered, shutting the empty cabinet with a certain defeat. “I wanted to be romantic… but you literally don’t have any food yet, so I think breakfast in bed will have to wait.”

Agatha let out a muffled little laugh.

The scene was absurd.

Rio Vidal — one of the most intimidating alphas she had ever known — clearly lost because she couldn’t put together breakfast.

“At least do we have coffee?” Agatha asked, walking toward her.

“We have water.”

Agatha laughed for real this time.

Rio opened her arms immediately, as if it were automatic, and Agatha stepped into them without thinking. The alpha’s body was still warm from a recent shower, far too comforting against hers.

Rio wrapped her arms around Agatha’s waist instantly.

Her large hands slid slowly across Agatha’s back while she lowered her face to kiss her forehead lingeringly.

“I’m gonna go out real quick and buy something for you to eat,” she murmured near her hair. “And vitamins. And those fruits you like.”

Agatha simply nodded, still clinging to her.

She didn’t want to let go.

It was strange realizing that so clearly. Strange how her body seemed to relax automatically inside Rio’s arms.

The alpha absentmindedly caressed her belly over the robe.

“Did they sleep well?” she asked softly.

Agatha smiled when she felt a small movement beneath her stomach.

“I think so… they’re calmer today.”

Rio closed her eyes for a second at that, as if that simple information rearranged something inside her. Then she tilted her head and kissed Agatha slowly and affectionately.

When they pulled apart, Agatha rested her forehead against Rio’s for a few more seconds, enjoying the moment, the silence, and the feeling of finally not being alone.

After a few minutes, Rio left to buy something for them to eat, and Agatha finally decided to shower.

Cool water ran slowly down Agatha’s body, washing away the heat of the night, the emotional weight of the last hours, and the exhaustion lodged deep in her chest.

She stood beneath the spray for several seconds, breathing deeply, feeling her muscles gradually relax.

When she stepped out, she wrapped a towel around her damp hair and walked back to the bedroom, still sleepy. The light robe only partially covered her body while she arranged some clothes on the messy bed.

Then the doorbell rang.

Three times in a row.

Agatha let out an automatic sigh, almost smiling.

“Rio…” she murmured to herself, shaking her head. “You definitely forgot the lock code.”

She kept drying her hair with the towel as she walked through the apartment. Her bare feet touched the cold floor of the living room, and Agatha unlocked the door already prepared to tease Rio.

But she froze, the second she opened the door, the world simply stopped. Standing there, perfectly still despite the cold morning wind, was Rita Vidal.

Elegant and untouchable, wearing a dark impeccably tailored coat, leather gloves, and a smile far too small to be kind. Her gray hair pinned perfectly in place made the woman seem even harsher, like a figure sculpted specifically to intimidate.

And it worked.

Rita’s eyes slowly traveled over Agatha, from the towel wrapped around her hair, to the loosely tied robe, and finally to the belly already visible beneath the fabric.

Her smile widened just slightly.

“Good morning, dear…”

Agatha felt her blood run cold and remained standing there for several seconds, completely motionless.

The robe was still slightly damp from the shower, the towel around her hair beginning to slip over one shoulder, but she barely noticed.

Agatha breathed deeply, feeling her heart slam violently against her ribs while Rita remained there, elegant as an old painting.

The dark coat perfectly fitted to her body, the immaculate hair despite the damp morning air, the expensive perfume filling the narrow hallway of Agatha’s modest building in a way that felt almost invasive.

She looked wrong there, like a predator inside a place too small for it.

“May I come in?” Rita repeated calmly. “Or do you intend to leave me in the hallway so the neighbors can overhear a conversation that, frankly, does not concern them?”

Agatha swallowed hard.

She should slam the door. She should call Rio. She should do something. But the problem was that Rita Vidal did not look like someone who accepted closed doors.

So Agatha simply stepped aside slowly.

“Come in.”

Rita smiled faintly, Victory. That was exactly what Agatha saw in that discreet smile.

The older alpha entered the apartment, observing everything without any hurry.

Her eyes moved over the moving boxes, the furniture still out of place, the folded sheets draped over a chair, the partially dismantled kitchen.

Analyzing, cataloguing, as if every detail revealed something about Agatha.

The door shut behind her with a soft click, and the silence that followed was suffocating.

Rita walked slowly toward the center of the room, dragging her fingers along the kitchen counter before finally looking at Agatha again.

“So this is where my great-grandchildren are living?”

The sentence made Agatha’s stomach twist.

“My great-grandchildren.”

Not “your children.” Not “the babies.”

My great-grandchildren.

Like possession. Like an irrevocable right of ownership.

Agatha tightened the robe instinctively over her stomach, and Rita noticed that too. Her eyes lowered briefly toward the belly hidden beneath the fabric, and something shifted in her expression.

Not softness, never softness, but attention and interest.

“How many months?” she asked.

Agatha hesitated.

“Six.”

Rita nodded slowly.

“Twins… from what I’ve heard.” Agatha’s blood turned cold.

Heard.

That meant Rita already knew far more than she should. Far more.

“What do you want?” Agatha finally asked, her voice sounding steadier than she felt inside.

Rita looked around the apartment again.

“To talk.”

“About what?”

“About Rio, about you, and about these children.” She tilted her head slightly. “About the disaster my granddaughter created.”

Something inside Agatha hardened immediately. Because she could accept fear. She could accept intimidation. But not that. Not like that.

“Don’t talk about her like that.”

Rita raised one eyebrow, mildly surprised, and strangely, that gave Agatha courage.

“You don’t understand what’s happening,” Rita replied with terrifying calm. “Rio doesn’t know what she’s doing.”

“She knows exactly.”

“No.” Rita interrupted her. “Rio is in love.”

The word crossed the apartment without the slightest shame.

Agatha went completely still, and Rita continued watching every microexpression on her face as if she were dismantling her mind piece by piece.

“And people in love make bad decisions. Especially the Vidals.”

Agatha took a deep breath, trying to control her body and her breathing.

 

Chapter 13: An Agreement

 

 

┕──━──━─┑◆┍─━──━──┙

Rita Vidal’s presence seemed to take up too much space inside the small apartment. She walked slowly through the living room still filled with moving boxes, resting her cane against the light wooden floor with soft rhythmic taps. The gray morning light crossed through the wide windows, illuminating a place far too simple for someone like her.

Agatha remained standing near the kitchen, still discreetly holding the belt of her robe over her stomach. Something inside her screamed danger, but she would not show fear. Rita let out a small sigh as she observed the place around her.

“I came in peace,” she said calmly, turning her gaze toward Agatha. “Seriously… actually, I should thank you.”

Agatha frowned slightly.

“Thank me?”

“Yes…” Rita replied with unsettling naturalness. “Even though you’re not… from a renowned family.” She waved one hand dismissively through the air, brushing it aside like an irrelevant detail. “You ruined my granddaughter’s ridiculous plans of ending my bloodline.”

Rita then slowly approached, stopping far too close. Elegant fingers touched a damp strand of Agatha’s hair, slowly sliding through it.

“And you’re beautiful…” she commented, studying her face as if analyzing a rare piece of art. “You’ll make good wife material in the future.”

A deeply uncomfortable shiver ran through Agatha’s entire body, but at the same time… there was something dangerously seductive in the way Rita spoke. That woman’s approval seemed to carry too much weight.

“That’s why I’m coming here as a friend,” Rita concluded, releasing the strand of hair.

Agatha took a deep breath.

“What do you want?”

Rita smiled faintly and walked toward the armchair in the living room, sitting down elegantly as she rested both hands over the cane.

“I want to make a proposal.” She lifted her gaze. “Sit.”

Agatha hesitated for a second before sitting in the armchair across from her.

“Having my friendship within this family is something important for you,” Rita began calmly. “So I expect you to listen and use your brain to understand that having me as an ally is much better than having me as an enemy.”

Agatha held her gaze.

“I’m listening.” Rita nodded slightly.

“You know Vidal Industries is currently the largest corporation in the world. We are everywhere. Cars, electronics, banking systems… even in that cellphone right there.”

“I know that…”

“I need someone managing the Vidal company headquarters in Europe.” Rita slowly crossed her legs. “And that person is my granddaughter.”

Agatha’s stomach tightened.

“But Rio doesn’t want to leave whatever she has here… in this place.” Rita gestured around with subtle disdain. “And that’s where you come in.”

Agatha stayed silent, feeling the weight of it slowly forming. Then Rita finally spoke:

“Convince Rio to leave everything behind and move to Paris… and I’ll let you live in peace.”

The words hit Agatha like a silent punch, because “leave everything behind” meant slowly destroying Rio. It meant taking her away from her mother, her sisters, her friends, and the life she built — from the place where memories of her father might still exist.

Erase everything, and turn Rio back into that family’s property again, all to benefit the very woman who had destroyed parts of her.

Agatha inhaled deeply, then slowly stood up.

“Look, ma’am… with all due respect… I don’t know what kind of woman you think I am, but I would never make Rio leave everything she loves to live far away from everyone she loves.” Her voice came out firm now. “And you, as her grandmother, should prioritize your granddaughter’s happiness.”

Rita stared at her silently for a few seconds before offering a cold, pitying smile, like someone watching a puppy rolling around on the floor.

“Love? That’s ridiculous. Love does not move the world.”

She stood up, leaning on her cane.

“You’re being naïve. Do you think Rio will stay in love with you forever?” Rita tilted her head slightly. “Wait and see. The moment you give birth to those children… that love will be over.”

“Maybe you’re right. Maybe love really doesn’t move companies or the world all the time,” Agatha said while staring directly at Rita. “But it exists. And what Rio and I feel is real.”

Rita remained still, and Agatha continued:

“And it’s a shame you can’t feel anything inside yourself anymore.”

“You still don’t understand the scale of what’s happening here. My granddaughter is not an ordinary woman, girl. She cannot simply disappear into a domestic life playing happy family inside a rented apartment.”

The words sliced through the room like thin blades. Agatha felt her jaw tighten, and Rita continued slowly walking through the living room, observing details far too small for someone used to Vidal mansions.

All of this seemed offensive to her.

“Rio was raised to command entire continents,” Rita continued, dragging her fingers along the side of one of the moving boxes. “She was born to stand above people. Not beside them.”

Agatha took a deep breath.

“Maybe that’s exactly the problem.”

Rita stopped. Her eyes slowly found Agatha’s again, and Agatha realized it immediately, she had crossed some invisible line, but she did not back down.

“You talk about her as if she were a company. An investment, not someone who feels things.”

Rita tilted her head slightly.

“Feelings are useful when controlled.”

“And destructive when buried,” Agatha shot back immediately.

The heavy silence returned. Rita then slowly approached, very slowly, until she stopped close enough for Agatha to smell her expensive perfume.

“You think you’re special because you managed to access parts of her no one else ever did,” she said quietly. “But let me tell you something about Rio Vidal…”

The older alpha slowly raised one hand and lightly touched her own cane.

“People like her love obsessively. Violently. Dependently. And then… they destroy everything around them trying to protect what they love.”

A chill crawled up Agatha’s spine because deep down, she knew. She had already seen it.

Seen it in Rio’s eyes, in the way she held her waist, and in the way her scent changed near the babies.

In the desperate devotion.

Rita noticed Agatha’s small silence and smiled faintly.

“Ah… so you’ve already noticed.”

Agatha swallowed hard.

“You talk about her like she’s a monster.”

“No.” Rita replied calmly. “I turned her into one.”

The air vanished from Agatha’s lungs for a moment. The brutal honesty of that sentence was worse than any threat. Rita then lowered her gaze toward Agatha’s stomach.

And her eyes hardened ever so slightly.

“But now heirs are involved. And that changes everything.”

Instinctively, Agatha stepped half a pace back, her hand tightening around the fabric of the robe over her belly. Rita noticed the movement immediately.

“Relax. If I wanted to take something from you… I wouldn’t come personally.”

Agatha’s heart pounded even harder.

“Then why did you come?”

Rita remained silent for a few seconds, and for the first time she seemed tired. Truly tired.

“Because Rio never stood against me for anyone before.”

The words caught Agatha off guard. Rita watched the thin rain sliding down the windows as she spoke.

“She always obeyed in the end. She always returned to where she belonged.” Her fingers tightened slightly around the cane. “But you…”

Her eyes slowly returned to Agatha.

“You made my granddaughter choose.”

Before Agatha could answer, the sound of the electronic lock echoed through the apartment.

The door had barely finished opening before Rio froze in place.

The bakery bag was still hanging from her fingers, raindrops sliding from her dark coat onto the entryway floor. Her eyes immediately found Rita.

Right there, moving around inside the apartment as if the apartment belonged to her too.

The air changed instantly, and Agatha saw the exact moment Rio’s entire body tensed. Her shoulders hardened, her jaw locked, and something extremely cold crossed the alpha’s gaze.

The bag dropped onto the kitchen counter carelessly.

“What are you doing here?”

Rita remained calm, elegantly leaning on her cane.

“Good morning to you too, dear.”

Rio didn’t even blink.

“I asked what you’re doing here.”

Agatha felt her own heart accelerate when Rio immediately walked over to her, instinctively positioning herself between them. The alpha’s hand slid directly to her waist, firm, possessive, as if she needed to confirm Agatha was still there.

Rita observed the gesture and smiled faintly.

“Ah… so it’s really serious.”

“Don’t speak to her like that,” Rio snapped immediately.

Silence fell heavily. Agatha could feel Rio’s body hot against hers, tense like a wire about to snap. Rita sat down on the couch and slowly crossed her legs.

“I only came to meet the mother of my great-grandchildren.”

Rio let out a short laugh devoid of humor.

“You have no rights over them.”

“Legally?” Rita arched an eyebrow. “I have several.”

Rio’s arm immediately tightened around Agatha’s waist.

“You are not touching them.”

“Rio—”

“No.” She cut her off without taking her eyes off her grandmother. “You are not going to do to them what you did to me.”

The atmosphere seemed to darken completely. Rita remained silent for a few seconds, observing her granddaughter as though analyzing a defective object.

“You’re still too emotional.”

“And you still think fear equals respect.”

The words came quickly.

Agatha felt her chest tighten when she realized this was not a normal argument. There was an entire lifetime of hidden wounds buried inside it.

Rita slowly stood up again. Even with the cane, she still looked intimidating.

“I’m trying to avoid an unnecessary war.”

“Funny,” Rio replied immediately. “Because showing up at my omega’s apartment without warning sounds exactly like a threat.”

Rita’s gaze slowly dropped to Rio’s hand around Agatha’s waist, then to her stomach, and something dangerously calculated crossed the older alpha’s eyes.

“Do you really think you can protect them from everything?”

Rio did not answer, but Agatha felt her arm tremble slightly with rage, fear, and desperation all at once. Rita noticed too.

Of course she did.

“You’re exactly like your mother,” she commented coldly. “Thinking love is enough to survive inside this family.”

Rio went pale instantly, as if she had been physically struck. Agatha saw it, and that was enough.

“That’s enough,” she said firmly for the first time.

Both women looked at her. Agatha took a deep breath despite feeling her heart racing.

“I let you in here because I thought maybe there was some humanity left inside you. But all you do is try to control Rio through fear.”

Rita remained still, and Agatha continued.

“And you know the worst part? You talk as if you love her… but all I can see is someone terrified because she finally lost control over her own granddaughter.”

The apartment sank into absolute silence.

Rio slowly turned her face toward Agatha, and her expression completely crumbled, because no one had ever stood in front of her to protect her from Rita Vidal.

Agatha held Rita’s gaze even while feeling her heart pound too hard against her chest. Her hands still trembled faintly beneath the robe, but she did not back down.

Not this time.

“I think you should leave now,” she said firmly, every word carefully controlled. “Immediately.”

Rita remained standing in the middle of the living room for a few seconds. The silence was suffocating.

The cold morning light crossed through the apartment windows, casting soft shadows across the wooden floor and partially illuminating the older alpha’s face.

She observed Agatha carefully, as if reevaluating something. She studied her for a long moment before a small smile appeared on her lips.

Rita’s elegant fingers rose to the antique necklace resting against her throat. The golden jewelry swayed faintly as she caressed the ruby pendant with her thumb.

“Interesting…” she murmured quietly. “You remind me of my dear Claire.”

The name cut through Rio like a blade. Agatha immediately felt the alpha’s body stiffen beside her.

“Don’t do this,” Rio said in a voice far too low.

Rita tilted her head slightly.

“Do what, dear?” she asked calmly. “I’m complimenting your partner.”

Agatha inhaled deeply once again, trying to ignore the discomfort growing inside her chest. Partner. The word sounded strange coming from her, wrong and artificial.

“Ma’am,” she called firmly. “I’m serious. Leave my house.” For a brief moment, something cold crossed the older alpha’s eyes.

“You still don’t understand the kind of world you’ve stepped into, Agatha.”

She took a slow step toward the door.

“And honestly? I hope you continue not understanding it for a little while longer.”

Rio immediately moved, opening space for her grandmother to pass, but without letting go of Agatha for even a second in a protective and territorial way. Of course Rita noticed that.

She stopped near the exit and partially turned her face.

“Our conversation isn’t over yet.”

That was directed straight at Agatha, a warning, not a promise, Agatha felt Rio’s arm tighten around her waist immediately.

“Yes, it is,” Rio shot back, her voice dangerously low. “You’re not coming back here without going through me first.”

Rita merely arched one eyebrow, then her eyes slid one last time toward Agatha’s stomach. A terrible chill crawled up the omega’s spine and Rio noticed, she immediately positioned herself even more in front of her. Rita let out a disappointed sigh.

“You still act like a wounded animal,” she commented to Rio. “It’s exhausting.”

“And you still act like a monster.”

This time Rita didn’t answer. She simply opened the door. The sound of the weak rain outside invaded the apartment for a moment along with the cold hallway wind.

Then she left without looking back. The door closed, and the silence left behind somehow felt worse than her actual presence.

Rio remained motionless for a few seconds, breathing deeply as if her entire body was still prepared for war.

Then she turned toward Agatha, and all the firmness simply cracked apart.

“Did she touch you?” she asked immediately, her voice breaking for the first time. “Did she say something absurd? Did she threaten you? Agatha… look at me… did she do anything to you?”

Agatha took a deep breath before finally managing to speak. Her hands were still trembling slightly as she pulled the robe tighter around her body.

“She didn’t hurt me… she…” the omega sighed, slowly walking to the couch before sitting down. “She came to talk about you.”

“About me?” Rio asked, stunned, immediately walking toward the open kitchen.

She grabbed a glass of water automatically, filling it too quickly before handing it to Agatha along with the bottle. Agatha held the glass with both hands.

“She knows everything, Rio,” she said quietly. “About us… about the twins… everything.”

Rio froze. Her jaw tightened subtly, and Agatha saw the exact moment panic started crossing her eyes.

“What did she say, Agatha?”

Agatha took a small sip of water before answering:

“She asked me to convince you to leave everything and move to Paris… to take over the company headquarters there.” She let out a nervous, disbelieving laugh. 

“She wanted me to be her ‘friend’ and help with that plan.”

Rio closed her eyes for a second, exhausted, as if that was exactly the kind of move she expected from Rita.

“I refused, obviously.” Rio’s eyes immediately opened toward her.

“You refused?” Agatha frowned slightly.

“Do you really think I’d make you abandon everything you love because of her?”

The question hit Rio painfully, because for a few seconds the alpha simply stared at Agatha in silence, as if she didn’t know exactly what to do with that kind of loyalty. Then a small smile appeared on her lips.

“No…” she murmured quietly. “I don’t.”

She finally walked to the couch and sat beside Agatha. Rio slowly slid her hand to her stomach, touching her belly over the robe fabric as if she needed that contact to stabilize herself again.

Agatha immediately noticed Rio’s breathing slowly calming beneath her hand.

“I’m going to have the building security reinforced… and she won’t have access here anymore,” Rio said, firmer now. “She’s not going to do anything insane… at least not for now.”

Agatha silently observed her profile. Rio looked tired. More than tired.

She looked like someone trying to stop her own life from collapsing piece by piece while still holding enough composure to protect everyone else.

“And in a way…” Rio continued, slowly caressing Agatha’s stomach in distracted circles, “I’m already speaking with lawyers.”

Agatha felt her stomach sink slightly.

“Lawyers?”

Rio nodded slowly.

“If Rita wants to turn this into a war… I need to be prepared before she makes the first move.”

Rio remained silent for a few seconds after mentioning the lawyers.

Her hand still slowly caressed Agatha’s belly now in an unconscious gesture, as if she needed to feel the babies there to stay rational.

Then she inhaled deeply, like someone gathering the courage to say something she had been thinking for a very long time.

“And if nothing works…” she began quietly. “I’ll leave like she wants.”

Agatha immediately turned toward her.

“Rio…”
